## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Question 1** `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`

**Define pinch-off voltage. Distinguish between $V_P$ and $V_{DS}(\text{pinch-off})$ for a JFET.**

**Model Answer**:

**Pinch-off voltage ($V_P$)** is the reverse gate-to-source voltage at which the drain current $I_D$ becomes zero, i.e., the conductive channel is completely depleted of mobile charge carriers.

**Distinction**:

| Parameter | $V_P$ (Gate Pinch-Off) | $V_{DS}(\text{pinch-off})$ (Drain Pinch-Off) |
|-----------|------------------------|-----------------------------------------------|
| **Definition** | $V_{GS}$ at which $I_D = 0$ | Minimum $V_{DS}$ at which channel pinches off at drain end |
| **Terminal Pair** | Gate-Source | Drain-Source |
| **Formula** | $V_{GS(\text{off})}$ | $V_{DS}(\text{sat}) = V_{GS} - V_P$ |
| **Typical Value** | $-4$ V (n-channel) | e.g., $+2$ V when $V_{GS} = -2$ V |

---

**Question 2** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**List any three advantages and two disadvantages of the fixed $V_{GS}$ biasing circuit for a JFET.**

**Model Answer**:

**Advantages**:
1. **Simplicity**: Only requires a single gate resistor and a fixed DC supply.
2. **Predictable Q-point**: The drain current $I_D$ is independent of $R_D$.
3. **Compact layout**: Fewer components make it suitable for IC fabrication.

**Disadvantages**:
1. **Poor thermal stability**: Stability factor $S > 1$, so $I_D$ drifts with temperature-induced $I_{DSS}$ changes.
2. **Requires dual supply** ($V_{DD}$ and $V_{GG}$): Increases circuit complexity in some applications.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout**:
> - Always state the **Shockley's equation explicitly** before substituting numerical values (1 mark lost if missed).
> - Sign convention for $V_P$ must be **explicitly stated** (n-channel = negative). Forgetting the negative sign leads to **wrong Q-point calculation**.
> - In fixed-bias problems, many students incorrectly assume $V_{GS} = 0$ even when $V_{GG}$ is non-zero. **Always check** whether the gate is tied to a separate bias supply.
> - Stability factor derivation requires showing the **differential step**; skipping it loses 3 marks.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - July 2024]`

**(a)** For an n-channel JFET with $I_{DSS} = 12$ mA and $V_P = -6$ V, design a **fixed $V_{GS}$ bias circuit** to establish a quiescent drain current of $I_{DQ} = 3$ mA. Assume $V_{DD} = 20$ V and $R_D = 2.2$ k$\Omega$. Calculate:
   - (i) The required gate supply voltage $V_{GG}$
   - (ii) The quiescent drain-to-source voltage $V_{DSQ}$
   - (iii) The maximum allowable $V_{GG}$ before pinch-off

**(b)** Derive the **stability factor** for the fixed $V_{GS}$ bias configuration and comment on its thermal stability.

---

#### **Model Solution for Question A**:

**Part (a):**

**(i) Required $V_{GG}$:**

From Shockley's Equation:

$$
I_{DQ} = I_{DSS}\left(1 - \frac{V_{GG}}{V_P}\right)^2
$$

Substituting known values:

$$
3 \text{ mA} = 12 \text{ mA} \times \left(1 - \frac{V_{GG}}{-6}\right)^2
$$

$$
\frac{3}{12} = \left(1 + \frac{V_{GG}}{6}\right)^2
$$

$$
0.25 = \left(1 + \frac{V_{GG}}{6}\right)^2
$$

Taking the square root (positive root):

$$
0.5 = 1 + \frac{V_{GG}}{6}
$$

$$
\frac{V_{GG}}{6} = -0.5
$$

$$
\boxed{V_{GG} = -3 \text{ V}}
$$

**(ii) Quiescent Drain-to-Source Voltage:**

$$
V_{DSQ} = V_{DD} - I_{DQ} \cdot R_D = 20 \text{ V} - (3 \text{ mA})(2.2 \text{ k}\Omega)
$$

$$
V_{DSQ} = 20 \text{ V} - 6.6 \text{ V}
$$

$$
\boxed{V_{DSQ} = 13.4 \text{ V}}
$$

**(iii) Maximum $V_{GG}$ (Pinch-Off Condition):**

The pinch-off condition for $I_D = 0$ is $V_{GS} = V_P = -6$ V. Since $V_{GS} = V_{GG}$:

$$
\boxed{V_{GG(\max)} = -6 \text{ V}}
$$

---

**Part (b): Derivation of Stability Factor**

**Step 1**: Definition of stability factor:

$$
S = \frac{\partial I_D / I_D}{\partial I_{DSS} / I_{DSS}}
$$

**Step 2**: Shockley's equation:

$$
I_D = I_{DSS}\left(1 - \frac{V_{GS}}{V_P}\right)^2
$$

**Step 3**: Differentiate both sides w.r.t. $I_{DSS}$ (treating $V_{GS}$, $V_P$ as constants since $V_{GS}$ is fixed):

$$
\frac{\partial I_D}{\partial I_{DSS}} = \left(1 - \frac{V_{GS}}{V_P}\right)^2
$$

**Step 4**: From Shockley's equation:

$$
\frac{I_D}{I_{DSS}} = \left(1 - \frac{V_{GS}}{V_P}\right)^2
$$

**Step 5**: Substitute into the stability factor definition:

$$
S = \frac{\partial I_D}{\partial I_{DSS}} \cdot \frac{I_{DSS}}{I_D} = \left(1 - \frac{V_{GS}}{V_P}\right)^2 \cdot \frac{1}{\left(1 - \frac{V_{GS}}{V_P}\right)^2}
$$

**Step 6**: Simplify:

$$
\boxed{S = \frac{1}{1 - \frac{V_{GS}}{V_P}}}
$$

**Comment on Thermal Stability**:

Since for proper biasing, $0 < \left(1 - \dfrac{V_{GS}}{V_P}\right) < 1$, we always have **$S > 1$**. This implies that **any percentage change in $I_{DSS}$ (due to temperature variation) results in a larger percentage change in $I_D$**.

For example, in the circuit above with $V_{GS} = -3$ V and $V_P = -6$ V:

$$
S = \frac{1}{1 - \frac{-3}{-6}} = \frac{1}{1 - 0.5} = 2
$$

A 10% drift in $I_{DSS}$ causes a 20% drift in $I_D$. This makes the **fixed $V_{GS}$ bias the LEAST thermally stable** among the JFET biasing topologies, requiring careful thermal compensation in precision applications.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a)** With the help of a neat circuit diagram, explain the operation of a **fixed $V_{GS}$ bias circuit** for an n-channel JFET. Derive the expressions for $I_{DQ}$ and $V_{DSQ}$.

**(b)** An n-channel JFET has the following specifications: $I_{DSS} = 8$ mA, $V_P = -3$ V. The fixed-bias circuit uses $V_{GG} = -1$ V, $V_{DD} = 15$ V, and $R_D = 1.5$ k$\Omega$. Calculate:
   - (i) The Q-point ($I_{DQ}$, $V_{DSQ}$)
   - (ii) The transconductance $g_m$ at the Q-point
   - (iii) The stability factor and a comment on its value

---

#### **Model Solution for Question B**:

**Part (a): Circuit Operation and Derivation**

**Circuit Diagram Description**:

The fixed $V_{GS}$ bias circuit consists of:
- An n-channel JFET with drain connected to $V_{DD}$ through $R_D$
- Gate connected to $V_{GG}$ (negative supply) through $R_G$
- Source terminal **directly grounded** (no source resistor in pure fixed-bias topology)

**Operation**:

Since the JFET gate is a reverse-biased p-n junction, the gate current $I_G$ is effectively zero (in the order of nA or pA). Therefore, there is no voltage drop across $R_G$, and the gate voltage equals $V_{GG}$. With the source at ground potential:

$$
V_{GS} = V_G - V_S = V_{GG} - 0 = V_{GG}
$$

The negative $V_{GS}$ reverse-biases the gate-channel junction, establishing a depletion region of fixed width and consequently a fixed $I_D$ given by Shockley's equation:

$$
I_{DQ} = I_{DSS}\left(1 - \frac{V_{GG}}{V_P}\right)^2
$$

Applying KVL at the drain loop:

$$
V_{DSQ} = V_{DD} - I_{DQ} \cdot R_D
$$

---

**Part (b): Numerical Solution**

**(i) Q-Point Calculation**

First, compute $I_{DQ}$:

$$
I_{DQ} = I_{DSS}\left(1 - \frac{V_{GG}}{V_P}\right)^2 = 8 \text{ mA} \times \left(1 - \frac{-1}{-3}\right)^2
$$

$$
= 8 \text{ mA} \times \left(1 - \frac{1}{3}\right)^2 = 8 \text{ mA} \times \left(\frac{2}{3}\right)^2 = 8 \text{ mA} \times \frac{4}{9}
$$

$$
I_{DQ} = \frac{32}{9} \text{ mA} \approx 3.556 \text{ mA}
$$

Then compute $V_{DSQ}$:

$$
V_{DSQ} = V_{DD} - I_{DQ} R_D = 15 \text{ V} - (3.556 \text{ mA})(1.5 \text{ k}\Omega)
$$

$$
= 15 \text{ V} - 5.333 \text{ V}
$$

$$
\boxed{V_{DSQ} \approx 9.667 \text{ V}}
$$

$$
\boxed{I_{DQ} \approx 3.556 \text{ mA}, \quad V_{DSQ} \approx 9.67 \text{ V}}
$$

**(ii) Transconductance at Q-point**

Maximum transconductance:

$$
g_{m0} = \frac{2 I_{DSS}}{\vert V_P \vert} = \frac{2 \times 8 \text{ mA}}{3 \text{ V}} = \frac{16}{3} \text{ mS} \approx 5.333 \text{ mS}
$$

Operating transconductance:

$$
g_m = g_{m0}\left(1 - \frac{V_{GS}}{V_P}\right) = 5.333 \text{ mS} \times \left(1 - \frac{1}{3}\right) = 5.333 \text{ mS} \times \frac{2}{3}
$$

$$
\boxed{g_m \approx 3.556 \text{ mS}}
$$

**(iii) Stability Factor**

$$
S = \frac{1}{1 - \frac{V_{GS}}{V_P}} = \frac{1}{1 - \frac{1}{3}} = \frac{1}{\frac{2}{3}}
$$

$$
\boxed{S = 1.5}
$$

**Comment**: Since $S = 1.5 > 1$, the circuit exhibits **moderate thermal instability**. A 10% change in $I_{DSS}$ due to temperature will cause a 15% change in $I_D$. For improved stability, **self-bias** or **voltage-divider bias** should be employed instead.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **RAPID-REVISION CHECKLIST — Pinch-Off & Fixed $V_{GS}$ Bias**

**Pinch-Off Fundamentals**:
- Pinch-off voltage $V_P$ = the $V_{GS}$ at which $I_D = 0$
- For **n-channel JFET**: $V_P < 0$ (typically $-2$ V to $-10$ V)
- For **p-channel JFET**: $V_P > 0$
- $V_{DS}(\text{pinch-off}) = V_{GS} - V_P$ (the minimum $V_{DS}$ for saturation onset)
- Below $V_{DS}(\text{pinch-off})$ → **Ohmic/Triode** region; Above → **Saturation** region

**Shockley's Equation**:
- $I_D = I_{DSS}\left(1 - \dfrac{V_{GS}}{V_P}\right)^2$ (valid in saturation only)
- $I_{DSS}$ = drain current at $V_{GS} = 0$
- Square-law device → distinct from BJTs (which are exponential)

**Fixed $V_{GS}$ Bias — Key Equations**:
- $V_{GS} = V_{GG}$ (because $I_G \approx 0$)
- $I_{DQ} = I_{DSS}\left(1 - \dfrac{V_{GG}}{V_P}\right)^2$
- $V_{DSQ} = V_{DD} - I_{DQ} \cdot R_D$
- Stability factor: $S = \dfrac{1}{1 - \dfrac{V_{GS}}{V_P}}$, always $> 1$

**Transconductance**:
- $g_{m0} = \dfrac{2 I_{DSS}}{\vert V_P \vert}$ (maximum, at $V_{GS} = 0$)
- $g_m = g_{m0}\left(1 - \dfrac{V_{GS}}{V_P}\right)$ (operating point)

**Common Pitfalls**:
- Forgetting the sign of $V_P$ in Shockley's equation
- Treating the gate like a BJT base (where $I_B \neq 0$)
- Confusing $V_P$ with $V_{DS}(\text{pinch-off})$
- Using $V_{GS} = 0$ when $V_{GG}$ is specified

**Exam Favourites**:
- "Design a fixed-bias JFET circuit for given $I_{DQ}$" → Solve for $V_{GG}$
- "Derive stability factor" → Differential of Shockley's equation
- "Find Q-point given $V_{GG}$" → Two-step calculation ($I_{DQ}$, then $V_{DSQ}$)
- "Compare stability of biasing topologies" → Fixed bias has worst stability

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Fixing Gate Voltage and Connecting Source Resistance
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCICT302 - ELECTRONIC CIRCUITS AND NETWORKS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Fixing Gate Voltage and Connecting Source Resistance

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

In **Junction Field Effect Transistor (JFET)** biasing, **"Fixing Gate Voltage and Connecting Source Resistance"** refers to the **Self-Bias** (also called *Source Bias* or *Automatic Bias*) configuration. In this method, the **DC gate voltage is held at a fixed reference** (typically **ground potential**, i.e., $V_G = 0$ V) by connecting a high-value gate resistor $R_G$ between the gate and the reference. Simultaneously, a **source resistor $R_S$** is inserted between the source terminal and ground to develop a voltage proportional to the drain current.

The governing DC bias relation is:

$$V_{GS} = V_G - V_S = 0 - I_D R_S = -I_D R_S$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This single equation, $V_{GS} = -I_D R_S$, is the **defining mathematical identity** of the self-bias technique. KTU examiners treat it as a **must-write** opening line in any 14-mark self-bias problem.

### Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

Imagine a **floating balloon (Gate)** tethered to the ground by a *very weak, long rope* ($R_G$ ≈ several MΩ). Because the rope is weak, the balloon stays near ground level ($V_G \approx 0$ V). Now, attach a **weight (drain current $I_D$) pulling the balloon upward**. To stop it from flying away, we connect an **elastic band ($R_S$)** from the balloon to a hook on the ceiling — the more current flows, the more the elastic stretches, holding the balloon down at a stable position.

That stable position is the **Q-point** (quiescent operating point) of the JFET. The "fixing" of the gate voltage gives a known reference, and the "source resistance" creates a self-regulating, **negative feedback** mechanism that stabilizes the operating point against device parameter variations and temperature changes.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why not connect $R_S$ alone without fixing the gate?**
> The JFET gate–channel junction is reverse-biased for normal operation. A purely floating gate can accumulate stray charge, leading to unpredictable $V_{GS}$. The $R_G$ resistor provides a defined DC path for the *negligibly small* gate leakage current ($I_{GSS}$), fixing $V_G$ at 0 V.

### Standard Metrics & Physical Constants

| Parameter | Symbol | Typical Range |
| :--- | :---: | :--- |
| Drain current at zero gate bias | $I_{DSS}$ | **2 mA – 20 mA** |
| Pinch-off voltage | $V_P$ (or $V_{GS(off)}$) | **−2 V to −10 V** |
| Gate–Source cutoff current | $I_{GSS}$ | **< 1 nA** (often negligible) |
| Gate resistor | $R_G$ | **1 MΩ – 10 MΩ** |
| Source resistor | $R_S$ | **100 Ω – 10 kΩ** |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### The Two Competing Curves in JFET Biasing

The Q-point of a JFET self-bias circuit is determined by the **intersection of two curves** drawn on the $I_D$ vs $V_{GS}$ plane:

1. **The Transfer (Device) Characteristic** — dictated purely by the JFET physics:

$$I_D = I_{DSS}\left(1 - \frac{V_{GS}}{V_P}\right)^2$$

This is **Shockley's Equation**, valid for $V_P \le V_{GS} \le 0$ (for an n-channel JFET).

2. **The Self-Bias Line (Load Line)** — dictated purely by the external circuit:

$$V_{GS} = -I_D R_S$$

   * This is a **straight line** in the $I_D$–$V_{GS}$ plane.
   * **X-intercept** (where $I_D = 0$): $V_{GS} = 0$ V.
   * **Y-intercept** (where $V_{GS} = 0$, extrapolated): $I_D = 0$ mA (origin).
   * **Slope** = $\dfrac{-1}{R_S}$ — steeper for smaller $R_S$.

### Step-by-Step Logical Procedure to Find Q-Point

- **Step 1 — Fix the gate:** Connect $R_G$ from gate to ground. Since $I_G \approx 0$, there is no voltage drop across $R_G$, so $V_G = 0$ V.
- **Step 2 — Connect source resistance:** Insert $R_S$ between source and ground. The current $I_D$ flowing through it develops $V_S = I_D R_S$.
- **Step 3 — Form the self-bias line:** $V_{GS} = V_G - V_S = -I_D R_S$.
- **Step 4 — Substitute into Shockley's equation:**

$$I_D = I_{DSS}\left(1 - \frac{-I_D R_S}{V_P}\right)^2 = I_{DSS}\left(1 + \frac{I_D R_S}{\vert V_P \vert}\right)^2$$

- **Step 5 — Solve the resulting quadratic** in $I_D$, or **graphically** intersect the two curves.
- **Step 6 — Back-substitute** to find $V_{GS}$, $V_{DS}$, and $V_D$.

> [!NOTE]
> The **stabilization** of the Q-point comes from this self-biasing action. If $I_D$ tries to *increase* (say, due to temperature rise), $V_{GS}$ becomes *more negative*, which *reduces* $I_D$ — classic **negative feedback**.

### Real-World Engineering Utility

Self-bias is the **most widely used** JFET biasing scheme in:
- **Audio pre-amplifiers** (low noise floor).
- **RF amplifier front-ends** (high input impedance of JFET avoids loading the antenna).
- **Source-follower / buffer stages** (naturally compatible with self-bias).
- **Sample-and-hold circuits** (where the high $R_G$ ensures negligible gate leakage).

### KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Meaning / Use | Units |
| :---: | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | $I_D = I_{DSS}\!\left(1 - \dfrac{V_{GS}}{V_P}\right)^{\!2}$ | Shockley's equation — JFET transfer curve | mA |
| 2 | $V_{GS} = -I_D R_S$ | Self-bias line (since $V_G = 0$) | V |
| 3 | $g_{m0} = \dfrac{2 I_{DSS}}{\vert V_P \vert}$ | Maximum transconductance (at $V_{GS} = 0$) | mA/V (mS) |
| 4 | $g_m = g_{m0}\!\left(1 - \dfrac{V_{GS}}{V_P}\right)$ | Transconductance at any Q-point | mA/V (mS) |
| 5 | $V_{DS} = V_{DD} - I_D(R_D + R_S)$ | Drain–Source voltage drop | V |
| 6 | $V_D = V_{DD} - I_D R_D$ | Drain DC voltage w.r.t. ground | V |
| 7 | $V_S = I_D R_S$ | Source DC voltage w.r.t. ground | V |
| 8 | $P_D = I_D V_{DS}$ | Quiescent power dissipation in JFET | mW |

> [!WARNING]
> KTU examiners **frequently deduct marks** for writing $\left(1 - \dfrac{V_{GS}}{V_P}\right)^2$ without explicitly stating the **valid range** $V_P \le V_{GS} \le 0$. Always **mention the domain** of applicability.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: From Self-Bias Line to a Quadratic in $I_D$

Starting from the two fundamental equations:

$$V_{GS} = -I_D R_S \qquad \text{(1: Self-bias line)}$$

$$I_D = I_{DSS}\!\left(1 - \frac{V_{GS}}{V_P}\right)^{\!2} \qquad \text{(2: Shockley's equation)}$$

**Step 1:** Substitute (1) into (2):

$$I_D = I_{DSS}\left(1 - \frac{-I_D R_S}{V_P}\right)^2$$

**Step 2:** Since $V_P$ is *negative* for an n-channel JFET, the ratio $\dfrac{I_D R_S}{V_P}$ is also *negative*. Pull the negative sign out using $\vert V_P \vert = -V_P$:

$$I_D = I_{DSS}\left(1 + \frac{I_D R_S}{\vert V_P \vert}\right)^2$$

**Step 3:** Define the **dimensionless bias ratio** $k = \dfrac{I_{DSS} R_S^{\,2}}{\vert V_P \vert^{\,2}}$ and let $x = I_D / I_{DSS}$. The equation becomes:

$$x = \left(1 + \frac{R_S I_{DSS}}{\vert V_P \vert}\sqrt{x}\,\right)^{\!2} \quad \text{(non-linear; usually solved graphically)}$$

**Step 4 — Exact analytic form:** Expand the square and re-arrange into a standard quadratic in $I_D$:

$$I_D = I_{DSS}\left[1 + \frac{2 I_D R_S}{\vert V_P \vert} + \frac{I_D^{\,2} R_S^{\,2}}{\vert V_P \vert^{\,2}}\right]$$

$$I_D = I_{DSS} + \frac{2 I_{DSS} R_S}{\vert V_P \vert} I_D + \frac{I_{DSS} R_S^{\,2}}{\vert V_P \vert^{\,2}} I_D^{\,2}$$

**Step 5:** Bring all terms to one side and divide by $I_{DSS}$:

$$\frac{R_S^{\,2}}{\vert V_P \vert^{\,2}} I_D^{\,2} + \left(\frac{2 R_S}{\vert V_P \vert} - \frac{1}{I_{DSS}}\right) I_D + 1 = 0$$

**Step 6 — General quadratic form $a I_D^{\,2} + b I_D + c = 0$:**

$$\boxed{\;\frac{R_S^{\,2}}{\vert V_P \vert^{\,2}}\, I_D^{\,2} + \left(\frac{2 R_S}{\vert V_P \vert} - \frac{1}{I_{DSS}}\right) I_D + 1 = 0\;}$$

**Step 7 — Solve** using the standard quadratic formula $I_D = \dfrac{-b \pm \sqrt{b^2 - 4ac}}{2a}$ and **discard the extraneous root** (the one lying outside $0 \le I_D \le I_{DSS}$).

---

### 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (KTU 14-Mark Style)

**Problem Statement (n-channel JFET self-bias):**
Given: $I_{DSS} = 8\ \text{mA}$, $V_P = -4\ \text{V}$, $R_D = 2.2\ \text{k}\Omega$, $R_S = 1\ \text{k}\Omega$, $R_G = 1\ \text{M}\Omega$, $V_{DD} = +12\ \text{V}$.

**Step 1 — Form the self-bias line:**

$$V_{GS} = -I_D R_S = -I_D (1\ \text{k}\Omega)$$

**Step 2 — Set up the quadratic.** With $a = \dfrac{R_S^{\,2}}{\vert V_P \vert^{\,2}} = \dfrac{(1\ \text{k}\Omega)^2}{(4\ \text{V})^2} = 0.0625\ \text{k}\Omega^2/\text{V}^2$, $b = \dfrac{2 R_S}{\vert V_P \vert} - \dfrac{1}{I_{DSS}} = \dfrac{2(1\ \text{k}\Omega)}{4\ \text{V}} - \dfrac{1}{8\ \text{mA}} = 0.5 - 125 = -124.5$ (with consistent units), $c = 1$.

**Step 3 — Compute discriminant:**

$$\Delta = b^2 - 4ac = (-124.5)^2 - 4(0.0625)(-124.5) \neq 0$$

> For KTU numericals, it is **faster and more standard** to tabulate $I_D$ values and graphically/iteratively find the Q-point, as shown below.

**Tabulation Method (Examiner-Preferred):**

| $I_D$ (mA) | $V_{GS} = -I_D R_S$ (V) | $I_{DSS}\!\left(1 - \dfrac{V_{GS}}{V_P}\right)^{\!2}$ (mA) |
| :---: | :---: | :---: |
| 0.0 | 0.0 | 8.000 |
| 1.0 | −1.0 | 4.500 |
| 2.0 | −2.0 | 2.000 |
| 3.0 | −3.0 | 0.500 |
| 4.0 | −4.0 | 0.000 |

The Q-point is where the **second column (bias line)** equals the **fourth column (device curve)**.

- At $I_D = 2.0$ mA: bias line gives $V_{GS} = -2.0$ V; device curve gives $I_D = 2.0$ mA. ✅ **Match!**

$$\boxed{\;I_{DQ} = 2.0\ \text{mA}, \quad V_{GSQ} = -2.0\ \text{V}\;}$$

**Step 4 — Compute the remaining DC quantities:**

$$V_S = I_{DQ} R_S = (2.0\ \text{mA})(1\ \text{k}\Omega) = +2.0\ \text{V}$$

$$V_D = V_{DD} - I_{DQ} R_D = 12\ \text{V} - (2.0\ \text{mA})(2.2\ \text{k}\Omega) = 12 - 4.4 = +7.6\ \text{V}$$

$$V_{DS} = V_D - V_S = 7.6 - 2.0 = +5.6\ \text{V}$$

**Step 5 — Transconductance at the Q-point:**

$$g_{m0} = \frac{2 I_{DSS}}{\vert V_P \vert} = \frac{2(8\ \text{mA})}{4\ \text{V}} = 4.0\ \text{mA/V}$$

$$g_m = g_{m0}\!\left(1 - \frac{V_{GSQ}}{V_P}\right) = 4.0\!\left(1 - \frac{-2.0}{-4}\right) = 4.0(0.5) = 2.0\ \text{mA/V} = 2.0\ \text{mS}$$

**Step 6 — Quiescent power dissipation:**

$$P_D = I_{DQ} V_{DS} = (2.0\ \text{mA})(5.6\ \text{V}) = 11.2\ \text{mW}$$

---

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — When to Use Self-Bias vs Voltage-Divider Bias

| Criterion | Self-Bias ($R_G$ only) | Voltage-Divider Bias |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Components required | Minimum: $R_D, R_S, R_G$ | Two extra: $R_1, R_2$ |
| Q-point stability | Good | Better (independent of $I_{DSS}$) |
| Use when $I_{DSS}$ varies a lot | Acceptable | Strongly recommended |
| Typical application | Small-signal AC amp | Precision DC amp |
| KTU exam frequency | **High** | High |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Self-Bias Circuit — Block-Level Functional Architecture

```mermaid
graph TD
    VDD[V_DD Supply] --> RD[Resistor R_D]
    RD --> D[Node D : Drain]
    D --> JFET[JFET n-channel device]
    JFET --> S[Node S : Source]
    S --> RS[Resistor R_S]
    RS --> GND1[Ground]
    VDD --> GND1
    G[Node G : Gate] --> RG[Resistor R_G : Fixes V_G to 0 V]
    GND1 --> RG
    JFET -. gate channel junction .-> G
    NODE1[Observed Q point intersection] --> XFER[Transfer curve I_D vs V_GS from Shockley]
    NODE1 --> BIAS[Self bias straight line V_GS = minus I_D times R_S]
```

### 4.2 Functional Flow of Bias-Point Establishment

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Step 1: Connect R_G from Gate to Ground] --> B[V_G is fixed at 0 V since I_G is negligible]
    B --> C[Step 2: Insert R_S from Source to Ground]
    C --> D[I_D develops voltage V_S across R_S]
    D --> E[Step 3: Self bias line emerges: V_GS = minus I_D R_S]
    E --> F[Step 4: Intersect with Shockley transfer curve]
    F --> G[Q point determined: I_DQ and V_GSQ]
    G --> H[Step 5: Compute V_D, V_S, V_DS, g_m, P_D]
```

### 4.3 Signal-Flow Block Diagram of Stabilization Loop

```mermaid
graph LR
    T[Temperature rise] --> INC[I_D tries to increase]
    INC --> VS[V_S increases since V_S = I_D R_S]
    VS --> NEG[V_GS becomes more negative]
    NEG --> DEC[I_D decreases back toward Q point]
    DEC -. negative feedback .-> INC
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The negative feedback loop shown above is the **single most important reason** KTU examiners credit self-bias over fixed-bias. Always draw or describe this feedback loop in long-answer questions to earn the full marks.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. `[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper]`**
*In a JFET self-bias circuit, what is the DC voltage at the gate terminal? Justify your answer in two lines.*

**Model Answer:**
The DC gate voltage $V_G = 0$ V. This is because the gate current $I_G$ in a JFET is negligibly small (typically nano-amperes), so the IR drop across the gate resistor $R_G$ is essentially zero. Therefore, the gate sits at the same potential as the ground end of $R_G$.

---

**Q2. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`**
*State the two equations that are solved simultaneously to find the Q-point of a JFET self-bias circuit. Name each equation.*

**Model Answer:**

1. **Shockley's Equation** (Device Transfer Characteristic):

$$I_D = I_{DSS}\left(1 - \frac{V_{GS}}{V_P}\right)^2$$

2. **Self-Bias Line Equation** (External Circuit):

$$V_{GS} = -I_D R_S$$

The Q-point is the intersection of the parabolic Shockley curve and the straight self-bias line on the $I_D$–$V_{GS}$ plane.

---

### Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

---

#### Question A (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 1]`

**(a)** With a neat circuit diagram, explain the **self-bias** method of biasing an n-channel JFET. Derive the expression for the **Q-point** using the transfer characteristic and the bias line. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** An n-channel JFET has $I_{DSS} = 10\ \text{mA}$ and $V_P = -5\ \text{V}$. In a self-bias configuration, $R_S = 1.2\ \text{k}\Omega$, $R_D = 2.7\ \text{k}\Omega$, $R_G = 1\ \text{M}\Omega$, and $V_{DD} = 15\ \text{V}$. Find $I_{DQ}$, $V_{GSQ}$, $V_{DS}$, $V_S$, and the quiescent power dissipation $P_D$. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution to (a):**

The self-bias circuit has the following elements (refer to diagrams in Section 4):
- $R_G$ connected between gate and ground (typically **1 MΩ** to provide a DC path for the tiny gate leakage current $I_{GSS}$).
- $R_S$ connected between source and ground.
- $R_D$ connected between drain and $V_{DD}$.
- The gate voltage $V_G$ is fixed at 0 V since the gate current is essentially zero.
- The source voltage $V_S = I_D R_S$, giving $V_{GS} = -I_D R_S$.

The two equations whose simultaneous solution yields the Q-point are:

$$V_{GS} = -I_D R_S \quad \text{(1)}$$

$$I_D = I_{DSS}\!\left(1 - \frac{V_{GS}}{V_P}\right)^{\!2} \quad \text{(2)}$$

Substituting (1) into (2):

$$I_D = I_{DSS}\!\left(1 + \frac{I_D R_S}{\vert V_P \vert}\right)^{\!2}$$

Re-arranging leads to a quadratic in $I_D$:

$$\frac{R_S^{\,2}}{\vert V_P \vert^{\,2}} I_D^{\,2} + \left(\frac{2 R_S}{\vert V_P \vert} - \frac{1}{I_{DSS}}\right) I_D + 1 = 0$$

Solving this quadratic (or using tabulation) gives the **Q-point** ($I_{DQ}$, $V_{GSQ}$). The graphical intersection of the self-bias line and the Shockley parabola on the $I_D$–$V_{GS}$ plane gives the same result.

**Model Solution to (b):**

**Step 1 — Identify the self-bias line equation:**

$$V_{GS} = -I_D R_S = -(1.2\ \text{k}\Omega) I_D$$

**Step 2 — Tabulate $I_D$ vs $V_{GS}$ for both the device curve and the bias line:**

| $I_D$ (mA) | $V_{GS}$ from bias line (V) | $I_{DSS}\!\left(1 - \dfrac{V_{GS}}{V_P}\right)^{\!2}$ (mA) |
| :---: | :---: | :---: |
| 0.0 | 0.0 | 10.00 |
| 2.0 | −2.4 | 10 × (1 − (−2.4/−5))² = 10 × (0.52)² = **2.704** |
| 3.0 | −3.6 | 10 × (1 − (−3.6/−5))² = 10 × (0.28)² = **0.784** |
| 5.0 | −6.0 | 10 × (1 − (−6.0/−5))² = 10 × (−0.2)² = **0.400** |
| 1.0 | −1.2 | 10 × (1 − (−1.2/−5))² = 10 × (0.76)² = **5.776** |

**Step 3 — Locate the intersection:** Between $I_D = 2.0$ mA (line gives −2.4 V; curve gives 2.7 mA) and $I_D = 3.0$ mA (line gives −3.6 V; curve gives 0.78 mA). The intersection lies very close to:

$$\boxed{I_{DQ} \approx 2.7\ \text{mA}, \quad V_{GSQ} \approx -3.24\ \text{V}}$$

**Step 4 — Compute remaining quantities:**

$$V_S = I_{DQ} R_S = (2.7\ \text{mA})(1.2\ \text{k}\Omega) = +3.24\ \text{V}$$

$$V_D = V_{DD} - I_{DQ} R_D = 15 - (2.7)(2.7) = 15 - 7.29 = +7.71\ \text{V}$$

$$V_{DS} = V_D - V_S = 7.71 - 3.24 = \boxed{+4.47\ \text{V}}$$

$$P_D = I_{DQ} \times V_{DS} = (2.7\ \text{mA})(4.47\ \text{V}) = \boxed{12.07\ \text{mW}}$$

---

#### Question B (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam – May 2023, Module 1]`

**(a)** Explain the **negative feedback stabilization** mechanism in a JFET self-bias circuit. Show how the Q-point stabilizes against temperature variations. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** For the self-bias circuit in Question A part (b), if the JFET is replaced by one with $I_{DSS} = 12\ \text{mA}$ and $V_P = -6\ \text{V}$ (same $R_S$, $R_D$, $R_G$, $V_{DD}$ as before), determine the **new Q-point** and comment on the **percentage change** in $I_{DQ}$ relative to the original. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution to (a):**

The self-bias configuration inherently provides **DC negative feedback**. The mechanism is:

1. Suppose the JFET temperature rises.
2. The drain current $I_D$ tends to **increase** (for a JFET, contrary to a BJT, drain current *increases* with temperature because the channel conductivity rises).
3. The increased $I_D$ flows through $R_S$ and produces a **larger** voltage drop $V_S = I_D R_S$.
4. Since the gate voltage is *fixed* at 0 V (via $R_G$), the gate–source voltage $V_{GS} = V_G - V_S = -I_D R_S$ becomes **more negative**.
5. A more negative $V_{GS}$ **reduces** $I_D$ (per Shockley's equation).
6. The increase in $I_D$ is therefore **countered**, and the Q-point remains nearly stable.

This automatic, self-regulating action is the essence of self-bias stabilization, and the *amount* of stabilization is proportional to $R_S$. Larger $R_S$ ⇒ stronger stabilization, but also lower $I_{DQ}$ (and hence lower $g_m$).

**Model Solution to (b):**

With $I_{DSS} = 12\ \text{mA}$, $V_P = -6\ \text{V}$, $R_S = 1.2\ \text{k}\Omega$:

**Tabulation:**

| $I_D$ (mA) | $V_{GS} = -I_D R_S$ (V) | $I_{DSS}\!\left(1 - \dfrac{V_{GS}}{V_P}\right)^{\!2}$ (mA) |
| :---: | :---: | :---: |
| 0.0 | 0.0 | 12.00 |
| 2.0 | −2.4 | 12 × (1 − (−2.4/−6))² = 12 × (0.6)² = **4.32** |
| 3.0 | −3.6 | 12 × (1 − (−3.6/−6))² = 12 × (0.4)² = **1.92** |
| 4.0 | −4.8 | 12 × (1 − (−4.8/−6))² = 12 × (0.2)² = **0.48** |
| 5.0 | −6.0 | 12 × (1 − (−6.0/−6))² = 0.00 |

The Q-point is between $I_D = 2.0$ mA and $I_D = 4.0$ mA. Closer inspection near 2.7 mA:

- At $I_D = 2.7$ mA: $V_{GS} = -3.24$ V; device gives $12 \times (1 - 3.24/6)^2 = 12 \times (0.46)^2 \approx 2.54$ mA.
- At $I_D = 2.5$ mA: $V_{GS} = -3.0$ V; device gives $12 \times (0.5)^2 = 3.00$ mA.

The intersection lies near $I_{DQ} \approx 2.65$ mA.

$$\boxed{I_{DQ}^{\text{new}} \approx 2.65\ \text{mA}, \quad V_{GSQ}^{\text{new}} \approx -3.18\ \text{V}}$$

**Percentage change in $I_{DQ}$:**

$$\%\,\Delta I_{DQ} = \frac{2.65 - 2.70}{2.70} \times 100\% \approx -1.85\%$$

**Comment:** A **50% increase in $I_{DSS}$** (from 10 mA to 12 mA) and a **20% change in $V_P$** (from −5 V to −6 V) caused only a **~1.85% shift in $I_{DQ}$**. This demonstrates the excellent Q-point stabilization of the self-bias scheme.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls (Avoid These!):**
> 1. **Forgetting to discard the extraneous root** of the quadratic. The self-bias quadratic gives *two* mathematically valid roots, but only one lies in the physical range $0 \le I_D \le I_{DSS}$. Always validate your answer.
> 2. **Sign errors on $V_P$.** It is **negative** for n-channel JFETs. The term $\dfrac{V_{GS}}{V_P}$ must be computed with the correct sign, otherwise the bracketed term becomes *negative* and the square of a negative is still positive but the resulting $I_D$ will be wrong.
> 3. **Ignoring the range of validity** $V_P \le V_{GS} \le 0$. If your computed $V_{GSQ}$ falls outside this range, the JFET is in cutoff or in the ohmic region — the Q-point is invalid.
> 4. **Writing $V_{GS} = +I_D R_S$** instead of $-I_D R_S$. This is the single most common error; it inverts the sign of $V_{GS}$ and shifts the Q-point to the wrong half of the transfer curve.
> 5. **Skipping the negative feedback explanation** in 14-mark questions — a guaranteed loss of 2 marks.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ **Self-bias = fix $V_G = 0$ V via $R_G$ + connect $R_S$ from source to ground.** This is the *defining* feature of the topic.
- ✅ **Master equation pair:** Shockley's equation $I_D = I_{DSS}\!\left(1 - \dfrac{V_{GS}}{V_P}\right)^{\!2}$ and the self-bias line $V_{GS} = -I_D R_S$.
- ✅ **Q-point is the intersection** of a parabola and a straight line — solvable analytically (quadratic) or graphically (tabulation).
- ✅ **Always mention the valid range** $V_P \le V_{GS} \le 0$ when quoting Shockley's equation.
- ✅ **Negative feedback** is the *engineering reason* self-bias stabilizes the Q-point — explain it in words, not just equations.
- ✅ **Formulas to memorize cold:** $V_{GS} = -I_D R_S$, $V_{DS} = V_{DD} - I_D(R_D + R_S)$, $g_m = g_{m0}\!\left(1 - \dfrac{V_{GSQ}}{V_P}\right)$, $P_D = I_{DQ} V_{DS}$.
- ✅ **Common numerical answer pattern:** $I_{DQ}$ typically falls in the range **40–70%** of $I_{DSS}$ for a properly designed self-bias stage.
- ✅ **Sign discipline:** $V_P$ is **negative** for n-channel; $|V_P|$ is used in magnitude form to avoid sign confusion.
- ✅ **$R_G$ is large (MΩ range)** to avoid loading the high-impedance gate, but small enough to leak off any accumulated gate charge.
- ✅ **Higher $R_S$** ⇒ **better stability** but **lower $g_m$ and lower gain** — a classic engineering trade-off.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Using Two Power Supplies
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCICT302 - ELECTRONIC CIRCUITS AND NETWORKS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Using Two Power Supplies

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Two-Supply Biasing (also called Dual-Polarity or Split-Supply Biasing)** is a Bipolar Junction Transistor (BJT) biasing configuration in which the transistor is energized by **two independent DC power supplies of opposite polarity**: a positive supply $+V_{CC}$ connected to the collector through $R_C$, and a negative supply $-V_{EE}$ connected to the emitter through $R_E$. The base is referenced to **ground (0 V)** through a base resistor $R_B$.

This arrangement provides a substantially improved **Operating Point (Q-point) stability** against variations in temperature, transistor parameters ($\beta$, $V_{BE}$), and supply fluctuations — quantified by the **stability factor $S$**, which is dramatically lower than that of the single-supply fixed-bias or emitter-bias circuits.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (Module 1):**
> "Biasing of BJT — Fixed bias, Collector-to-Base bias, Emitter bias (with and without dual supply), and **Biasing using two power supplies**." The treatment of two-supply biasing is *explicitly listed* in the KTU 2024 scheme and is a high-frequency question in previous university examinations.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Real-World Intuition

Imagine a heavy wooden plank balanced across a triangular fulcrum (a seesaw). A child (the **BJT**) sits exactly at the center, and two adults stand at the two ends — one pushing **down** with force $+V_{CC}$ and the other pulling **up** with force $-V_{EE}$. Because the child is balanced between two opposing *anchored* forces, any small push (transistor parameter change due to temperature) is **resisted equally from both sides**, keeping the child (the Q-point) remarkably still. This is exactly what dual-supply biasing does for the BJT's operating point.

**Single supply** = the seesaw is held only on one side → easily disturbed.
**Two supplies** = the seesaw is held on both sides → inherently self-correcting.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Design Constants (silicon BJT at 25 °C):**
> - Thermal Voltage $V_T \approx \mathbf{25.85\ mV}$
> - Base-Emitter ON Voltage $V_{BE} \approx \mathbf{0.7\ V}$ (silicon), $\mathbf{0.3\ V}$ (germanium)
> - Temperature coefficient of $V_{BE}$: $\mathbf{-2.5\ mV/°C}$
> - Temperature coefficient of $I_{CO}$ (leakage current): **doubles every 10 °C rise**

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Circuit Operation — Step-by-Step Logic

The canonical two-supply biasing circuit has the following topology:

1. **Collector branch:** $+V_{CC} \rightarrow R_C \rightarrow$ Collector (C)
2. **Emitter branch:** Emitter (E) $\rightarrow R_E \rightarrow -V_{EE}$
3. **Base branch:** Ground (0 V) $\rightarrow R_B \rightarrow$ Base (B)

**Why it works so well:**
- The base is held firmly at near-ground potential by $R_B$ (independent of $I_B$ variations).
- The negative supply $-V_{EE}$ "pulls" the emitter below ground, creating a stable $V_{BE} \approx 0.7$ V regardless of $\beta$ changes.
- Any increase in collector leakage $I_{CO}$ or $\beta$ (due to temperature) increases $I_C$, which increases the voltage drop across $R_E$, making the emitter *less negative*. This **reverse-biases** the B-E junction slightly, reducing $I_B$, and hence $I_C$ — a built-in **negative feedback loop**.

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Quantity | Formula | Physical Meaning / Condition |
|---|----------|---------|------------------------------|
| 1 | Base Current $I_B$ | $I_B = \dfrac{V_{EE} - V_{BE}}{R_B + (\beta+1)R_E}$ | Exact form |
| 2 | Base Current (approx.) | $I_B \approx \dfrac{V_{EE} - V_{BE}}{R_B + \beta R_E}$ | Valid for $\beta \gg 1$ |
| 3 | Collector Current $I_C$ | $I_C = \beta I_B$ | Active region operation |
| 4 | Emitter Current $I_E$ | $I_E = (\beta+1) I_B \approx I_C$ | KCL at transistor |
| 5 | Emitter Voltage $V_E$ | $V_E = -V_{EE} + I_E R_E$ | Node voltage at E |
| 6 | Collector Voltage $V_C$ | $V_C = V_{CC} - I_C R_C$ | Node voltage at C |
| 7 | Collector-Emitter Voltage $V_{CE}$ | $V_{CE} = V_{CC} + V_{EE} - I_C(R_C + R_E)$ | Quiescent output |
| 8 | Stability Factor $S$ | $S = \dfrac{\partial I_C}{\partial I_{CO}} = \dfrac{1+\beta}{1 + \beta \cdot \dfrac{R_E}{R_E + R_B}}$ | $I_{CO}$ sensitivity |
| 9 | Stability Factor (limit) | $S \rightarrow 1$ when $R_E \gg R_B$ | Best stability |
| 10 | Stability Factor (worst) | $S \rightarrow 1+\beta$ when $R_E \rightarrow 0$ | Degenerates to fixed bias |

> [!TIP]
> **For KTU numerical problems, always:**
> - Assume silicon BJT → $V_{BE} = 0.7$ V
> - Use $I_C \approx I_E$ to simplify calculations
> - Check $V_{CE} > 0.2$ V to confirm **active region** operation

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Op-Amp Front-Ends:** The classic $\pm 15$ V dual supply in instrumentation amplifiers is a direct descendant of two-supply biasing — used in every ECG machine, audio mixer, and industrial sensor signal conditioner.
- **Audio Power Amplifiers (Class-AB):** Output stages use dual supplies to handle AC signals centered at 0 V with maximum symmetric swing.
- **Automotive ECU & Sensor Conditioning:** Operating point must remain stable from $-40\ °C$ to $+125\ °C$ — dual supply (or its equivalent virtual-ground dual supply) is mandatory.
- **Precision Analog ICs (e.g., AD8552, OPA227):** Internal biasing uses the dual-supply principle at the silicon level.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Base Current $I_B$

**Step 1:** Apply KVL around the **Base–Emitter loop** (ground → $R_B$ → Base–Emitter junction → $R_E$ → $-V_{EE}$). Define the loop such that we go from ground, through $R_B$ (against the base current $I_B$ flowing from base to ground), then through the B-E junction (drop $V_{BE}$), then through $R_E$ (in the direction of $I_E$ flowing from emitter to $-V_{EE}$), and finally up to $-V_{EE}$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
0 - I_B R_B - V_{BE} - I_E R_E - (-V_{EE}) &= 0 \\
V_{EE} - V_{BE} &= I_B R_B + I_E R_E
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2:** Use the transistor relation $I_E = (\beta + 1) I_B$ to eliminate $I_E$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{EE} - V_{BE} &= I_B R_B + (\beta+1) I_B R_E \\
V_{EE} - V_{BE} &= I_B \bigl[ R_B + (\beta+1) R_E \bigr]
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3:** Solve explicitly for $I_B$:

$$
I_B = \frac{V_{EE} - V_{BE}}{R_B + (\beta+1) R_E}
$$

For $\beta \gg 1$ (almost always true in practice, e.g., $\beta = 100$):

$$
I_B \approx \frac{V_{EE} - V_{BE}}{R_B + \beta R_E}
$$

This is the **fundamental KTU result** for two-supply biasing.

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Quiescent $V_{CE}$

**Step 1:** Write KVL around the **Collector–Emitter loop** (from $+V_{CC}$ through $R_C$, through the C-E terminals, through $R_E$, down to $-V_{EE}$):

$$
V_{CC} - I_C R_C - V_{CE} - I_E R_E - (-V_{EE}) = 0
$$

**Step 2:** Rearrange for $V_{CE}$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{CE} &= V_{CC} + V_{EE} - I_C R_C - I_E R_E
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3:** Apply the approximation $I_C \approx I_E$ (valid for $\beta \gg 1$):

$$
V_{CE} = V_{CC} + V_{EE} - I_C (R_C + R_E)
$$

> [!NOTE]
> Notice the elegant symmetry: the Q-point depends on the **sum** of the two supplies. This is why designers often pick $V_{CC} = V_{EE}$ to obtain a symmetric swing of $\pm(V_{CC} - V_{CE(sat)})$.

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Stability Factor $S = \partial I_C / \partial I_{CO}$

**Step 1:** Express $I_C$ as the sum of the leakage and amplified base current (Ebers–Moll model):

$$
I_C = \beta I_B + (1+\beta) I_{CO}
$$

**Step 2:** Solve for $I_B$ from the KVL equation derived earlier:

$$
I_B = \frac{V_{EE} - V_{BE} - I_E R_E}{R_B}
$$

**Step 3:** Substitute $I_E = I_C + I_B$ into the KVL result and re-arrange:

$$
I_C = \beta \cdot \frac{V_{EE} - V_{BE} - (I_C + I_B) R_E}{R_B} + (1+\beta) I_{CO}
$$

**Step 4:** After algebraic elimination (collected terms of $I_C$ on one side), the partial derivative $\partial I_C / \partial I_{CO}$ evaluates to:

$$
S = \frac{\partial I_C}{\partial I_{CO}} = \frac{1+\beta}{1 + \beta \cdot \dfrac{R_E}{R_E + R_B}}
$$

**Step 5:** Analyze the boundary behaviour:

- If $R_E \gg R_B$: $\dfrac{R_E}{R_E + R_B} \to 1$, hence $S \to \dfrac{1+\beta}{1+\beta} = 1$ (ideal).
- If $R_E = 0$: $S = 1+\beta$ (worst case, same as fixed bias).

This is the **core stability result** every KTU examiner tests.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow

The diagram below depicts the functional flow of currents and voltages in a two-supply biased BJT, showing the **negative feedback mechanism** that gives the circuit its superior stability.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    VCC["+VCC Supply"] -- "IC flow" --> RC["Collector Resistor RC"]
    RC -- "IC into node C" --> NodeC["Collector Node C"]
    NodeC -- "IC = BETA * IB" --> BJT["BJT Active Region"]
    BJT -- "IE = IC + IB" --> RE["Emitter Resistor RE"]
    RE -- "IE flow" --> VEE["-VEE Supply"]
    GND["Ground 0V"] -- "IB flow" --> RB["Base Resistor RB"]
    RB -- "IB into node B" --> BJT
    VEEfeedback["-VEE acts as anchor"] -. "negative feedback" .-> BJT
    BJT -. "VC = VCC - ICRC" .-> OutV["VCE Output Span = VCC + VEE"]
```

### 4.2 Node-Voltage Reference Map

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph SupplyRail["SUPPLY RAILS"]
        VCCnode["+VCC at top rail"]
        GNDnode["GND at middle rail 0V reference"]
        VEEnode["-VEE at bottom rail"]
    end
    subgraph CircuitLoop["TWO SUPPLY BIAS LOOP"]
        RBtop["RB connects GND to Base B"]
        RCtop["RC connects +VCC to Collector C"]
        REbot["RE connects Emitter E to -VEE"]
    end
    VCCnode --- RCtop
    GNDnode --- RBtop
    VEEnode --- REbot
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the schematic:** Trace a continuous path from $+V_{CC}$ → $R_C$ → C → (through BJT) → E → $R_E$ → $-V_{EE}$. The base sits on a **third rail at 0 V (ground)**. This three-rail architecture is the defining feature of two-supply biasing.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — June 2024 Model]**
*Define the stability factor $S$ for a BJT biasing circuit. Why is it desirable to have $S$ close to unity?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **stability factor** $S$ is defined as the rate of change of collector current $I_C$ with respect to the reverse saturation current (leakage current) $I_{CO}$ of the BJT, keeping all other parameters constant:

$$ S = \frac{\partial I_C}{\partial I_{CO}} \Big|_{\beta,\, V_{BE} \text{ constant}} $$

It quantifies the **sensitivity of the Q-point to temperature variations**, since $I_{CO}$ doubles every 10 °C rise in temperature.

It is desirable to have $S$ close to **unity (1)** because:
1. The collector current $I_C$ becomes nearly **independent of temperature** and leakage current variations.
2. The operating point (Q-point) becomes **stable and predictable**, ensuring the amplifier operates in the desired active region.
3. A small $S$ implies strong **negative feedback** (large $R_E$ in the two-supply configuration), which is the engineering goal of bias design.

In the ideal two-supply bias case with $R_E \gg R_B$, $S \to 1$, which is the **best possible stability**.

*(Award: 1 mark for definition + 2 marks for the three justifications.)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model]**
*Mention any three advantages of biasing a BJT using two power supplies over a single-supply emitter bias configuration.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

1. **Better Q-point stability:** The stability factor $S$ is much closer to unity (especially when $R_E \gg R_B$), compared to single-supply emitter bias where $S = \frac{1+\beta}{1+\beta(R_E/(R_E+R_B))}$ but with reduced dynamic range.
2. **Independent control of bias current:** The base current is set primarily by $V_{EE}$ and $R_B$ (the equation $I_B = \frac{V_{EE}-V_{BE}}{R_B + \beta R_E}$ shows $I_B$ is largely decoupled from $\beta$ when $\beta R_E \gg R_B$), reducing dependency on transistor parameters.
3. **Larger output voltage swing:** $V_{CE} = V_{CC} + V_{EE} - I_C(R_C + R_E)$ allows the quiescent point to be centered at $\frac{V_{CC} + V_{EE}}{2}$, giving maximum symmetric AC swing — critical for Class-A and Class-AB amplifier design.
4. *(Bonus point)* Better **thermal stability** because the negative supply keeps the emitter negative, providing strong DC negative feedback.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (with Internal Choice)

---

#### ⭐ Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam — June 2023 Model]**

**(a)** With a neat circuit diagram, explain the operation of a BJT biased using two power supplies. Derive the expression for the stability factor $S$. **\[7 marks\]**

**(b)** A silicon BJT with $\beta = 100$ and $V_{BE} = 0.7$ V is biased using $+V_{CC} = 12$ V, $-V_{EE} = -6$ V, $R_B = 10\ \text{k}\Omega$, $R_C = 2\ \text{k}\Omega$, and $R_E = 1\ \text{k}\Omega$. Calculate the quiescent values of $I_C$, $V_{CE}$, and the stability factor $S$. **\[7 marks\]**

---

#### ✅ Model Solution — Question A

**(a) Circuit Diagram and Operation \[7 marks\]**

The circuit consists of an NPN BJT with:
- Collector connected to $+V_{CC}$ via $R_C$
- Emitter connected to $-V_{EE}$ via $R_E$
- Base connected to ground (0 V) via $R_B$

**Operation:**
- The negative supply $-V_{EE}$ forward-biases the B-E junction by pulling the emitter below ground.
- The base is held near ground potential by $R_B$, and the resulting $V_{BE} \approx 0.7$ V drives a stable base current.
- A negative feedback action exists: if $I_C$ tends to increase (e.g., due to temperature), $I_E R_E$ increases, making the emitter *less* negative. This **reduces $V_{BE}$**, reducing $I_B$ and hence $I_C$ — restoring the Q-point.

**Derivation of Stability Factor:**

Starting from the KVL of the base loop:

$$
V_{EE} - V_{BE} = I_B R_B + I_E R_E
$$

Substitute $I_E = I_C + I_B$ and the relation $I_C = \beta I_B + (1+\beta) I_{CO}$:

$$
V_{EE} - V_{BE} = I_B R_B + (I_C + I_B) R_E
$$

Solving for $I_B$ and substituting back, then differentiating with respect to $I_{CO}$:

$$
S = \frac{\partial I_C}{\partial I_{CO}} = \frac{1+\beta}{1 + \beta \cdot \dfrac{R_E}{R_E + R_B}}
$$

*(4 marks for explanation + 3 marks for derivation)*

---

**(b) Numerical Solution \[7 marks\]**

**Step 1 — Compute $I_B$:**

Using the exact formula:

$$
I_B = \frac{V_{EE} - V_{BE}}{R_B + (\beta+1) R_E}
$$

$$
I_B = \frac{6 - 0.7}{10\,000 + (101)(1\,000)} = \frac{5.3}{111\,000}
$$

$$
I_B = 47.75\ \mu\text{A}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute $I_C$:**

$$
I_C = \beta I_B = 100 \times 47.75\ \mu\text{A} = 4.775\ \text{mA}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute $V_{CE}$:**

Using $V_{CE} = V_{CC} + V_{EE} - I_C(R_C + R_E)$ (with $I_C \approx I_E$):

$$
V_{CE} = 12 + 6 - (4.775 \times 10^{-3})(2\,000 + 1\,000)
$$

$$
V_{CE} = 18 - (4.775 \times 10^{-3})(3\,000) = 18 - 14.325
$$

$$
\boxed{V_{CE} = 3.675\ \text{V}}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute Stability Factor $S$:**

$$
S = \frac{1+\beta}{1 + \beta \cdot \dfrac{R_E}{R_E + R_B}} = \frac{101}{1 + 100 \cdot \dfrac{1\,000}{1\,000 + 10\,000}}
$$

$$
S = \frac{101}{1 + 100 \cdot \dfrac{1}{11}} = \frac{101}{1 + 9.0909} = \frac{101}{10.0909}
$$

$$
\boxed{S \approx 10.01}
$$

Since $S \approx 10.01$ is very close to **1**, this confirms the two-supply biasing gives **excellent Q-point stability**.

---

#### ⭐ Question B (Alternative — 14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2022 Model]**

**(a)** Compare the stability factor of a two-supply bias circuit with that of a fixed-bias circuit. Under what condition does the two-supply circuit give the **best possible stability**? **\[7 marks\]**

**(b)** A BJT in two-supply bias has $V_{CC} = 15$ V, $V_{EE} = 10$ V, $R_B = 20\ \text{k}\Omega$, $R_C = 2\ \text{k}\Omega$, $R_E = 2\ \text{k}\Omega$, $\beta = 150$, and $V_{BE} = 0.7$ V. Find the Q-point ($I_C$, $V_{CE}$) and the stability factor $S$. **\[7 marks\]**

---

#### ✅ Model Solution — Question B

**(a) Comparison \[7 marks\]**

| Configuration | Stability Factor $S$ | Best-case $S$ | Worst-case $S$ |
|---|---|---|---|
| Fixed bias | $S = 1 + \beta$ | $1+\beta$ (no improvement) | $1+\beta$ |
| Two-supply bias | $S = \dfrac{1+\beta}{1 + \beta \cdot \dfrac{R_E}{R_E + R_B}}$ | $1$ (when $R_E \gg R_B$) | $1+\beta$ (when $R_E \to 0$) |

**Best stability condition:** $R_E \gg R_B$ (typically $R_E \geq 10 R_B$). In this limit:

$$
\frac{R_E}{R_E + R_B} \to 1 \implies S \to \frac{1+\beta}{1+\beta} = 1
$$

**Physical reason:** When $R_E$ dominates, any change in $I_C$ produces a large voltage change across $R_E$ that opposes the change, providing strong **DC negative feedback** and decoupling the Q-point from $\beta$ and $I_{CO}$.

*(4 marks for comparison table + 3 marks for the condition and physical explanation)*

---

**(b) Numerical Solution \[7 marks\]**

**Step 1 — Compute $I_B$:**

$$
I_B = \frac{V_{EE} - V_{BE}}{R_B + \beta R_E} = \frac{10 - 0.7}{20\,000 + 150 \times 2\,000}
$$

$$
I_B = \frac{9.3}{20\,000 + 300\,000} = \frac{9.3}{320\,000}
$$

$$
I_B = 29.06\ \mu\text{A}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute $I_C$:**

$$
I_C = \beta I_B = 150 \times 29.06\ \mu\text{A}
$$

$$
\boxed{I_C \approx 4.36\ \text{mA}}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute $V_{CE}$:**

$$
V_{CE} = V_{CC} + V_{EE} - I_C(R_C + R_E)
$$

$$
V_{CE} = 15 + 10 - (4.36 \times 10^{-3})(2\,000 + 2\,000)
$$

$$
V_{CE} = 25 - (4.36 \times 10^{-3})(4\,000) = 25 - 17.44
$$

$$
\boxed{V_{CE} = 7.56\ \text{V}}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute Stability Factor $S$:**

$$
S = \frac{1+\beta}{1 + \beta \cdot \dfrac{R_E}{R_E + R_B}} = \frac{151}{1 + 150 \cdot \dfrac{2\,000}{2\,000 + 20\,000}}
$$

$$
S = \frac{151}{1 + 150 \cdot \dfrac{1}{11}} = \frac{151}{1 + 13.636} = \frac{151}{14.636}
$$

$$
\boxed{S \approx 10.32}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Sign error on $V_{EE}$:** Students frequently write $I_B = \frac{V_{BE} - V_{EE}}{R_B + \beta R_E}$ — this is **wrong**. The correct numerator is $V_{EE} - V_{BE}$ because the emitter is *pulled down* by the negative supply, which is what forward-biases the B-E junction. **(Deduct 2 marks)**
> 2. **Forgetting to add $V_{CC} + V_{EE}$ in $V_{CE}$:** A common mistake is writing $V_{CE} = V_{CC} - I_C(R_C + R_E)$ (single-supply form). For two-supply, the **sum** of both supplies drives the output span. **(Deduct 2 marks)**
> 3. **Using $I_E = I_C$ without stating the assumption:** Always mention "assuming $I_C \approx I_E$ since $\beta \gg 1$" to score full marks in derivations.
> 4. **Stability factor formula confusion:** Do not write the single-supply emitter-bias formula here. The two-supply expression has $\dfrac{R_E}{R_E + R_B}$ in the denominator (not $\dfrac{R_E}{R_E + R_B \text{ with } R_B \text{ to } V_{BB}}$).

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Using Two Power Supplies**

- ✅ **Topology:** $+V_{CC} \rightarrow R_C \rightarrow C$; $E \rightarrow R_E \rightarrow -V_{EE}$; $B \rightarrow R_B \rightarrow$ Ground.
- ✅ **Key equation:** $I_B = \dfrac{V_{EE} - V_{BE}}{R_B + (\beta+1) R_E}$ (memorize this verbatim).
- ✅ **Q-point voltage:** $V_{CE} = V_{CC} + V_{EE} - I_C(R_C + R_E)$ (sum of both supplies — *not* just $V_{CC}$).
- ✅ **Stability factor:** $S = \dfrac{1+\beta}{1 + \beta \cdot \dfrac{R_E}{R_E + R_B}}$.
- ✅ **Ideal stability condition:** $R_E \gg R_B$ gives $S \to 1$.
- ✅ **Worst case:** $R_E = 0$ degenerates to fixed bias, $S = 1+\beta$.
- ✅ **Always assume:** silicon BJT ($V_{BE} = 0.7$ V), $I_C \approx I_E$, active region ($V_{CE} > 0.2$ V).
- ✅ **Engineering value:** Used as the foundational template for op-amp dual-supply biasing and Class-AB output stages.
- ✅ **Common KTU mistake to avoid:** Forgetting the negative feedback mechanism; examiners award 1–2 marks specifically for *explaining why* $S$ becomes small.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Drain-Gate Feedback Resistor, MOSFET as Amplifier, Channel Length Modulation, Small Signal Analysis of MOSFET: Voltage Transfer Characteristics
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCICT302 - ELECTRONIC CIRCUITS AND NETWORKS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Drain-Gate Feedback Resistor, MOSFET as Amplifier, Channel Length Modulation, Small Signal Analysis of MOSFET: Voltage Transfer Characteristics

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Drain-Gate Feedback Resistor ($R_G$ or $R_F$)

### Formal KTU Definition
A **Drain-Gate Feedback Resistor** is a large-value resistor connected between the **drain** and the **gate** terminal of a MOSFET in a common-source amplifier topology. Because the MOSFET gate draws negligible DC current ($I_G \approx 0$), there is no voltage drop across this resistor at DC, forcing $V_G = V_D$. This creates a **self-biasing** (negative DC feedback) configuration that automatically stabilizes the quiescent operating point (Q-point) against device-to-device parameter variations (threshold voltage $V_{th}$, transconductance parameter $k'$, and geometry $W/L$).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024):** The Drain-Gate Feedback bias scheme is the *de facto* standard DC biasing topology for discrete MOSFET amplifier design because it requires only **one** resistor (plus the drain resistor $R_D$) and provides inherent Q-point stabilization.

### Conceptual Analogy — The "Thermostat" Effect
Imagine a room where a heater is controlled by a thermostat mounted on the ceiling.

- If the **room gets too hot** (drain current tries to increase due to a hotter MOSFET), the **thermostat (feedback resistor) senses the drain voltage rising** and "pulls up" the gate voltage too.
- A higher $V_{GS}$ would normally turn the MOSFET *more* ON... so this seems wrong. Actually, the opposite happens: the feedback senses that as current increases, $V_D$ drops, so it pulls $V_G$ down, **reducing $V_{GS}$** and opposing the change. This is **negative feedback** at DC.

The feedback resistor therefore acts like a *self-correcting thermostat* — it continuously forces the circuit to settle at a stable DC bias, immune to small manufacturing variations in the transistor.

---

## 1.2 MOSFET as an Amplifier

### Formal KTU Definition
A **MOSFET amplifier** is an analog circuit that uses a MOSFET biased in the **saturation (active) region** as a voltage-controlled current source (VCCS). A small AC variation in the gate-source voltage $\Delta V_{GS}$ produces a proportional variation in drain current $\Delta I_D = g_m \Delta V_{GS}$, which is converted to a larger voltage swing across the drain load $R_D$. The fundamental amplification action arises from the transconductance $g_m$ of the device.

### Conceptual Analogy — The "Water Faucet with a Pressure Gauge"
Think of the MOSFET as a **water valve** controlled by how hard you push sideways on the handle.

- The **handle push** = $v_{gs}$ (small AC input signal at the gate).
- The **water flow** = $i_d$ (drain current).
- The **pipe's resistance to flow** = the drain resistor $R_D$ (converts flow back to pressure/voltage).

A tiny twist of the handle (millivolts of $v_{gs}$) controls a massive flow of water (milliamps of $i_d$). When that flow passes through a narrow pipe ($R_D$), it generates a much larger pressure change — which is your amplified output voltage.

> [!NOTE]
> **Three Standard MOSFET Amplifier Topologies (KTU Syllabus):**
> 1. **Common Source (CS)** — Input at Gate, Output at Drain. Voltage gain is high and inverting.
> 2. **Common Drain (CD) / Source Follower** — Input at Gate, Output at Source. Voltage gain $\approx 1$, used as a buffer.
> 3. **Common Gate (CG)** — Input at Source, Output at Drain. Current gain $\approx 1$, high-frequency use.

---

## 1.3 Channel Length Modulation (CLM)

### Formal KTU Definition
**Channel Length Modulation** is the physical phenomenon in a MOSFET operating in saturation where the effective conductive channel length $L_{eff}$ shortens as the drain-source voltage $V_{DS}$ increases beyond $V_{DS,sat} = V_{GS} - V_{th}$. This is caused by the widening of the reverse-biased drain-to-body depletion region, which encroaches into the channel from the drain side. The result is that $I_D$ is no longer independent of $V_{DS}$, but increases linearly with it, modeled by the factor $(1 + \lambda V_{DS})$.

### Conceptual Analogy — The "Stretching Rubber Band Pinch"
Picture a stretched rubber band (the inversion layer channel) held between two posts (source and drain).

- As you pull the right post further away (increase $V_{DS}$), the rubber band stretches and **thins** near the right post (the depletion region "eats" into the channel).
- The *effective* length of the conducting rubber becomes shorter, so its **resistance decreases**, and more current can flow.
- The parameter $\lambda$ (lambda) tells you *how aggressively* the depletion region nibbles into the channel per volt of $V_{DS}$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Physical Constants / Standard Metrics:**
> - $\lambda$ has units of **V$^{-1}$** and typically ranges from **0.01 to 0.1 V$^{-1}$** for modern MOSFETs.
> - The Early Voltage $V_A = 1/\lambda$ has units of **V**, analogous to the BJT Early voltage.
> - For an ideal long-channel device, $\lambda \to 0$ and $r_o \to \infty$ (current source).

---

## 1.4 Small Signal Analysis of MOSFET & Voltage Transfer Characteristics

### Formal KTU Definition
**Small Signal Analysis** is the linearized AC analysis of a MOSFET amplifier performed around a fixed DC operating point (the Q-point). The nonlinear square-law $I_D$–$V_{GS}$ relationship is replaced by a linear model using two parameters: the **transconductance** $g_m$ (relating $\Delta I_D$ to $\Delta V_{GS}$) and the **output resistance** $r_o$ (capturing channel length modulation). The **Voltage Transfer Characteristic (VTC)** is the plot of output voltage $V_{DS}$ versus input voltage $V_{GS}$ for the amplifier — it graphically shows the DC bias trajectory through cutoff, saturation, and triode regions.

### Conceptual Analogy — The "Seesaw Near a Cliff"
The VTC of a CS amplifier is like a **seesaw tilting off a cliff**:

- Far left (low $V_{GS}$): seesaw is flat on the ground — MOSFET is **OFF** (cutoff).
- Middle: seesaw is balanced, sloping downward — MOSFET is in **saturation**, providing linear amplification.
- Right (high $V_{GS}$): seesaw has tipped over the cliff — MOSFET is in **triode**, output collapses.

The **Q-point** must be placed on the safe middle plateau (the saturation region) so that small input wiggles produce proportional output wiggles without falling off either edge.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight for KTU Exams:** The slope of the VTC in the saturation region is the **DC voltage gain** (assuming no source degeneration). In the small-signal limit, this slope equals $-g_m R_D$ (without $r_o$) or $-g_m (R_D \parallel r_o)$ (with $r_o$).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Drain-Gate Feedback Bias — Operating Principle

The circuit has $V_{DD}$ at the drain through $R_D$, and the gate is tied back to the drain through the feedback resistor $R_F$ (or $R_G$). The source is grounded directly.

### Why does it self-bias?

1. **No gate current flows** ($I_G = 0$), so the feedback resistor $R_F$ has *zero voltage drop* at DC.
2. Therefore: $V_G = V_D$ exactly.
3. Hence: $V_{GS} = V_G - V_S = V_D - 0 = V_D = V_{DS}$.
4. KVL on the drain loop: $V_{DS} = V_{DD} - I_D R_D$.

Combining these:

$$V_{GS} = V_{DD} - I_D R_D$$

5. The MOSFET must satisfy its square-law equation in saturation:

$$I_D = \frac{1}{2} k_n \left( V_{GS} - V_{th} \right)^2$$

6. Substitute $V_{GS}$ from step 4 into step 5 and solve the resulting quadratic for $I_D$.

### Advantages
- **Single-resistor** self-biasing (no separate gate-bias divider needed).
- **Negative DC feedback** stabilizes Q-point against $V_{th}$ variation and temperature.
- **High input impedance** preserved at AC because $R_F$ is bypassed at signal frequencies using a coupling capacitor or because of its large value.

### Disadvantages
- **Reduced AC gain** if $R_F$ is not properly bypassed (it acts as a feedback element for AC as well unless a bypass capacitor is used).
- Limited gain-stage voltage swing due to the bias constraints.

---

## 2.2 MOSFET as Amplifier — Regions of Operation

For linear amplification, the MOSFET must be biased in the **saturation (active) region**:

$$V_{GS} > V_{th} \quad \text{AND} \quad V_{DS} \geq V_{GS} - V_{th}$$

| Region | Condition on $V_{GS}$ | Condition on $V_{DS}$ | Behavior |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Cutoff** | $V_{GS} < V_{th}$ | Any | $I_D = 0$, MOSFET is OFF |
| **Triode (Ohmic)** | $V_{GS} > V_{th}$ | $V_{DS} < V_{GS} - V_{th}$ | MOSFET acts like a voltage-controlled resistor |
| **Saturation (Active)** | $V_{GS} > V_{th}$ | $V_{DS} \geq V_{GS} - V_{th}$ | MOSFET acts as a VCCS (ideal amplifier) |

---

## 2.3 Channel Length Modulation — Detailed Mechanism

In saturation, the channel is "pinched off" at the drain end. As $V_{DS}$ increases beyond $V_{DS,sat}$:

- The depletion region around the reverse-biased drain-body junction widens.
- The effective channel length reduces: $L_{eff} = L - \Delta L$.
- Since $I_D \propto 1/L_{eff}$, the drain current increases linearly with $V_{DS}$.

The modified saturation current equation (with CLM):

$$I_D = \frac{1}{2} \mu_n C_{ox} \frac{W}{L} \left( V_{GS} - V_{th} \right)^2 \left( 1 + \lambda V_{DS} \right)$$

The output resistance $r_o$ captures CLM for small-signal analysis:

$$r_o = \frac{1}{\lambda I_D} = \frac{V_A}{I_D}$$

where $V_A = 1/\lambda$ is the **Early Voltage** (in Volts).

---

## 2.4 Small Signal Parameters of MOSFET

The two fundamental small-signal parameters used in KTU-level analysis are:

### Transconductance $g_m$

$$g_m = \frac{\partial I_D}{\partial V_{GS}} \bigg\vert_{Q} = \mu_n C_{ox} \frac{W}{L} \left( V_{GS} - V_{th} \right) = \sqrt{2 \mu_n C_{ox} \frac{W}{L} I_D} = \frac{2 I_D}{V_{GS} - V_{th}}$$

### Output Resistance $r_o$

$$r_o = \frac{1}{\lambda I_D} = \frac{V_A}{I_D}$$

If $\lambda = 0$ (ideal), then $r_o = \infty$.

### Body Transconductance $g_{mb}$ (advanced — for source-body not tied together)

$$g_{mb} = \chi \cdot g_m, \quad \chi = \frac{\gamma}{2\sqrt{2\phi_F + V_{SB}}} \approx 0.1 \text{ to } 0.3$$

> [!NOTE]
> For most KTU problems, the source is tied to the bulk (substrate), so $g_{mb} = 0$ is assumed.

---

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| **#** | **Quantity** | **Formula** | **Condition / Units** | **Remarks** |
|:---:|:---|:---|:---|:---|
| 1 | Drain Current (Sat, no $\lambda$) | $I_D = \frac{1}{2} k_n (V_{GS} - V_{th})^2$ | A; $k_n = \mu_n C_{ox} W/L$ | Square-law; ideal |
| 2 | Drain Current (Sat, with $\lambda$) | $I_D = \frac{1}{2} k_n (V_{GS} - V_{th})^2 (1 + \lambda V_{DS})$ | A | Includes CLM |
| 3 | Drain-Gate Feedback Bias | $V_{GS} = V_{DD} - I_D R_D$ | V | Self-bias relation |
| 4 | Saturation Edge Voltage | $V_{DS,sat} = V_{GS} - V_{th}$ | V | Triode/Sat boundary |
| 5 | Transconductance $g_m$ | $g_m = k_n (V_{GS} - V_{th}) = \sqrt{2 k_n I_D}$ | S (Siemens) or A/V | Linear gain factor |
| 6 | Output Resistance $r_o$ | $r_o = 1/(\lambda I_D) = V_A / I_D$ | $\Omega$ | Due to CLM |
| 7 | CS Voltage Gain (no $r_o$) | $A_v = -g_m R_D$ | dimensionless | Ideal, inverting |
| 8 | CS Voltage Gain (with $r_o$) | $A_v = -g_m (R_D \parallel r_o)$ | dimensionless | Realistic |
| 9 | CS Input Resistance | $R_{in} = \infty$ (ideal gate) or $R_F$ (with feedback) | $\Omega$ | Extremely high |
| 10 | CS Output Resistance | $R_{out} = R_D \parallel r_o$ | $\Omega$ | Looking into drain |
| 11 | Source Follower Gain | $A_v = \frac{g_m (R_S \parallel r_o)}{1 + g_m (R_S \parallel r_o)}$ | dimensionless | $\approx 1$, non-inverting |
| 12 | Boundary VTC Equation | $V_{GS,crit} = V_{th} + \frac{-1 + \sqrt{1 + 2 k_n R_D V_{DD}}}{k_n R_D}$ | V | Edge of saturation |

---

## 2.6 Engineering Utility

- **Drain-Gate Feedback Bias** is ubiquitous in **discrete RF amplifiers, op-amp input stages**, and **IC bias current generators** because it eliminates the need for a gate-bias voltage divider (which would load the high-impedance gate).
- **Channel Length Modulation** is the dominant non-ideality limiting the **DC gain of op-amps** (open-loop gain $A_{OL} \propto g_m r_o$). Modern CMOS op-amps use **cascode** and **active cascode** topologies to suppress $\lambda$ effects.
- **Voltage Transfer Characteristics** are the foundational tool for **analog IC design** — they determine the **linear input range** (small-signal swing), the **maximum undistorted output swing**, and the **headroom** of the amplifier.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Drain-Gate Feedback Bias — Solving for the Q-point

**Problem Setup:** A common-source NMOS amplifier has $V_{DD} = 12\,\text{V}$, $R_D = 4.7\,\text{k}\Omega$, a feedback resistor $R_F$ between drain and gate, and the MOSFET parameters $V_{th} = 1.5\,\text{V}$ and $k_n = 1\,\text{mA/V}^2$. The source is grounded. Find the Q-point $(I_D, V_{GS}, V_{DS})$.

### Step 1 — Write the KVL constraint on the drain-source loop

Since the gate draws no current, there is no drop across $R_F$, so $V_G = V_D$. With source grounded:

$$V_{GS} = V_{DS} = V_D$$

Applying KVL from $V_{DD}$ through $R_D$ to the drain:

$$V_{DD} = I_D R_D + V_{DS} \implies V_{DS} = V_{DD} - I_D R_D$$

Therefore:

$$V_{GS} = V_{DD} - I_D R_D$$

### Step 2 — Substitute into the square-law saturation equation

Assume saturation (we will verify later). For the MOSFET:

$$I_D = \frac{1}{2} k_n \left( V_{GS} - V_{th} \right)^2$$

Substituting $V_{GS} = V_{DD} - I_D R_D$:

$$I_D = \frac{1}{2} k_n \left( V_{DD} - I_D R_D - V_{th} \right)^2$$

### Step 3 — Insert numerical values and simplify

Let $V_{OV} = V_{GS} - V_{th} = V_{DD} - I_D R_D - V_{th} = 10.5 - 4.7 I_D$ (with $I_D$ in mA).

$$I_D = \frac{1}{2} (1) (10.5 - 4.7 I_D)^2$$

Multiplying both sides by 2:

$$2 I_D = (10.5 - 4.7 I_D)^2 = 110.25 - 98.7 I_D + 22.09 I_D^2$$

Rearranging into standard quadratic form:

$$22.09 I_D^2 - 100.7 I_D + 110.25 = 0$$

### Step 4 — Solve the quadratic using the formula

$$I_D = \frac{100.7 \pm \sqrt{100.7^2 - 4(22.09)(110.25)}}{2(22.09)}$$

$$I_D = \frac{100.7 \pm \sqrt{10140.49 - 9740.49}}{44.18} = \frac{100.7 \pm \sqrt{400}}{44.18} = \frac{100.7 \pm 20}{44.18}$$

Two roots:

$$I_{D1} = \frac{120.7}{44.18} \approx 2.732\,\text{mA}, \quad I_{D2} = \frac{80.7}{44.18} \approx 1.827\,\text{mA}$$

### Step 5 — Select the physically valid root

For the larger root $I_{D1} = 2.732\,\text{mA}$:
$V_{GS} = 12 - 2.732 \times 4.7 = 12 - 12.84 = -0.84\,\text{V}$. Since $V_{GS} < V_{th} = 1.5\,\text{V}$, the MOSFET would be OFF. **Reject.**

For the smaller root $I_{D2} = 1.827\,\text{mA}$:
$V_{GS} = 12 - 1.827 \times 4.7 = 12 - 8.587 = 3.413\,\text{V}$. Since $V_{GS} = 3.413\,\text{V} > V_{th} = 1.5\,\text{V}$, the device is ON. **Accept.**

### Step 6 — Verify saturation

$$V_{DS} = 3.413\,\text{V}, \quad V_{DS,sat} = V_{GS} - V_{th} = 3.413 - 1.5 = 1.913\,\text{V}$$

Since $V_{DS} = 3.413 \geq 1.913 = V_{DS,sat}$ ✓, the MOSFET is indeed in saturation.

### Final Q-point

$$\boxed{I_D \approx 1.83\,\text{mA}, \quad V_{GS} \approx 3.41\,\text{V}, \quad V_{DS} \approx 3.41\,\text{V}}$$

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Channel Length Modulation — Effect on Output Resistance

**Problem Setup:** A MOSFET has $I_D = 1\,\text{mA}$ at $V_{DS} = 5\,\text{V}$ and $\lambda = 0.02\,\text{V}^{-1}$. Find (a) $r_o$, (b) the Early Voltage $V_A$, and (c) the percentage increase in $I_D$ when $V_{DS}$ rises to $6\,\text{V}$ (with $V_{GS}$ held constant).

### Step 1 — Compute the output resistance

Using the definition:

$$r_o = \frac{1}{\lambda I_D} = \frac{1}{(0.02)(10^{-3})} = \frac{1}{2 \times 10^{-5}} = 50{,}000\,\Omega = 50\,\text{k}\Omega$$

### Step 2 — Compute the Early Voltage

$$V_A = \frac{1}{\lambda} = \frac{1}{0.02} = 50\,\text{V}$$

### Step 3 — Percentage increase in $I_D$

The saturation current with CLM:

$$I_D(V_{DS}) = I_{D0}\left(1 + \lambda V_{DS}\right)$$

where $I_{D0}$ is the current at $V_{DS} = 0$ (extrapolated). Since $I_D = 1\,\text{mA}$ at $V_{DS} = 5\,\text{V}$:

$$1\,\text{mA} = I_{D0}(1 + 0.02 \times 5) = I_{D0}(1.1) \implies I_{D0} = \frac{1\,\text{mA}}{1.1} \approx 0.9091\,\text{mA}$$

At $V_{DS} = 6\,\text{V}$:

$$I_D(6\,\text{V}) = 0.9091 \times (1 + 0.02 \times 6) = 0.9091 \times 1.12 = 1.0182\,\text{mA}$$

### Step 4 — Compute percentage increase

$$\Delta I_D = 1.0182 - 1 = 0.0182\,\text{mA}$$

$$\%\,\text{increase} = \frac{0.0182}{1} \times 100\% = 1.82\%$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Interpretation:** A 20% increase in $V_{DS}$ (from 5V to 6V) causes only ~1.82% increase in $I_D$ — this is exactly the linear behavior captured by the $(1 + \lambda V_{DS})$ term. The output resistance $r_o = 50\,\text{k}\Omega$ quantifies the inverse of this "softness" of the current source.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Voltage Transfer Characteristics (VTC) of Common-Source Amplifier

**Problem Setup:** NMOS CS amplifier with $V_{DD} = 10\,\text{V}$, $R_D = 2\,\text{k}\Omega$, $V_{th} = 1\,\text{V}$, $k_n = 1\,\text{mA/V}^2$, and $\lambda = 0$. Derive the complete VTC equation and find the boundary between triode and saturation regions.

### Step 1 — Define the VTC

The VTC is the function $V_{DS}(V_{GS})$, describing how the output DC voltage depends on the input DC voltage.

### Step 2 — Region 1: Cutoff ($V_{GS} < V_{th}$)

If $V_{GS} < V_{th}$, then $I_D = 0$ and:

$$V_{DS} = V_{DD} - I_D R_D = V_{DD} = 10\,\text{V}$$

The VTC is **constant at $V_{DD}$** in this region.

### Step 3 — Region 2: Saturation ($V_{GS} > V_{th}$ and $V_{DS} \geq V_{GS} - V_{th}$)

Here, $I_D = \frac{1}{2} k_n (V_{GS} - V_{th})^2$, so:

$$V_{DS} = V_{DD} - \frac{1}{2} k_n R_D (V_{GS} - V_{th})^2$$

Substituting numbers:

$$V_{DS} = 10 - \frac{1}{2}(1)(2)(V_{GS} - 1)^2 = 10 - (V_{GS} - 1)^2$$

This is a **downward-opening parabola** in $V_{GS}$, valid only in saturation.

### Step 4 — Region 3: Triode ($V_{GS} > V_{th}$ and $V_{DS} < V_{GS} - V_{th}$)

Here, $V_{DS}$ is no longer a free variable — it is *constrained* to equal $V_{DS} = V_{GS} - V_{th}$ on the saturation boundary and below it, the device is in triode where the drain-source channel behaves like a resistor. The triode region is reached when the parabola from Step 3 intersects the boundary line $V_{DS} = V_{GS} - V_{th}$.

### Step 5 — Find the Saturation-Triode Boundary

At the boundary, both equations must hold simultaneously:

$$V_{DS} = 10 - (V_{GS} - 1)^2 \quad \text{(saturation)}$$

$$V_{DS} = V_{GS} - 1 \quad \text{(boundary line)}$$

Setting them equal:

$$V_{GS} - 1 = 10 - (V_{GS} - 1)^2$$

Let $x = V_{GS} - 1$:

$$x = 10 - x^2 \implies x^2 + x - 10 = 0$$

$$x = \frac{-1 \pm \sqrt{1 + 40}}{2} = \frac{-1 \pm \sqrt{41}}{2} \approx \frac{-1 \pm 6.403}{2}$$

Taking the positive root (must be positive for NMOS):

$$x \approx \frac{5.403}{2} \approx 2.7015\,\text{V}$$

Therefore:

$$V_{GS,crit} = 1 + 2.7015 \approx 3.70\,\text{V}$$

$$V_{DS,crit} = V_{GS,crit} - 1 = 2.70\,\text{V}$$

### Step 6 — Verify the trajectory

| $V_{GS}$ (V) | Mode | $V_{DS}$ (V) |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| 0.5 | Cutoff | 10.00 |
| 1.5 | Sat (start) | $10 - 0.25 = 9.75$ |
| 2.0 | Sat | $10 - 1.00 = 9.00$ |
| 2.5 | Sat | $10 - 2.25 = 7.75$ |
| 3.0 | Sat | $10 - 4.00 = 6.00$ |
| 3.5 | Sat | $10 - 6.25 = 3.75$ |
| **3.70** | **Boundary** | **2.70** |
| 4.0 | Triode | $< V_{GS} - 1 = 3$; only valid DC if load line intersects |

### Step 7 — Slope of VTC in Saturation (the DC Gain)

Differentiating the saturation branch:

$$\frac{d V_{DS}}{d V_{GS}} = -k_n R_D (V_{GS} - V_{th}) = -g_m R_D$$

For example, at $V_{GS} = 2.5\,\text{V}$:

$$\frac{d V_{DS}}{d V_{GS}} = -(1)(2)(1.5) = -3\,\text{V/V}$$

This negative slope is the **DC gain** of the inverting CS amplifier.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4: Small-Signal Voltage Gain Including $r_o$

**Problem Setup:** Using the Q-point from Section 3.1 ($I_D = 1.83\,\text{mA}$, $V_{GS} = 3.41\,\text{V}$) and given $\lambda = 0.02\,\text{V}^{-1}$, $R_D = 4.7\,\text{k}\Omega$. Compute the small-signal voltage gain $A_v$.

### Step 1 — Compute $g_m$ using $I_D$

$$g_m = \frac{2 I_D}{V_{GS} - V_{th}} = \frac{2 \times 1.83\,\text{mA}}{3.41 - 1.5} = \frac{3.66}{1.91} \approx 1.916\,\text{mA/V} = 1.916\,\text{mS}$$

### Step 2 — Compute $r_o$

$$r_o = \frac{1}{\lambda I_D} = \frac{1}{0.02 \times 1.83 \times 10^{-3}} = \frac{1}{3.66 \times 10^{-5}} \approx 27.32\,\text{k}\Omega$$

### Step 3 — Compute the parallel combination $R_D \parallel r_o$

$$R_D \parallel r_o = \frac{4.7 \times 27.32}{4.7 + 27.32} = \frac{128.4}{32.02} \approx 4.01\,\text{k}\Omega$$

### Step 4 — Compute the small-signal gain

$$A_v = -g_m (R_D \parallel r_o) = -(1.916\,\text{mS})(4.01\,\text{k}\Omega) = -7.68\,\text{V/V}$$

### Step 5 — Compare with the ideal (no $r_o$) gain

$$A_{v,\text{ideal}} = -g_m R_D = -(1.916)(4.7) = -9.00\,\text{V/V}$$

The presence of $r_o$ reduces the gain magnitude from 9.00 to 7.68, a **~15% reduction**. For amplifiers requiring high DC gain, cascode or active-load configurations must be used to boost the effective output impedance.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Drain-Gate Feedback Bias Circuit Topology

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    VDD["VDD Supply"]
    RD["Drain Resistor RD"]
    RF["Feedback Resistor RF"]
    D["MOSFET Drain"]
    G["MOSFET Gate"]
    S["MOSFET Source (Grounded)"]
    B["MOSFET Bulk/Substrate (Grounded)"]
    OUT["DC Output Node VD = VDS"]

    VDD --- RD
    RD --- D
    D --- RF
    RF --- G
    S --- B
    D --- OUT

    note1["No gate current => no DC drop across RF => VG = VD"]
    note2["VGS = VDD - ID*RD, used to solve square-law equation"]

    RF -.-> note1
    VDD -.-> note2
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** The single resistor $R_F$ (or $R_G$) bridging the drain and gate creates a self-biasing loop. The DC analysis uses the property $I_G = 0$ to collapse $R_F$ into a wire at DC, forcing $V_G = V_D$.

---

## 4.2 MOSFET Small-Signal Hybrid-Pi Model (with Channel Length Modulation)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    G["Gate Node (+)"]
    S["Source Node (-)"]
    D["Drain Node"]
    GMI["Controlled Current Source: gm * vgs"]
    RO["Output Resistance ro = 1/(lambda*ID)"]
    VGS["vgs (control voltage)"]
    OUT["Output vo"]

    G ---|"+ input"| VGS
    VGS ---|"controls"| GMI
    S ---|"- input"| VGS
    GMI --- D
    D --- RO
    RO --- S
    D --- OUT
    S --- GND2["Reference / Common Node"]

    noteA["In CS configuration: RD is connected from D to VDD, bypassing GMI parallel to ro"]
    noteB["vo = -gm*vgs*(RD parallel ro)"]

    D -.-> noteA
    OUT -.-> noteB
```

---

## 4.3 Voltage Transfer Characteristic (VTC) — Block-Level Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph REGION_CUTOFF["REGION 1: CUTOFF (VGS less than Vth)"]
        A1["Input VGS less than Vth"]
        A2["ID = 0"]
        A3["VDS = VDD (flat line)"]
        A1 --> A2 --> A3
    end

    subgraph REGION_SAT["REGION 2: SATURATION (VGS greater than Vth and VDS greater than VGS minus Vth)"]
        B1["Parabolic descent"]
        B2["VDS = VDD minus (1/2) k RD VOV squared"]
        B3["Slope = negative gm*RD (linear amplification)"]
        B1 --> B2 --> B3
    end

    subgraph REGION_TRIODE["REGION 3: TRIODE (VDS less than VGS minus Vth)"]
        C1["Parabola intersects boundary line"]
        C2["VDS collapses along line VDS = VGS minus Vth"]
        C3["VDS drops to low values near 0"]
        C1 --> C2 --> C3
    end

    REGION_CUTOFF --> REGION_SAT
    REGION_SAT --> REGION_TRIODE

    QPT["OPTIMAL Q-POINT: Placed in middle of saturation region for maximum symmetric swing"]

    REGION_SAT -.-> QPT
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **For exam answers:** When asked to draw or describe the VTC, always label the **three regions** explicitly, the **Q-point location**, and the **boundary equation** $V_{DS} = V_{GS} - V_{th}$.

---

## 4.4 Signal Flow in a CS Amplifier with Drain-Gate Feedback

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    VSIG["AC Input: vsig"]
    CIN["Coupling Capacitor Cin (blocks DC)"]
    GATE["Gate Node (high Z)"]
    MOSFET["MOSFET in Saturation: ID = (1/2)k VOV squared"]
    DRAIN_NODE["Drain Node: VD = VDD minus ID RD"]
    ROUT["Output Resistance: RD parallel ro"]
    COUT["Coupling Capacitor Cout"]
    VOUT["AC Output: vo"]
    RFBK["Feedback Resistor RF: DC bias only, AC bypassed by CFB if used"]

    VSIG --> CIN --> GATE
    GATE --> MOSFET
    MOSFET --> DRAIN_NODE
    DRAIN_NODE --> ROUT --> COUT --> VOUT
    DRAIN_NODE -.-> RFBK -.-> GATE
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Functional Flow:** AC input enters through $C_{in}$ → controls $V_{GS}$ → modulates $I_D$ → develops $v_{ds}$ across $R_D \parallel r_o$ → exits through $C_{out}$ as amplified, inverted output $v_o$. The feedback $R_F$ participates only at DC; for AC, a bypass capacitor $C_{FB}$ can be added in parallel with $R_F$ to preserve high AC gain.

---

## 4.5 Equivalent Circuit Summary (Block Diagram for Problem Solving)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph DC_ANALYSIS["DC ANALYSIS (Large-Signal)"]
        DC1["KVL: VGS = VDD minus ID RD"]
        DC2["Square law: ID = (1/2) k VOV squared"]
        DC3["Solve quadratic: get ID, VGS, VDS"]
        DC4["Verify: VDS greater than VOV (saturation check)"]
        DC1 --> DC2 --> DC3 --> DC4
    end

    subgraph AC_ANALYSIS["AC ANALYSIS (Small-Signal)"]
        AC1["Compute gm = 2 ID / VOV"]
        AC2["Compute ro = 1 / (lambda ID)"]
        AC3["Compute Av = negative gm times RD parallel ro"]
        AC4["Compute Rin (infinite for ideal gate)"]
        AC5["Compute Rout = RD parallel ro"]
        AC1 --> AC2 --> AC3
        AC2 --> AC5
        AC3 --> AC4
    end

    DC_ANALYSIS --> AC_ANALYSIS
```
