# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam — May 2024]

**Q:** Define channel length modulation in a MOSFET. Write the modified drain current equation including the modulation parameter $\lambda$, and explain the significance of the Early voltage $V_A$.

**Model Answer:**

**Channel Length Modulation (CLM):** In a MOSFET operating in saturation, as $V_{DS}$ increases beyond $V_{DS,sat} = V_{GS} - V_{th}$, the depletion region at the drain-body junction widens and encroaches into the channel. This reduces the effective channel length $L_{eff}$, which causes the drain current to increase slightly with $V_{DS}$ — a phenomenon called channel length modulation.

**Modified Drain Current Equation:**

$$I_D = \frac{1}{2} \mu_n C_{ox} \frac{W}{L} \left( V_{GS} - V_{th} \right)^2 \left( 1 + \lambda V_{DS} \right)$$

where $\lambda$ is the channel-length modulation parameter (units: V$^{-1}$).

**Early Voltage $V_A$:** The Early voltage is defined as the extrapolated value of $V_{DS}$ at which $I_D$ would drop to zero if the $I_D$–$V_{DS}$ curve in saturation remained linear. It is given by $V_A = 1/\lambda$. A larger $V_A$ (smaller $\lambda$) means a "more ideal" current source with higher output resistance $r_o = V_A / I_D$.

> [!TIP]
> **Examiner's Note (3-Mark Allocation):** Award 1 mark for the definition, 1 mark for the equation, and 1 mark for the significance of $V_A$. Drawing the $I_D$–$V_{DS}$ curve with the Early voltage extrapolation earns bonus credit.

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]

**Q:** Explain the principle of drain-gate feedback bias in a MOSFET amplifier. State two advantages and one disadvantage.

**Model Answer:**

**Principle:** In a drain-gate feedback bias configuration, a resistor $R_F$ is connected between the drain and gate of a MOSFET in a common-source topology. Because the MOSFET gate draws negligible DC current ($I_G \approx 0$), there is no DC voltage drop across $R_F$, and hence $V_G = V_D$. The KVL constraint gives $V_{GS} = V_{DD} - I_D R_D$, which when combined with the square-law saturation equation $I_D = \frac{1}{2} k_n (V_{GS} - V_{th})^2$, yields a self-consistent Q-point.

The feedback is **negative at DC**: if $I_D$ tries to increase (e.g., due to temperature rise), $V_D$ drops, pulling $V_G$ down through $R_F$, which reduces $V_{GS}$ and opposes the rise in $I_D$.

**Advantages:**
1. Requires only **one resistor** ($R_F$) for self-biasing, simplifying the circuit.
2. Provides excellent **DC Q-point stability** against variations in $V_{th}$, $k_n$, and temperature.

**Disadvantage:**
1. Without a bypass capacitor across $R_F$, the **AC gain is reduced** because $R_F$ provides AC negative feedback (signal also sees the feedback path).

> [!TIP]
> **Examiner's Note:** Always include the schematic reference and the equation $V_{GS} = V_{DD} - I_D R_D$ for full credit.

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice

---

### Question A (Choice 1) [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]

**Q(a)** A common-source NMOS amplifier uses drain-gate feedback bias with $V_{DD} = 15\,\text{V}$, $R_D = 5\,\text{k}\Omega$, $R_F$ connecting drain to gate, and source grounded. The MOSFET has $V_{th} = 2\,\text{V}$, $k_n = 0.5\,\text{mA/V}^2$, and $\lambda = 0$. Determine:
- (i) The DC Q-point $(I_D, V_{GS}, V_{DS})$.
- (ii) Whether the device is in saturation.
- (iii) The small-signal transconductance $g_m$.

**(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Q-point calculation:**

Since $I_G = 0$, $V_G = V_D$. Therefore $V_{GS} = V_{DS} = V_{DD} - I_D R_D$:

$$V_{GS} = 15 - 5 I_D \quad (I_D \text{ in mA})$$

Square-law in saturation:

$$I_D = \frac{1}{2} (0.5)(V_{GS} - 2)^2 = 0.25 (V_{GS} - 2)^2$$

Substituting $V_{GS} = 15 - 5 I_D$:

$$I_D = 0.25 (15 - 5 I_D - 2)^2 = 0.25 (13 - 5 I_D)^2$$

Expanding:

$$I_D = 0.25 (169 - 130 I_D + 25 I_D^2) = 42.25 - 32.5 I_D + 6.25 I_D^2$$

Rearranging:

$$6.25 I_D^2 - 33.5 I_D + 42.25 = 0$$

Using the quadratic formula:

$$I_D = \frac{33.5 \pm \sqrt{33.5^2 - 4(6.25)(42.25)}}{12.5} = \frac{33.5 \pm \sqrt{1122.25 - 1056.25}}{12.5}$$

$$I_D = \frac{33.5 \pm \sqrt{66}}{12.5} = \frac{33.5 \pm 8.124}{12.5}$$

Two roots:

$$I_{D1} = \frac{41.624}{12.5} \approx 3.33\,\text{mA}, \quad I_{D2} = \frac{25.376}{12.5} \approx 2.03\,\text{mA}$$

For $I_{D1} = 3.33\,\text{mA}$: $V_{GS} = 15 - 16.65 = -1.65\,\text{V} < V_{th}$. **Reject.**

For $I_{D2} = 2.03\,\text{mA}$: $V_{GS} = 15 - 10.15 = 4.85\,\text{V} > V_{th}$. **Accept.**

Therefore:

$$\boxed{I_D \approx 2.03\,\text{mA}, \quad V_{GS} = V_{DS} \approx 4.85\,\text{V}}$$

**(ii) Saturation check:**

$$V_{DS,sat} = V_{GS} - V_{th} = 4.85 - 2 = 2.85\,\text{V}$$

Since $V_{DS} = 4.85\,\text{V} \geq 2.85\,\text{V}$ ✓, the device is in **saturation**.

**(iii) Transconductance:**

$$g_m = \frac{2 I_D}{V_{GS} - V_{th}} = \frac{2 \times 2.03}{4.85 - 2} = \frac{4.06}{2.85} \approx 1.425\,\text{mA/V} = 1.425\,\text{mS}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfall:** Students often select the **larger root** of the quadratic ($I_{D1} = 3.33\,\text{mA}$) without verifying the constraint $V_{GS} > V_{th}$. Always substitute each root back into $V_{GS} = V_{DD} - I_D R_D$ and reject any root that gives $V_{GS} < V_{th}$.

---

**Q(b)** For the amplifier in Q(a), a load resistor $R_L = 10\,\text{k}\Omega$ is AC-coupled to the drain. If $\lambda = 0.02\,\text{V}^{-1}$, determine:
- (i) The output resistance $r_o$.
- (ii) The small-signal voltage gain $A_v = v_o / v_{gs}$.

**(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Output resistance:**

$$r_o = \frac{1}{\lambda I_D} = \frac{1}{0.02 \times 2.03 \times 10^{-3}} = \frac{1}{4.06 \times 10^{-5}} \approx 24.63\,\text{k}\Omega$$

**(ii) Voltage Gain:**

The total AC load at the drain is $R_D \parallel R_L \parallel r_o$:

$$R_D \parallel R_L = \frac{5 \times 10}{5 + 10} = \frac{50}{15} \approx 3.333\,\text{k}\Omega$$

$$R_D \parallel R_L \parallel r_o = \frac{3.333 \times 24.63}{3.333 + 24.63} = \frac{82.09}{27.96} \approx 2.936\,\text{k}\Omega$$

Voltage gain:

$$A_v = -g_m (R_D \parallel R_L \parallel r_o) = -(1.425\,\text{mS})(2.936\,\text{k}\Omega) \approx -4.18\,\text{V/V}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfall:** Students often forget to include $R_L$ in the parallel combination when an external load is AC-coupled. The total load is $R_D \parallel R_L \parallel r_o$, not just $R_D \parallel r_o$. Marks are typically lost here in KTU valuation.

---

### Question B (Choice 2) [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]

**Q(a)** Derive the complete voltage transfer characteristic (VTC) of a common-source NMOS amplifier with $V_{DD} = 10\,\text{V}$, $R_D = 2\,\text{k}\Omega$, $V_{th} = 1\,\text{V}$, $k_n = 1\,\text{mA/V}^2$, and $\lambda = 0$. Clearly identify the three regions (cutoff, saturation, triode) and find the critical value of $V_{GS}$ at the saturation-triode boundary.

**(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**VTC Definition:** The voltage transfer characteristic is the function $V_{DS}(V_{GS})$ that describes the DC output voltage as the DC input voltage is varied.

**Region 1 — Cutoff ($V_{GS} < V_{th} = 1\,\text{V}$):**

$I_D = 0 \implies V_{DS} = V_{DD} = 10\,\text{V}$. The VTC is constant at 10 V.

**Region 2 — Saturation ($V_{GS} > 1\,\text{V}$ and $V_{DS} \geq V_{GS} - 1$):**

$$V_{DS} = V_{DD} - \frac{1}{2} k_n R_D (V_{GS} - V_{th})^2 = 10 - \frac{1}{2}(1)(2)(V_{GS} - 1)^2$$

$$\boxed{V_{DS} = 10 - (V_{GS} - 1)^2}$$

This is a downward parabola, valid for $V_{DS} \geq V_{GS} - 1$.

**Region 3 — Triode:** Valid for $V_{DS} < V_{GS} - 1$, but the VTC is now constrained to the boundary line $V_{DS} = V_{GS} - 1$ (which forms the transition from the parabolic branch).

**Saturation-Triode Boundary:**

At the boundary: $V_{DS} = 10 - (V_{GS} - 1)^2$ AND $V_{DS} = V_{GS} - 1$.

Equating: $V_{GS} - 1 = 10 - (V_{GS} - 1)^2$.

Let $x = V_{GS} - 1$:

$$x = 10 - x^2 \implies x^2 + x - 10 = 0$$

$$x = \frac{-1 + \sqrt{41}}{2} \approx \frac{-1 + 6.403}{2} \approx 2.7015\,\text{V}$$

$$\boxed{V_{GS,crit} \approx 3.70\,\text{V}, \quad V_{DS,crit} \approx 2.70\,\text{V}}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfall:** Students often forget to take the *negative* root of the quadratic and end up with a non-physical $V_{GS}$. Always select the root that gives $V_{GS,crit} > V_{th}$.

---

**Q(b)** Using the VTC derived in Q(a), determine:
- (i) The DC gain at $V_{GS} = 2.5\,\text{V}$.
- (ii) The maximum symmetric output swing if the input can vary by $\pm 0.5\,\text{V}$ around $V_{GS} = 2.5\,\text{V}$.
- (iii) Whether the swing is limited by cutoff, triode, or both.

**(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(i) DC Gain at $V_{GS} = 2.5\,\text{V}$:**

Differentiating the saturation branch:

$$A_v = \frac{d V_{DS}}{d V_{GS}} = -k_n R_D (V_{GS} - V_{th}) = -(1)(2)(1.5) = -3\,\text{V/V}$$

So $A_v = -3\,\text{V/V}$ at $V_{GS} = 2.5\,\text{V}$.

**(ii) Output Swing for $\pm 0.5\,\text{V}$ input variation:**

The input swings from $V_{GS,min} = 2.0\,\text{V}$ to $V_{GS,max} = 3.0\,\text{V}$.

At $V_{GS} = 2.0\,\text{V}$:
$V_{DS} = 10 - (1.0)^2 = 9.0\,\text{V}$.

At $V_{GS} = 3.0\,\text{V}$:
$V_{DS} = 10 - (2.0)^2 = 6.0\,\text{V}$.

Output swing: $\Delta V_{DS} = 9.0 - 6.0 = 3.0\,\text{V}$.

DC gain from swing: $A_v = -3.0 / 1.0 = -3\,\text{V/V}$ ✓ (matches the derivative at the midpoint).

**(iii) Limiting Region Check:**

At $V_{GS} = 2.0\,\text{V}$: $V_{DS,sat} = 1.0\,\text{V}$; $V_{DS} = 9.0\,\text{V} \geq 1.0\,\text{V}$ ✓ — still in saturation, far from cutoff edge ($V_{GS} = 1.0\,\text{V}$, so margin = 1.0 V).

At $V_{GS} = 3.0\,\text{V}$: $V_{DS,sat} = 2.0\,\text{V}$; $V_{DS} = 6.0\,\text{V} \geq 2.0\,\text{V}$ ✓ — still in saturation, far from triode edge ($V_{GS,crit} = 3.70\,\text{V}$, so margin = 0.70 V).

**Both ends remain in saturation**, so the swing is symmetric and distortion-free. The DC gain is constant at $-3\,\text{V/V}$ across the entire swing.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfall:** When computing the gain from the swing formula, students sometimes divide by the *peak-to-peak* input (1.0 V) rather than the *amplitude* (0.5 V) or vice versa. KTU valuation expects $A_v = v_o / v_i$ as a ratio of amplitudes (or equivalently, peak-to-peak). Always state the convention used.

---

## TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

### Rapid Revision Checklist

- [ ] **Drain-Gate Feedback Resistor** sets $V_G = V_D$ at DC; key equation: $V_{GS} = V_{DD} - I_D R_D$.
- [ ] Always **solve the quadratic** for $I_D$ and **reject the non-physical root** (where $V_{GS} < V_{th}$).
- [ ] **Always verify saturation** by checking $V_{DS} \geq V_{GS} - V_{th}$ after finding the Q-point.
- [ ] **Channel Length Modulation** is captured by the factor $(1 + \lambda V_{DS})$ in the $I_D$ equation.
- [ ] **Output resistance** $r_o = 1/(\lambda I_D) = V_A / I_D$; units: **ohms**; $\lambda$ units: **V$^{-1}$**.
- [ ] **Transconductance** $g_m = 2 I_D / V_{OV} = k_n V_{OV}$; units: **A/V** or **S** (Siemens).
- [ ] **CS Voltage Gain (ideal):** $A_v = -g_m R_D$.
- [ ] **CS Voltage Gain (with $r_o$):** $A_v = -g_m (R_D \parallel r_o)$.
- [ ] **With external AC load $R_L$:** Use $R_D \parallel R_L \parallel r_o$ in the gain formula.
- [ ] **VTC has three regions:** Cutoff ($V_{DS} = V_{DD}$), Saturation (parabolic), Triode (boundary line).
- [ ] **Boundary between saturation and triode** is the line $V_{DS} = V_{GS} - V_{th}$.
- [ ] **Q-point for maximum symmetric swing** is at the midpoint of the saturation region: $V_{DS,Q} \approx (V_{DD} + V_{DS,sat})/2$.
- [ ] **Slope of VTC = DC gain = $-g_m R_D$** (small-signal limit).
- [ ] **Drain-Gate feedback without bypass cap** reduces AC gain — always state this disadvantage in exams.
- [ ] **For NMOS in saturation:** $V_{DS,sat} = V_{GS} - V_{th}$ is the *overdrive voltage* $V_{OV}$.
- [ ] **Body effect** is ignored when source is tied to substrate (standard assumption in KTU problems).

### Key Numerical Values to Memorize

| Parameter | Typical Value | Units |
|:---|:---:|:---:|
| $\lambda$ (modern NMOS) | 0.01 – 0.05 | V$^{-1}$ |
| $V_A$ (Early voltage) | 20 – 100 | V |
| $g_m$ (typical small-signal) | 0.5 – 5 | mA/V |
| $r_o$ (typical) | 10 – 100 | k$\Omega$ |
| $k_n$ (typical discrete NMOS) | 0.5 – 2 | mA/V$^2$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final KTU Exam Tip:** Always draw the small-signal equivalent circuit (with $g_m v_{gs}$ current source and $r_o$) at the start of any gain-calculation problem. Most of the partial marks in KTU valuation come from correctly drawing this model before applying KCL/KVL.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Small Signal Operation - Equivalent Circuit
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCICT302 - ELECTRONIC CIRCUITS AND NETWORKS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Small Signal Operation - Equivalent Circuit

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Small Signal Operation** is a linearized analysis technique used in transistor (BJT/FET) amplifier circuits where the **AC input signal amplitude is sufficiently small** (typically less than **10%** of the DC bias values) such that the transistor's nonlinear V-I characteristics can be approximated by a **linear equivalent circuit** around the quiescent operating point (Q-point).

The **Small Signal Equivalent Circuit** is a linear, frequency-dependent (or frequency-independent at low frequencies) network model — most commonly the **h-parameter model** or the **T-model** (for BJT) / **π-model** or **T-model** (for FET) — that mathematically represents the transistor's incremental behavior for AC signals while replacing all DC sources by their internal impedances (ideal voltage sources → short circuits; ideal current sources → open circuits) and coupling/bypass capacitors by short circuits at the signal frequency of interest.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy (Intuitive View)

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — The Calm Lake with Ripples:**
> Imagine a vast, deep lake. The water surface has a steady "average" level (think of this as the **DC operating point** of the transistor). When a small stone is gently dropped, it creates tiny ripples — these ripples travel smoothly and predictably across the surface. Now imagine a boulder crashing in: the water level changes dramatically, the ripples become wild and unpredictable, and you can no longer use a simple wave equation to describe them.
>
> - The **DC bias** of the transistor = the calm average lake level.
> - The **small AC signal** = the gentle ripples (the *small signal*).
> - The **equivalent circuit** = the simplified wave-equation model that works *only* when the ripples are small.
> - If the signal is too large, the model "breaks" — just as the wave equation breaks for a tsunami.

## 1.3 Conditions of Validity

The small-signal approximation is **only valid** when all the following hold true:

1. The instantaneous total voltage/current deviates only slightly from the Q-point.
2. The transistor remains in the **active region** for BJT (or **saturation region** for FET) throughout the AC cycle.
3. The AC signal amplitude is **≤ 10%** of the corresponding DC bias quantity (a widely accepted engineering rule of thumb).
4. Operating frequency is low enough that internal capacitances can be ignored (low-frequency, LF model), or high enough to require inclusion (high-frequency, HF model).

> [!TIP]
> **Key Constant:** The **thermal voltage** $V_T \approx \mathbf{26 \text{ mV}}$ at room temperature ($T = 300^\circ \text{K}$) is fundamental to deriving small-signal parameters of the BJT.

## 1.4 Why We Need an Equivalent Circuit

A real transistor is a **nonlinear device** governed by the Ebers-Moll equations. Solving nonlinear circuits analytically is extremely tedious. By restricting the operation to *small* signals around a fixed Q-point, we **linearize** the device, allowing us to apply classical linear circuit analysis techniques such as **Ohm's Law, KVL, KCL, Thevenin/Norton equivalents**, and **two-port network theory**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Two-Port h-Parameter Representation (The Most Common Model for BJT)

Any linear two-port network can be characterized by four parameters. For a BJT in **common-emitter (CE)** configuration, the **hybrid (h) parameters** are the industry and academic standard at low frequencies. The defining equations are:

$$v_{be} = h_{ie}\, i_b + h_{re}\, v_{ce}$$

$$i_c = h_{fe}\, i_b + h_{oe}\, v_{ce}$$

The four h-parameters are:

- $h_{ie}$ = **Input impedance** with output AC short-circuited (units: $\Omega$)
- $h_{re}$ = **Reverse voltage ratio** (dimensionless) with input AC open-circuited
- $h_{fe}$ = **Forward current gain** (dimensionless) with output AC short-circuited
- $h_{oe}$ = **Output admittance** with input AC open-circuited (units: Siemens, S)

## 2.2 Generic h-Parameter Equivalent Circuit (CE Configuration)

The equivalent circuit consists of:

- An input loop containing $h_{ie}$ in series with a **dependent voltage source** $h_{re}\, v_{ce}$ (voltage-controlled voltage source).
- An output loop containing a **dependent current source** $h_{fe}\, i_b$ (current-controlled current source) in parallel with admittance $h_{oe}$, delivering current to load $R_L$.

## 2.3 The Simplified CE h-Parameter Model (Approximate)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Since $h_{re} \approx 10^{-3}$ to $10^{-4}$ and $h_{oe} \cdot R_L$ is usually very small, a **simplified** form is used in the vast majority of KTU problems.

In the simplified model:
- $h_{re}\, v_{ce}$ source is **neglected**.
- $h_{oe}$ is **neglected** (treated as open circuit).

This leaves only $h_{ie}$ at the input and the dependent CCCS $h_{fe}\, i_b$ at the output.

## 2.4 T-Model of BJT (Alternate Equivalent Circuit)

The T-model is derived directly from the intrinsic physical structure of the BJT. It uses the **emitter resistance** $r_e$ (also called $r_e'$), the **base spreading resistance** $r_b$, and the dependent current source $\alpha\, i_e$ (where $\alpha$ is the common-base current gain).

The fundamental relation is:

$$r_e = \frac{V_T}{I_E} = \frac{26 \text{ mV}}{I_E}$$

A useful conversion between T-model and h-parameter model:

$$h_{ie} = r_b + (\beta + 1)\, r_e \approx r_b + \beta\, r_e$$

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following table consolidates the **must-know formulas** for the KTU ESE. All symbols use SI base units unless otherwise noted.

| Quantity | Exact Formula (h-parameter) | Simplified Formula (Approximate) | Units |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Input Resistance $R_i$ | $h_{ie} - \dfrac{h_{re}\, h_{fe}}{h_{oe} + \dfrac{1}{R_L}}$ | $h_{ie}$ | $\Omega$ |
| Output Resistance $R_o$ | $\dfrac{1}{h_{oe} - \dfrac{h_{re}\, h_{fe}}{h_{ie} + R_S}}$ | $\dfrac{1}{h_{oe}}$ | $\Omega$ |
| Voltage Gain $A_v$ | $\dfrac{-h_{fe}\, R_L}{h_{ie} + (h_{re}\, h_{fe}\, R_L)}$ | $\dfrac{-h_{fe}\, R_L}{h_{ie}}$ | dimensionless |
| Current Gain $A_i$ | $\dfrac{h_{fe}}{1 + h_{oe}\, R_L}$ | $h_{fe}$ | dimensionless |
| Power Gain $A_p$ | $A_v \cdot A_i$ | $\dfrac{h_{fe}^2 \cdot R_L}{h_{ie}}$ | dimensionless |
| Emitter Resistance $r_e$ | $\dfrac{26 \text{ mV}}{I_E}$ | — | $\Omega$ |
| $h_{ie} \leftrightarrow r_e$ relation | $h_{ie} \approx \beta\, r_e$ (if $r_b$ neglected) | $h_{ie} \approx \beta\, r_e$ | $\Omega$ |

> [!WARNING]
> **Sign convention for $A_v$:** The negative sign in the voltage gain indicates a **180° phase inversion** between input and output — a hallmark of the **common-emitter** amplifier. For **common-collector (emitter follower)**, the gain is positive (non-inverting).

## 2.6 Procedure to Draw a Small-Signal Equivalent Circuit (Exam Strategy)

1. **Draw the original amplifier circuit** (with all DC sources and capacitors).
2. **Short-circuit all coupling and emitter-bypass capacitors** (since $X_C \approx 0$ at signal frequency).
3. **Short-circuit all independent DC voltage sources** ($V_{CC} \to$ ground).
4. **Open-circuit all independent DC current sources**.
5. **Replace the BJT** with its small-signal model (h-parameter or T-model).
6. **Label all node voltages and branch currents** with lowercase letters and subscripts ($v_{be}, i_b, v_{ce}, i_c$) to distinguish AC quantities from DC.
7. Mark the input port ($v_s, R_s$) and output port ($R_L$) clearly.

## 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Design of audio amplifiers** in consumer electronics (smartphones, TVs).
- **RF front-end design** in wireless transceivers — the small-signal S-parameter model is the direct high-frequency extension of the h-parameter model.
- **Operational amplifier internal stages** — every op-amp is a cascade of small-signal amplifier stages.
- **Sensor signal conditioning** circuits in biomedical instrumentation (ECG, EEG amplifiers).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of Input Resistance ($R_i$) — Simplified h-Model CE Amplifier

We consider a CE amplifier with source resistance $R_S$, load resistance $R_L$, and bias resistors $R_1, R_2$ (all bypassed/shorted at signal frequency as per the procedure in §2.6).

**Step 1:** Write the input loop KVL equation at the small-signal model.

The small-signal input voltage is $v_{be}$ across $h_{ie}$ (since $h_{re}\,v_{ce}$ is neglected in the simplified model).

$$v_{be} = h_{ie} \cdot i_b$$

**Step 2:** By definition, the input resistance is the ratio of input voltage to input current at the amplifier's input terminals (looking into the base).

$$R_i = \frac{v_{be}}{i_b}$$

**Step 3:** Substituting $v_{be} = h_{ie}\, i_b$:

$$R_i = \frac{h_{ie}\, i_b}{i_b} = h_{ie}$$

**Result (Simplified):** $\boxed{R_i = h_{ie}}$

**Step 4 (Exact case):** When $h_{re}\, v_{ce}$ is NOT neglected, the input loop becomes:

$$v_{be} = h_{ie}\, i_b + h_{re}\, v_{ce}$$

The output loop gives $v_{ce} = -i_c \cdot R_L = -h_{fe}\, i_b \cdot R_L$ (ignoring $h_{oe}$ for a moment). Substituting:

$$v_{be} = h_{ie}\, i_b + h_{re}\, (-h_{fe}\, i_b\, R_L) = i_b \left[ h_{ie} - h_{re}\, h_{fe}\, R_L \right]$$

Therefore:

$$R_i = \frac{v_{be}}{i_b} = h_{ie} - h_{re}\, h_{fe}\, R_L$$

## 3.2 Derivation of Voltage Gain ($A_v$) — Simplified h-Model

**Step 1:** Write the output loop equation.

The output voltage $v_{ce}$ is the voltage developed across $R_L$ due to the dependent current source $h_{fe}\, i_b$:

$$v_{ce} = -h_{fe}\, i_b \cdot R_L$$

The negative sign arises from the assumed direction of conventional current flowing out of the collector node.

**Step 2:** Write the input voltage $v_{be} = h_{ie}\, i_b$ (from §3.1).

**Step 3:** By definition:

$$A_v = \frac{v_{out}}{v_{in}} = \frac{v_{ce}}{v_{be}}$$

**Step 4:** Substitute:

$$A_v = \frac{-h_{fe}\, i_b \cdot R_L}{h_{ie}\, i_b} = \frac{-h_{fe}\, R_L}{h_{ie}}$$

**Result:** $\boxed{A_v = \dfrac{-h_{fe}\, R_L}{h_{ie}}}$

> [!NOTE]
> The negative sign indicates a **180° phase reversal** — a fundamental property of the CE configuration. The magnitude of gain can be increased by choosing a transistor with higher $h_{fe}$ or by using a larger $R_L$.

## 3.3 Derivation of Current Gain ($A_i$)

**Step 1:** Load current $i_L = -i_c = -h_{fe}\, i_b$ (since the current through $R_L$ flows opposite to $i_c$).

**Step 2:** Input current to the amplifier base is $i_b$.

**Step 3:**

$$A_i = \frac{i_L}{i_b} = \frac{-h_{fe}\, i_b}{i_b} = -h_{fe}$$

**Result:** $\boxed{A_i = -h_{fe}}$

The magnitude of current gain is essentially the transistor's small-signal forward current gain.

## 3.4 Derivation of Output Resistance ($R_o$)

**Step 1:** To find $R_o$, deactivate the independent input source (set $v_s = 0$, so $i_b = 0$).

**Step 2:** With $i_b = 0$, the dependent current source $h_{fe}\, i_b$ becomes **zero** (open circuit).

**Step 3:** Looking back into the output terminals, we see only the admittance $h_{oe}$.

**Step 4:**

$$R_o = \frac{1}{h_{oe}}$$

**Result (Simplified):** $\boxed{R_o = \dfrac{1}{h_{oe}}}$

## 3.5 Numerical Example (Typical KTU-Style Problem)

> [!TIP]
> **Given:** A CE amplifier uses a BJT with $h_{ie} = 1100\,\Omega$, $h_{fe} = 100$, $h_{re} = 2.5 \times 10^{-4}$, $h_{oe} = 25\,\mu\text{A/V}$, and a load $R_L = 10\,\text{k}\Omega$.
> **Find:** $R_i$, $A_v$, $A_i$, and $R_o$ (using the simplified formulas).

**Solution:**

$$R_i = h_{ie} = 1100\,\Omega = 1.1\,\text{k}\Omega$$

$$A_v = \frac{-h_{fe}\, R_L}{h_{ie}} = \frac{-(100)(10\,000)}{1100} = \frac{-1\,000\,000}{1100} \approx -909.09$$

$$A_i = -h_{fe} = -100$$

$$R_o = \frac{1}{h_{oe}} = \frac{1}{25 \times 10^{-6}} = 40\,000\,\Omega = 40\,\text{k}\Omega$$

**Verification using the exact formula for $A_v$:**

$$A_v = \frac{-h_{fe}\, R_L}{h_{ie} + (h_{re}\, h_{fe}\, R_L)} = \frac{-(100)(10^4)}{1100 + (2.5 \times 10^{-4})(100)(10^4)} = \frac{-10^6}{1100 + 250} = \frac{-10^6}{1350} \approx -740.74$$

This shows that neglecting $h_{re}$ introduces about a **22%** error in this case — a substantial error that the examiner may test.

## 3.6 Analytical Comparison Matrix: h-Model vs T-Model

| Feature | h-Parameter Model | T-Model (BJT) | π-Model (FET) |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Parameters used | $h_{ie}, h_{re}, h_{fe}, h_{oe}$ | $r_e, r_b, \alpha$ | $g_m, r_d$ |
| Best suited for | CE configuration analysis | CB / CC configuration | CS / CD / CG FET stages |
| Frequency range | Low frequency (LF) | Low frequency | Low to mid frequency |
| Complexity | Moderate | Simple | Simple |
| Direct physical meaning | No (hybrid mix of V and I) | Yes (terminal resistances) | Yes (transconductance) |
| KTU exam preference | **Highest** | High | Moderate |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of Small-Signal Analysis

The following Mermaid block diagram illustrates the **functional workflow** that an engineer follows when performing small-signal analysis on a transistor amplifier:

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Original Amplifier Circuit] --> B[Identify Q-point DC Bias]
    B --> C[Coupling Capacitors]
    B --> D[DC Voltage Sources]
    B --> E[DC Current Sources]
    C --> F[Short Circuit at Signal Freq]
    D --> G[Short to Ground]
    E --> H[Open Circuit]
    F --> I[Draw Small Signal AC Circuit]
    G --> I
    H --> I
    I --> J{Choose Model}
    J -->|CE Stage| K[h-parameter Model]
    J -->|CB or CC Stage| L[T-Model]
    J -->|FET Stage| M[pi-Model]
    K --> N[Apply KVL and KCL]
    L --> N
    M --> N
    N --> O[Compute Ri, Ro, Av, Ai]
    O --> P[Compute Input Output Impedance]
    P --> Q[Compute Overall Gain]
```

## 4.2 h-Parameter Equivalent Circuit Topology (Functional Map)

The diagram below shows the **functional interaction map** of the four h-parameters in a CE small-signal equivalent circuit:

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph InputLoop
        IN[Input Port vs, Rs] --> Hie[hie Impedance]
        Hie --> G1[Node B]
        Hre[Dependent V Source hre x vce] --> G1
    end
    subgraph OutputLoop
        G1 --> Hfe[Dependent I Source hfe x ib]
        Hfe --> G2[Node C]
        Hoe[Output Admittance hoe] --- G2
        G2 --> RL[Load RL]
    end
    G2 --> Gnd[Ground Node]
    G1 --> Gnd
```

## 4.3 Three-Stage Signal Flow Architecture

A real-world amplifier is rarely a single stage. The block diagram below shows how small-signal equivalent circuits are used in cascade to design a multi-stage amplifier:

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    Src[Source vs, Rs] --> S1[Stage 1 CE Amp with Av1, Ri1, Ro1]
    S1 --> S2[Stage 2 CE Amp with Av2, Ri2, Ro2]
    S2 --> S3[Stage 3 Emitter Follower Buffer]
    S3 --> Out[Load RL]
    S1 -.->|Coupling Cap C1| S2
    S2 -.->|Coupling Cap C2| S3
```

**Key Design Relationships** (KTU-favorite derivation topic):

$$A_{v,\text{total}} = A_{v1} \cdot A_{v2} \cdot A_{v3}$$

$$R_{i,\text{total}} = R_{i1}$$

$$R_{o,\text{total}} = R_{o3}$$

**Loading Effect:** The actual gain of stage 1 is reduced because $R_{i2}$ acts as a load on stage 1:

$$A_{v1,\text{actual}} = A_{v1,\text{no load}} \cdot \frac{R_{i2}}{R_{i2} + R_{o1}}$$

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Define small-signal operation of a BJT. State the necessary conditions for a transistor to operate in the small-signal region.**

**Model Answer:**

Small-signal operation of a BJT refers to the mode of operation in which the AC input signal causes only a **small variation** in voltages and currents around the quiescent operating (Q-) point, allowing the nonlinear transistor to be modeled by a **linear equivalent circuit** (such as the h-parameter or T-model).

The necessary conditions are:

1. The AC input signal amplitude must be small — typically **less than 10%** of the corresponding DC bias value (e.g., $v_s \ll V_{BE}$, $i_b \ll I_B$).
2. The transistor must remain in the **active region** throughout the entire AC cycle (no cutoff or saturation).
3. The operating temperature must be approximately constant (no thermal runaway).
4. The signal frequency must be within the transistor's specified bandwidth so that internal capacitances can be either neglected (low-frequency model) or accounted for (high-frequency model).

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** Students often forget to mention the **"active region"** condition. If you miss this, you lose 1 mark instantly.

---

### Question 2: `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**List the four h-parameters of a CE-configured BJT and state their physical meanings.**

**Model Answer:**

The four h-parameters of a BJT in CE configuration are:

1. **$h_{ie}$ — Input impedance** (units: $\Omega$): It is the ratio of input voltage to input current with the output AC **short-circuited**, i.e., $h_{ie} = \left.\dfrac{v_{be}}{i_b}\right|_{v_{ce}=0}$. Typical value: $1\,\text{k}\Omega$ to $5\,\text{k}\Omega$.

2. **$h_{re}$ — Reverse voltage ratio** (dimensionless): It is the ratio of feedback voltage to input voltage with the input AC **open-circuited**, i.e., $h_{re} = \left.\dfrac{v_{be}}{v_{ce}}\right|_{i_b=0}$. Typical value: $10^{-3}$ to $10^{-4}$ (very small, often neglected).

3. **$h_{fe}$ — Forward current gain** (dimensionless): It is the ratio of output current to input current with the output AC **short-circuited**, i.e., $h_{fe} = \left.\dfrac{i_c}{i_b}\right|_{v_{ce}=0}$. Typical value: 50 to 300.

4. **$h_{oe}$ — Output admittance** (units: S): It is the ratio of output current to output voltage with the input AC **open-circuited**, i.e., $h_{oe} = \left.\dfrac{i_c}{v_{ce}}\right|_{i_b=0}$. Typical value: $10$ to $100\,\mu\text{S}$.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** Forgetting the **units** of $h_{ie}$ ($\Omega$) and $h_{oe}$ (S) is a common mark-loss. Also, the **short-circuit vs open-circuit** conditions for each parameter are frequently confused.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question A (Choice 1) — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]`

**(a)** With the help of a clear circuit diagram, derive the **generalized h-parameter equivalent circuit of a BJT in CE configuration**. Define all four h-parameters and state their typical ranges. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** For a CE amplifier using a BJT with $h_{ie} = 2\,\text{k}\Omega$, $h_{fe} = 120$, $h_{re} = 2 \times 10^{-4}$, $h_{oe} = 20\,\mu\text{A/V}$, and load $R_L = 5\,\text{k}\Omega$ (source resistance $R_S = 1\,\text{k}\Omega$):
- (i) Calculate the **exact voltage gain** $A_v$.
- (ii) Calculate the **simplified voltage gain** $A_v$ (neglecting $h_{re}$ and $h_{oe}$).
- (iii) Comment on the **percentage error** introduced by the simplification. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution to Question A:

**Part (a) — 7 Marks:**

The two-port h-parameter representation of any linear two-port network is given by:

$$v_1 = h_{11}\, i_1 + h_{12}\, v_2$$

$$i_2 = h_{21}\, i_1 + h_{22}\, v_2$$

For a BJT in CE configuration, identifying $v_1 = v_{be}$, $i_1 = i_b$, $v_2 = v_{ce}$, $i_2 = i_c$:

$$v_{be} = h_{ie}\, i_b + h_{re}\, v_{ce} \quad \text{...(i)}$$

$$i_c = h_{fe}\, i_b + h_{oe}\, v_{ce} \quad \text{...(ii)}$$

The equivalent circuit contains:
- Input port: $h_{ie}$ in series with a **voltage-controlled voltage source** $h_{re}\, v_{ce}$.
- Output port: a **current-controlled current source** $h_{fe}\, i_b$ in parallel with output admittance $h_{oe}$.

Definitions and typical ranges:

| Parameter | Definition | Typical Range |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| $h_{ie}$ | $\left.\dfrac{v_{be}}{i_b}\right\vert_{v_{ce}=0}$ | $1\,\text{k}\Omega$ – $5\,\text{k}\Omega$ |
| $h_{re}$ | $\left.\dfrac{v_{be}}{v_{ce}}\right\vert_{i_b=0}$ | $10^{-4}$ – $10^{-3}$ |
| $h_{fe}$ | $\left.\dfrac{i_c}{i_b}\right\vert_{v_{ce}=0}$ | $50$ – $300$ |
| $h_{oe}$ | $\left.\dfrac{i_c}{v_{ce}}\right\vert_{i_b=0}$ | $10\,\mu\text{S}$ – $100\,\mu\text{S}$ |

**Part (b) — 7 Marks:**

**(i) Exact voltage gain:**

$$A_v = \frac{-h_{fe}\, R_L}{h_{ie} + (h_{re}\, h_{fe}\, R_L)}$$

Substituting the values:

$$h_{re}\, h_{fe}\, R_L = (2 \times 10^{-4})(120)(5000) = 120$$

$$A_v = \frac{-(120)(5000)}{2000 + 120} = \frac{-600\,000}{2120} \approx -283.02$$

**(ii) Simplified voltage gain:**

$$A_v \approx \frac{-h_{fe}\, R_L}{h_{ie}} = \frac{-(120)(5000)}{2000} = \frac{-600\,000}{2000} = -300$$

**(iii) Percentage error:**

$$\% \text{ Error} = \left| \frac{A_{v,\text{simplified}} - A_{v,\text{exact}}}{A_{v,\text{exact}}} \right| \times 100\%$$

$$= \left| \frac{-300 - (-283.02)}{-283.02} \right| \times 100\% = \left| \frac{-16.98}{283.02} \right| \times 100\% \approx 6\%$$

**Comment:** The simplified formula overestimates the gain by approximately **6%**, which is generally acceptable for first-order hand calculations in design.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall for Part (b):** Many students incorrectly compute $h_{re}\, h_{fe}\, R_L$ as $h_{re} \times R_L$ or $h_{re} \times h_{fe}$ alone. The correct product is **all three quantities multiplied**. Also, do not forget the **negative sign** in the voltage gain formula.

---

### Question B (Choice 2) — `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`

**(a)** Explain the **T-model of a BJT** with a neat diagram. Derive the relationship between the T-model parameters ($r_e, r_b, \alpha$) and the h-parameters. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** For a CE amplifier with $R_S = 600\,\Omega$, $R_L = 4\,\text{k}\Omega$, $h_{ie} = 1.2\,\text{k}\Omega$, $h_{fe} = 150$, $h_{re} = 2.5 \times 10^{-4}$, and $h_{oe} = 20\,\mu\text{A/V}$:
- (i) Compute the **overall voltage gain from source to load** $A_{vs} = \dfrac{v_{out}}{v_s}$.
- (ii) Compute the **current gain** $A_i$ and the **power gain** $A_p$ in decibels (dB). **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution to Question B:

**Part (a) — 7 Marks:**

The T-model of a BJT represents the intrinsic transistor as a T-network with three elements: **base resistance $r_b$**, **emitter resistance $r_e$**, and a **dependent current source $\alpha\, i_e$** between collector and emitter.

The key relationships are:

$$r_e = \frac{V_T}{I_E} = \frac{26 \text{ mV}}{I_E}$$

where $V_T = \dfrac{kT}{q}$ is the thermal voltage ($\approx 26$ mV at 300 K).

Conversion to h-parameters:

$$h_{ie} = r_b + (\beta + 1)\, r_e \approx r_b + \beta\, r_e$$

$$h_{fe} = \beta = \frac{\alpha}{1 - \alpha}$$

$$h_{re} = \frac{r_e}{r_b + r_e} \approx \frac{r_e}{r_b} \quad \text{(very small)}$$

$$h_{oe} = \frac{1}{r_c} \quad \text{where } r_c \text{ is the collector-body resistance}$$

**Part (b) — 7 Marks:**

**(i) Source-to-load voltage gain:**

The total voltage gain from the source $v_s$ to the load is:

$$A_{vs} = A_v \cdot \frac{R_i}{R_i + R_S}$$

First, compute the simplified $A_v$ (using the simplified h-model):

$$A_v = \frac{-h_{fe}\, R_L}{h_{ie}} = \frac{-(150)(4000)}{1200} = \frac{-600\,000}{1200} = -500$$

Next, $R_i = h_{ie} = 1200\,\Omega$.

$$\frac{R_i}{R_i + R_S} = \frac{1200}{1200 + 600} = \frac{1200}{1800} = \frac{2}{3} = 0.6667$$

Therefore:

$$A_{vs} = (-500) \cdot (0.6667) = -333.33$$

**(ii) Current gain and power gain:**

$$A_i = -h_{fe} = -150$$

$$A_p = A_v \cdot A_i = (-500)(-150) = 75\,000$$

In decibels:

$$A_p \text{ (dB)} = 10 \log_{10}(75\,000) = 10 \times 4.875 = 48.75 \text{ dB}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall for Question B:**
> 1. Confusing **$A_v$ (input-to-output)** with **$A_{vs}$ (source-to-load)**. The latter accounts for the voltage divider at the input formed by $R_S$ and $R_i$.
> 2. For power gain in dB, use the formula $A_p \text{ (dB)} = 10 \log_{10}(A_p)$ — **not** $20 \log_{10}$, because power is proportional to the square of voltage/current.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist**

- [x] **Small-signal** = AC signal amplitude ≤ 10% of DC bias.
- [x] Equivalent circuit drawn by **shorting coupling caps** and **DC sources**, **opening DC current sources**.
- [x] **h-parameter model** is the standard for **CE configuration** at low frequencies.
- [x] The four h-parameters: $h_{ie}$ (input Z, $\Omega$), $h_{re}$ (reverse V ratio), $h_{fe}$ (forward I gain), $h_{oe}$ (output Y, S).
- [x] **$h_{re}$ and $h_{oe}$ are usually negligible** in simplified analysis.
- [x] Voltage gain (CE) is **negative** (180° phase inversion): $A_v = -h_{fe} R_L / h_{ie}$.
- [x] Input resistance (simplified): $R_i = h_{ie}$.
- [x] Output resistance (simplified): $R_o = 1/h_{oe}$.
- [x] Current gain: $A_i = -h_{fe}$.
- [x] Emitter resistance: $r_e = 26\,\text{mV} / I_E$ — remember the **26 mV** thermal voltage.
- [x] Conversion: $h_{ie} \approx \beta\, r_e$ (when $r_b$ is neglected).
- [x] **T-model** is preferred for **CB and CC** configurations; **h-model** for CE.
- [x] For multi-stage amplifiers, $A_{v,\text{total}}$ is the product of individual stage gains (with **loading effect** accounted for).
- [x] Power gain in dB: $A_p \text{ (dB)} = 10 \log_{10}(A_p)$.
- [x] Always **label AC quantities with lowercase letters** ($v_{be}, i_b, v_{ce}, i_c$) to distinguish from DC ($V_{BE}, I_B, V_{CE}, I_C$).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Hybrid Pi Model, CS Configuration
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCICT302 - ELECTRONIC CIRCUITS AND NETWORKS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Hybrid Pi Model, CS Configuration

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **Hybrid-Pi (h-parameter) model** is a small-signal, high-frequency equivalent circuit used to analyze the behavior of a **Field Effect Transistor (FET)** in the **Common Source (CS) configuration**. It represents the FET as a two-port network comprising dependent and independent current sources, along with inter-electrode capacitances that become significant at high frequencies. The model is widely used because it accurately predicts frequency response, input/output impedances, and voltage gain of CS amplifiers operating in the saturation region.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** The Hybrid-Pi model is a foundational tool for analyzing the **high-frequency response** of CS amplifiers, specifically for deriving the **Miller Effect**, **cutoff frequencies**, and the **short-circuit current gain bandwidth product**.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a water tap (the FET) controlling the flow of water (current) from a tank (drain) to a sink (source). The amount of water flowing depends on how much you twist the knob (input voltage at the gate). Now, suppose the water pipe has a slight elasticity — when pressure changes rapidly (high frequency), the pipe stretches a bit before settling. This "stretching" represents the **parasitic capacitances** ($C_{gs}$ and $C_{gd}$) in the Hybrid-Pi model. The model captures both the steady-flow behavior (low frequency) and the dynamic, time-delayed response (high frequency) of the tap.

- The **gate** is the control terminal (input)
- The **source** is common to both input and output
- The **drain** is the output terminal
- The **transconductance ($g_m$)** quantifies the control efficiency

## 1.3 Key Physical Parameters and Standard Metrics

| Parameter | Symbol | Typical Value (JFET) | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| Transconductance | $g_m$ | $1 \times 10^{-3}$ to $5 \times 10^{-3}$ | Siemens (S) or A/V |
| Drain Resistance | $r_d$ | $100$ to $500$ | kΩ |
| Gate-Source Capacitance | $C_{gs}$ | $1$ to $10$ | pF |
| Gate-Drain Capacitance | $C_{gd}$ | $0.1$ to $5$ | pF |

> [!NOTE]
> The constants $\mathbf{g_m}$, $\mathbf{r_d}$, $\mathbf{C_{gs}}$, and $\mathbf{C_{gd}}$ are all **bias-dependent**. As the quiescent drain current $I_{DQ}$ increases, $g_m$ increases linearly while $C_{gs}$ and $C_{gd}$ remain approximately constant (set by device geometry and oxide thickness).

## 1.4 Why Common Source (CS) Configuration?

The CS configuration is the most widely used FET amplifier topology because it provides:
- **High voltage gain** (inverting amplifier)
- **Moderate to high input impedance**
- **180° phase inversion** between input and output
- **Suitability for cascading** multiple stages

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Hybrid-Pi Equivalent Circuit of a CS Amplifier

The Hybrid-Pi model replaces the physical FET with an equivalent circuit comprising:
- A **voltage-controlled current source** ($g_m \cdot V_{gs}$) between drain and source
- A **resistance $r_d$** (drain resistance) in parallel with the current source
- A **capacitance $C_{gs}$** between gate and source
- A **capacitance $C_{gd}$** between gate and drain

### 2.1.1 Circuit Elements Explained

1. **$g_m \cdot V_{gs}$ (Dependent Current Source):** This represents the amplifying action. The drain current variation $\Delta I_d$ is proportional to the gate-source voltage variation $\Delta V_{gs}$, with $g_m$ as the proportionality constant.
   $$i_d = g_m \cdot v_{gs}$$

2. **$r_d$ (Output Resistance):** Represents the finite slope of the $I_D$ vs $V_{DS}$ curve in the saturation (active) region. It models the **channel-length modulation** effect.

3. **$C_{gs}$ (Gate-Source Capacitance):** Arises from the **depletion region** between the gate and the channel. It is the dominant capacitance at low-to-mid frequencies.

4. **$C_{gd}$ (Gate-Drain Capacitance):** Arises from the depletion region between the gate and the drain. Although physically smaller than $C_{gs}$, it is amplified by the **Miller Effect** at high frequencies.

## 2.2 Step-by-Step Working Principle

**Step 1 — Input Signal Application:**
A small AC signal $v_{gs}$ is applied between the gate and source. The gate draws negligible current due to the high input impedance of the FET.

**Step 2 — Current Source Activation:**
The dependent current source $g_m \cdot v_{gs}$ produces a current in the drain circuit that is a linear function of the input voltage.

**Step 3 — Output Voltage Generation:**
This drain current flows through the load resistance $R_D$ (and $r_d$ in parallel), producing an amplified, inverted output voltage $v_{ds}$.

**Step 4 — Frequency-Dependent Behavior:**
At low frequencies, the capacitors $C_{gs}$ and $C_{gd}$ behave as **open circuits**, and the model reduces to the low-frequency equivalent. At high frequencies, they introduce phase shift and gain roll-off.

**Step 5 — Miller Multiplication:**
The capacitor $C_{gd}$ appears effectively multiplied by $(1 + g_m R_D)$ at the input due to the **Miller Theorem**, drastically reducing the high-frequency input impedance.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $g_m = \dfrac{\partial I_D}{\partial V_{GS}} \bigg\vert_{V_{DS}}$ | Definition of transconductance | S (A/V) |
| 2 | $g_m = \dfrac{2 I_{DSS}}{\vert V_P \vert} \cdot \left(1 - \dfrac{V_{GS}}{V_P}\right)$ | $g_m$ in terms of JFET parameters | S |
| 3 | $g_{m0} = \dfrac{2 I_{DSS}}{\vert V_P \vert}$ | $g_m$ at $V_{GS} = 0$ (maximum) | S |
| 4 | $r_d = \dfrac{\partial V_{DS}}{\partial I_D} \bigg\vert_{V_{GS}}$ | Output/drain resistance of FET | Ω |
| 5 | $C_{in(Miller)} = C_{gs} + C_{gd}(1 + g_m R_D)$ | Miller-augmented input capacitance | F |
| 6 | $f_H = \dfrac{1}{2 \pi R_{sig} C_{in(Miller)}}$ | Upper cutoff frequency (CS amp) | Hz |
| 7 | $A_v = -g_m (r_d \parallel R_D)$ | Midband voltage gain | dimensionless |
| 8 | $Z_{in} = \dfrac{1}{j \omega C_{gs}} \parallel \dfrac{1}{j \omega C_{gd}(1+A_v)}$ | High-frequency input impedance | Ω |
| 9 | $Z_{out} = r_d \parallel R_D$ | Output impedance (CS) | Ω |
| 10 | $f_T = \dfrac{g_m}{2 \pi (C_{gs} + C_{gd})}$ | Unity-gain (transition) frequency | Hz |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical KTU Tip:** When solving problems, **never confuse $r_d$ (intrinsic drain resistance of the device) with $R_D$ (external drain load resistor)**. The midband gain uses $r_d \parallel R_D$, while Miller multiplication uses only $R_D$ in the gain term.

## 2.4 Real-World Utility in Engineering

The Hybrid-Pi CS model is essential in:
- **RF and Microwave Amplifier Design:** Predicting gain bandwidth and stability.
- **Oscillator Design:** Determining the Barkhausen criterion using loop gain.
- **High-Speed Op-Amps:** Internal FET stages use this model.
- **Communication Receivers:** Low-noise amplifiers (LNAs) in the front-end.
- **Integrated Circuit (IC) Design:** SPICE simulations use Hybrid-Pi as the default FET model.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of Transconductance $g_m$ for a JFET

Starting from Shockley's equation for a JFET in saturation:

$$I_D = I_{DSS} \left(1 - \frac{V_{GS}}{V_P}\right)^2$$

**Step 1 — Differentiate $I_D$ with respect to $V_{GS}$ at constant $V_{DS}$:**

$$g_m = \frac{\partial I_D}{\partial V_{GS}} \bigg\vert_{V_{DS}}$$

**Step 2 — Apply the chain rule to Shockley's equation:**

$$g_m = I_{DSS} \cdot 2 \left(1 - \frac{V_{GS}}{V_P}\right) \cdot \left(-\frac{1}{V_P}\right)$$

**Step 3 — Simplify the expression:**

$$g_m = -\frac{2 I_{DSS}}{V_P} \left(1 - \frac{V_{GS}}{V_P}\right)$$

**Step 4 — Recognize that $V_P$ is negative for an n-channel JFET, so $g_m$ is positive:**

$$g_m = \frac{2 I_{DSS}}{\vert V_P \vert} \left(1 - \frac{V_{GS}}{V_P}\right)$$

**Step 5 — Define $g_{m0}$ as the transconductance at $V_{GS} = 0$:**

$$g_{m0} = \frac{2 I_{DSS}}{\vert V_P \vert}$$

**Step 6 — Write the compact form:**

$$g_m = g_{m0} \left(1 - \frac{V_{GS}}{V_P}\right)$$

## 3.2 Derivation of Midband Voltage Gain

Consider the small-signal Hybrid-Pi equivalent of the CS amplifier with load $R_D$.

**Step 1 — Output current through $R_D$ (ignoring $r_d$ for simplicity):**

$$i_d = g_m \cdot v_{gs}$$

**Step 2 — Output voltage across $R_D$:**

$$v_{out} = -i_d \cdot R_D = -g_m \cdot v_{gs} \cdot R_D$$

> The negative sign indicates the **180° phase inversion** characteristic of CS configuration.

**Step 3 — Voltage gain is the ratio of output to input voltage:**

$$A_v = \frac{v_{out}}{v_{in}} = \frac{v_{out}}{v_{gs}}$$

**Step 4 — Substitute $v_{out}$:**

$$A_v = -g_m \cdot R_D$$

**Step 5 — Include the intrinsic drain resistance $r_d$ in parallel with $R_D$:**

$$A_v = -g_m (r_d \parallel R_D)$$

**Step 6 — Express the parallel combination:**

$$A_v = -g_m \cdot \frac{r_d \cdot R_D}{r_d + R_D}$$

## 3.3 Derivation of the Miller Effect on $C_{gd}$

The **Miller Theorem** states that an impedance $Z$ connected between input and output of an inverting amplifier with gain $A_v$ can be split into two parts: one at the input ($Z_1 = Z/(1-A_v)$) and one at the output ($Z_2 = Z \cdot A_v/(A_v-1)$).

**Step 1 — Apply Miller's Theorem to $C_{gd}$ between gate and drain:**

The voltage across $C_{gd}$ is $V_{gd} = V_{gs} - V_{ds}$. For a CS amplifier, $V_{ds} = A_v \cdot V_{gs}$, so:

$$V_{gd} = V_{gs} - A_v V_{gs} = V_{gs}(1 - A_v)$$

**Step 2 — Current through $C_{gd}$:**

$$I_{C_{gd}} = \frac{V_{gd}}{1/(j\omega C_{gd})} = j \omega C_{gd} V_{gs}(1 - A_v)$$

**Step 3 — Equivalent input capacitance:**

$$C_{gd,in} = \frac{I_{C_{gd}}}{j \omega V_{gs}} = C_{gd}(1 - A_v)$$

**Step 4 — Since $A_v$ is negative for CS, $(1 - A_v) = (1 + \vert A_v \vert)$:**

$$C_{gd,in} = C_{gd}(1 + \vert A_v \vert) = C_{gd}(1 + g_m R_D)$$

**Step 5 — Total input capacitance (Miller-augmented):**

$$C_{in} = C_{gs} + C_{gd}(1 + g_m R_D)$$

## 3.4 Derivation of Upper Cutoff Frequency $f_H$

**Step 1 — The input circuit at high frequencies has an equivalent RC network with:**
- Resistance: $R_{sig}$ (source resistance)
- Capacitance: $C_{in}$ (Miller-augmented)

**Step 2 — The transfer function in the frequency domain:**

$$\frac{V_{gs}}{V_{sig}} = \frac{1}{1 + j \omega R_{sig} C_{in}}$$

**Step 3 — The cutoff frequency occurs when $\omega R_{sig} C_{in} = 1$:**

$$\omega_H = \frac{1}{R_{sig} C_{in}}$$

**Step 4 — Convert to Hertz:**

$$f_H = \frac{1}{2 \pi R_{sig} C_{in}}$$

**Step 5 — Substitute $C_{in}$:**

$$f_H = \frac{1}{2 \pi R_{sig} [C_{gs} + C_{gd}(1 + g_m R_D)]}$$

## 3.5 Derivation of Short-Circuit Current Gain and Unity-Gain Frequency

**Step 1 — Short-circuit drain current (output shorted):**

$$i_d = g_m \cdot v_{gs} \quad \text{(at output short)}$$

**Step 2 — Input current through the gate-source capacitance path:**

$$i_{in} = j \omega (C_{gs} + C_{gd}) v_{gs}$$

**Step 3 — Short-circuit current gain:**

$$A_{is} = \frac{i_d}{i_{in}} = \frac{g_m}{j \omega (C_{gs} + C_{gd})}$$

**Step 4 — Magnitude of the current gain:**

$$\vert A_{is} \vert = \frac{g_m}{\omega (C_{gs} + C_{gd})}$$

**Step 5 — The unity-gain (transition) frequency $f_T$ occurs when $\vert A_{is} \vert = 1$:**

$$1 = \frac{g_m}{2 \pi f_T (C_{gs} + C_{gd})}$$

**Step 6 — Solve for $f_T$:**

$$f_T = \frac{g_m}{2 \pi (C_{gs} + C_{gd})}$$

**Step 7 — Express the cutoff frequency in terms of $f_T$ and Miller capacitance:**

$$f_H = \frac{f_T}{g_m R_D}$$

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]

**Q: Define the Hybrid-Pi model of a FET in CS configuration. List the four key parameters of the model.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The Hybrid-Pi model is a **small-signal equivalent circuit** used to represent a FET in the Common Source configuration for high-frequency analysis. It models the device using a voltage-controlled current source and inter-electrode capacitances.

The four key parameters are:
1. **$g_m$ (Transconductance):** The gain parameter relating output current to input voltage, $i_d = g_m v_{gs}$. Unit: Siemens (A/V).
2. **$r_d$ (Drain Resistance):** The output resistance of the device in the saturation region. Unit: Ohms.
3. **$C_{gs}$ (Gate-Source Capacitance):** The capacitance between gate and source terminals. Unit: Farads.
4. **$C_{gd}$ (Gate-Drain Capacitance):** The capacitance between gate and drain terminals, which is responsible for the Miller effect. Unit: Farads.

---

### Question 2: [KTU University Exam - June 2024]

**Q: What is the Miller effect? How does it affect the input capacitance of a CS amplifier?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **Miller Effect** (or Miller Theorem) states that an impedance connected between the input and output of an inverting amplifier appears multiplied by a factor of $(1 - A_v)$ when viewed from the input side, where $A_v$ is the voltage gain.

For a CS amplifier, the feedback capacitance $C_{gd}$ between gate and drain is effectively multiplied by $(1 + g_m R_D)$ at the input.

The total input capacitance becomes:

$$C_{in} = C_{gs} + C_{gd}(1 + g_m R_D)$$

This drastically increases the input capacitance at high frequencies, lowering the **upper cutoff frequency $f_H$** and limiting the bandwidth of the amplifier. The Miller effect is the primary reason FET amplifiers have reduced high-frequency response compared to ideal midband predictions.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — KTU Internal Choice Pattern)

### Question A (14 Marks): [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]

**For a CS amplifier using a JFET with parameters $I_{DSS} = 8$ mA, $V_P = -4$ V, biased at $V_{GS} = -1$ V, with $R_D = 2.2$ kΩ, $R_{sig} = 1$ kΩ, $C_{gs} = 5$ pF, $C_{gd} = 2$ pF, and $r_d = 50$ kΩ, calculate: (a) The transconductance $g_m$ and the midband voltage gain. (b) The Miller-augmented input capacitance and the upper cutoff frequency $f_H$.**

### Part (a) — Model Solution (7 marks)

**Step 1 — Calculate $g_m$ using the JFET transconductance formula:**

$$g_m = \frac{2 I_{DSS}}{\vert V_P \vert} \left(1 - \frac{V_{GS}}{V_P}\right)$$

$$g_m = \frac{2 \times 8 \times 10^{-3}}{4} \left(1 - \frac{-1}{-4}\right)$$

$$g_m = 4 \times 10^{-3} \times \left(1 - 0.25\right)$$

$$g_m = 4 \times 10^{-3} \times 0.75$$

$$g_m = 3 \times 10^{-3} \text{ S} = 3 \text{ mS}$$

**Step 2 — Calculate the effective load resistance ($r_d \parallel R_D$):**

$$r_d \parallel R_D = \frac{50 \times 10^3 \times 2.2 \times 10^3}{50 \times 10^3 + 2.2 \times 10^3}$$

$$r_d \parallel R_D = \frac{110 \times 10^6}{52.2 \times 10^3} = 2107.28 \text{ Ω} \approx 2.107 \text{ kΩ}$$

**Step 3 — Calculate midband voltage gain:**

$$A_v = -g_m (r_d \parallel R_D)$$

$$A_v = -3 \times 10^{-3} \times 2107.28$$

$$A_v = -6.32$$

**Result:** $g_m = 3$ mS, $A_v = -6.32$

### Part (b) — Model Solution (7 marks)

**Step 1 — Calculate the Miller multiplier for $C_{gd}$:**

$$\text{Miller factor} = 1 + g_m R_D = 1 + 3 \times 10^{-3} \times 2.2 \times 10^3$$

$$= 1 + 6.6 = 7.6$$

> Note: We use $R_D$ alone (not $r_d \parallel R_D$) in the Miller factor for the cutoff frequency calculation, as per standard KTU textbook conventions.

**Step 2 — Calculate the Miller-augmented input capacitance:**

$$C_{in} = C_{gs} + C_{gd} (1 + g_m R_D)$$

$$C_{in} = 5 \text{ pF} + 2 \text{ pF} \times 7.6$$

$$C_{in} = 5 \text{ pF} + 15.2 \text{ pF}$$

$$C_{in} = 20.2 \text{ pF}$$

**Step 3 — Calculate the upper cutoff frequency:**

$$f_H = \frac{1}{2 \pi R_{sig} C_{in}}$$

$$f_H = \frac{1}{2 \pi \times 1 \times 10^3 \times 20.2 \times 10^{-12}}$$

$$f_H = \frac{1}{2 \pi \times 20.2 \times 10^{-9}}$$

$$f_H = \frac{1}{1.269 \times 10^{-7}}$$

$$f_H = 7.88 \text{ MHz}$$

**Result:** $C_{in} = 20.2$ pF, $f_H = 7.88$ MHz

---

### Question B (14 Marks): [KTU University Exam - June 2024]

**Derive the expression for: (a) The Miller-augmented input capacitance of a CS amplifier, starting from the small-signal Hybrid-Pi equivalent circuit. (b) The unity-gain bandwidth $f_T$ in terms of $g_m$, $C_{gs}$, and $C_{gd}$, and show that $f_H = f_T / (g_m R_D)$.**

### Part (a) — Model Solution (7 marks)

**Step 1 — Start with the small-signal Hybrid-Pi equivalent circuit of the CS amplifier.**

The circuit has:
- Input $v_{gs}$ applied between gate and source.
- Current source $g_m v_{gs}$ between drain and source.
- $C_{gs}$ between gate and source.
- $C_{gd}$ between gate and drain.
- Load $R_D$ at the drain.

**Step 2 — Express the voltage across $C_{gd}$:**

The voltage at the drain is $v_{ds} = -g_m v_{gs} R_D = A_v v_{gs}$, where $A_v = -g_m R_D$.

The voltage across $C_{gd}$ is:

$$v_{C_{gd}} = v_{gs} - v_{ds} = v_{gs} - A_v v_{gs} = v_{gs}(1 - A_v)$$

**Step 3 — Express the current through $C_{gd}$:**

$$i_{C_{gd}} = C_{gd} \frac{d v_{C_{gd}}}{dt} = j \omega C_{gd} v_{gs}(1 - A_v)$$

**Step 4 — Calculate the input current contribution from $C_{gd}$:**

The current entering the gate from $C_{gd}$ equals $i_{C_{gd}}$. This current is equivalent to a capacitance $C_{gd,in}$ at the input:

$$C_{gd,in} = \frac{i_{C_{gd}}}{j \omega v_{gs}} = C_{gd}(1 - A_v)$$

**Step 5 — Substitute $A_v = -g_m R_D$ (negative for CS):**

$$C_{gd,in} = C_{gd}(1 - (-g_m R_D)) = C_{gd}(1 + g_m R_D)$$

**Step 6 — Total input capacitance (Miller-augmented):**

$$\boxed{C_{in} = C_{gs} + C_{gd}(1 + g_m R_D)}$$

### Part (b) — Model Solution (7 marks)

**Step 1 — Start with the short-circuit current gain:**

For a short-circuit load, $A_v \to \infty$, and the dominant input capacitance is $(C_{gs} + C_{gd})$ since the Miller multiplication becomes a short across $C_{gd}$.

$$A_{is} = \frac{i_d}{i_{in}} = \frac{g_m v_{gs}}{j \omega (C_{gs} + C_{gd}) v_{gs}} = \frac{g_m}{j \omega (C_{gs} + C_{gd})}$$

**Step 2 — Magnitude of current gain:**

$$\vert A_{is} \vert = \frac{g_m}{\omega (C_{gs} + C_{gd})}$$

**Step 3 — Define unity-gain frequency $f_T$ as the frequency where $\vert A_{is} \vert = 1$:**

$$1 = \frac{g_m}{2 \pi f_T (C_{gs} + C_{gd})}$$

$$f_T = \frac{g_m}{2 \pi (C_{gs} + C_{gd})}$$

**Step 4 — Express $f_H$ in terms of $f_T$:**

Recall that $f_H = \frac{1}{2 \pi R_{sig} C_{in}}$ and assuming $R_{sig} = R_D$ for a normalized analysis:

$$f_H = \frac{1}{2 \pi R_D [C_{gs} + C_{gd}(1 + g_m R_D)]}$$

**Step 5 — Approximate $C_{gs} + C_{gd}(1 + g_m R_D) \approx g_m R_D (C_{gs} + C_{gd})$ for large gain:**

$$f_H \approx \frac{1}{2 \pi R_D \cdot g_m R_D (C_{gs} + C_{gd})}$$

$$f_H \approx \frac{1}{2 \pi g_m R_D^2 (C_{gs} + C_{gd})}$$

**Step 6 — Divide $f_T$ by $g_m R_D$:**

$$\frac{f_T}{g_m R_D} = \frac{g_m}{2 \pi (C_{gs} + C_{gd}) \cdot g_m R_D} = \frac{1}{2 \pi R_D (C_{gs} + C_{gd})}$$

**Step 7 — Note that the Miller-augmented capacitance dominates:**

$$f_H \approx \frac{f_T}{g_m R_D} \quad \text{(for large } g_m R_D \text{)}$$

This is the **Gain-Bandwidth Product (GBW) relation** for CS amplifiers: $A_v \cdot f_H \approx f_T$.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Exams:**
>
> 1. **Confusing $r_d$ and $R_D$:** Students frequently use $R_D$ alone in the midband gain formula, forgetting that the intrinsic drain resistance $r_d$ appears in parallel. This gives an **overestimated gain** and loses 1–2 marks.
>
> 2. **Forgetting the Miller Multiplication:** When asked for "input capacitance," students often write just $C_{gs}$ or $C_{gs} + C_{gd}$, ignoring the $(1 + g_m R_D)$ factor. The Miller effect is the **central concept** of the Hybrid-Pi model and omitting it is a guaranteed 2-mark penalty.
>
> 3. **Sign Error in $V_P$:** JFET pinch-off voltage $V_P$ is negative for n-channel devices. Plugging in $-V_P$ incorrectly leads to a negative $g_m$, which is dimensionally and physically nonsensical.
>
> 4. **Unit Inconsistency:** Capacitances in pF and resistances in kΩ require careful conversion to base units (F and Ω) before computing $f_H$ in Hz. A common error is using $R_{sig}$ in kΩ while $C_{in}$ is in pF, yielding a wrong $f_H$ by a factor of $10^9$.
>
> 5. **Phase Sign in Miller Theorem:** The Miller factor is $(1 - A_v)$, not $(1 + A_v)$. For CS, $A_v$ is negative, so $(1 - A_v) = (1 + \vert A_v \vert)$. Writing $(1 + A_v)$ gives the wrong answer.
>
> 6. **Skipping Intermediate Steps:** KTU examiners award step marks. Always show: formula → substitution → numerical evaluation → final answer with units.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- The **Hybrid-Pi model** is a small-signal, high-frequency equivalent circuit for FETs in CS configuration.
- It contains four parameters: **$g_m$, $r_d$, $C_{gs}$, $C_{gd}$**.
- The **transconductance** $g_m$ is bias-dependent: $g_m = g_{m0}(1 - V_{GS}/V_P)$.
- The **midband voltage gain** of a CS amplifier is $A_v = -g_m (r_d \parallel R_D)$.
- The **Miller Effect** multiplies $C_{gd}$ by $(1 + g_m R_D)$ at the input.
- The **Miller-augmented input capacitance** is $C_{in} = C_{gs} + C_{gd}(1 + g_m R_D)$.
- The **upper cutoff frequency** is $f_H = \dfrac{1}{2 \pi R_{sig} C_{in}}$.
- The **unity-gain bandwidth** is $f_T = \dfrac{g_m}{2 \pi (C_{gs} + C_{gd})}$.
- The **gain-bandwidth product** is approximately constant: $A_v \cdot f_H \approx f_T$.
- The CS amplifier provides **180° phase inversion** between input and output.
- $C_{gs}$ is the dominant low-to-mid frequency capacitance; $C_{gd}$ dominates the high-frequency response via the Miller effect.
- Always convert all quantities to **SI base units** (Farads, Ohms, Hertz) before computation.
- **Draw the equivalent circuit** at the start of every numerical problem for full marks.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: CD Configurations
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCICT302 - ELECTRONIC CIRCUITS AND NETWORKS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** CD Configurations

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

The **Common Drain (CD) Configuration**, also universally known as the **Source Follower**, is a fundamental single-stage **MOSFET amplifier topology** in which the **Drain terminal is tied to a fixed DC supply (V_DD)** and is therefore at **AC ground potential**. The **input signal (V_in)** is applied to the **Gate**, and the **output signal (V_out)** is taken from the **Source**. The output voltage "follows" the input voltage with a phase shift of **0°** (in-phase) and a magnitude **slightly less than unity**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** In the CD configuration, the **drain is common to both the input and output AC loops** — hence the name "Common Drain." This is the single most tested conceptual point in Module 1 university examinations.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy: The Volume Knob on a Speaker**
> Imagine a high-impedance audio source (like a guitar pickup) trying to drive a low-impedance speaker directly. The sound becomes distorted and weak because the source cannot deliver enough current. The **Source Follower** acts like a **buffer amplifier** — it takes a high-impedance input signal and produces an identical, low-impedance replica that can drive heavy loads without distortion. The "volume" (voltage) remains almost the same, but the "stamina" (current capability) dramatically increases.

## 1.3 Why CD is Called "Source Follower"

The Source terminal voltage changes in the **exact same direction** as the Gate voltage. If V_GS increases by +1 V, then V_S also rises by approximately +0.9 V (less than 1 due to body effect), keeping V_GS almost constant. Hence, the source **"follows"** the gate.

## 1.4 Standard Physical Constants & Metrics

- **Thermal Voltage (V_T):** **25.87 mV** at 300 K (room temperature)
- **Transconductance (g_m):** Measured in **Amperes per Volt (A/V)** or **Siemens (S)**
- **Process Transconductance (k_n' = μ_n C_ox):** Process parameter in **μA/V²**
- **Threshold Voltage (V_tn):** Typically **0.5 V to 1.5 V** for standard NMOS

> [!TIP]
> For all KTU numericals, use **V_T = 25 mV** (unless otherwise specified) to maintain consistency with model solutions.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Circuit Topology — The "Why" Behind Each Element

| Component | Role in CD Configuration | Engineering Rationale |
|-----------|--------------------------|----------------------|
| **MOSFET (M1)** | Active amplifying device | Provides transconductance (g_m) to convert V_GS variation into I_D variation |
| **Drain Resistor (R_D)** | **Absent in pure CD** — drain goes directly to V_DD | Eliminates Miller effect; drain is AC grounded through V_DD |
| **Source Resistor (R_S)** | Biasing + Load element | Sets DC bias point; doubles as the output load |
| **Gate Bias Resistor (R_G)** | High-impedance DC path to ground | Establishes V_G = 0 (or reference); provides infinite DC input impedance |
| **Coupling Capacitor (C_in, C_out)** | AC coupling | Blocks DC, passes AC signal |
| **Bypass Capacitor (C_S)** | **Not used here** — R_S is part of the signal path | Unlike CS, here R_S cannot be bypassed without losing the output |

## 2.2 DC Analysis (Setting the Q-Point)

The DC equivalent circuit is obtained by **opening all capacitors**. The gate is pulled to ground through R_G (no gate current flows in a MOSFET), so:

$$V_G = 0 \text{ V}$$

Assuming the source is grounded through R_S, the source voltage is:

$$V_S = I_D \cdot R_S$$

Therefore, the gate-to-source voltage is:

$$V_{GS} = V_G - V_S = -I_D \cdot R_S$$

Applying the MOSFET saturation equation:

$$I_D = \frac{1}{2} k_n' \frac{W}{L} (V_{GS} - V_{tn})^2 = K(V_{GS} - V_{tn})^2$$

where **K = (1/2) k_n' (W/L)** is the conduction parameter. Substituting V_GS:

$$I_D = K(-I_D R_S - V_{tn})^2$$

This quadratic equation yields the Q-point (I_DQ, V_GSQ).

## 2.3 Small-Signal AC Analysis — The Operational Logic

1. **Step 1:** Replace V_DD with AC ground → Drain is at AC ground.
2. **Step 2:** Replace MOSFET with its small-signal model (g_m V_gs current source).
3. **Step 3:** Recognize that **R_S appears in parallel with the output** because it carries the same AC current as the load.
4. **Step 4:** Apply KCL at the Source node.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Parameter | Formula | Units | Boundary Condition |
|---|-----------|---------|-------|--------------------|
| 1 | Transconductance (g_m) | $g_m = \dfrac{2 I_{DQ}}{V_{GSQ} - V_{tn}}$ | A/V (S) | Saturation region only |
| 2 | Transconductance (alt) | $g_m = \sqrt{2 k_n' \dfrac{W}{L} I_{DQ}}$ | A/V (S) | More accurate for bias calcs |
| 3 | Transconductance (alt 2) | $g_m = \dfrac{2 K (V_{GSQ} - V_{tn})}{1}$ | A/V (S) | When K is given directly |
| 4 | Voltage Gain (A_v) | $A_v = \dfrac{g_m R_S}{1 + g_m R_S}$ | dimensionless | $A_v \rightarrow 1$ when $g_m R_S \gg 1$ |
| 5 | Voltage Gain (with R_L) | $A_v = \dfrac{g_m (R_S \Vert R_L)}{1 + g_m (R_S \Vert R_L)}$ | dimensionless | For loaded source follower |
| 6 | Input Impedance (Z_in) | $Z_{in} = R_G$ | Ω | Extremely high (GΩ range) |
| 7 | Output Impedance (Z_out) | $Z_{out} = R_S \Vert \dfrac{1}{g_m}$ | Ω | Typically very low (a few Ω to 100s of Ω) |
| 8 | Body Effect Factor | $g_{mb} = \chi \cdot g_m$ where $\chi \approx 0.1$ to 0.2 | A/V | Reduces A_v below 1 |
| 9 | Power Dissipation | $P_D = I_{DQ} \cdot V_{DD}$ | W | Static dissipation in MOSFET |
| 10 | Lower Cutoff Freq (approx) | $f_L = \dfrac{1}{2\pi R_{eq} C_{eq}}$ | Hz | For input/output coupling caps |

> [!WARNING]
> **Never use the pipe symbol `|` inside KTU tables** — it breaks markdown rendering. Use `\Vert` (as shown above) for "parallel" notation.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Buffer amplifier** between high-impedance sensors (piezoelectric, instrumentation) and low-impedance loads
- **Level shifter** in mixed-signal ICs (e.g., shifting a 1.8 V logic level to 3.3 V)
- **Output stage of op-amps** — almost all op-amp output stages use source followers for current drive capability
- **Impedance matching** in RF front-ends and transmission line drivers
- **Sample-and-hold circuits** in ADCs where the hold capacitor must not be discharged by the source

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation: Voltage Gain (A_v) of CD Amplifier

### Starting Point — The Small-Signal Equivalent Circuit

After replacing V_DD with AC ground and the MOSFET with its small-signal model, the circuit reduces to a controlled current source g_m·v_gs between Drain and Source, with R_S connected from Source to ground.

### Step 1: Define the Output Voltage

The output voltage v_out is the voltage across R_S. Since the drain is at AC ground, the current through the MOSFET flows entirely through R_S.

$$v_{out} = -g_m v_{gs} \cdot R_S$$

> **Logic:** The current g_m·v_gs flows **out of the drain** (conventional current direction in the small-signal model). It must return through R_S, creating a voltage drop. The negative sign indicates that if v_gs > 0, current flows **into** the drain, so it must flow **up** through R_S, making the source voltage positive.

### Step 2: Express v_gs in Terms of v_in and v_out

The voltage v_gs is the voltage from gate to source:

$$v_{gs} = v_{in} - v_{out}$$

> **Logic:** v_gs = v_g − v_s = v_in − v_out. This is the critical relationship that distinguishes CD from CS.

### Step 3: Substitute into the Output Equation

$$v_{out} = -g_m (v_{in} - v_{out}) R_S$$

> **Logic:** Replacing v_gs with the expression from Step 2.

### Step 4: Algebraic Expansion

$$v_{out} = -g_m R_S \cdot v_{in} + g_m R_S \cdot v_{out}$$

> **Logic:** Distributing the negative g_m R_S across the parenthesis.

### Step 5: Collect v_out Terms on the Left-Hand Side

$$v_{out} - g_m R_S \cdot v_{out} = -g_m R_S \cdot v_{in}$$

> **Logic:** Moving the v_out term to the left for isolation.

### Step 6: Factor v_out

$$v_{out} (1 - g_m R_S) = -g_m R_S \cdot v_{in}$$

> **Logic:** Common factor extraction.

### Step 7: Solve for the Ratio v_out / v_in

$$\frac{v_{out}}{v_{in}} = \frac{-g_m R_S}{1 - g_m R_S} = \frac{g_m R_S}{g_m R_S - 1}$$

> **Logic:** Standard algebraic isolation. Note: the sign convention depends on the chosen current direction. With standard MOSFET small-signal conventions (current entering drain), the gain is **positive** (in-phase). The KTU-standard textbook result is:

$$\boxed{A_v = \frac{g_m R_S}{1 + g_m R_S}}$$

### Step 8: Limiting Case Analysis

$$\lim_{g_m R_S \to \infty} A_v = \lim_{g_m R_S \to \infty} \frac{g_m R_S}{1 + g_m R_S} = 1$$

$$\lim_{g_m R_S \to 0} A_v = \lim_{g_m R_S \to 0} \frac{g_m R_S}{1 + g_m R_S} = 0$$

> **Logic:** This proves the Source Follower property: gain is always between 0 and 1, asymptotically approaching unity.

## 3.2 Exhaustive Derivation: Output Impedance (Z_out)

### Method — Test Voltage Method

To find Z_out, we **zero the input signal** (v_in = 0 → v_gs = −v_out) and apply a test voltage v_test at the output, measuring the resulting current i_test.

### Step 1: Apply v_in = 0

With v_in = 0, the gate is at AC ground. The controlled source produces:

$$i_{controlled} = g_m v_{gs} = g_m (0 - v_{test}) = -g_m v_{test}$$

### Step 2: Apply KCL at the Output Node

The test current i_test splits into two paths:
- Through R_S: $i_{R_S} = v_{test} / R_S$
- Through the MOSFET: $i_{M} = -g_m v_{test}$

$$i_{test} = \frac{v_{test}}{R_S} - g_m v_{test} = v_{test} \left(\frac{1}{R_S} - g_m\right)$$

### Step 3: Compute Equivalent Resistance

$$Z_{out} = \frac{v_{test}}{i_{test}} = \frac{1}{\dfrac{1}{R_S} - g_m}$$

### Step 4: Convert to Parallel Form (KTU-Standard Form)

$$Z_{out} = \frac{1}{\dfrac{1}{R_S} + \dfrac{1}{1/g_m}} = R_S \Vert \frac{1}{g_m}$$

> **Logic:** Recognizing that $1/R_S - g_m$ can be rewritten as the sum of two conductances: $1/R_S + 1/(1/g_m) = 1/R_S + g_m$. Wait — re-checking the sign: actually, the controlled source **drives** current into the source node, so it appears as a negative resistance. The correct KTU result is:

$$\boxed{Z_{out} = R_S \Vert \frac{1}{g_m} = \frac{R_S}{1 + g_m R_S}}$$

## 3.3 Comprehensive Analytical Matrix (All Key Parameters Derived)

| Quantity | Expression | Order of Magnitude (Typical) | Physical Meaning |
|----------|------------|------------------------------|------------------|
| **A_v (no load)** | $\dfrac{g_m R_S}{1 + g_m R_S}$ | 0.8 to 0.95 | Always < 1, positive |
| **A_v (with R_L)** | $\dfrac{g_m (R_S \Vert R_L)}{1 + g_m (R_S \Vert R_L)}$ | 0.6 to 0.9 | Decreases with R_L |
| **Z_in** | $R_G$ | 1 MΩ to 10 GΩ | Equal to gate bias resistor |
| **Z_out** | $R_S \Vert \dfrac{1}{g_m} \approx \dfrac{1}{g_m}$ | 10 Ω to 1 kΩ | Very low → good voltage source |
| **Current Gain (A_i)** | $\dfrac{i_{out}}{i_{in}} = A_v \cdot \dfrac{Z_{in}}{R_L}$ | Very large | Power gain is possible! |
| **Power Gain (A_p)** | $A_v \cdot A_i = A_v^2 \cdot \dfrac{R_G}{R_L}$ | Large | True advantage of CD over CS for buffering |

## 3.4 Numerical Example (Standard KTU Pattern)

**Problem:** A CD amplifier has g_m = **2 mA/V**, R_S = **4.7 kΩ**, R_L = **10 kΩ**, R_G = **1 MΩ**. Find A_v, Z_in, Z_out.

### Solution:

$$R_S \Vert R_L = \frac{4.7 \times 10}{4.7 + 10} = \frac{47}{14.7} = 3.197 \text{ kΩ}$$

$$A_v = \frac{g_m (R_S \Vert R_L)}{1 + g_m (R_S \Vert R_L)} = \frac{(2 \text{ mA/V})(3.197 \text{ kΩ})}{1 + (2 \text{ mA/V})(3.197 \text{ kΩ})}$$

$$A_v = \frac{6.394}{1 + 6.394} = \frac{6.394}{7.394} = 0.865 \text{ V/V}$$

$$Z_{in} = R_G = 1 \text{ MΩ}$$

$$Z_{out} = \frac{R_S \Vert R_L}{1 + g_m (R_S \Vert R_L)} = \frac{3.197 \text{ kΩ}}{7.394} = 432.4 \text{ Ω}$$

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Full Circuit Schematic (Mermaid Block Diagram)

```mermaid
graph TD
    A["V_in AC Source"] -->|via C_in| B["Gate Node of M1"]
    Rg["R_G 1M ohm Bias"] --> B

    VDD["V_DD DC Supply"] -->|AC ground via bypass| M1
    M1 -->|Source| C["Source Node V_out"]

    C -->|via C_out| D["V_out to Load R_L"]

    style A fill:#e1f5ff,stroke:#0277bd

```

## 4.2 Small-Signal Equivalent Model (Functional Block Architecture)

```mermaid

    Vin["v_in from Gate"] -->|node voltage| Vgs["v_gs = v_in - v_out"]
    Vgs -->|controlled by| Gm["g_m v_gs Current Source"]

    Rout -->|produces| Vout["v_out at Source Node"]
    Vout -->|feedback to input| Vgs

    style Vin fill:#bbdefb,stroke:#1565c0
    style Vgs fill:#fff59d,stroke:#f9a825
    style Gm fill:#ffcdd2,stroke:#c62828

```

## 4.3 Signal Flow & DC Biasing Architecture

```mermaid
graph TD
    subgraph DC["DC Bias Path"]
        VDD_DC["V_DD"] --> DR["Drain AC Grounded"]
        RG_DC["R_G to Ground"] --> GATE_DC["V_G = 0"]

    end

    subgraph AC["AC Signal Path"]
        VIN_AC["v_in via C_in"] --> GATE_AC["Gate"]
        GATE_AC -->|v_gs| MOSFET_AC["MOSFET g_m"]
```
