```mermaid
        SOURCE_AC -->|via C_out| VOUT_AC["v_out to Load"]
    end

    style DC fill:#e3f2fd,stroke:#0d47a1
    style AC fill:#fff3e0,stroke:#e65100
```

## 4.4 Comparison Block: CD vs CS vs CG

```mermaid
graph TD
    A["Three Basic MOSFET Amplifier Configurations"] --> B["CS Common Source"]
    A --> C["CD Common Drain Source Follower"]
    A --> D["CG Common Gate"]

    B --> B1["Inverting 180 degree Phase Shift"]
    B --> B2["High Voltage Gain A_v = -g_m R_D"]
    B --> B3["Moderate Z_in, High Z_out"]

    C --> C1["Non-Inverting 0 degree Phase Shift"]
    C --> C2["Unity Voltage Gain A_v approx 1"]
    C --> C3["High Z_in, Low Z_out BUFFER"]

    D --> D1["Non-Inverting 0 degree Phase Shift"]
    D --> D2["High Voltage Gain A_v = g_m R_D"]
    D --> D3["Low Z_in, High Z_out"]

    style C fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#1b5e20,stroke-width:3px
    style C1 fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#1b5e20
    style C2 fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#1b5e20
    style C3 fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#1b5e20
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**Define Common Drain (CD) configuration. Why is it called a "Source Follower"?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
- **(1 Mark)** CD configuration is a MOSFET amplifier where the **drain is connected to V_DD** (AC grounded), **input is applied to the gate**, and **output is taken from the source**.
- **(1 Mark)** The drain is "common" between input and output AC loops.
- **(1 Mark)** The output voltage at the source "follows" the input voltage at the gate with a gain approaching unity and **0° phase shift**, hence the name "Source Follower."

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**List two advantages and one disadvantage of the CD amplifier.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
- **(1 Mark) Advantage 1:** Very high input impedance (≈ R_G) — ideal for connecting to high-impedance sources.
- **(1 Mark) Advantage 2:** Very low output impedance (≈ 1/g_m) — ideal for driving low-impedance loads.
- **(1 Mark) Disadvantage:** Voltage gain is always less than unity (no voltage amplification), so it cannot be used where voltage gain is required.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice

### **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – May 2023 Pattern]**

**(a)** For the CD amplifier shown below, derive the expression for the **small-signal voltage gain A_v** and the **output impedance Z_out**. Assume MOSFET is in saturation with transconductance g_m. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A CD amplifier has g_m = **3 mA/V**, R_S = **2.2 kΩ**, and is connected to a load R_L = **4.7 kΩ**. Calculate **(i)** A_v, **(ii)** Z_out, and **(iii)** the overall voltage gain if the source has an internal resistance R_sig = **100 kΩ**. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a):

**Step 1 (1 Mark):** Draw the small-signal equivalent circuit. Drain is at AC ground (since V_DD is bypassed). Replace MOSFET with g_m·v_gs current source between drain and source.

**Step 2 (1 Mark):** Write the output equation. The current g_m·v_gs flows through R_S (since drain is grounded):

$$v_{out} = g_m v_{gs} \cdot R_S$$

Wait — correcting the sign convention. With the current source **entering the drain**, it flows **up** through R_S from ground:

$$v_{out} = g_m v_{gs} R_S$$

**Step 3 (1 Mark):** Express v_gs:

$$v_{gs} = v_{in} - v_{out}$$

**Step 4 (1 Mark):** Substitute and solve:

$$v_{out} = g_m R_S (v_{in} - v_{out})$$
$$v_{out} (1 + g_m R_S) = g_m R_S \cdot v_{in}$$
$$A_v = \frac{v_{out}}{v_{in}} = \frac{g_m R_S}{1 + g_m R_S}$$

**Step 5 (1 Mark):** For Z_out, set v_in = 0, apply test voltage v_x at output:

$$Z_{out} = \frac{v_x}{i_x} = R_S \Vert \frac{1}{g_m} = \frac{R_S}{1 + g_m R_S}$$

**Step 6 (1 Mark):** State the special case: when g_m R_S >> 1, A_v → 1 and Z_out → 1/g_m.

**Step 7 (1 Mark):** Conclude that CD acts as an impedance buffer with near-unity voltage gain.

#### Model Solution — Part (b):

**(i) Voltage Gain A_v (2 Marks):**

$$R_{eq} = R_S \Vert R_L = \frac{2.2 \times 4.7}{2.2 + 4.7} = \frac{10.34}{6.9} = 1.499 \text{ kΩ}$$

$$A_v = \frac{g_m R_{eq}}{1 + g_m R_{eq}} = \frac{(3)(1.499)}{1 + (3)(1.499)} = \frac{4.497}{5.497} = 0.818 \text{ V/V}$$

**(ii) Output Impedance Z_out (2 Marks):**

$$Z_{out} = R_{eq} \Vert \frac{1}{g_m} = \frac{1.499 \text{ k} \times 333.3 \text{ }\Omega}{1.499 \text{ k} + 0.333 \text{ k}} = \frac{0.4997}{1.832} = 272.7 \text{ }\Omega$$

where $1/g_m = 1/0.003 = 333.3 \text{ }\Omega$.

**(iii) Overall Gain with R_sig (3 Marks):**

The voltage at the gate is divided by R_sig and R_G (where R_G is the gate bias resistor, here assumed to be very large or infinite):

$$v_{gs} = v_{in} \cdot \frac{R_G}{R_{sig} + R_G}$$

For a typical case, assume R_G = 1 MΩ:

$$v_{gs} = v_{in} \cdot \frac{1 \text{ M}}{1 \text{ M} + 100 \text{ k}} = 0.909 \cdot v_{in}$$

Overall gain:

$$A_{overall} = 0.909 \times 0.818 = 0.744 \text{ V/V}$$

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022 Pattern] — Alternative Choice**

**(a)** Draw the **small-signal equivalent circuit** of a CD amplifier and explain why the output voltage **follows** the input voltage. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Compare the **CS, CD, and CG** configurations in terms of (i) phase shift, (ii) voltage gain, (iii) input impedance, and (iv) output impedance. Highlight one specific application of the CD configuration. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a):

**Step 1 (2 Marks):** Draw the circuit:
- Drain → AC ground
- Gate → v_in
- Source → v_out
- MOSFET replaced by g_m v_gs current source
- R_S from source to ground

**Step 2 (2 Marks):** Write the KCL equation. The current g_m v_gs flows through R_S. But since v_gs = v_in − v_out, increasing v_in increases v_gs, which increases the drain current, which increases the voltage drop across R_S, raising v_out.

**Step 3 (2 Marks):** Show mathematically that A_v ≈ 1 when g_m R_S is large. Hence, v_out ≈ v_in (in phase, nearly equal magnitude).

**Step 4 (1 Mark):** Conclude: the source "follows" the gate because the feedback through v_gs = v_in − v_out automatically stabilizes the gain near unity.

#### Model Solution — Part (b):

**Comparison Table (6 Marks):**

| Parameter | CS (Common Source) | CD (Common Drain) | CG (Common Gate) |
|-----------|--------------------|--------------------|-------------------|
| **(i) Phase Shift** | 180° (inverting) | 0° (non-inverting) | 0° (non-inverting) |
| **(ii) Voltage Gain** | High ($\vert g_m R_D \vert$) | ≈ 1 (buffer) | High ($g_m R_D$) |
| **(iii) Input Impedance** | High (R_G) | Very high (R_G) | Low (1/g_m) |
| **(iv) Output Impedance** | High (R_D) | Very low (1/g_m) | High (R_D) |

**Specific Application of CD (1 Mark):**
The CD configuration is used as a **buffer amplifier** at the output stage of operational amplifiers to provide high current drive capability to low-impedance loads without loading the high-gain intermediate stage.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Sign Convention Trap:** Many students lose 2–3 marks by writing $A_v = -g_m R_S / (1 + g_m R_S)$ — this is the **CS** formula, not CD! For CD, the gain is **positive**.
> 2. **Z_out Mistake:** Z_out is **NOT** just $1/g_m$. It is $R_S \Vert (1/g_m)$. If R_S is not given, you must state "R_S is absent" or include it explicitly. (-1 to -2 marks if missed)
> 3. **Body Effect:** For exam questions mentioning "integrated MOSFET" or "substrate connected to source," remember to include the body-effect transconductance $g_{mb}$, which further reduces A_v below 1.
> 4. **DC vs AC Mistake:** The drain is at AC ground but at V_DD for DC. Many students write "drain is grounded" without the AC qualifier. (-1 mark)
> 5. **Units:** Always express g_m in **A/V** or **mA/V** consistently. Mixing mS with kΩ or A/V with Ω without conversion loses 1 mark.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist**

- ✅ **CD = Common Drain = Source Follower** — drain is AC grounded at V_DD.
- ✅ **Input at Gate, Output at Source** — never the other way around.
- ✅ **Voltage Gain:** $A_v = \dfrac{g_m R_S}{1 + g_m R_S}$ — always **positive** and **less than 1**.
- ✅ **Phase Shift:** **0°** (in-phase) — a defining characteristic.
- ✅ **Input Impedance:** $Z_{in} = R_G$ — very high (set by bias resistor).
- ✅ **Output Impedance:** $Z_{out} = R_S \Vert (1/g_m) \approx 1/g_m$ — very low.
- ✅ **Limiting Case:** $g_m R_S \to \infty \Rightarrow A_v \to 1$ and $Z_{out} \to 0$.
- ✅ **No Miller Effect:** Unlike CS, CD has no Miller multiplication of C_gd → high bandwidth.
- ✅ **Primary Use:** **Impedance Buffer** — high Z_in, low Z_out, near-unity gain.
- ✅ **Body Effect:** Reduces A_v below unity in discrete MOSFETs (substrate not at source).
- ✅ **Cannot be used as a voltage amplifier** — use CS or CG for that purpose.
- ✅ **R_S cannot be bypassed** with a capacitor — doing so eliminates the output!
- ✅ **R_G has no AC current** in a MOSFET — therefore Z_in is purely resistive and equal to R_G.
- ✅ **Power Gain is High** even though voltage gain is < 1, because current gain is large.
- ✅ **DC Bias:** V_G = 0 (via R_G), V_S = I_DQ R_S, V_GS = −I_DQ R_S (self-bias topology).
- ✅ **Saturation Requirement:** $V_{DS} \geq V_{GS} - V_{tn}$ — must be verified after finding Q-point.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Expressions for Characteristic Parameters: Input and Output Impedance
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCICT302 - ELECTRONIC CIRCUITS AND NETWORKS > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Expressions for Characteristic Parameters: Input and Output Impedance # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Two-Port Network Recap A **two-port network** is an electrical network encapsulated inside a black box, accessible only through **two distinct pairs of terminals**: the input port (1-1′) and the output port (2-2′). It is fully described by four variables: the input voltage $V_1$, the input current $I_1$, the output voltage $V_2$, and the output current $I_2$. > [!NOTE] > **KTU 2024 Scheme Emphasis** > In Module 1, two-port network analysis is the foundation for filter design, transistor modeling, and cascade amplifier analysis. The "characteristic parameters" refer to the impedance and admittance quantities that uniquely describe a two-port from an external behavioural standpoint. ## 1.2 What Are Characteristic Parameters? **Characteristic parameters** are the measurable quantities that completely characterize a linear, reciprocal, two-port network at its external terminals. The principal members are: 1. **Z-parameters** (Open-circuit Impedance parameters) 2. **Y-parameters** (Short-circuit Admittance parameters) 3. **h-parameters** (Hybrid parameters) 4. **g-parameters** (Inverse Hybrid parameters) 5. **ABCD-parameters** (Transmission parameters) 6. **Image Parameters** ($Z_{i1}, Z_{i2}$ and $\gamma$) ## 1.3 Input Impedance ($Z_{in}$) — Formal Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **$Z_{in}$** is defined as the impedance presented by the network at the input port (1-1′) when a load impedance $Z_L$ is connected at the output port (2-2′). Mathematically: $$Z_{in} = \left. \frac{V_1}{I_1} \right|_{Z_L \text{ connected at port 2}}$$ ## 1.4 Output Impedance ($Z_{out}$) — Formal Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **$Z_{out}$** is defined as the impedance looking back into the output port (2-2′) with the independent source deactivated (voltage source shorted, current source opened) and the source impedance $Z_S$ retained at the input port. Mathematically: $$Z_{out} = \left. \frac{V_2}{I_2} \right|_{V_S = 0, \, Z_S \text{ retained at port 1}}$$ ## 1.5 Conceptual Analogy — The "Pipe and Valve" Model Imagine a long water pipe with a **valve** somewhere in the middle. The pipe's behaviour depends on whether you push water in from one end and the valve is open or closed at the other end. - **Input Impedance**: Stand at the source end of the pipe and "feel" the resistance to flow. This depends on how the valve at the far end is set (open, closed, or partially open via $Z_L$). - **Output Impedance**: Now, disconnect the pump, look back into the pipe from the valve end. The "resistance" you feel is the output impedance. It depends on what's happening at the source end (the source impedance $Z_S$ and any internal plumbing). In electronics, these "feelings" of resistance are the *driving-point impedances* and they are the keys to impedance matching, maximum power transfer, and cascade design. ## 1.6 Physical Constants & Conventions - The angular frequency $\omega$ has units of **rad/s** (assumed constant in narrowband analysis). - For **reciprocal** networks: $Z_{12} = Z_{21}$, $Y_{12} = Y_{21}$, $h_{12} = -h_{21}$ (for hybrid), $AD - BC = 1$ (for ABCD). - For **symmetric** networks: $Z_{11} = Z_{22}$, $Y_{11} = Y_{22}$, $h_{11}h_{22} - h_{12}h_{21} = 1$ (often), $A = D$ (for ABCD). # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Operational Logic — Step-by-Step Reasoning To derive $Z_{in}$ and $Z_{out}$ for any given parameter set, the engineer follows a uniform **three-step protocol**: **Step 1 — Select the parameter definition.** Write the two governing equations in terms of the chosen parameters (e.g., Z, Y, h, ABCD). **Step 2 — Apply the appropriate boundary condition.** - For $Z_{in}$: terminate port 2 with $Z_L$, which imposes $V_2 = -I_2 Z_L$ (passive sign convention with current entering port 2). - For $Z_{out}$: deactivate the source, which imposes $V_1 = -I_1 Z_S$. **Step 3 — Algebraic elimination.** Solve the system to eliminate the "internal" variable, leaving $V_1/I_1$ (for $Z_{in}$) or $V_2/I_2$ (for $Z_{out}$). ## 2.2 The "Why" Behind Each Parameter Choice - **Z-parameters** are preferred when the network is naturally series-connected or when open-circuit conditions are easy to realize. - **Y-parameters** suit parallel-connected networks and short-circuit tests. - **h-parameters** are vital for **transistor small-signal modelling** (Common-Emitter, Common-Base) because $h_{11}$ mimics $Z_{in}$ and $1/h_{22}$ mimics $Z_{out}$. - **ABCD-parameters** are indispensable for **cascade** analysis because the overall matrix is the product of individual stages. ## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > The table below is the **single most important revision resource** for Module 1 problems. All KTU problems on this topic are direct applications of these nine expressions. | Parameter Set | Input Impedance $Z_{in}$ | Output Impedance $Z_{out}$ | Determinant | | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | | **Z-parameters** | $\dfrac{Z_{11} Z_L + \Delta Z}{Z_{22} + Z_L}$ | $\dfrac{Z_{22} Z_S + \Delta Z}{Z_{11} + Z_S}$ | $\Delta Z = Z_{11}Z_{22} - Z_{12}Z_{21}$ | | **Y-parameters** | $\dfrac{1 + Y_{22} Z_L}{Y_{11} + \Delta Y \cdot Z_L}$ | $\dfrac{1 + Y_{11} Z_S}{Y_{22} + \Delta Y \cdot Z_S}$ | $\Delta Y = Y_{11}Y_{22} - Y_{12}Y_{21}$ | | **h-parameters** | $\dfrac{h_{11} + \Delta h \cdot Z_L}{1 + h_{22} Z_L}$ | $\dfrac{Z_S + h_{11}}{\Delta h + h_{22} Z_S}$ | $\Delta h = h_{11}h_{22} - h_{12}h_{21}$ | | **g-parameters** | $\dfrac{g_{22} + Z_L}{\Delta g + g_{11} Z_L}$ | $\dfrac{\Delta g + g_{11} Z_S}{1 + g_{22} Z_S}$ | $\Delta g = g_{11}g_{22} - g_{12}g_{21}$ | | **ABCD-parameters** | $\dfrac{A Z_L + B}{C Z_L + D}$ | $\dfrac{D Z_S + B}{A + C Z_S}$ | $\Delta T = AD - BC$ | > [!NOTE] > **Critical Observation:** The expressions for $Z_{in}$ and $Z_{out}$ in any single parameter set are **dual forms** — they are interchanged by swapping the "input" and "output" subscripts. For example, in Z-parameters, $Z_{in}$ and $Z_{out}$ differ only in the swap $1 \leftrightarrow 2$. ## 2.4 Special Case — Image Impedances (True Characteristic Parameters) When a two-port is terminated by its own characteristic image impedance, the impedance looking into the other port equals that same image impedance. The image impedances are: $$Z_{i1} = \sqrt{\frac{\Delta Z \cdot Z_{11}}{Z_{22}}} \quad , \quad Z_{i2} = \sqrt{\frac{\Delta Z \cdot Z_{22}}{Z_{11}}}$$ ## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Filter design:** Lattice and bridged-T networks are characterized via image parameters because they form a basis for **constant-k** and **m-derived** filter sections used in RF and audio. - **Antenna matching:** $Z_{in}$ formulas directly guide impedance-matching network design (e.g., L-section, Pi-network). - **Transistor amplifiers:** h-parameter $Z_{in}$ formula gives the input loading of a CE stage, critical for cascading multiple amplifier stages. - **Transmission line cascades:** ABCD parameters model each line section; $Z_{in}$ at each junction determines signal integrity. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation 1 — $Z_{in}$ in Terms of Z-Parameters **Starting Equations:** $$V_1 = Z_{11} I_1 + Z_{12} I_2$$ $$V_2 = Z_{21} I_1 + Z_{22} I_2$$ **Step 1 — Apply Output Boundary Condition.** At port 2, a load $Z_L$ is connected. With the convention that $I_2$ enters the port, KVL gives $V_2 = -I_2 Z_L$. Substituting into the second Z-equation: $$-I_2 Z_L = Z_{21} I_1 + Z_{22} I_2$$ **Step 2 — Solve for $I_2$ in terms of $I_1$.** $$\begin{aligned} -I_2 Z_L - Z_{22} I_2 &= Z_{21} I_1 \\ -I_2 (Z_L + Z_{22}) &= Z_{21} I_1 \\ I_2 &= -\frac{Z_{21}}{Z_L + Z_{22}} \, I_1 \end{aligned}$$ **Step 3 — Substitute back into the $V_1$ equation.** $$\begin{aligned} V_1 &= Z_{11} I_1 + Z_{12} \left( -\frac{Z_{21}}{Z_L + Z_{22}} I_1 \right) \\ V_1 &= I_1 \left[ Z_{11} - \frac{Z_{12} Z_{21}}{Z_L + Z_{22}} \right] \\ V_1 &= I_1 \left[ \frac{Z_{11}(Z_L + Z_{22}) - Z_{12} Z_{21}}{Z_L + Z_{22}} \right] \\ V_1 &= I_1 \left[ \frac{Z_{11} Z_L + Z_{11} Z_{22} - Z_{12} Z_{21}}{Z_L + Z_{22}} \right] \end{aligned}$$ **Step 4 — Form the ratio $V_1/I_1$ and identify the determinant.** $$\begin{aligned} Z_{in} = \frac{V_1}{I_1} &= \frac{Z_{11} Z_L + (Z_{11} Z_{22} - Z_{12} Z_{21})}{Z_L + Z_{22}} \\ \boxed{Z_{in} &= \frac{Z_{11} Z_L + \Delta Z}{Z_{22} + Z_L}} \end{aligned}$$ where $\Delta Z = Z_{11} Z_{22} - Z_{12} Z_{21}$ is the **determinant of the Z-matrix**. ## 3.2 Derivation 2 — $Z_{out}$ in Terms of Z-Parameters **Starting Equations:** (same as above) **Step 1 — Apply Input Boundary Condition.** Deactivate the source: $V_S = 0$, so the source impedance $Z_S$ forces $V_1 = -I_1 Z_S$. Substituting into the first Z-equation: $$-I_1 Z_S = Z_{11} I_1 + Z_{12} I_2$$ **Step 2 — Solve for $I_1$ in terms of $I_2$.** $$\begin{aligned} -I_1 Z_S - Z_{11} I_1 &= Z_{12} I_2 \\ -I_1 (Z_S + Z_{11}) &= Z_{12} I_2 \\ I_1 &= -\frac{Z_{12}}{Z_S + Z_{11}} \, I_2 \end{aligned}$$ **Step 3 — Substitute back into the $V_2$ equation.** $$\begin{aligned} V_2 &= Z_{21} \left( -\frac{Z_{12}}{Z_S + Z_{11}} I_2 \right) + Z_{22} I_2 \\ V_2 &= I_2 \left[ Z_{22} - \frac{Z_{12} Z_{21}}{Z_S + Z_{11}} \right] \\ V_2 &= I_2 \left[ \frac{Z_{22}(Z_S + Z_{11}) - Z_{12} Z_{21}}{Z_S + Z_{11}} \right] \\ V_2 &= I_2 \left[ \frac{Z_{22} Z_S + Z_{11} Z_{22} - Z_{12} Z_{21}}{Z_S + Z_{11}} \right] \end{aligned}$$ **Step 4 — Form the ratio $V_2/I_2$.** $$\begin{aligned} Z_{out} = \frac{V_2}{I_2} &= \frac{Z_{22} Z_S + (Z_{11} Z_{22} - Z_{12} Z_{21})}{Z_S + Z_{11}} \\ \boxed{Z_{out} &= \frac{Z_{22} Z_S + \Delta Z}{Z_{11} + Z_S}} \end{aligned}$$ ## 3.3 Derivation 3 — $Z_{in}$ in Terms of h-Parameters **Starting Equations:** $$V_1 = h_{11} I_1 + h_{12} V_2$$ $$I_2 = h_{21} I_1 + h_{22} V_2$$ **Step 1 — Apply Output Boundary Condition:** $V_2 = -I_2 Z_L$. $$\begin{aligned} I_2 &= h_{21} I_1 + h_{22} (-I_2 Z_L) \\ I_2 + h_{22} Z_L I_2 &= h_{21} I_1 \\ I_2 (1 + h_{22} Z_L) &= h_{21} I_1 \\ I_1 &= \frac{I_2 (1 + h_{22} Z_L)}{h_{21}} \end{aligned}$$ **Step 2 — Substitute into the $V_1$ equation.** $$\begin{aligned} V_1 &= h_{11} \cdot \frac{I_2 (1 + h_{22} Z_L)}{h_{21}} + h_{12} (-I_2 Z_L) \\ V_1 &= I_2 \left[ \frac{h_{11}(1 + h_{22} Z_L) - h_{12} h_{21} Z_L}{h_{21}} \right] \end{aligned}$$ **Step 3 — Form the ratio $V_1/I_1$.** $$\begin{aligned} Z_{in} = \frac{V_1}{I_1} &= \frac{ \dfrac{h_{11}(1 + h_{22} Z_L) - h_{12} h_{21} Z_L}{h_{21}} }{ \dfrac{1 + h_{22} Z_L}{h_{21}} } \\ Z_{in} &= \frac{h_{11}(1 + h_{22} Z_L) - h_{12} h_{21} Z_L}{1 + h_{22} Z_L} \\ Z_{in} &= \frac{h_{11} + h_{11} h_{22} Z_L - h_{12} h_{21} Z_L}{1 + h_{22} Z_L} \\ \boxed{Z_{in} &= \frac{h_{11} + \Delta h \cdot Z_L}{1 + h_{22} Z_L}} \end{aligned}$$ where $\Delta h = h_{11} h_{22} - h_{12} h_{21}$. ## 3.4 Derivation 4 — $Z_{in}$ in Terms of ABCD-Parameters **Starting Equations (with $I_2$ leaving the output port — this is the *transmission* convention):** $$V_1 = A V_2 + B I_2$$ $$I_1 = C V_2 + D I_2$$ **Step 1 — Apply Load Condition:** At port 2, $V_2 = I_2 Z_L$ (voltage across $Z_L$ with current $I_2$ flowing *into* the load, hence into the port with the transmission sign convention). **Step 2 — Substitute.** $$\begin{aligned} V_1 &= A (I_2 Z_L) + B I_2 = I_2 (A Z_L + B) \\ I_1 &= C (I_2 Z_L) + D I_2 = I_2 (C Z_L + D) \end{aligned}$$ **Step 3 — Form the ratio.** $$\boxed{Z_{in} = \frac{V_1}{I_1} = \frac{A Z_L + B}{C Z_L + D}}$$ ## 3.5 Derivation 5 — $Z_{out}$ in Terms of ABCD-Parameters **Step 1 — Apply Source-Deactivation Boundary Condition:** $V_1 = -I_1 Z_S$. **Step 2 — Solve for $I_1$.** $$\begin{aligned} V_1 &= A V_2 + B I_2 \quad \Rightarrow \quad -I_1 Z_S = A V_2 + B I_2 \\ I_1 &= C V_2 + D I_2 \quad \Rightarrow \quad I_1 = C V_2 + D I_2 \end{aligned}$$ Equating: $-(C V_2 + D I_2) Z_S = A V_2 + B I_2$. $$\begin{aligned} -C Z_S V_2 - D Z_S I_2 &= A V_2 + B I_2 \\ -C Z_S V_2 - A V_2 &= B I_2 + D Z_S I_2 \\ -V_2 (C Z_S + A) &= I_2 (B + D Z_S) \\ \frac{I_2}{V_2} &= -\frac{C Z_S + A}{B + D Z_S} \end{aligned}$$ **Step 3 — Form $Z_{out}$.** $$\boxed{Z_{out} = \frac{V_2}{I_2} = \frac{D Z_S + B}{A + C Z_S}}$$ ## 3.6 Derivation 6 — Image Impedances (True Characteristic Parameters) The image impedance at port 1, $Z_{i1}$, is defined as the load $Z_L$ that makes the input impedance $Z_{in}$ equal to $Z_{i1}$: $$Z_{in} = Z_{i1} \quad \text{when} \quad Z_L = Z_{i2}$$ Substituting into the Z-parameter $Z_{in}$ formula: $$Z_{i1} = \frac{Z_{11} Z_{i2} + \Delta Z}{Z_{22} + Z_{i2}} \quad \cdots (1)$$ By duality, for $Z_{out}$: $$Z_{i2} = \frac{Z_{22} Z_{i1} + \Delta Z}{Z_{11} + Z_{i1}} \quad \cdots (2)$$ **Cross-multiply equation (1):** $$Z_{i1} Z_{22} + Z_{i1} Z_{i2} = Z_{11} Z_{i2} + \Delta Z$$ Rearranging: $$Z_{i1} Z_{i2} - Z_{11} Z_{i2} = \Delta Z - Z_{i1} Z_{22}$$ $$Z_{i2} (Z_{i1} - Z_{11}) = \Delta Z - Z_{i1} Z_{22} \quad \cdots (3)$$ **Cross-multiply equation (2):** $$Z_{i2} Z_{11} + Z_{i1} Z_{i2} = Z_{22} Z_{i1} + \Delta Z$$ Rearranging: $$Z_{i1} Z_{i2} - Z_{22} Z_{i1} = \Delta Z - Z_{i2} Z_{11}$$ $$Z_{i1} (Z_{i2} - Z_{22}) = \Delta Z - Z_{i1} Z_{i2} \quad \cdots (4)$$ **Multiply (3) and (4):** $$Z_{i1} Z_{i2} (Z_{i1} - Z_{11})(Z_{i2} - Z_{22}) = (\Delta Z - Z_{i1} Z_{22})(\Delta Z - Z_{i1} Z_{i2})$$ **Cleaner approach — Multiply (1) and (2) directly:** $$Z_{i1} \cdot Z_{i2} = \frac{Z_{11} Z_{i2} + \Delta Z}{Z_{22} + Z_{i2}} \cdot \frac{Z_{22} Z_{i1} + \Delta Z}{Z_{11} + Z_{i1}}$$ This gives, after algebraic manipulation (the $Z_{i1} Z_{i2}$ terms cancel symmetrically): $$(Z_{i1} Z_{i2})^2 = \frac{\Delta Z \cdot Z_{i1} \cdot Z_{i2} \cdot (Z_{11} + Z_{22}) + \Delta Z^2}{(Z_{11} + Z_{i1})(Z_{22} + Z_{i2})/Z_{i1}Z_{i2}}$$ A more elegant route is to **substitute (1) into (2)** or use the iterative relation. The classical result is: $$\boxed{Z_{i1} = \sqrt{\frac{\Delta Z \cdot Z_{11}}{Z_{22}}} \quad , \quad Z_{i2} = \sqrt{\frac{\Delta Z \cdot Z_{22}}{Z_{11}}}}$$ **Verification:** Multiply the two: $$Z_{i1} \cdot Z_{i2} = \sqrt{\frac{\Delta Z \cdot Z_{11}}{Z_{22}} \cdot \frac{\Delta Z \cdot Z_{22}}{Z_{11}}} = \sqrt{(\Delta Z)^2} = \Delta Z$$ This matches the well-known identity $Z_{i1} Z_{i2} = \Delta Z$ for image parameters. ✓ ## 3.7 Analytical Matrix — Numerical Worked Example > [!NOTE] > **Worked Example (KTU-typical style):** > For a two-port, the Z-parameters are: $Z_{11} = 10 \, \Omega$, $Z_{12} = 3 \, \Omega$, $Z_{21} = 3 \, \Omega$, $Z_{22} = 6 \, \Omega$. Find $Z_{in}$ and $Z_{out}$ when $Z_L = 4 \, \Omega$ and $Z_S = 2 \, \Omega$. **Solution:** $$\Delta Z = (10)(6) - (3)(3) = 60 - 9 = 51 \, \Omega^2$$ $$Z_{in} = \frac{Z_{11} Z_L + \Delta Z}{Z_{22} + Z_L} = \frac{(10)(4) + 51}{6 + 4} = \frac{91}{10} = 9.1 \, \Omega$$ $$Z_{out} = \frac{Z_{22} Z_S + \Delta Z}{Z_{11} + Z_S} = \frac{(6)(2) + 51}{10 + 2} = \frac{63}{12} = 5.25 \, \Omega$$ # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Two-Port Network with Source and Load ```mermaid flowchart LR srcNode[("Voltage Source
V_S")] -->|I_1| zSNode["Source Impedance
Z_S"] zSNode -->|V_1, I_1| port1A(("Port 1
Terminal 1")) port1A --> twoport{{"TWO-PORT
NETWORK
N"}} port1A --> port1B(("Port 1
Terminal 1'")) port1B --> gnd1[("Ground")] twoport --> port2A(("Port 2
Terminal 2")) twoport --> port2B(("Port 2
Terminal 2'")) port2A -->|V_2, I_2| zLNode["Load Impedance
Z_L"] zLNode --> gnd2[("Ground")] port2B --> gnd2 ``` **Interpretation:** The block shows a generic two-port $N$ with: - Source $V_S$ feeding $Z_S$ at port 1 - Load $Z_L$ connected at port 2 - $Z_{in}$ is the impedance looking *into* the network from the dotted line at port 1 (after $Z_S$) - $Z_{out}$ is the impedance looking *into* the network from the dotted line at port 2 (after $Z_L$ is removed and $V_S = 0$) ## 4.2 Mermaid Flowchart — Algorithm to Find $Z_{in}$ and $Z_{out}$ ```mermaid flowchart TD startA([Start: Two-Port Problem]) --> pickParam{Which parameter
set is given?} pickParam -- Z-param --> zForm["Write V1, V2 equations
V1 = Z11 I1 + Z12 I2
V2 = Z21 I1 + Z22 I2"] pickParam -- h-param --> hForm["Write V1, I2 equations
V1 = h11 I1 + h12 V2
I2 = h21 I1 + h22 V2"] pickParam -- ABCD --> tForm["Write V1, I1 equations
V1 = A V2 + B I2
I1 = C V2 + D I2"] zForm --> bcZin[/"Apply V2 = -I2 ZL"/] hForm --> bcZinH[/"Apply V2 = -I2 ZL"/] tForm --> bcZinT[/"Apply V2 = I2 ZL"/] bcZin --> elimZin["Eliminate I2 in terms of I1"] bcZinH --> elimZinH["Eliminate V2 in terms of I2"] bcZinT --> elimZinT["Eliminate V2 using I2 ZL"] elimZin --> finalZin[/"Form Zin = V1 over I1"/] elimZinH --> finalZinH[/"Form Zin = V1 over I1"/] elimZinT --> finalZinT[/"Form Zin = V1 over I1"/] finalZin --> answerZin([Result: Zin expression]) finalZinH --> answerZin finalZinT --> answerZin answerZin --> zoutLoop{Now find Zout?} zoutLoop -- Yes --> bcZout[/"Apply V1 = -I1 ZS"/] bcZout --> elimZout["Eliminate I1 in terms of I2"] elimZout --> finalZout[/"Form Zout = V2 over I2"/] finalZout --> answerZout([Result: Zout expression]) zoutLoop -- No --> stopNode([End]) answerZout --> stopNode ``` ## 4.3 Block Diagram — Relationship Between Parameter Families ```mermaid flowchart LR zParams["Z-PARAMETERS
Open-Circuit
Impedance"] -- "Matrix Inversion
Y = Z to power -1" --> yParams["Y-PARAMETERS
Short-Circuit
Admittance"] zParams -- "Hybrid
Transformation" --> hParams["h-PARAMETERS
Hybrid Series
at Input"] yParams -- "Hybrid
Transformation" --> gParams["g-PARAMETERS
Hybrid Inverse
Hybrid"] zParams -- "Cascade
Transformation" --> abcdParams["ABCD-PARAMETERS
Transmission
Matrix"] abcdParams -- "Transposed and
Normalized" --> abcdParams hParams -- "Cascade
Transformation" --> abcdParams zParams -- "Image
Impedance
Definition" --> imageParams["IMAGE
PARAMETERS
Z_i1, Z_i2, gamma"] ``` > [!NOTE] > **Diagram Interpretation:** > The figure shows that all parameter sets are equivalent representations of the same two-port network. The choice depends on convenience: Z for open-circuits, Y for short-circuits, h for transistors, ABCD for cascades, and image parameters for filter sections. # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Pattern Note:** Part A questions are short-answer, requiring a crisp definition or a one-line derivation. Marks are awarded for keyword accuracy. ### Question A1. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023] **Define input impedance and output impedance of a two-port network. State how they are useful in the analysis of cascaded networks.** **Model Answer:** - **Input Impedance ($Z_{in}$):** It is the impedance offered by the two-port network at its input terminals (port 1) when a load $Z_L$ is connected at the output port (port 2). $Z_{in} = V_1/I_1$ with $Z_L$ at port 2. - **Output Impedance ($Z_{out}$):** It is the impedance looking back into the output port (port 2) of the network when the independent source is deactivated (replaced by its internal impedance $Z_S$). $Z_{out} = V_2/I_2$ with $V_S = 0$ and $Z_S$ at port 1. - **Use in cascaded networks:** $Z_{in}$ of the next stage acts as the load for the previous stage, while $Z_{out}$ of the previous stage acts as the source for the next. This allows modular analysis of cascades. ### Question A2. [KTU University Exam - May 2024] **For a two-port network, $Z_{11} = 5 \, \Omega$, $Z_{22} = 8 \, \Omega$, $Z_{12} = Z_{21} = 2 \, \Omega$. Calculate the image impedances $Z_{i1}$ and $Z_{i2}$.** **Model Answer:** $$\Delta Z = Z_{11} Z_{22} - Z_{12} Z_{21} = (5)(8) - (2)(2) = 40 - 4 = 36 \, \Omega^2$$ $$Z_{i1} = \sqrt{\frac{\Delta Z \cdot Z_{11}}{Z_{22}}} = \sqrt{\frac{36 \times 5}{8}} = \sqrt{22.5} \approx 4.743 \, \Omega$$ $$Z_{i2} = \sqrt{\frac{\Delta Z \cdot Z_{22}}{Z_{11}}} = \sqrt{\frac{36 \times 8}{5}} = \sqrt{57.6} \approx 7.589 \, \Omega$$ ## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Pattern Note:** Part B carries 14 marks, typically with internal choice. The structure is sub-part (a) for 7 marks and sub-part (b) for 7 marks. Always show full boundary conditions and determinant calculation. --- ### Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023] **(a)** Derive the expressions for the input impedance $Z_{in}$ and output impedance $Z_{out}$ of a two-port network in terms of **Z-parameters**. **(7 marks)** **(b)** For the bridged-T network shown, the Z-parameters are: $Z_{11} = 7 \, \Omega$, $Z_{22} = 5 \, \Omega$, $Z_{12} = Z_{21} = 3 \, \Omega$. If the load $Z_L = 4 \, \Omega$ and the source impedance $Z_S = 3 \, \Omega$, calculate $Z_{in}$ and $Z_{out}$. Also find the image impedances. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **(a)** *Refer to Section 3.1 and 3.2 for the complete step-by-step derivation. The final boxed results are:* $$Z_{in} = \frac{Z_{11} Z_L + \Delta Z}{Z_{22} + Z_L} \quad , \quad Z_{out} = \frac{Z_{22} Z_S + \Delta Z}{Z_{11} + Z_S}$$ *Key steps to write in exam:* - Start from $V_1 = Z_{11} I_1 + Z_{12} I_2$ and $V_2 = Z_{21} I_1 + Z_{22} I_2$. - Apply $V_2 = -I_2 Z_L$ for $Z_{in}$, get $I_2 = -Z_{21} I_1 / (Z_L + Z_{22})$. - Substitute back, get $Z_{in} = V_1/I_1 = (Z_{11} Z_L + \Delta Z)/(Z_{22} + Z_L)$. - Apply $V_1 = -I_1 Z_S$ for $Z_{out}$, get $I_1 = -Z_{12} I_2 / (Z_S + Z_{11})$. - Substitute back, get $Z_{out} = (Z_{22} Z_S + \Delta Z)/(Z_{11} + Z_S)$. **(b)** Given: $Z_{11} = 7$, $Z_{22} = 5$, $Z_{12} = Z_{21} = 3$, $Z_L = 4$, $Z_S = 3$ (all in $\Omega$). **Step 1 — Compute determinant:** $$\Delta Z = Z_{11} Z_{22} - Z_{12} Z_{21} = (7)(5) - (3)(3) = 35 - 9 = 26 \, \Omega^2$$ **Step 2 — Compute $Z_{in}$:** $$\begin{aligned} Z_{in} &= \frac{Z_{11} Z_L + \Delta Z}{Z_{22} + Z_L} \\ &= \frac{(7)(4) + 26}{5 + 4} \\ &= \frac{28 + 26}{9} = \frac{54}{9} = 6 \, \Omega \end{aligned}$$ **Step 3 — Compute $Z_{out}$:** $$\begin{aligned} Z_{out} &= \frac{Z_{22} Z_S + \Delta Z}{Z_{11} + Z_S} \\ &= \frac{(5)(3) + 26}{7 + 3} \\ &= \frac{15 + 26}{10} = \frac{41}{10} = 4.1 \, \Omega \end{aligned}$$ **Step 4 — Image Impedances:** $$\begin{aligned} Z_{i1} &= \sqrt{\frac{\Delta Z \cdot Z_{11}}{Z_{22}}} = \sqrt{\frac{26 \times 7}{5}} = \sqrt{36.4} \approx 6.033 \, \Omega \\ Z_{i2} &= \sqrt{\frac{\Delta Z \cdot Z_{22}}{Z_{11}}} = \sqrt{\frac{26 \times 5}{7}} = \sqrt{18.57} \approx 4.310 \, \Omega \end{aligned}$$ **Verification:** $Z_{i1} \cdot Z_{i2} = \sqrt{26 \times 26} = 26 = \Delta Z$ ✓ --- ### Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam - May 2024] **(a)** Derive the expression for the input impedance $Z_{in}$ of a two-port network in terms of **h-parameters** and the load impedance $Z_L$. **(7 marks)** **(b)** The h-parameters of a transistor in CE configuration are: $h_{11} = 1100 \, \Omega$, $h_{12} = 2.5 \times 10^{-4}$, $h_{21} = 50$, $h_{22} = 25 \times 10^{-6}$ S. The source impedance is $Z_S = 600 \, \Omega$ and the load is $Z_L = 4 \, k\Omega$. Calculate: (i) $Z_{in}$, (ii) $Z_{out}$, (iii) the overall voltage gain $A_V = V_2/V_S$. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **(a)** *Refer to Section 3.3 for the derivation. The boxed result is:* $$Z_{in} = \frac{h_{11} + \Delta h \cdot Z_L}{1 + h_{22} Z_L}, \quad \text{where } \Delta h = h_{11} h_{22} - h_{12} h_{21}$$ **(b)** Given values: $h_{11} = 1100$, $h_{12} = 2.5 \times 10^{-4}$, $h_{21} = 50$, $h_{22} = 25 \times 10^{-6}$, $Z_S = 600$, $Z_L = 4000$. **Step 1 — Compute the h-determinant:** $$\begin{aligned} \Delta h &= h_{11} h_{22} - h_{12} h_{21} \\ &= (1100)(25 \times 10^{-6}) - (2.5 \times 10^{-4})(50) \\ &= 0.0275 - 0.0125 = 0.015 \end{aligned}$$ **Step 2 — Compute $Z_{in}$:** $$\begin{aligned} Z_{in} &= \frac{h_{11} + \Delta h \cdot Z_L}{1 + h_{22} Z_L} \\ &= \frac{1100 + (0.015)(4000)}{1 + (25 \times 10^{-6})(4000)} \\ &= \frac{1100 + 60}{1 + 0.1} = \frac{1160}{1.1} \approx 1054.5 \, \Omega \end{aligned}$$ **Step 3 — Compute $Z_{out}$:** Using the formula $Z_{out} = (Z_S + h_{11})/(\Delta h + h_{22} Z_S)$: $$\begin{aligned} Z_{out} &= \frac{600 + 1100}{0.015 + (25 \times 10^{-6})(600)} \\ &= \frac{1700}{0.015 + 0.015} = \frac{1700}{0.03} \approx 56666.7 \, \Omega \end{aligned}$$ **Step 4 — Voltage Gain:** The overall voltage gain with source and load is: $$A_V = \frac{V_2}{V_S} = \frac{-h_{21}}{(h_{11} + Z_S)(h_{22} + 1/Z_L) + h_{12} h_{21}}$$ Wait — careful with sign. The output voltage is across the load. The current $I_2$ flows into the load, so $V_2 = -I_2 Z_L$ (using the hybrid sign convention where $I_2$ enters port 2). So: $$A_V = \frac{V_2}{V_S} = -\frac{h_{21} Z_L}{(h_{11} + Z_S) + \Delta h \cdot Z_L} \cdot \frac{1}{\text{... } }$$ Cleaner derivation: $V_S = I_1 (h_{11} + Z_S)$ — wait, $V_1 = h_{11} I_1 + h_{12} V_2$, and $V_S = I_1 Z_S + V_1 = I_1 (Z_S + h_{11}) + h_{12} V_2$. Also: $V_2 = -I_2 Z_L$ and $I_2 = h_{21} I_1 + h_{22} V_2$, so $V_2 = -Z_L (h_{21} I_1 + h_{22} V_2)$, giving $V_2 (1 + h_{22} Z_L) = -h_{21} Z_L I_1$, hence $V_2 = -h_{21} Z_L I_1 / (1 + h_{22} Z_L)$. Substituting into $V_S$: $$\begin{aligned} V_S &= I_1 (Z_S + h_{11}) + h_{12} \left( -\frac{h_{21} Z_L I_1}{1 + h_{22} Z_L} \right) \\ &= I_1 \left[ (Z_S + h_{11}) - \frac{h_{12} h_{21} Z_L}{1 + h_{22} Z_L} \right] \\ &= I_1 \left[ \frac{(Z_S + h_{11})(1 + h_{22} Z_L) - h_{12} h_{21} Z_L}{1 + h_{22} Z_L} \right] \end{aligned}$$ Therefore: $$\begin{aligned} A_V &= \frac{V_2}{V_S} = \frac{-h_{21} Z_L / (1 + h_{22} Z_L)}{ \left[ (Z_S + h_{11})(1 + h_{22} Z_L) - h_{12} h_{21} Z_L \right] / (1 + h_{22} Z_L)} \\ A_V &= \frac{-h_{21} Z_L}{(Z_S + h_{11})(1 + h_{22} Z_L) - h_{12} h_{21} Z_L} \end{aligned}$$ Plugging in: $$\begin{aligned} \text{Denominator} &= (600 + 1100)(1 + 0.1) - (2.5 \times 10^{-4})(50)(4000) \\ &= (1700)(1.1) - 50 \\ &= 1870 - 50 = 1820 \\ A_V &= \frac{-(50)(4000)}{1820} = \frac{-200000}{1820} \approx -109.89 \end{aligned}$$ The negative sign indicates a **180° phase inversion** characteristic of a Common-Emitter amplifier. --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls** > > 1. **Sign Convention Blunder**: The boundary condition $V_2 = -I_2 Z_L$ assumes $I_2$ enters the network at port 2. For ABCD-parameters, the convention is $I_2$ *leaves* port 2, so the condition becomes $V_2 = I_2 Z_L$. Mixing these up is the #1 cause of wrong signs. > > 2. **Determinant Calculation**: Forgetting to compute $\Delta Z$, $\Delta h$, or $\Delta Y$ before substituting into the formula. Always compute the determinant in a separate step. > > 3. **Misapplying the $Z_{out}$ Boundary Condition**: Many students write $V_1 = 0$ instead of $V_1 = -I_1 Z_S$. The correct condition is $V_1 = -I_1 Z_S$ because the source is deactivated (short-circuited), and the only voltage drop across $Z_S$ is due to the current flowing *back* into the port. > > 4. **Units**: Z-parameters are in $\Omega$, Y-parameters in S, h-parameters are mixed ($h_{11}$ in $\Omega$, $h_{22}$ in S), and ABCD is dimensionless for $A$ and $D$, $\Omega$ for $B$, S for $C$. Don't mix them up in $Z_{in}$/$Z_{out}$ calculations. > > 5. **Image Impedance**: A common mistake is to write $Z_{i1} = \sqrt{\Delta Z / Z_{22}}$. The correct form is $Z_{i1} = \sqrt{\Delta Z \cdot Z_{11}/Z_{22}}$. > > 6. **Symmetry Shortcut**: For a *symmetric* two-port ($Z_{11} = Z_{22}$), the image impedances simplify to $Z_{i1} = Z_{i2} = \sqrt{\Delta Z}$. Use this shortcut when applicable. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember - [x] $Z_{in} = V_1/I_1$ is computed with load $Z_L$ at port 2; $Z_{out} = V_2/I_2$ is computed with source deactivated and $Z_S$ at port 1. - [x] Every parameter set yields a $Z_{in}$ and $Z_{out}$ expression, all of which contain a **determinant** term ($\Delta Z$, $\Delta Y$, $\Delta h$, $\Delta g$, $\Delta T$). - [x] The Z-parameter expressions are the **canonical form**; Y, h, and ABCD expressions are obtained by algebraic transformation. - [x] The **dual structure** of $Z_{in}$ and $Z_{out}$ in any parameter set: swap subscripts "1" and "2", and replace $Z_L$ with $Z_S$. - [x] **Boundary conditions** are the heart of every derivation: $V_2 = -I_2 Z_L$ (for $Z_{in}$) and $V_1 = -I_1 Z_S$ (for $Z_{out}$). - [x] **Image impedances** $Z_{i1}$ and $Z_{i2}$ satisfy $Z_{i1} Z_{i2} = \Delta Z$. For a symmetric network, $Z_{i1} = Z_{i2} = \sqrt{\Delta Z}$. - [x] ABCD-parameters are preferred for **cascaded** networks; h-parameters for **transistor** circuits; Z-parameters for series networks; Y-parameters for parallel networks. - [x] For **reciprocal** networks: $Z_{12} = Z_{21}$, $Y_{12} = Y_{21}$, $h_{21} = -h_{12}$ (for hybrid), $AD - BC = 1$ (for ABCD). - [x] Always compute the determinant in a dedicated step before plugging into the $Z_{in}$/$Z_{out}$ formula — this prevents algebra errors. - [x] Units check: $\Delta Z$ has units $\Omega^2$, $\Delta Y$ has units $S^2$, $\Delta h$ is dimensionless. - [x] For the **transmission convention** in ABCD, the sign in $V_1 = A V_2 + B I_2$ assumes $I_2$ leaves port 2. - [x] KTU 2024 emphasis: Module 1 questions frequently combine $Z_{in}$/$Z_{out}$ derivation with numerical evaluation for transistor or filter parameters. - [x] **Image parameters** vs **Iterative parameters**: Image parameters terminate the network in $Z_{i1}$ and $Z_{i2}$ such that the input impedance equals the image impedance at that port. Iterative parameters make the *same* impedance appear at both ports. - [x] Master the **h-parameter $Z_{out}$** formula — it is the most commonly asked in KTU board exams. - [x] For ABCD $Z_{out}$ derivation, the trick is to apply $V_1 = -I_1 Z_S$ and then use the *current equation* to eliminate $I_1$.

topics.title: Voltage Gain and Transconductance
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCICT302 - ELECTRONIC CIRCUITS AND NETWORKS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Voltage Gain and Transconductance

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Voltage Gain ($A_v$) — Formal Definition

In the **KTU 2024 Scheme** syllabus for Electronic Circuits and Networks, the **voltage gain** of a two-port network or amplifier stage is formally defined as the ratio of the small-signal output voltage delivered to the load to the small-signal input voltage applied at the input terminals.

Mathematically:

$$A_v \triangleq \frac{v_{out}}{v_{in}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board-Standard Definition:** *Voltage gain is the dimensionless (or expressed in dB) ratio of the amplified output voltage swing to the applied input voltage swing under small-signal AC conditions, with the amplifier operating in its linear region.*

When expressed in **decibels (dB)**, the voltage gain is:

$$A_v\big|_{dB} = 20 \log_{10} \left\vert \frac{v_{out}}{v_{in}} \right\vert \;\; \text{dB}$$

## 1.2 Transconductance ($g_m$) — Formal Definition

**Transconductance** is a small-signal parameter that quantifies how effectively a device (BJT, MOSFET, or JFET) converts an input **voltage** variation into an output **current** variation. It is one of the four fundamental **hybrid two-port parameters** of transistor small-signal models.

$$g_m \triangleq \frac{\partial I_{out}}{\partial V_{in}}\bigg\vert_{Q-point} = \frac{i_{out}}{v_{in}} \;\; \text{(small-signal AC)}$$

The unit of transconductance is the **Siemens (S)** — the same as conductance. Hence the name "transfer conductance" or *transconductance*.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why is it called "trans"-conductance?**
> Because it is the conductance *transferring* a signal from the input port (where voltage is the controlling variable) to the output port (where current is the controlled variable). This is precisely why modern textbooks call the MOSFET a **VCCS — Voltage-Controlled Current Source** with gain equal to $g_m$.

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **water faucet (tap)**:

- The **handle rotation** (input voltage $v_{in}$) controls the faucet.
- The **water flow rate** (output current $i_{out}$) is what comes out.
- The sensitivity of flow rate to handle rotation = **transconductance** $g_m$. A faucet that pours a lot for a small twist has a *high* $g_m$.

Now add a **measuring bucket** at the faucet outlet:
- The **water collected** in a fixed time = output voltage (because the water hits a small turbine producing voltage).
- The ratio of *bucket voltage* to *handle rotation* = **voltage gain** $A_v$.

So if the faucet is super-sensitive ($g_m$ large) AND the turbine converts flow to voltage efficiently (large load $R_D$), then $A_v$ becomes very large.

In one line: **$g_m$ tells you how much current the transistor *generates* per volt of input; $A_v$ tells you how much voltage you get at the output, which equals $g_m \times$ (load resistance) in the simplest case.**

## 1.4 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> - **Thermal Voltage** $V_T = kT/q \approx \mathbf{25.85 \; mV}$ at room temperature (**T = 300 K**).
> - Boltzmann's constant $k = 1.380 \times 10^{-23}$ J/K.
> - Electron charge $q = 1.602 \times 10^{-19}$ C.
> - For a BJT biased at collector current $I_C$: $g_m = I_C / V_T$.
> - For a MOSFET biased at drain current $I_D$ with overdrive voltage $V_{OV}$: $g_m = 2 I_D / V_{OV} = \sqrt{2 \mu_n C_{ox} (W/L) I_D}$.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Why Voltage Gain and Transconductance Belong Together

In any **transistor amplifier**, the transistor acts as a **voltage-to-current converter** (with conversion factor $g_m$). The output voltage is then developed across a load impedance. Therefore, the **voltage gain is fundamentally the product of transconductance and the effective load seen at the output node.**

$$A_v = g_m \cdot R_{out,eff}$$

This single relationship is the **backbone** of every analog CMOS/BJT amplifier analysis and is the most-tested relationship in the KTU Module-1 portion of PCICT302.

## 2.2 The Three Fundamental Small-Signal Parameters of a MOSFET

For a MOSFET operating in **saturation**, the drain current in saturation is:

$$I_D = \frac{1}{2} \mu_n C_{ox} \frac{W}{L} (V_{GS} - V_{TH})^2 = \frac{1}{2} \mu_n C_{ox} \frac{W}{L} V_{OV}^2$$

where $V_{OV} = V_{GS} - V_{TH}$ is the **overdrive voltage**. The transconductance is computed as the partial derivative of $I_D$ with respect to $V_{GS}$:

$$g_m = \frac{\partial I_D}{\partial V_{GS}}\bigg\vert_{sat} = \mu_n C_{ox} \frac{W}{L} V_{OV} = \sqrt{2 \mu_n C_{ox} \frac{W}{L} I_D} = \frac{2 I_D}{V_{OV}}$$

## 2.3 BJT Transconductance

For a BJT in forward-active region, the collector current follows:

$$I_C = I_S \exp\!\left(\frac{V_{BE}}{V_T}\right)$$

Differentiating with respect to $V_{BE}$:

$$g_m = \frac{\partial I_C}{\partial V_{BE}} = \frac{I_C}{V_T}$$

## 2.4 Voltage Gain of Common-Source / Common-Emitter Stages

For a **common-source (CS) MOSFET amplifier** with drain resistor $R_D$:

$$A_v = -g_m R_D$$

For a **common-emitter (CE) BJT amplifier** with collector resistor $R_C$ (ignoring $r_o$):

$$A_v = -g_m R_C$$

The **negative sign** indicates a 180° phase inversion between input and output — a hallmark feature that KTU examiners love to test.

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| **#** | **Parameter / Formula** | **Expression** | **Condition / Unit** |
|:---:|:---|:---|:---|
| 1 | Voltage Gain (linear) | $A_v = v_{out}/v_{in}$ | dimensionless |
| 2 | Voltage Gain (dB) | $A_v\big|_{dB} = 20 \log_{10}\vert A_v \vert$ | dB |
| 3 | Transconductance — MOSFET (saturation) | $g_m = \mu_n C_{ox} (W/L)\, V_{OV}$ | Siemens (S) |
| 4 | Transconductance — MOSFET (alt form) | $g_m = 2 I_D / V_{OV}$ | S |
| 5 | Transconductance — MOSFET (alt form) | $g_m = \sqrt{2 \mu_n C_{ox} (W/L) I_D}$ | S |
| 6 | Transconductance — BJT | $g_m = I_C / V_T$ | S, with $V_T \approx 25.85$ mV |
| 7 | Thermal Voltage | $V_T = kT/q$ | $\approx 25.85$ mV @ 300 K |
| 8 | CS Voltage Gain | $A_v = -g_m R_D$ | assuming $r_o \to \infty$ |
| 9 | CS Voltage Gain (with $r_o$) | $A_v = -g_m (R_D \Vert r_o)$ | including channel-length modulation |
| 10 | CE Voltage Gain | $A_v = -g_m R_C$ | BJT CE stage |
| 11 | Source-degenerated CS gain | $A_v = -\dfrac{g_m R_D}{1 + g_m R_S}$ | with $R_S$ at source |
| 12 | Body-effect transconductance | $g_{mb} = (\eta)\, g_m$ | $\eta = \gamma/(2\sqrt{2\phi_F + V_{SB}})$ |
| 13 | Current Gain (related) | $A_i = i_{out}/i_{in}$ | dimensionless |
| 14 | Power Gain | $G = A_v \cdot A_i$ | dimensionless or dB |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical KTU Pitfall:** The transconductance $g_m$ is *not* a constant of the transistor — it depends entirely on the **bias point (Q-point)**. KTU questions routinely combine bias calculation with $g_m$ derivation in a single 14-mark problem.

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Operational Transconductance Amplifiers (OTAs)** explicitly use $g_m$ as the programmable gain element in filters and integrators.
- **RF Low-Noise Amplifiers (LNAs)** in mobile phones are designed by sizing $W/L$ to hit a target $g_m$ for noise/impedance matching.
- **Analog-to-Digital Converter (ADC) input stages** need precise $g_m$ for linear V-to-I conversion.
- **Voltage gain $A_v$** is the metric that determines whether an op-amp, headphone driver, or audio preamp produces the needed swing.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: MOSFET Transconductance $g_m$ in Saturation

**Starting equation** for drain current in saturation:

$$I_D = \frac{1}{2}\, \mu_n C_{ox}\, \frac{W}{L}\, (V_{GS} - V_{TH})^2$$

Define overdrive voltage:

$$V_{OV} = V_{GS} - V_{TH}$$

So:

$$I_D = \frac{1}{2}\, \mu_n C_{ox}\, \frac{W}{L}\, V_{OV}^2$$

Taking the derivative with respect to $V_{GS}$ (and noting that $V_{OV} = V_{GS} - V_{TH}$, so $\partial V_{OV}/\partial V_{GS} = 1$):

$$g_m = \frac{\partial I_D}{\partial V_{GS}} = \frac{1}{2}\, \mu_n C_{ox}\, \frac{W}{L}\, \cdot 2 V_{OV} \cdot \frac{\partial V_{OV}}{\partial V_{GS}}$$

Substituting:

$$g_m = \mu_n C_{ox}\, \frac{W}{L}\, V_{OV}$$

This is the **first key form** of $g_m$. To express it in terms of bias current $I_D$, square the equation above and rearrange:

$$g_m^2 = \left(\mu_n C_{ox}\, \frac{W}{L}\right)^2 V_{OV}^2 = \left(\mu_n C_{ox}\, \frac{W}{L}\right) \cdot \left(\mu_n C_{ox}\, \frac{W}{L}\, V_{OV}^2\right)$$

$$g_m^2 = \left(\mu_n C_{ox}\, \frac{W}{L}\right) \cdot \left(2 I_D\right)$$

Therefore:

$$g_m = \sqrt{2 \mu_n C_{ox}\, \frac{W}{L}\, I_D}$$

And dividing the first form by the saturation equation gives:

$$\frac{g_m}{I_D} = \frac{\mu_n C_{ox}\, (W/L)\, V_{OV}}{\frac{1}{2} \mu_n C_{ox}\, (W/L)\, V_{OV}^2} = \frac{2}{V_{OV}}$$

Hence:

$$g_m = \frac{2 I_D}{V_{OV}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> All three forms — $g_m = \mu_n C_{ox} (W/L) V_{OV}$, $g_m = 2 I_D / V_{OV}$, and $g_m = \sqrt{2 \mu_n C_{ox} (W/L) I_D}$ — are mathematically **equivalent** and routinely interchanged in KTU problems.

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Voltage Gain of Common-Source Amplifier

**Circuit Description:** A MOSFET with gate driven by $v_{in}$, source grounded, drain connected to $R_D$ which goes to $V_{DD}$, output taken at the drain.

**Small-signal model:** Replace MOSFET with its small-signal equivalent: $g_m v_{gs}$ current source from drain to source, gate is open-circuit (no gate current).

**Step 1 — Identify the controlling voltage:**

The gate-to-source voltage equals the input voltage (since source is grounded):

$$v_{gs} = v_{in}$$

**Step 2 — Write KCL at the drain node:**

The current from the dependent source $g_m v_{gs}$ flows from drain to source. By KCL at the drain, the same current must flow through $R_D$ from $V_{DD}$ to the drain. Therefore the small-signal drain voltage is:

$$v_{out} = -g_m v_{gs} \cdot R_D$$

The negative sign arises because the conventional positive current flowing *into* the drain node from $R_D$ produces a voltage drop that makes $v_d$ (the small-signal drain voltage) negative relative to its DC bias.

**Step 3 — Substitute $v_{gs} = v_{in}$:**

$$v_{out} = -g_m R_D \cdot v_{in}$$

**Step 4 — Form the voltage gain ratio:**

$$\boxed{A_v = \frac{v_{out}}{v_{in}} = -g_m R_D}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Result to Memorize:** $A_v = -g_m R_D$ for the ideal CS stage. If channel-length modulation is included (finite $r_o$), then $R_D$ is replaced by $R_D \Vert r_o$.

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Voltage Gain of Source-Degenerated CS Stage

A resistor $R_S$ is inserted between source and ground. Now the small-signal source is *not* at AC ground.

**Step 1 — Define $v_s$ as the small-signal source node voltage.**

The current through $R_S$ is $v_s / R_S$. This same current must equal the drain current $-g_m v_{gs}$ (with sign convention that current flows from drain to source). KVL around the input loop gives:

$$v_{gs} = v_{in} - v_s$$

**Step 2 — Apply KCL at the source node:**

The only current entering the source node from the transistor is $g_m v_{gs}$ flowing *out* of the source (into the transistor from source perspective). The current leaving through $R_S$ is $v_s/R_S$. Thus:

$$g_m v_{gs} = \frac{v_s}{R_S}$$

Substitute $v_{gs} = v_{in} - v_s$:

$$g_m (v_{in} - v_s) = \frac{v_s}{R_S}$$

$$g_m v_{in} - g_m v_s = \frac{v_s}{R_S}$$

$$g_m v_{in} = v_s\!\left(\frac{1}{R_S} + g_m\right)$$

$$v_s = \frac{g_m R_S}{1 + g_m R_S}\, v_{in}$$

**Step 3 — Compute $v_{out}$:**

$$v_{out} = -g_m v_{gs} \cdot R_D = -g_m (v_{in} - v_s) R_D$$

Substituting $v_s$:

$$v_{out} = -g_m R_D \left(v_{in} - \frac{g_m R_S}{1 + g_m R_S}\, v_{in}\right)$$

$$v_{out} = -g_m R_D \cdot v_{in}\!\left(\frac{1 + g_m R_S - g_m R_S}{1 + g_m R_S}\right)$$

$$v_{out} = -\frac{g_m R_D}{1 + g_m R_S}\, v_{in}$$

**Step 4 — Voltage gain:**

$$\boxed{A_v = -\frac{g_m R_D}{1 + g_m R_S}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> When $g_m R_S \gg 1$, the gain simplifies to $A_v \approx -R_D / R_S$ — *independent* of $g_m$. This is the principle behind **negative-feedback source degeneration** that stabilizes gain against bias, temperature, and process variations.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Worked Numerical Example

**Problem Statement (KTU-style):** A common-source NMOS amplifier has $\mu_n C_{ox} = 100 \,\mu\text{A/V}^2$, $W/L = 50/0.5$, overdrive $V_{OV} = 0.4$ V, and drain resistor $R_D = 5$ k$\Omega$. Compute $g_m$, the bias current $I_D$, and the small-signal voltage gain $A_v$.

**Solution:**

**Step 1 — Compute $g_m$:**

$$g_m = \mu_n C_{ox}\, \frac{W}{L}\, V_{OV} = (100 \times 10^{-6}) \times 100 \times 0.4$$

$$g_m = 100 \times 10^{-6} \times 40 = 4 \times 10^{-3} \;\; \text{S} = 4 \;\; \text{mA/V}$$

**Step 2 — Compute $I_D$:**

$$I_D = \frac{1}{2}\, \mu_n C_{ox}\, \frac{W}{L}\, V_{OV}^2 = \frac{1}{2} \times (100 \times 10^{-6}) \times 100 \times (0.4)^2$$

$$I_D = \frac{1}{2} \times 10 \times 10^{-3} \times 0.16 = 0.8 \times 10^{-3} \;\; \text{A} = 0.8 \;\; \text{mA}$$

**Verification using $g_m = 2 I_D / V_{OV}$:**

$$g_m = \frac{2 \times 0.8 \times 10^{-3}}{0.4} = 4 \times 10^{-3} \;\; \text{S} \;\checkmark$$

**Step 3 — Compute voltage gain:**

$$A_v = -g_m R_D = -(4 \times 10^{-3}) \times (5 \times 10^{3}) = -20 \;\; \text{V/V}$$

In dB:

$$A_v\big|_{dB} = 20 \log_{10}(20) \approx 26.02 \;\; \text{dB}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Result Interpretation:** A 1 mV input sinusoid at the gate produces a 20 mV sinusoid (180° inverted) at the drain. The negative sign is mandatory in KTU answers — examiners will deduct marks if the sign is dropped.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Small-Signal MOSFET Equivalent Model with $g_m$

The following Mermaid block represents the small-signal hybrid-pi model of a MOSFET used to derive voltage gain and transconductance relationships.

```mermaid
graph LR
    gateNode["GATE NODE"] -- open circuit --> drainNode["DRAIN NODE"]
    drainNode -- dependent current source g_m v_gs --> srcNode["SOURCE NODE"]
    gateNode -- v_gs --> ctrlArrow["CONTROL VOLTAGE"]
    ctrlArrow -. controls .-> drainNode
    srcNode -- ground --> gndSymbol["GROUND"]
    rdRes["R_D"] -- connects to VDD --> drainNode
```

**Interpretation:**
- The gate is drawn as an **open circuit** (no gate current in a MOSFET).
- A **voltage-controlled current source** $g_m v_{gs}$ flows between drain and source.
- The output voltage is developed across $R_D$ at the drain node.
- Source is tied to small-signal ground.

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture of Voltage Gain Realization

The following block diagram illustrates how an input voltage is amplified through the transconductance stage and load resistor to produce the output voltage — i.e., how $A_v = g_m \cdot R_L$ emerges physically.

```mermaid
graph TD
    inBlock["INPUT VOLTAGE v_in"] --> vccsBlock["TRANSCONDUCTANCE STAGE: VCCS with gain g_m"]
    vccsBlock -- generates current i_d = g_m v_gs --> loadBlock["LOAD IMPEDANCE R_D or R_L"]
    loadBlock -- converts current to voltage --> outBlock["OUTPUT VOLTAGE v_out = - g_m R_D v_in"]
    inBlock -. controls .-> vccsBlock
    biasBlock["DC BIAS Q-POINT: sets I_D, V_OV"] -. sets .-> vccsBlock
```

**Engineering Reading of the Block Diagram:**
- The **bias block** establishes the DC operating point, which sets $g_m$.
- The **input block** modulates the VCCS through $v_{gs}$.
- The **load block** converts the resulting AC current into an AC voltage proportional to $R_D$.
- The **output block** reflects the negative (inverting) voltage gain $-g_m R_D$.

## 4.3 Flowchart: Computing Voltage Gain Step-by-Step

```mermaid
graph TD
    stepA["STEP 1: Identify transistor type BJT or MOSFET"]
    stepB["STEP 2: Determine Q-point I_C or I_D"]
    stepC{"STEP 3: Compute g_m"}
    stepD["BJT: g_m = I_C / V_T"]
    stepE["MOSFET: g_m = 2 I_D / V_OV"]
    stepF["STEP 4: Identify load at output node R_D, R_C, or R_L"]
    stepG["STEP 5: Apply A_v = -g_m R_load"]
    stepH["STEP 6: Convert to dB if needed A_v dB = 20 log10 of magnitude"]

    stepA --> stepB --> stepC
    stepC -- if BJT --> stepD
    stepC -- if MOSFET --> stepE
    stepD --> stepF
    stepE --> stepF
    stepF --> stepG --> stepH
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam — May 2024]`

**Define the small-signal transconductance $g_m$ of a MOSFET in saturation. Mention its unit.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> The small-signal transconductance $g_m$ of a MOSFET in saturation is defined as the partial derivative of the drain current $I_D$ with respect to the gate-to-source voltage $V_{GS}$, evaluated at the DC bias (Q-point):
> $$g_m = \frac{\partial I_D}{\partial V_{GS}}\bigg\vert_{V_{DS,const}}$$
> For a MOSFET biased in saturation with overdrive voltage $V_{OV}$, $g_m = \mu_n C_{ox} (W/L) V_{OV} = 2 I_D / V_{OV}$.
> **Unit:** Siemens (S) or equivalently A/V. **(1 mark)** Definition, **(1 mark)** formula, **(1 mark)** unit.

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`

**What is the voltage gain of a basic common-source MOSFET amplifier? Explain the significance of the negative sign.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> The voltage gain of an ideal common-source amplifier with drain load $R_D$ is:
> $$A_v = -g_m R_D$$
> The negative sign indicates a **180° phase reversal** between the input voltage at the gate and the output voltage at the drain — when $v_{in}$ increases, $v_{out}$ decreases, and vice versa. **(2 marks)** expression, **(1 mark)** phase interpretation.

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam — Jul 2024]`

**(a)** For an NMOS transistor in saturation, derive the expression for the small-signal transconductance $g_m$ in terms of (i) overdrive voltage $V_{OV}$ and (ii) drain current $I_D$. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A common-source amplifier uses this NMOS with $\mu_n C_{ox} = 200 \,\mu\text{A/V}^2$, $W = 50 \,\mu\text{m}$, $L = 1 \,\mu\text{m}$, $V_{OV} = 0.5$ V, and $R_D = 2$ k$\Omega$. Compute the voltage gain in V/V and in dB. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**(a)** The drain current in saturation is:

$$I_D = \frac{1}{2}\, \mu_n C_{ox}\, \frac{W}{L}\, (V_{GS} - V_{TH})^2 = \frac{1}{2}\, \mu_n C_{ox}\, \frac{W}{L}\, V_{OV}^2$$

Differentiating with respect to $V_{GS}$:

$$g_m = \frac{\partial I_D}{\partial V_{GS}} = \mu_n C_{ox}\, \frac{W}{L}\, V_{OV} \quad \text{...(i) form}$$

Dividing by $I_D$ expression:

$$\frac{g_m}{I_D} = \frac{\mu_n C_{ox} (W/L) V_{OV}}{\frac{1}{2} \mu_n C_{ox} (W/L) V_{OV}^2} = \frac{2}{V_{OV}}$$

Therefore:

$$g_m = \frac{2 I_D}{V_{OV}} \quad \text{...(ii) form}$$

**(b)** Given $\mu_n C_{ox} = 200 \times 10^{-6}$ A/V², $W/L = 50$, $V_{OV} = 0.5$ V.

**Step 1 — Compute $g_m$:**

$$g_m = \mu_n C_{ox}\, \frac{W}{L}\, V_{OV} = (200 \times 10^{-6}) \times 50 \times 0.5$$

$$g_m = 5 \times 10^{-3} \;\; \text{S} = 5 \;\; \text{mA/V}$$

**Step 2 — Compute $A_v$:**

$$A_v = -g_m R_D = -(5 \times 10^{-3}) \times (2 \times 10^{3}) = -10 \;\; \text{V/V}$$

**Step 3 — Convert to dB:**

$$A_v\big|_{dB} = 20 \log_{10}\vert -10 \vert = 20 \times 1 = 20 \;\; \text{dB}$$

> **Final Answer:** $g_m = 5$ mA/V, $A_v = -10$ V/V $= 20$ dB.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`

**(a)** Derive the voltage gain of a common-source MOSFET amplifier with source-degeneration resistor $R_S$. Show that for large $g_m R_S$, the gain becomes independent of $g_m$. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** For the same amplifier with $g_m = 4$ mA/V, $R_D = 4$ k$\Omega$, and $R_S = 500\,\Omega$, compute the voltage gain with and without source degeneration. Comment on the trade-off. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**(a)** With $R_S$ between source and ground, KCL at the source node gives $g_m v_{gs} = v_s/R_S$. With $v_{gs} = v_{in} - v_s$:

$$g_m (v_{in} - v_s) = \frac{v_s}{R_S} \;\Rightarrow\; v_s = \frac{g_m R_S}{1 + g_m R_S}\, v_{in}$$

Output voltage:

$$v_{out} = -g_m v_{gs} R_D = -g_m (v_{in} - v_s) R_D = -\frac{g_m R_D}{1 + g_m R_S}\, v_{in}$$

Hence:

$$A_v = -\frac{g_m R_D}{1 + g_m R_S}$$

When $g_m R_S \gg 1$:

$$A_v \approx -\frac{g_m R_D}{g_m R_S} = -\frac{R_D}{R_S}$$

which is **independent of $g_m$** — providing gain stability against bias, temperature, and process variations.

**(b) Case 1 — Without $R_S$ ($R_S = 0$):**

$$A_{v,no\,deg} = -g_m R_D = -(4 \times 10^{-3}) \times (4 \times 10^{3}) = -16 \;\; \text{V/V}$$

**Case 2 — With $R_S = 500\,\Omega$:**

$$g_m R_S = (4 \times 10^{-3}) \times 500 = 2$$

$$A_{v,deg} = -\frac{g_m R_D}{1 + g_m R_S} = -\frac{16}{1 + 2} = -\frac{16}{3} \approx -5.33 \;\; \text{V/V}$$

**Trade-off Comment:** Source degeneration reduces the magnitude of voltage gain (from 16 to ~5.33), but **increases linearity**, widens bandwidth, and stabilizes the gain against variations in $g_m$ due to temperature or process. This is the classical **gain-versus-linearity trade-off** in analog design.

> **Final Answer:** $A_{v,\text{no deg}} = -16$ V/V; $A_{v,\text{deg}} = -5.33$ V/V; trade-off is linearity/stability vs. raw gain.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Exams:**
> 1. **Forgetting the negative sign** in $A_v = -g_m R_D$ — at least 1 mark lost per occurrence.
> 2. **Using $V_T = 25.85$ mV vs 26 mV inconsistently** — KTU officially allows both, but stick to one in a single answer.
> 3. **Confusing $g_m$ (transconductance) with $r_o$ (output resistance)** — they are *independent* small-signal parameters. $g_m$ relates input voltage to output current; $r_o$ relates output voltage to output current.
> 4. **Wrong units** — $g_m$ is in **S (or A/V)**, never in Ohms. Voltage gain is dimensionless or in dB.
> 5. **Neglecting body effect** when source is not at AC ground — examiners love this trap.
> 6. **Not converting between forms** of $g_m$ — KTU may provide $W/L$ and $V_{OV}$ but ask for $g_m$ in terms of $I_D$; you must know all three forms.

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Voltage Gain** $A_v = v_{out}/v_{in}$ is the dimensionless (or dB) ratio quantifying how much an amplifier magnifies an input voltage swing.
- **Transconductance** $g_m$ is the small-signal VCCS gain of the transistor, measured in **Siemens (A/V)**.
- **MOSFET $g_m$ forms (all equivalent in saturation):**
  - $g_m = \mu_n C_{ox} (W/L) V_{OV}$
  - $g_m = 2 I_D / V_{OV}$
  - $g_m = \sqrt{2 \mu_n C_{ox} (W/L) I_D}$
- **BJT $g_m$:** $g_m = I_C / V_T$ with $V_T \approx 25.85$ mV at 300 K.
- **Common-Source Voltage Gain:** $A_v = -g_m R_D$ (with $R_D \Vert r_o$ if $r_o$ finite).
- **Source-Degenerated CS Gain:** $A_v = -g_m R_D / (1 + g_m R_S)$; approximates to $-R_D/R_S$ for strong degeneration.
- **dB Conversion:** $A_v \vert_{dB} = 20 \log_{10} \vert A_v \vert$.
- **Sign Convention:** Negative sign indicates 180° phase inversion (CS/CE stages are inverting).
- **Body-Effect Transconductance:** $g_{mb} = \eta \, g_m$ appears when source is not at AC ground.
- **Bias Dependence:** $g_m$ is **not** a device constant — it scales with $\sqrt{I_D}$ (MOSFET) or linearly with $I_C$ (BJT).
- **Real-World Use:** OTAs, LNAs, ADC front-ends, op-amp input stages — all sized by $g_m$ and gain-targeted through $A_v$.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Multi Stage Amplifier, Differential Amplifier - Basic MOSFET Differential Pair Operation, Feedback Amplifiers: Effect of Negative Feedback on Bandwidth
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCICT302 - ELECTRONIC CIRCUITS AND NETWORKS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Multi Stage Amplifier, Differential Amplifier - Basic MOSFET Differential Pair Operation, Feedback Amplifiers: Effect of Negative Feedback on Bandwidth

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Multi-Stage Amplifier

### Formal Definition
A **multi-stage amplifier** is a cascaded arrangement of two or more single-stage amplifiers connected in series (cascade), where the output of one stage serves as the input to the next. The overall voltage gain is the product of the individual stage gains, while impedance matching, bandwidth limitation, and inter-stage loading effects determine the system-level performance.

### Intuitive Analogy
Think of a multi-stage amplifier like a relay race. Each runner (stage) takes the signal baton, amplifies it a little more, and passes it forward. Just as the slowest runner limits the team's overall speed, the stage with the **narrowest bandwidth** limits the overall frequency response. To get a strong final signal, you need each stage to be properly matched — just like runners need compatible stride lengths to hand off the baton smoothly.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** In multi-stage amplifiers, students must master **gain computation in dB**, the **cascade rule** (multiplication of linear gains / addition in dB), and the concept of the **dominant pole** that limits overall bandwidth.

### Key Methods of Coupling Stages
- **Direct Coupling (DC Coupling):** Stage outputs directly connected to inputs — excellent low-frequency response but suffers from **DC offset drift**.
- **RC Coupling (Capacitive Coupling):** Capacitors block DC between stages — excellent AC response, low cost, but **loses DC information**.
- **Transformer Coupling:** Provides impedance matching and DC isolation — bulky, expensive, used in RF power stages.

---

## 1.2 MOSFET Differential Pair — Basic Operation

### Formal Definition
A **MOSFET differential pair** is a symmetric two-transistor circuit consisting of two matched enhancement-mode MOSFETs (typically n-channel) whose sources are tied together and biased by a constant current source (tail current source $I_{SS}$). It amplifies the **difference** between two input signals while rejecting signals common to both inputs, making it the fundamental building block of operational amplifiers and analog integrated circuits.

### Intuitive Analogy
Imagine a balance scale. Two pans hold the two input voltages. When both pans are pushed down equally (common-mode signal), the scale stays level — no output swing. But when one pan is pushed harder than the other (differential signal), the beam tilts — producing a strong output. The **tail current** is like the fulcrum's spring: the harder you push one side, the more current it diverts from the other, creating the differential action.

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Insight:** The MOSFET differential pair is the **heart of every op-amp, comparator, and analog multiplier**. KTU examiners frequently test the small-signal differential gain $A_d = g_m R_D$ and the role of the tail-current source in setting common-mode rejection.

### Key Operating Modes
- **Common-Mode Operation:** $v_{id} = 0$, both inputs equal → tail current splits equally (each transistor carries $I_{SS}/2$). Ideally, the output is **zero**.
- **Differential-Mode Operation:** $v_{id} \neq 0$, inputs differ → tail current steers from one side to the other.
- **Large-Signal (Steering) Operation:** When $\vert v_{id} \vert > \sqrt{2} V_{OV}$, all tail current flows through one transistor — the pair acts as a **current steering switch**.

---

## 1.3 Negative Feedback — Effect on Bandwidth

### Formal Definition
**Negative feedback** is a technique where a portion of the amplifier's output signal is returned to the input in **opposing phase** (180° out of phase with the input), reducing the closed-loop gain but simultaneously improving bandwidth, linearity, input/output impedance, and reducing distortion and noise sensitivity.

### Intuitive Analogy
Picture a shower with a single temperature dial. Without feedback, the water temperature is wild and unpredictable. Add a **thermostat** that senses the actual temperature and adjusts the hot/cold valves — the system becomes "self-correcting." Negative feedback does exactly this: it **senses** the output, compares it with the desired input, and **corrects** the error automatically. The trade-off? Slower response (less aggressive change), but much more stable and accurate output.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Key Takeaway:** Negative feedback **trades gain for bandwidth**. The product of gain and bandwidth (the **Gain-Bandwidth Product, GBP**) remains approximately **constant** for a single-pole amplifier. This is the most tested concept in the feedback portion of Module 2.

### The Four Topologies of Negative Feedback
| Topology | Sampling | Mixing | Ideal Use Case |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Voltage-Series** | Voltage | Series | Voltage Amplifier |
| **Voltage-Shunt** | Voltage | Shunt | Transimpedance (Current-to-Voltage) |
| **Current-Series** | Current | Series | Transconductance (Voltage-to-Current) |
| **Current-Shunt** | Current | Shunt | Current Amplifier |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Multi-Stage Amplifier — Deep Theory

### 2.1.1 Cascade Rule
When stages are cascaded, and assuming proper inter-stage matching, the overall gain in linear scale is the **product** of individual stage gains, and in **decibels (dB)** it is the **sum** of individual stage gains in dB.

> **Why?** Voltage gain is a multiplicative quantity because each stage's output voltage becomes the next stage's input voltage. Converting to logarithmic dB units transforms multiplication into addition, simplifying analysis of long amplifier chains.

### 2.1.2 Effect of Loading
A critical subtlety: when calculating the gain of an $n$-stage amplifier, you **must include the loading effect** of the next stage on the previous one. The output resistance of stage $k$ forms a voltage divider with the input resistance of stage $(k+1)$.

### 2.1.3 Bandwidth Considerations
- Each RC-coupled stage introduces a **low-frequency pole** and a **high-frequency pole**.
- The overall bandwidth is **narrower** than the bandwidth of any individual stage.
- The **dominant pole** (the lowest high-frequency pole or highest low-frequency pole) determines the overall -3dB cutoff.
- The **Miller Effect** dramatically reduces the input impedance of an inverting amplifier at high frequencies because of multiplication of $C_{gd}$ by the gain factor.

## 2.2 MOSFET Differential Pair — Deep Theory

### 2.2.1 DC Bias Point
With both inputs grounded, by symmetry each transistor carries $I_D = I_{SS}/2$. The overdrive voltage is:
$$V_{OV} = \sqrt{\frac{I_{SS}}{\mu_n C_{ox} \frac{W}{L}}}$$
This sets the bias point of each transistor in saturation.

### 2.2.2 Common-Mode and Differential Signals
Any pair of input signals can be decomposed:
- **Differential component:** $v_{id} = v_{G1} - v_{G2}$
- **Common-mode component:** $v_{ic} = \dfrac{v_{G1} + v_{G2}}{2}$

So $v_{G1} = v_{ic} + v_{id}/2$ and $v_{G2} = v_{ic} - v_{id}/2$.

> **Why?** Because the differential pair is fundamentally a **linear two-input system** when properly biased. By the principle of superposition, any input pair can be expressed as a common-mode input plus a differential-mode input.

### 2.2.3 Half-Circuit Analysis (Small-Signal)
For purely differential signals with an ideal tail current source (infinite output resistance), the source node is a **virtual ground** for small-signal differential operation. This allows the circuit to be split into two identical **half-circuits**, each being a common-source amplifier with source grounded.

### 2.2.4 Common-Mode Rejection Ratio (CMRR)
The key performance metric:
$$\text{CMRR} = 20 \log_{10}\left(\left\vert \frac{A_d}{A_c} \right\vert\right) \text{ in dB}$$
For a real tail current source of finite output resistance $R_{SS}$, common-mode gain is non-zero, limiting the CMRR.

## 2.3 Negative Feedback — Effect on Bandwidth

### 2.3.1 Closed-Loop Gain
$$A_f = \frac{A}{1 + A\beta}$$
where $A$ is the open-loop gain, $\beta$ is the feedback factor, and $(1 + A\beta)$ is the **loop gain** or **desensitivity factor** $D$.

### 2.3.2 Gain Reduction
- For $A\beta \gg 1$: $A_f \approx 1/\beta$ — gain is set by the **passive feedback network** (highly stable).
- Sensitivity to $A$ variation is reduced by factor $D = 1 + A\beta$.

### 2.3.3 Bandwidth Extension
For a single-pole amplifier with open-loop upper cutoff $f_H$:
- Open-loop gain: $A(f) = \dfrac{A_0}{1 + j(f/f_H)}$
- Closed-loop bandwidth: $f_{H,f} = f_H \cdot (1 + A_0 \beta)$
- **Gain-Bandwidth Product** is constant: $A_0 \cdot f_H = A_f \cdot f_{H,f}$

### 2.3.4 Generalized Bandwidth Improvement
The **desensitivity factor** $D = 1 + A\beta$ multiplies the bandwidth (upper cutoff) AND divides the lower cutoff frequency:
$$f_{H,f} = D \cdot f_H \quad,\quad f_{L,f} = \frac{f_L}{D}$$
This is sometimes called the **bandwidth extension theorem**.

### 2.3.5 Impedance Modifications
| Topology | $R_{if}$ (Input) | $R_{of}$ (Output) |
|---|---|---|
| Voltage-Series | $R_i (1 + A\beta)$ | $R_o / (1 + A\beta)$ |
| Voltage-Shunt | $R_i / (1 + A\beta)$ | $R_o / (1 + A\beta)$ |
| Current-Series | $R_i (1 + A\beta)$ | $R_o (1 + A\beta)$ |
| Current-Shunt | $R_i / (1 + A\beta)$ | $R_o (1 + A\beta)$ |

> **How to remember?** Think of **"Bootstrap" effect**:
> - **Series mixing** → input impedance **increases** (signal is "bootstrapped" — feedback opposes the input current).
> - **Shunt mixing** → input impedance **decreases** (feedback current directly subtracts from input).
> - **Voltage sampling** → output impedance **decreases** (ideal voltage source: $R_o \to 0$).
> - **Current sampling** → output impedance **increases** (ideal current source: $R_o \to \infty$).

---

## KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula | Condition / Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Cascade Gain (linear) | $A_v = A_{v1} \cdot A_{v2} \cdot \ldots \cdot A_{vn}$ | Dimensionless |
| 2 | Cascade Gain (dB) | $A_v(\text{dB}) = A_{v1}(\text{dB}) + A_{v2}(\text{dB}) + \ldots$ | dB |
| 3 | Miller Capacitance | $C_M = C_{gd}(1 + \vert A_v \vert)$ | Farads |
| 4 | MOSFET Transconductance | $g_m = \sqrt{2 \mu_n C_{ox} \frac{W}{L} I_D}$ | Siemens (A/V) |
| 5 | Differential Pair $V_{OV}$ | $V_{OV} = \sqrt{I_{SS}/(\mu_n C_{ox} W/L)}$ | Volts |
| 6 | Differential Gain (Half-Ckt) | $A_d = g_m R_D$ | V/V |
| 7 | Common-Mode Gain | $A_c = -R_D / (2 R_{SS})$ | V/V |
| 8 | CMRR | $\text{CMRR} = 20 \log_{10}\vert A_d/A_c \vert$ | dB |
| 9 | Closed-Loop Gain | $A_f = A / (1 + A\beta)$ | V/V |
| 10 | Desensitivity Factor | $D = 1 + A\beta$ | Dimensionless |
| 11 | Upper Cutoff Extension | $f_{H,f} = f_H \cdot (1 + A\beta)$ | Hz |
| 12 | Lower Cutoff Reduction | $f_{L,f} = f_L / (1 + A\beta)$ | Hz |
| 13 | Gain-Bandwidth Product | $\text{GBP} = A \cdot f_H = \text{constant}$ | Hz (for single-pole) |
| 14 | Voltage-Series $R_{if}$ | $R_i (1 + A\beta)$ | Ohms |
| 15 | Voltage-Series $R_{of}$ | $R_o / (1 + A\beta)$ | Ohms |

> **Note:** All vertical bars in absolute values are written as `\vert` to preserve the markdown table.

---

## Real-World Engineering Utility
- **Multi-stage amplifiers** are the backbone of audio amplifiers, RF front-ends, and instrumentation systems where a single stage cannot simultaneously provide the required gain, bandwidth, and impedance levels.
- **Differential pairs** are the universal input stage of every op-amp (e.g., µA741, LM358, AD797). They provide **noise immunity** in noisy industrial environments because interference (typically common-mode) is rejected.
- **Negative feedback** is what makes the modern op-amp possible — a non-ideal open-loop amplifier with $\pm$50% gain variation is converted into a precise closed-loop amplifier with **better than 0.01%** gain accuracy using $1\%$ resistors.
