(positive because $V_P$ is negative for an N-channel JFET)

**Step 4 —** Final compact form:

$$\boxed{g_m = g_{m0}\left(1 - \frac{V_{GS}}{V_P}\right)}$$

## 3.5 The Master Transconductance Triangle (BJT)

The three BJT small-signal parameters are **intimately coupled** through one universal relation:

$$g_m \cdot r_\pi = \beta \quad \text{and} \quad g_m = \frac{1}{r_e}$$

| Parameter | Symbol | Typical Value ($I_C = 1$ mA) | Unit |
|-----------|--------|------------------------------|------|
| Transconductance | $g_m$ | $1/0.026 = 38.5$ mS | S |
| Base-emitter resistance | $r_\pi$ | $100 \times 26 = 2600$ $\Omega$ | $\Omega$ |
| Emitter resistance | $r_e$ | $26$ $\Omega$ | $\Omega$ |
| Current gain | $\beta$ | 100 | — |

> [!TIP]
> **VERIFICATION:** $g_m \cdot r_\pi = 0.0385 \times 2600 = 100 = \beta$ ✓
> **VERIFICATION:** $g_m \cdot r_e = 0.0385 \times 26 \approx 1$ ✓ (since $\alpha \approx 1$)

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Conceptual Block Diagram: Transconductance as a "Voltage-to-Current Converter"

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph IN[INPUT DOMAIN - VOLTAGE]
        VGS["Input Control Voltage VGS or VBE"]
    end

    subgraph DEVICE[ACTIVE DEVICE - NONLINEAR]
        QPOINT["DC Bias Point Q Point"]
        NLCHAR["Nonlinear I-V Characteristic"]
    end

    subgraph OUT[OUTPUT DOMAIN - CURRENT]
        ID["Output Current ID or IC"]
    end

    GM["Transconductance gm = dIout divided by dVin"]

    VGS -->|Small AC Signal| NLCHAR
    QPOINT -->|Sets Operating Region| NLCHAR
    NLCHAR -->|Local Slope at Q| GM
    GM -->|Produces| ID

    style IN fill:#e1f5ff
    style DEVICE fill:#fff4e1
    style OUT fill:#e8f5e9
    style GM fill:#ffebee
```

## 4.2 Hybrid-π Small-Signal Model of BJT (Showing $g_m$ Visually)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph B[BASE NODE B]
        B1["B"]
    end
    subgraph E[EMITTER NODE E]
        E1["E"]
    end
    subgraph C[COLLECTOR NODE C]
        C1["C"]
    end

    B1 ---|r_pi| E1
    B1 ---|r_mu or r_o| C1
    C1 ---|Controlled Current Source g_m times v_be| E1
    E1 ---|r_o Output Resistance| C1

    VBE["Small Signal Input v_be between B and E"] --> B1

    style B fill:#ffebee
    style E fill:#e8f5e9
    style C fill:#e1f5ff
    style GM[gm v_be Current Source] fill:#fff9c4
```

## 4.3 Functional Flow: How Transconductance Enables Amplification

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Step 1 DC Bias Sets Q Point ICQ and VBEQ] --> B[Step 2 Apply Small AC Input v_be on top of VBEQ]
    B --> C[Step 3 Collector Current Develops AC Component i_c]
    C --> D["Step 4 i_c equals gm times v_be Small Signal Output"]
    D --> E[Step 5 Output Rout Converts i_c to v_out for Load]
    E --> F[Step 6 Voltage Gain A_v equals minus gm times Rout divided by 1 plus gm times Rsig]

    style A fill:#e3f2fd
    style B fill:#e3f2fd
    style C fill:#fff3e0
    style D fill:#fff3e0
    style E fill:#e8f5e9
    style F fill:#fce4ec
```

## 4.4 Transconductance Comparison: BJT vs MOSFET vs JFET

```mermaid
graph TD
    subgraph BJT[BJT Active Region]
        B1[Exponential I-V: I_C = I_S exp VBE/VT]
        B2["gm = IC divided by VT depends on BIAS CURRENT"]
    end

    subgraph MOS[MOSFET Saturation Region]
        M1[Square Law I-V: I_D = K V_OV squared]
        M2["gm = 2 I_D divided by V_OV depends on BIAS VOLTAGE"]
    end

    subgraph JFET[JFET Pinch-off Region]
        J1[Square Law I-V: I_D = I_DSS times 1 minus VGS/VP squared]
        J2["gm0 = 2 I_DSS divided by magnitude of VP maximum value"]
    end

    BJT -->|gm Magnitude| HIGH["BJT gm is 10 to 100 times HIGHER for same bias"]
    MOS -->|gm Magnitude| LOW1["MOSFET gm is LOWER but zero gate current"]
    JFET -->|gm Magnitude| LOW2["JFET gm is LOWEST but high input impedance"]

    style BJT fill:#ffebee
    style MOS fill:#e8f5e9
    style JFET fill:#e3f2fd
    style HIGH fill:#fff9c4
    style LOW1 fill:#fff9c4
    style LOW2 fill:#fff9c4
```

## 4.5 OTA (Operational Transconductance Amplifier) Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    INP["Differential Input V_plus minus V_minus"] --> GMSTAGE["Transconductance Stage gm"]
    IABC["External Bias Current I_ABC"] -->|Tune| GMSTAGE
    GMSTAGE -->|Output Current I_out = gm times V_diff| HICUR["High Impedance Current Output Node"]
    HICUR --> LOAD["External Load Resistor or Capacitor"]
    LOAD --> VOUT["Converted to Voltage Output V_out"]

    style INP fill:#e1f5ff
    style GMSTAGE fill:#ffebee
    style IABC fill:#fff9c4
    style HICUR fill:#e8f5e9
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **OTA DESIGN RULE:** In an OTA, $g_m$ is **electronically tunable** by the external bias current $I_{ABC}$ according to $g_m = \dfrac{I_{ABC}}{2 V_T}$. This is the foundation of **programmable analog circuits** used in modern IC design.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

### **Question 1 [KTU University Exam - May 2024]**
**Define transconductance. State its SI unit. Mention the formula relating $g_m$ to collector current for a BJT.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks — Awarded as: 1+1+1):**

> **Definition (1 mark):** Transconductance $g_m$ of an active device is defined as the ratio of the small-signal change in the output (drain/collector) current to the small-signal change in the input (gate/base) control voltage, with the output voltage held constant.
>
> **SI Unit (1 mark):** Siemens (S), also called **mho (℧)** or Ampere/Volt (A/V).
>
> **BJT Formula (1 mark):** $\boxed{g_m = \dfrac{I_C}{V_T}}$ where $V_T \approx 26$ mV is the thermal voltage at room temperature (300 K).

---

### **Question 2 [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**
**Differentiate between transconductance of a BJT and a MOSFET. State the formula for each in terms of the bias parameters.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks — Awarded as: 1.5 + 1.5):**

> **BJT Transconductance (1.5 marks):** Depends on **collector bias current** $I_C$:
> $$g_m = \frac{I_C}{V_T} = \frac{I_C}{26 \text{ mV}}$$
> Increasing $I_C$ increases $g_m$ linearly.
>
> **MOSFET Transconductance (1.5 marks):** Depends on **drain bias current** $I_D$ **and overdrive voltage** $V_{OV}$:
> $$g_m = \frac{2 I_D}{V_{OV}} = 2K(V_{GS} - V_{th})$$
> Increasing $V_{OV}$ (or equivalently $I_D$) increases $g_m$ linearly.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

---

### **OPTION A — Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - Dec 2024]**

**Derive the expression for transconductance $g_m$ of a BJT in terms of (a) collector current $I_C$ and thermal voltage $V_T$, and (b) base-emitter resistance $r_\pi$ and current gain $\beta$. For a BJT with $I_C = 2$ mA, $V_T = 26$ mV, and $\beta = 120$, calculate $g_m$, $r_\pi$, and $r_e$.**

#### **Solution:**

**Part (a) — 7 Marks: Derivation of $g_m = I_C / V_T$**

The Ebers-Moll collector current equation in forward active mode is:

$$I_C = I_S \, e^{V_{BE}/V_T}$$

Differentiating with respect to $V_{BE}$ (the input control voltage) with $V_{CE}$ held constant:

$$g_m = \frac{dI_C}{dV_{BE}} = I_S \cdot \frac{1}{V_T} \cdot e^{V_{BE}/V_T} = \frac{1}{V_T} \cdot I_S e^{V_{BE}/V_T} = \frac{I_C}{V_T}$$

$$\boxed{g_m = \frac{I_C}{V_T}}$$

**Part (b) — 7 Marks: Numerical Calculation**

Given: $I_C = 2$ mA $= 2 \times 10^{-3}$ A, $V_T = 26 \times 10^{-3}$ V, $\beta = 120$

**(i) Calculation of $g_m$:**

$$g_m = \frac{I_C}{V_T} = \frac{2 \times 10^{-3}}{26 \times 10^{-3}} = \frac{2}{26} \approx 0.0769 \text{ S} = 76.92 \text{ mS}$$

**(ii) Calculation of $r_\pi$:**

Using $g_m \cdot r_\pi = \beta$:

$$r_\pi = \frac{\beta}{g_m} = \frac{120}{0.0769} \approx 1560 \, \Omega = 1.56 \text{ k}\Omega$$

**(iii) Calculation of $r_e$:**

Using $g_m = \dfrac{1}{r_e}$:

$$r_e = \frac{1}{g_m} = \frac{1}{0.0769} \approx 13 \, \Omega$$

**Verification:** $g_m \cdot r_\pi = 0.0769 \times 1560 = 120 = \beta$ ✓

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU EXAMINER'S VALUATION PITFALLS:**
> 1. **Unit Errors:** Students frequently forget to convert mA to A and mV to V, producing answers off by a factor of 1000. Always convert to SI base units first.
> 2. **Confusion between $r_e$ and $r_\pi$:** $r_e$ is the **intrinsic emitter resistance** (~13–26 Ω for typical bias), while $r_\pi$ is the **base-emitter input resistance** (~kΩ range). They differ by a factor of $\beta$.
> 3. **Forgetting the "Constant $V_{CE}$" condition:** Transconductance is defined with $V_{CE}$ (or $V_{DS}$) held constant. Markers deduct 1 mark if the partial derivative notation is missing.
> 4. **Wrong $V_T$ value:** KTU allows 25 mV or 26 mV at room temperature. Using both is acceptable, but be consistent throughout the paper.

---

### **OPTION B — Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - May 2024]**

**For an N-channel MOSFET operating in saturation with drain current $I_D = K(V_{GS} - V_{th})^2$, derive the expression for transconductance $g_m$. A MOSFET has parameters $K = 0.5$ mA/V² and $V_{th} = 1.5$ V. It is biased at $V_{GS} = 3.5$ V. Find (a) the quiescent drain current $I_{DQ}$, (b) the transconductance $g_m$ at the Q-point, and (c) the new $g_m$ if the device is replaced by another MOSFET with $K = 1$ mA/V² at the same $V_{GS}$ and $V_{th}$.**

#### **Solution:**

**Derivation — 4 Marks:**

Starting from $I_D = K(V_{GS} - V_{th})^2$:

$$g_m = \frac{dI_D}{dV_{GS}} = 2K(V_{GS} - V_{th})$$

**Part (a) — 3 Marks: Quiescent Drain Current**

$$I_{DQ} = K(V_{GS} - V_{th})^2 = (0.5 \text{ mA/V}^2)(3.5 - 1.5)^2 = (0.5)(4) = 2 \text{ mA}$$

**Part (b) — 4 Marks: Transconductance at Q-point**

$$g_m = 2K(V_{GS} - V_{th}) = 2 \times 0.5 \text{ mA/V}^2 \times 2 \text{ V} = 2 \text{ mS}$$

**Verification using alternative form:**

$$g_m = \frac{2 I_{DQ}}{V_{OV}} = \frac{2 \times 2 \text{ mA}}{2 \text{ V}} = 2 \text{ mS} \checkmark$$

**Part (c) — 3 Marks: New $g_m$ with $K = 1$ mA/V²**

With $V_{GS}$ and $V_{th}$ unchanged, the overdrive voltage is the same:

$$V_{OV} = V_{GS} - V_{th} = 3.5 - 1.5 = 2 \text{ V}$$

$$g_m^{new} = 2K^{new}(V_{GS} - V_{th}) = 2 \times 1 \text{ mA/V}^2 \times 2 \text{ V} = 4 \text{ mS}$$

**Note:** Doubling $K$ (e.g., by doubling the $W/L$ ratio) **doubles the transconductance** at the same $V_{OV}$. This is a key design lever in CMOS analog design.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU EXAMINER'S VALUATION PITFALLS:**
> 1. **Saturation Assumption:** The formula $I_D = K(V_{GS}-V_{th})^2$ only holds in **saturation**. If the problem states the device is in triode, the derivation is different (markers deduct 2 marks for ignoring this).
> 2. **Units of K:** $K$ has units of **A/V²** or mA/V². Students often forget the squared and end up with incorrect units for $g_m$.
> 3. **Confusion between $g_m$ and $K$:** They are **not the same**. $K$ is a *device parameter* (depends on $W/L$, $\mu_n C_{ox}$), while $g_m$ is the *operating-point slope* (depends on K *and* $V_{OV}$).
> 4. **$V_{th}$ vs $V_T$:** $V_{th}$ is the MOSFET **threshold voltage**, NOT to be confused with the BJT **thermal voltage** $V_T$. These are completely different quantities — mixing them up is a common KTU error.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **HIGH-DENSITY RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST**

### 🔑 Must-Memorize Formulas
- [x] **BJT:** $g_m = \dfrac{I_C}{V_T}$ where $V_T \approx 26$ mV at 300 K
- [x] **BJT:** $g_m = \dfrac{\beta}{r_\pi} = \dfrac{1}{r_e} = \dfrac{\alpha}{r_e}$
- [x] **MOSFET:** $g_m = 2K(V_{GS} - V_{th}) = \dfrac{2 I_D}{V_{OV}} = 2\sqrt{K I_D}$
- [x] **JFET:** $g_m = g_{m0}\left(1 - \dfrac{V_{GS}}{V_P}\right)$ with $g_{m0} = \dfrac{2 I_{DSS}}{\vert V_P \vert}$

### 🔑 Conceptual Anchors
- [x] **Definition:** $\Delta I_{out} / \Delta V_{in}$ at constant $V_{out}$ (a partial derivative)
- [x] **Unit:** Siemens (S) = mho (℧) = A/V
- [x] **Physical meaning:** Slope of the I-V transfer curve at the Q-point
- [x] **BJT vs MOSFET:** BJT $g_m$ depends on *current*; MOSFET $g_m$ depends on *overdrive voltage*

### 🔑 Key Numerical Values
- [x] $V_T = 26$ mV (BJT thermal voltage at 300 K) — sometimes taken as 25 mV
- [x] Typical BJT $g_m$ at 1 mA bias ≈ 38.5 mS
- [x] Typical MOSFET $g_m$ at 100 µA bias with $V_{OV}$ = 0.5 V ≈ 0.4 mS (much smaller than BJT!)

### 🔑 Common KTU Exam Traps
- [x] Forgetting to **convert units** (mA to A, mV to V)
- [x] Confusing $V_T$ (thermal voltage) with $V_{th}$ (threshold voltage)
- [x] Confusing $r_\pi$ (kΩ) with $r_e$ (Ω)
- [x] Using triode-region formula in saturation (or vice versa) for MOSFET
- [x] Not specifying the **constant $V_{DS}$/$V_{CE}$** condition in the definition

### 🔑 Engineering Insights
- [x] **Higher $g_m$ = Higher voltage gain potential** (for same load resistance)
- [x] **OTA (LM13700, CA3080)** — $g_m$ is electronically tunable via bias current
- [x] **Gilbert cell multiplier** — exploits square-law $I_D$–$V_{GS}$ relation
- [x] **Translinear loop** — exploits exponential BJT relation for analog computation
- [x] **BJT advantage:** Higher $g_m$ per unit bias current than MOSFET
- [x] **MOSFET advantage:** Zero gate current → infinite input impedance

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCICT302 - ELECTRONIC CIRCUITS AND NETWORKS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** and Transresistance Amplifiers, Power Amplifiers: Classifications (Basic Operation using Input Output Signal and Circuit Diagram) and Power Efficiency - Class A (Series Fed/Transformer Coupled) - Class B (Transformer Coupled Push Pull Amplifier

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Transresistance Amplifier (Transimpedance Amplifier)

### Formal Academic Definition
A **Transresistance Amplifier (TIA)**, also called a *Transimpedance Amplifier*, is an amplifier topology whose input is a **current signal** $i_{in}(t)$ and whose output is a **voltage signal** $v_{out}(t)$, with the proportionality constant being a resistance — hence the name "transresistance." The defining transfer function is:

$$\boxed{\,R_{M} \;=\; \frac{v_{out}}{i_{in}} \;\; [\text{Unit: } \Omega]\,}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** A Transresistance amplifier is classified as one of the four fundamental amplifier types (alongside voltage, current, and transconductance amplifiers). It is an **active current-to-voltage (I-to-V) converter** and is fundamentally distinct from a voltage amplifier, even though it often uses an op-amp internally.

### Conceptual Analogy — The Water-Flow Meter
Imagine a water tap where the input is **flow rate** (current, in litres/second) and the output is the **reading on a dial** (voltage, in volts). The meter's "transresistance" is the dial's reading divided by the flow rate. Doubling the flow doubles the dial reading. A *transresistance amplifier* does exactly this with electrical signals — it converts a small input current (often from a photodiode or current-sensor) into a usable, proportional voltage.

### Block-Level Intuition
- **Input domain:** Current $\rightarrow$ $i_{in}$
- **Output domain:** Voltage $\rightarrow$ $v_{out}$
- **Conversion gain:** Measured in **ohms ($\Omega$)** — note this is *not* a physical resistance but a *gain parameter* with resistance units.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Transducer Interface Use:** Transresistance amplifiers are the standard front-end for **photodiodes**, **phototransistors**, **current-output DACs**, and **electrochemical sensors** — all of which naturally produce current and require conversion to voltage for further signal processing.

---

## 1.2 Power Amplifiers — Overview & Need

### Formal Definition
A **Power Amplifier (PA)** is a large-signal amplifier designed to deliver substantial **power** to a low-impedance load (such as a loudspeaker, antenna, or motor) with high **conversion efficiency** and acceptable **fidelity (low distortion)**. Unlike small-signal amplifiers that maximize voltage/current gain, the PA's central design metrics are:

1. **Output Power** $P_L$ delivered to load
2. **Efficiency** $\eta = P_{L(ac)} / P_{DC}$
3. **Distortion** (harmonics of the output waveform)

### Conceptual Analogy — The Public Address System
A small-signal amplifier is like whispering through a microphone to one person. A **power amplifier** is the loudspeaker system that takes that whisper and projects it across a stadium. It does not necessarily "amplify the information more accurately" — instead, it delivers the information with **sufficient muscle (power)** to drive a heavy load. The price is **heat** (wasted DC power), and the engineering challenge is to minimize the heat while maximizing the sound.

### Classification of Power Amplifiers (by Conduction Angle)

| Class | Conduction Angle $\theta_c$ | Operating Mode | Typical Application |
|-------|----------------------------|----------------|---------------------|
| **A** | $360°$ | Linear, full cycle | Audio pre-final, high-fidelity small-signal |
| **B** | $180°$ | Each device conducts half cycle | Audio push-pull stages |
| **AB** | $180° < \theta_c < 360°$ | Bias between A and B | General-purpose audio |
| **C** | $< 180°$ | Highly non-linear, tuned load | RF transmitters, oscillators |
| **D** | Switching (0° or 180° only) | PWM/Switching | High-efficiency audio, subwoofers |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Module Focus:** The syllabus requires detailed analysis of **Class A (Series Fed and Transformer Coupled)** and **Class B (Transformer Coupled Push-Pull)** amplifiers, including input/output signal behaviour, circuit operation, and **power efficiency derivations**.

---

## 1.3 Class A Power Amplifier — Topology Basics

### 1.3.1 Series-Fed Class A Amplifier
A **Series-Fed Class A** amplifier places the load $R_C$ directly in the collector circuit, in **series** with the transistor and the supply $V_{CC}$. The transistor is biased at the **midpoint of the DC load line** ($I_{CQ} = V_{CC}/(2R_C)$, $V_{CEQ} = V_{CC}/2$) so that the output can swing symmetrically.

### 1.3.2 Transformer-Coupled Class A Amplifier
A **Transformer-Coupled Class A** amplifier replaces the collector resistor with the **primary of a transformer**; the load $R_L$ (e.g., a speaker) is connected across the **secondary**. The transformer blocks DC, allowing the collector to swing from near $0$ V to near $2V_{CC}$ (twice the supply) on AC peaks, while the quiescent current sets the DC operating point.

---

## 1.4 Class B Power Amplifier — Push-Pull Topology

A **Class B Push-Pull** amplifier uses **two transistors** ($Q_1$ and $Q_2$) arranged in complementary symmetry. Each transistor conducts for exactly **one half-cycle** ($180°$):
- $Q_1$ conducts during the **positive half-cycle** of the input
- $Q_2$ conducts during the **negative half-cycle** of the input

A **center-tapped transformer** combines the two half-cycles into a complete sinusoid at the load.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Crossover Distortion:** At the instant the input crosses zero, neither transistor is conducting — producing a small "dead zone" in the output. This is a defining limitation of pure Class B. Class AB (with small bias) is used in practice to eliminate it.

---

## 1.5 Key Physical & Design Constants (Bolded Standards)

- **Conduction angle of Class A:** $360°$
- **Conduction angle of Class B:** $180°$
- **Maximum theoretical efficiency of Class A (Series-Fed):** $\mathbf{25\%}$
- **Maximum theoretical efficiency of Class A (Transformer-Coupled):** $\mathbf{50\%}$
- **Maximum theoretical efficiency of Class B (Push-Pull):** $\mathbf{\pi/4 \approx 78.5\%}$
- **RMS-to-Peak factor for sinusoid:** $1/\sqrt{2}$
- **Average-to-Peak factor for half-sinusoid:** $1/\pi$

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 Transresistance Amplifier — Deep Analysis

### 2.1.1 Why a Transresistance Amplifier is Needed
Many real-world sensors (photodiodes, current-output sensors) inherently behave as **current sources** with very high internal impedance. Loading them with a resistor to convert current to voltage causes:
- Loading of the source (small current signal is drained)
- Bandwidth limitation (large $R$ × diode capacitance forms a low-pass filter)
- Sensitivity to cable capacitance

A transresistance amplifier solves this by presenting a **virtual ground** at the input (in the op-amp implementation), achieving **near-zero input impedance** while converting the input current to an output voltage with high accuracy and bandwidth.

### 2.1.2 Ideal Transfer Function
For an ideal transresistance amplifier:

$$v_{out}(t) = R_M \cdot i_{in}(t)$$

where $R_M$ is the transresistance gain (in $\Omega$). There is no dependence on the source impedance or load impedance (in the ideal case).

### 2.1.3 Implementation using Op-Amp (Conceptual)
The op-amp with a feedback resistor $R_f$ forms the most common implementation:

$$v_{out} = -i_{in} \cdot R_f$$

The negative sign indicates inversion, but the magnitude gives the transresistance $R_M = R_f$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world engineering utility:** TIAs are the *front-end of every optical fibre receiver*, every digital oscilloscope's current probe interface, every CT (Computed Tomography) scanner's photodetector chain, and every lock-in amplifier's current input stage. They are indispensable in instrumentation.

---

## 2.2 Power Amplifier Efficiency — Conceptual Framework

### 2.2.1 Definition of Efficiency
The **power conversion efficiency** $\eta$ (also called *collector efficiency* or *drain efficiency*) is defined as:

$$\eta = \frac{P_{L(ac)}}{P_{DC}} \times 100\%$$

where:
- $P_{L(ac)}$ = AC power delivered to the load
- $P_{DC}$ = DC power drawn from the supply

The complementary quantity $(1 - \eta) \cdot P_{DC}$ is dissipated as **heat** in the active device, which determines the required **heat-sink rating**.

### 2.2.2 Why Some Classes are More Efficient
The efficiency is fundamentally limited by how much of the **DC supply current** is converted to **AC load current** and how much is wasted. Class A wastes the most because even with no input signal, the full quiescent current $I_{CQ}$ flows. Class B wastes nothing at zero signal (theoretically), and only wastes the "tail" of each half-cycle.

---

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

### Transresistance Amplifier

| Parameter | Formula | Units | Notes |
|-----------|---------|-------|-------|
| Transfer Function | $v_{out} = R_M \cdot i_{in}$ | V | Ideal case |
| Transresistance Gain | $R_M = v_{out} / i_{in}$ | $\Omega$ (V/A) | Not a physical resistor |
| Op-Amp Implementation | $R_M = R_f$ | $\Omega$ | $v_{out} = -i_{in} R_f$ |

### Class A — Series-Fed

| Parameter | Formula | Boundary Condition |
|-----------|---------|--------------------|
| DC Load Line | $V_{CE} = V_{CC} - I_C R_C$ | — |
| Q-point (midpoint bias) | $I_{CQ} = V_{CC} / (2R_C)$ | $V_{CEQ} = V_{CC}/2$ |
| Max AC Output Voltage (peak) | $V_{CE(peak)} = V_{CC}/2$ | Limited by cut-off & saturation |
| Max AC Output Current (peak) | $I_{C(peak)} = I_{CQ} = V_{CC}/(2R_C)$ | — |
| Max AC Power to Load | $P_{ac(max)} = V_{CC}^2 / (8 R_C)$ | When fully driven |
| DC Power from Supply | $P_{DC} = V_{CC} I_{CQ} = V_{CC}^2 / (2 R_C)$ | Constant |
| **Maximum Efficiency** | $\boxed{\eta_{max} = 25\%}$ | $\eta = P_{ac(max)} / P_{DC}$ |
| Power Dissipated by Transistor | $P_D = P_{DC} - P_{ac}$ | Max at $P_{ac} = 0$ |

### Class A — Transformer-Coupled

| Parameter | Formula | Boundary Condition |
|-----------|---------|--------------------|
| Q-point | $I_{CQ}$, $V_{CEQ} = V_{CC}$ | Set by bias network |
| Reflected Load | $R_L' = n^2 R_L$ | $n$ = turns ratio |
| Max Collector Voltage Swing (peak) | $V_{CE(peak)} = V_{CC}$ | Can swing to $2V_{CC}$ |
| Max AC Power to Load | $P_{ac(max)} = V_{CC} I_{CQ} / 2$ | With $I_{c(peak)} = I_{CQ}$ |
| DC Power from Supply | $P_{DC} = V_{CC} I_{CQ}$ | Transformer blocks DC |
| **Maximum Efficiency** | $\boxed{\eta_{max} = 50\%}$ | Doubled over series-fed |

### Class B — Transformer-Coupled Push-Pull

| Parameter | Formula | Boundary Condition |
|-----------|---------|--------------------|
| Conduction Angle | $\theta_c = 180°$ per transistor | Each conducts half cycle |
| Average Supply Current | $I_{DC} = (2 I_{peak}) / \pi$ | Half-sinusoid average |
| DC Power from Supply | $P_{DC} = (2 V_{CC} I_{peak}) / \pi$ | Depends on signal |
| AC Power to Load | $P_{ac} = V_{CC} I_{peak} / 2$ | At full drive |
| **Maximum Efficiency** | $\boxed{\eta_{max} = \frac{\pi}{4} \approx 78.5\%}$ | $I_{peak} = V_{CC}/R_L'$ |
| Power Dissipated per Transistor | $P_{D1} = P_{D2} = P_{DC}/2 - P_{ac}/2$ | At full drive |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Memory Aid for KTU Exams:** Remember the magic numbers — **25% – 50% – 78.5%** for Series-Fed A, Transformer-Coupled A, and Push-Pull B respectively. This triplet is the most-frequently asked set in KTU Module-2 questions.

---

## 2.4 Engineering Utility

| Topology | Real-World Use |
|----------|----------------|
| Transresistance Amplifier | Photodiode front-ends, optical receivers, oscilloscope current probes, lock-in amplifiers |
| Class A Series-Fed | Low-power audio stages, instrumentation where linearity is paramount |
| Class A Transformer-Coupled | Vintage hi-fi amplifiers (e.g., single-ended tube amps), early transistor radios |
| Class B Push-Pull | High-power audio amplifiers, public address systems, motor drivers |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why efficiency matters in practice:** A Class A amplifier rated at 100 W output typically requires a 400 W power supply and must dissipate 300 W as heat (requiring a large, expensive heat sink). A Class B amplifier achieving the same 100 W output draws only ~127 W from the supply — far more compact and economical.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Class A Series-Fed Amplifier — Efficiency Derivation

### Step 1: Circuit Description and Operating Point
Consider a transistor with collector load $R_C$ connected to supply $V_{CC}$. The Q-point is set at the **midpoint of the DC load line** for maximum symmetrical swing:

$$I_{CQ} = \frac{V_{CC}}{2 R_C}, \qquad V_{CEQ} = \frac{V_{CC}}{2}$$

The DC load line equation (Kirchhoff's Voltage Law on output loop):

$$V_{CE} = V_{CC} - I_C R_C$$

### Step 2: DC Power Supplied by the Battery
The DC current drawn from $V_{CC}$ is the constant quiescent collector current $I_{CQ}$ (assuming base current is negligible compared to collector current):

$$P_{DC} = V_{CC} \cdot I_{CQ} = V_{CC} \cdot \frac{V_{CC}}{2 R_C} = \frac{V_{CC}^2}{2 R_C}$$

### Step 3: AC Power Delivered to the Load (Maximum Case)
When an input sinusoid drives the amplifier to its maximum undistorted swing, the output collector voltage $v_{ce}$ swings from $V_{CC}/2$ down to near $0$ and up to near $V_{CC}$. Thus the **peak** of the AC voltage across $R_C$ is:

$$V_{m} = \frac{V_{CC}}{2}$$

Similarly, the **peak** of the AC collector current is:

$$I_{m} = I_{CQ} = \frac{V_{CC}}{2 R_C}$$

The AC power delivered to $R_C$ is:

$$\begin{aligned}
P_{ac} &= \frac{V_m}{\sqrt{2}} \cdot \frac{I_m}{\sqrt{2}} = \frac{V_m I_m}{2} \\
       &= \frac{1}{2} \cdot \frac{V_{CC}}{2} \cdot \frac{V_{CC}}{2 R_C} \\
       &= \frac{V_{CC}^2}{8 R_C}
\end{aligned}$$

### Step 4: Compute Maximum Efficiency

$$\begin{aligned}
\eta_{max} &= \frac{P_{ac(max)}}{P_{DC}} = \frac{V_{CC}^2 / (8 R_C)}{V_{CC}^2 / (2 R_C)} \\
           &= \frac{1}{8} \cdot \frac{2}{1} = \frac{2}{8} = \frac{1}{4} = 0.25
\end{aligned}$$

$$\boxed{\eta_{max,\; A\; \text{series-fed}} = 25\%}$$

### Step 5: Transistor Power Dissipation
The transistor itself dissipates the difference:

$$P_{D(transistor)} = P_{DC} - P_{ac} = \frac{V_{CC}^2}{2 R_C} - \frac{V_{CC}^2}{8 R_C} = \frac{3 V_{CC}^2}{8 R_C}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Insight:** The transistor dissipates **maximum power** ($P_D = 3 V_{CC}^2 / (8 R_C)$) at *full signal drive*, not at zero signal. Wait — verify: at zero signal $P_D = P_{DC} = V_{CC}^2/(2R_C) = 4 V_{CC}^2/(8R_C)$. The maximum dissipation is actually $P_{DC}$ at *no signal* (i.e., $P_{D,max} = V_{CC} \cdot I_{CQ}$). At full signal, dissipation is $3V_{CC}^2/(8R_C)$ — lower than at quiescence. The exact peak dissipation across all signal levels is at $V_m = 2 V_{CC}/\pi$, giving $P_{D,peak} = V_{CC}^2 / (2\pi^2 R_C) \approx V_{CC}^2 / (1.93 R_C)$ which is slightly higher than $V_{CC}^2/(2R_C)$? No, recomputing: $P_D(V_m) = P_{DC} - V_m^2/(4R_C)$ — this is a downward parabola in $V_m$, max at $V_m = 0$ → $P_{D,max} = P_{DC} = V_{CC}^2/(2R_C)$.

---

## 3.2 Class A Transformer-Coupled Amplifier — Efficiency Derivation

### Step 1: Circuit Setup
The transformer primary replaces $R_C$ in the collector circuit. The DC resistance of the primary is near zero (ideal transformer), so the **DC load line is essentially vertical** (no DC drop across primary). The Q-point is:

$$I_{CQ}, \qquad V_{CEQ} = V_{CC}$$

### Step 2: AC Load Line
The load $R_L$ (e.g., speaker) is reflected to the primary side as:

$$R_L' = n^2 \cdot R_L$$

where $n = N_1 / N_2$ is the turns ratio. The AC load line passes through the Q-point with slope $-1/R_L'$.

### Step 3: Maximum AC Voltage Swing
The collector voltage $v_{CE}$ can swing from $V_{CC} - V_{m}$ to $V_{CC} + V_{m}$, where $V_m$ is the peak AC collector voltage. The maximum swing is bounded by:

- Lower bound: transistor saturation $\rightarrow v_{CE} \geq V_{CE(sat)} \approx 0$
- Upper bound: transistor cut-off $\rightarrow i_C \geq 0$

For maximum symmetrical swing:

$$V_{m,max} = V_{CC}, \qquad I_{m,max} = I_{CQ}$$

(assuming $R_L' = V_{CC} / I_{CQ}$ — i.e., load matched to Q-point).

### Step 4: AC Power Delivered

$$\begin{aligned}
P_{ac(max)} &= \frac{V_m \cdot I_m}{2} = \frac{V_{CC} \cdot I_{CQ}}{2}
\end{aligned}$$

### Step 5: DC Power Supplied
The transformer primary has zero DC resistance, so the **DC current drawn from $V_{CC}$ is just $I_{CQ}$** (the quiescent current), constant regardless of signal:

$$P_{DC} = V_{CC} \cdot I_{CQ}$$

### Step 6: Maximum Efficiency

$$\begin{aligned}
\eta_{max} &= \frac{P_{ac(max)}}{P_{DC}} = \frac{V_{CC} I_{CQ} / 2}{V_{CC} I_{CQ}} = \frac{1}{2} = 0.5
\end{aligned}$$

$$\boxed{\eta_{max,\; A\; \text{transformer-coupled}} = 50\%}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why efficiency doubles:** In the series-fed case, half the supply voltage is wasted as a *static* DC drop across $R_C$. The transformer eliminates this DC drop (because it presents a DC short), allowing the *full* $V_{CC}$ to participate in the AC swing — and the DC supply current remains at $I_{CQ}$ (not $2I_{CQ}$), so the DC power is *halved* compared to series-fed. The combined effect doubles the efficiency.

---

## 3.3 Class B Transformer-Coupled Push-Pull Amplifier — Efficiency Derivation

### Step 1: Circuit Description
Two transistors $Q_1$ (NPN) and $Q_2$ (PNP, or vice-versa) share a common emitter connection and feed a **center-tapped transformer primary**. The supply $V_{CC}$ connects to the center tap.

### Step 2: Current Waveforms
Each transistor conducts for **half the input cycle**. The collector current of $Q_1$ is a **half-sinusoid**:

$$i_{C1}(t) = \begin{cases} I_{peak} \sin(\omega t), & 0 \leq \omega t \leq \pi \\ 0, & \pi \leq \omega t \leq 2\pi \end{cases}$$

$Q_2$ conducts the opposite half.

### Step 3: Average DC Current Drawn from Supply
The average value of a half-sinusoid of peak $I_{peak}$ is:

$$I_{DC} = \frac{1}{T} \int_0^{T/2} I_{peak} \sin(\omega t) \, dt = \frac{I_{peak}}{\pi}$$

Since both halves contribute (alternately), the total average current drawn from the supply is:

$$I_{DC,\; supply} = \frac{2 I_{peak}}{\pi}$$

### Step 4: DC Power from Supply

$$\begin{aligned}
P_{DC} &= V_{CC} \cdot I_{DC,\; supply} \\
       &= V_{CC} \cdot \frac{2 I_{peak}}{\pi} \\
       &= \frac{2 V_{CC} I_{peak}}{\pi}
\end{aligned}$$

### Step 5: AC Power to Load
The transformer secondary sees a full sinusoid of peak current $I_{peak}$ (the two halves combine). The load voltage is sinusoidal with peak $V_{CC}$ (assuming matched load). The AC power delivered to the reflected load is:

$$\begin{aligned}
P_{ac} &= \frac{V_m I_m}{2} = \frac{V_{CC} \cdot I_{peak}}{2}
\end{aligned}$$

### Step 6: Maximum Efficiency (when $I_{peak} = V_{CC}/R_L'$)

Substituting:

$$\begin{aligned}
\eta_{max} &= \frac{P_{ac(max)}}{P_{DC}} = \frac{V_{CC} I_{peak} / 2}{2 V_{CC} I_{peak} / \pi} \\
           &= \frac{1/2}{2/\pi} = \frac{\pi}{4} \approx 0.7854
\end{aligned}$$

$$\boxed{\eta_{max,\; B\; \text{push-pull}} = \frac{\pi}{4} \approx 78.5\%}$$

### Step 7: Power Dissipated per Transistor
At full drive, each transistor dissipates half the total dissipation:

$$P_{D,\; per\; transistor} = \frac{P_{DC} - P_{ac}}{2} = \frac{1}{2} \left( \frac{2 V_{CC} I_{peak}}{\pi} - \frac{V_{CC} I_{peak}}{2} \right) = V_{CC} I_{peak} \left( \frac{1}{\pi} - \frac{1}{4} \right)$$

---

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Parameter | Class A Series | Class A Transformer | Class B Push-Pull |
|-----------|---------------|---------------------|-------------------|
| Q-point Current | $I_{CQ}$ | $I_{CQ}$ | $\approx 0$ (no bias) |
| Supply Current (signal-dependent?) | **No** (constant $I_{CQ}$) | **No** (constant $I_{CQ}$) | **Yes** ($\propto I_{peak}$) |
| $P_{DC}$ | $V_{CC} I_{CQ}$ | $V_{CC} I_{CQ}$ | $(2 V_{CC} I_{peak})/\pi$ |
| $P_{ac(max)}$ | $V_{CC}^2 / (8 R_C)$ | $V_{CC} I_{CQ} / 2$ | $V_{CC} I_{peak} / 2$ |
| $\eta_{max}$ | **25%** | **50%** | **78.5%** |
| Crossover Distortion | None | None | **Present** (needs Class AB fix) |
| Heat-sink Required | Large | Large | Moderate |
| Transformer Needed? | No | Yes | Yes (center-tapped) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Trap:** A common student error is to assume that the DC current in a Class A transformer-coupled stage is $2 I_{CQ}$ (because the AC peak is $I_{CQ}$ on top of $I_{CQ}$). This is wrong. The *average* current from the supply remains $I_{CQ}$ because the transformer blocks DC — the supply sees only the DC quiescent current, not the AC ripple.

---

## 3.5 General Expression for Class B Efficiency (as a function of drive level)

Let $k = I_{peak} / I_{max}$ be the drive level (0 to 1). Then:

$$P_{DC} = \frac{2 V_{CC} \cdot k I_{max}}{\pi}, \qquad P_{ac} = \frac{V_{CC} \cdot k I_{max}}{2}$$

$$\eta(k) = \frac{P_{ac}}{P_{DC}} = \frac{V_{CC} k I_{max} / 2}{2 V_{CC} k I_{max} / \pi} = \frac{\pi}{4} \quad \text{(independent of } k\text{!)}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Surprising Result:** The efficiency of an *ideal* Class B push-pull amplifier is **constant at $\pi/4$** for any drive level above zero. This is because both $P_{DC}$ and $P_{ac}$ scale linearly with $I_{peak}$, so their ratio is constant. At $k \to 0$ (very small signal), $P_{DC} \to 0$ and $P_{ac} \to 0$, but their ratio still approaches $\pi/4$. Real-world devices deviate from this due to saturation voltage and bias network consumption.

---

## 3.6 Power Dissipation in Transistor — Maximum Point (Class A Series-Fed)

The power dissipated by the transistor is:

$$P_D = P_{DC} - P_{ac} = \frac{V_{CC}^2}{2 R_C} - \frac{V_m^2}{4 R_C}$$

Taking the derivative with respect to $V_m$ to find the maximum:

$$\frac{dP_D}{dV_m} = -\frac{2 V_m}{4 R_C} = -\frac{V_m}{2 R_C} = 0 \;\Rightarrow\; V_m = 0$$

So $P_D$ is maximum at **zero signal** ($V_m = 0$), giving:

$$P_{D(max)} = \frac{V_{CC}^2}{2 R_C} = V_{CC} I_{CQ}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Design Rule for Class A:** The transistor must be rated to dissipate the **entire DC power** $P_{DC} = V_{CC} I_{CQ}$ continuously, even though this occurs only at zero signal. This is why Class A amplifiers need oversized transistors and large heat-sinks.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

---

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A.1 — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]
**Define a transresistance amplifier. What are its input and output quantities? Give one practical application.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A transresistance amplifier is an amplifier that accepts a **current input** $i_{in}$ and produces a **voltage output** $v_{out}$, with the proportionality constant being the transresistance gain $R_M$ (in ohms). The transfer function is:

$$v_{out} = R_M \cdot i_{in}$$

**Application (1 mark):** Used as the front-end of **optical receivers** with photodiodes — the photodiode produces a small current proportional to incident light, and the TIA converts this current into a usable voltage signal for further amplification.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistake:** Students often confuse transresistance with transconductance. Transresistance = V-out / I-in (units $\Omega$); Transconductance = I-out / V-in (units Siemens or mhos). Mixing these up costs easy marks.

---

### Question A.2 — [KTU University Exam – June 2024]
**What is the maximum theoretical efficiency of a Class B push-pull amplifier? At what conduction angle does each transistor operate?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Maximum efficiency:** $\eta_{max} = \dfrac{\pi}{4} \approx 78.5\%$
- **Conduction angle per transistor:** $180°$ (each transistor conducts for half the input cycle)
- **Mechanism:** $Q_1$ conducts during the positive half-cycle; $Q_2$ conducts during the negative half-cycle; the transformer combines them to reconstruct the full sinusoid.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice)

---

### **Question B.OPTION A** (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Modified]

**A Class A transformer-coupled power amplifier operates from $V_{CC} = 20\text{ V}$ with quiescent collector current $I_{CQ} = 100\text{ mA}$ and a load $R_L = 8\ \Omega$ (speaker) coupled via an ideal transformer with turns ratio $n = 5:1$.**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Draw the circuit diagram. Derive the expression for maximum efficiency and show that it equals 50%. Calculate the maximum AC power delivered to the load and the DC power drawn from the supply.

**(b) [7 Marks]** If the amplifier is reconfigured as a **series-fed** Class A amplifier with the same $V_{CC}$ and $I_{CQ}$, calculate: (i) the required $R_C$, (ii) the maximum AC power delivered to $R_C$, (iii) the maximum efficiency. Compare the two configurations in a tabular form.

---

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Step 1: Circuit Description (2 marks)**
The Class A transformer-coupled amplifier consists of a single BJT $Q_1$ with:
- Collector connected to the primary of an ideal transformer
- Transformer primary's other end connected to $V_{CC}$
- Emitter connected to ground
- Input signal applied to base via coupling capacitor
- Speaker load $R_L$ connected to transformer secondary

**Step 2: Derive Maximum Efficiency Expression (3 marks)**

The Q-point: $V_{CEQ} = V_{CC} = 20\text{ V}$, $I_{CQ} = 100\text{ mA}$.

The reflected load on the primary side:

$$R_L' = n^2 \cdot R_L = 5^2 \times 8 = 200\ \Omega$$

For maximum undistorted swing, the AC peak collector voltage and current are:

$$V_m = V_{CC} = 20\text{ V}, \qquad I_m = I_{CQ} = 100\text{ mA}$$

AC power delivered to the load:

$$P_{ac} = \frac{V_m I_m}{2} = \frac{20 \times 0.1}{2} = 1\text{ W}$$

DC power drawn from supply:

$$P_{DC} = V_{CC} \cdot I_{CQ} = 20 \times 0.1 = 2\text{ W}$$

Maximum efficiency:

$$\eta_{max} = \frac{P_{ac(max)}}{P_{DC}} = \frac{1}{2} = 50\%$$

This proves the standard result that the maximum efficiency of a Class A transformer-coupled amplifier is **50%**.

**Step 3: Comparison Comment (2 marks)**
The transformer eliminates the static DC drop across the collector load, allowing the full $V_{CC}$ to appear as AC swing. This doubles the efficiency compared to the series-fed case.

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Step 1: Required $R_C$ (2 marks)**
For Class A series-fed, Q-point at $V_{CEQ} = V_{CC}/2 = 10\text{ V}$:

$$R_C = \frac{V_{CC} - V_{CEQ}}{I_{CQ}} = \frac{20 - 10}{0.1} = 100\ \Omega$$

**Step 2: Maximum AC Power (2 marks)**
Maximum AC peak voltage and current:

$$V_m = \frac{V_{CC}}{2} = 10\text{ V}, \qquad I_m = I_{CQ} = 100\text{ mA}$$

Maximum AC power:

$$P_{ac(max)} = \frac{V_m I_m}{2} = \frac{10 \times 0.1}{2} = 0.5\text{ W}$$

**Step 3: Maximum Efficiency (2 marks)**
DC power:

$$P_{DC} = V_{CC} \cdot I_{CQ} = 20 \times 0.1 = 2\text{ W}$$

Efficiency:

$$\eta_{max} = \frac{P_{ac(max)}}{P_{DC}} = \frac{0.5}{2} = 0.25 = 25\%$$

**Step 4: Comparison Table (1 mark)**

| Parameter | Transformer-Coupled | Series-Fed |
|-----------|---------------------|------------|
| $R_C$ (or $R_L'$) | $200\ \Omega$ (reflected) | $100\ \Omega$ |
| $V_m$ | $20\text{ V}$ | $10\text{ V}$ |
| $P_{ac(max)}$ | $1\text{ W}$ | $0.5\text{ W}$ |
| $P_{DC}$ | $2\text{ W}$ | $2\text{ W}$ |
| $\eta_{max}$ | **50%** | **25%** |

---

### **Question B.OPTION B** (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model]

**A Class B push-pull transformer-coupled amplifier operates from $V_{CC} = 25\text{ V}$ and drives a load $R_L = 10\ \Omega$ through an ideal center-tapped transformer with turns ratio $N_1 : N_2 = 2 : 1$ (primary total: $N_1$, secondary: $N_2$).**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Draw the circuit and explain its operation with input and output waveforms. Derive the expression for maximum efficiency and show that $\eta_{max} = \pi/4$.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Calculate the maximum AC power delivered to the load, the DC power drawn from the supply at maximum drive, and the power dissipated by each transistor. If the input signal is reduced to half its maximum amplitude, what is the new efficiency?

---

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Step 1: Circuit Description (2 marks)**
Two BJTs $Q_1$ (NPN) and $Q_2$ (PNP) share a common emitter. The transformer primary is **center-tapped**: the top half feeds $Q_1$, the bottom half feeds $Q_2$, and the center tap connects to $V_{CC}$. The secondary drives the load $R_L$.

**Step 2: Operation with Waveforms (2 marks)**
- During the **positive half-cycle** of the input, $Q_1$ conducts (acting as an emitter follower); $Q_2$ is off.
- During the **negative half-cycle**, $Q_2$ conducts; $Q_1$ is off.
- The transformer combines the two half-cycles into a complete sinusoid at the secondary.
- **Crossover distortion** appears at the zero-crossing points (neither transistor conducts).

**Step 3: Efficiency Derivation (3 marks)**
For a sinusoidal input with peak $I_{peak}$:
- Each transistor's collector current is a half-sinusoid
- Average current from supply: $I_{DC} = \dfrac{2 I_{peak}}{\pi}$
- DC power: $P_{DC} = V_{CC} \cdot I_{DC} = \dfrac{2 V_{CC} I_{peak}}{\pi}$
- AC power to load: $P_{ac} = \dfrac{V_{CC} I_{peak}}{2}$

$$\eta = \frac{P_{ac}}{P_{DC}} = \frac{V_{CC} I_{peak} / 2}{2 V_{CC} I_{peak} / \pi} = \frac{\pi}{4}$$

$$\boxed{\eta_{max} = \frac{\pi}{4} \approx 78.5\%}$$

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Step 1: Reflected Load (1 mark)**
Total primary turns $N_1$, secondary turns $N_2$. Each half of the primary has $N_1/2$ turns. The total primary-to-secondary ratio is $N_1/N_2 = 2$, so the total reflected load is:

$$R_L' = \left(\frac{N_1}{N_2}\right)^2 R_L = 2^2 \times 10 = 40\ \Omega$$

**Step 2: Maximum AC Power (2 marks)**
Maximum peak current: $I_{peak} = V_{CC}/R_L' = 25/40 = 0.625\text{ A}$

$$P_{ac(max)} = \frac{V_{CC} I_{peak}}{2} = \frac{25 \times 0.625}{2} = 7.8125\text{ W}$$

**Step 3: DC Power at Maximum Drive (2 marks)**

$$P_{DC} = \frac{2 V_{CC} I_{peak}}{\pi} = \frac{2 \times 25 \times 0.625}{\pi} = \frac{31.25}{\pi} \approx 9.947\text{ W}$$

**Step 4: Power Dissipated per Transistor (1 mark)**
Total dissipation: $P_D = P_{DC} - P_{ac} = 9.947 - 7.8125 \approx 2.135\text{ W}$

Per transistor: $P_{D1} = P_{D2} = 2.135/2 \approx 1.067\text{ W}$

**Step 5: Efficiency at Half-Amplitude (1 mark)**
For an ideal Class B, $\eta = \pi/4$ *regardless of drive level*. So at half amplitude:

$$\eta = \frac{\pi}{4} \approx 78.5\%$$

(AC power and DC power both scale linearly with $I_{peak}$, so their ratio is invariant.)

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks:**
>
> 1. **Confusing supply current with peak current** in Class B: Many students write $P_{DC} = V_{CC} I_{peak}$, forgetting the average factor $2/\pi$. Always use $I_{DC} = 2I_{peak}/\pi$ for a half-sinusoidal waveform.
> 2. **Forgetting the reflected load** in transformer-coupled stages: The $R_L$ on the secondary is *not* the same as the load seen by the transistor; you must compute $R_L' = n^2 R_L$ first.
> 3. **Assuming Class A efficiency exceeds Class B**: Some students wrongly believe that the constant DC current in Class A is "more efficient." In reality, the constant DC current is the *source of waste*; Class B's signal-dependent supply current is what enables its high efficiency.
> 4. **Confusing "transformer-coupled" with "transformerless":** In transformerless (direct-coupled) Class B, the two transistors are often configured as a complementary pair (NPN + PNP) without any transformer. The KTU syllabus specifies the *transformer-coupled* version.
> 5. **Ignoring crossover distortion:** A pure Class B push-pull stage produces crossover distortion at every zero-crossing. Real designs use Class AB (small forward bias) to eliminate this — KTU may ask about it as a follow-up.

---

## 5.3 TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

### 🔑 Rapid Revision Checklist

- [x] **Transresistance Amplifier:** Converts current to voltage; gain has units of ohms ($\Omega$); implemented using op-amp with feedback resistor $R_f$.
- [x] **Transresistance = Transimpedance:** Same thing, two names. KTU uses "transresistance" in the syllabus.
- [x] **Power Amplifier Efficiency Definition:** $\eta = P_{L(ac)} / P_{DC} \times 100\%$
- [x] **Class A Conduction:** Full $360°$ — transistor is *always on*, even with no input.
- [x] **Class A Series-Fed Efficiency:** $\boxed{25\%}$ (the lowest among the three).
- [x] **Class A Transformer-Coupled Efficiency:** $\boxed{50\%}$ — transformer blocks DC, eliminating static drop across $R_C$.
- [x] **Class B Conduction:** Each transistor conducts for $180°$ (half cycle).
- [x] **Class B Push-Pull Efficiency:** $\boxed{\pi/4 \approx 78.5\%}$ — and this is *constant* for all drive levels above zero (ideal case).
- [x] **Key Average Value:** For a half-sinusoid of peak $I_{peak}$, average = $I_{peak}/\pi$.
- [x] **Key Average Value:** For a full sinusoid of peak $V_m$, average = $0$ (over full cycle), but $\vert V \vert_{avg} = 2V_m/\pi$.
- [x] **Crossover Distortion:** A Class B problem at zero-crossings; eliminated by Class AB biasing.
- [x] **Heat-sink Sizing:** Class A transistor must dissipate $P_{DC} = V_{CC} I_{CQ}$ at zero signal — heat-sink sized for this.
- [x] **Reflected Load Formula:** $R_L' = n^2 R_L$ (always compute this for transformer-coupled stages).
- [x] **Q-point for Class A:** $V_{CEQ} = V_{CC}/2$ (series-fed) or $V_{CEQ} = V_{CC}$ (transformer-coupled).
- [x] **Maximum AC Power Formula (universal):** $P_{ac} = V_m I_m / 2 = V_{rms} I_{rms}$.

### 🎯 High-Yield Mnemonics for KTU Exam

- **"25 – 50 – 78.5"** — the three efficiency numbers in increasing order: Series-Fed A → Transformer-Coupled A → Push-Pull B.
- **"A = Always On, B = By halves"** — Class A conducts always, Class B by halves.
- **"Transformer $\Rightarrow$ No DC drop"** — whenever you see a transformer in a Class A stage, remember the DC load line is vertical (no DC drop across primary).
- **"$\pi/4$ for any signal"** — Class B efficiency is *drive-independent* (ideal case).

### 📚 KTU-Specific Tip
For ESE (End Semester Exam) Module-2 questions, the most-tested formulas are:
1. $\eta_{max}$ for all three topologies (4–5 marks just for stating these).
2. Numerical problems with given $V_{CC}$ and $I_{CQ}$ to compute $P_{ac}$, $P_{DC}$, and $\eta$.
3. Brief derivations (not full-page proofs) of efficiency, focusing on the 3–4 key steps.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCICT302 - ELECTRONIC CIRCUITS AND NETWORKS
> - **Module:** Module 2

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Complementary Symmetry Power Amplifier (Class B Push-Pull)

**Formal Definition:**
A **Complementary Symmetry Push-Pull Power Amplifier** is a Class B power amplifier configuration that uses a pair of *matched* transistors of *opposite polarity* — an **NPN** transistor (Q₁) and a **PNP** transistor (Q₂) — connected in series across a dual (or single) power supply, such that each device amplifies one half-cycle of the input signal without requiring a center-tapped input/output transformer. Q₁ (NPN) conducts during the **positive half-cycle**, and Q₂ (PNP) conducts during the **negative half-cycle**, and the two halves are recombined at the load to reconstruct the full waveform.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** This is the standard transformer-less Class B output stage. It is the building block of nearly every audio amplifier IC (LM386, TDA2030, op-amp output stages).

**Conceptual Analogy / Intuition:**
Imagine a **two-person rowing team** in a long boat. The boat moves only when both rowers pull in perfect synchrony. Now replace the rowers with two transistors:
- The **NPN transistor** is the rower who pulls the oar only when the instructor (input signal) says "PUSH FORWARD" (positive half-cycle).
- The **PNP transistor** is the rower who pulls only on "PULL BACK" (negative half-cycle).

Neither one works the full time, but together they propel the boat (the load) smoothly. The clever trick is that the two rowers are *mirror images* of each other (complementary), so the same single-phase control signal automatically addresses the right rower at the right time. No coach (transformer) is needed to split the work.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** Because each transistor only handles half the waveform, it can rest (cool down) during the other half — dramatically improving efficiency over Class A.

---

### 1.2 Class C Power Amplifier

**Formal Definition:**
A **Class C Power Amplifier** is a power amplifier in which the transistor is *biased below cutoff* (i.e., $V_{BB} < V_{BE(cut\text{-}in)}$ or, for a JFET, $V_{GG} < V_{GS(off)}$), so that collector current flows for **less than 180°** of the input cycle (conduction angle typically **90° or less**). A high-Q **tuned (LC) tank circuit** in the collector is used to reconstruct a sinusoidal output from the narrow current pulses.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Class C amplifiers are the *workhorse* of RF transmitter final stages — radio, TV, radar, and wireless communication transmitters. The conduction angle is the central design parameter.

**Conceptual Analogy / Intuition:**
Think of a **camera's flash** (or a strobe light). It is OFF for most of the time, and emits a *very brief, very intense burst* of light periodically. Your eyes (and the tuned circuit) integrate these short bursts and *perceive* a steady, continuous glow at the strobe's fundamental frequency. The Class C amplifier behaves the same way:
- The transistor "flashes" a short, tall current pulse (conduction angle ≪ 180°).
- The **LC tank circuit acts like the eye/brain** — it stores the energy of each pulse and oscillates at its natural resonant frequency, effectively "filling in" the missing portions of the waveform.
- The output is a clean sine wave at the resonant frequency.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why "below cutoff"?** Biasing below cutoff ensures the transistor is OFF most of the cycle. The input signal only turns it ON for a small slice near its positive peak, producing narrow current pulses. The tuned load then converts these pulses back into a sine wave.

> [!WARNING]
> **Distortion Warning:** Class C produces the *highest distortion* of any class (because of the heavily clipped current waveform). It is **never used for audio** — only for narrow-band RF applications where the LC tank filters out the harmonics.

---

### 1.3 Classification Reminder (KTU Context)

| Class | Conduction Angle | Bias Point | Max Theoretical Efficiency |
|:-----:|:----------------:|:----------:|:---------------------------:|
| **A** | 360° | Midpoint of load line | **25 %** (with coupling xmer) / 50 % (direct-coupled) |
| **B** | 180° | At cutoff | **78.5 %** (π/4) |
| **AB** | 180°–360° | Slightly above cutoff | Between A and B |
| **C** | < 180° (typ. ≤ 90°) | Below cutoff | **100 %** (theoretical), 80–90 % practical |

> [!NOTE]
> Standard reference metric for Class B efficiency: $\eta_{max} = \dfrac{\pi}{4} \approx 0.785 = 78.5\%$.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Complementary Symmetry (Class B) — Operational Analysis

**Circuit Topology (Single-Supply Emitter Follower Version):**
- Q₁ (NPN) — emitter follower, conducts on positive half-cycle.
- Q₂ (PNP) — emitter follower, conducts on negative half-cycle.
- Emitters tied together and connected to load $R_L$ (often through a coupling capacitor to AC-couple the load).
- Bases tied together as the input node.
- Supply: typically $V_{CC}$ and $-V_{CC}$ (split supply) for full swing, or $+V_{CC}$ only (single supply) with a coupling capacitor.

**Operational Logic (Step-by-step):**

1. **No input signal (quiescent state):** Both Q₁ and Q₂ are OFF (or barely ON at the edge of conduction in Class AB). Total quiescent current is essentially zero → very low standby power dissipation.
2. **Positive half-cycle of $v_{in}$:** Q₁ (NPN) turns ON when $v_{in} > V_{BE1} \approx 0.7$ V. Q₂ remains OFF. Q₁ acts as an emitter follower: $v_{out} \approx v_{in} - V_{BE1}$.
3. **Negative half-cycle of $v_{in}$:** Q₂ (PNP) turns ON when $v_{in} < -V_{BE2} \approx -0.7$ V. Q₁ is OFF. Q₂ emitter-follows the negative excursion.
4. **Near zero-crossing ($-0.7$ V $< v_{in} < +0.7$ V):** Both transistors are OFF. Output remains at 0 V. This produces **crossover distortion**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why "Complementary"?** Because Q₁ and Q₂ are *electrical complements* of each other (one NPN, one PNP) with matched characteristics, the same input signal naturally activates the correct one for each half-cycle — no phase-splitting transformer is needed.

---

### 2.2 Crossover Distortion (KTU Frequently Tested)

Because the BE junction requires $\approx 0.7$ V to turn ON, there is a "dead zone" of $V_{BE1} + V_{BE2} \approx 1.4$ V around zero-crossing during which neither transistor conducts. The output fails to follow the input in this region, creating a **notch** in the waveform called **crossover distortion**.

**Mitigation:** Apply a small forward bias (Class AB operation) using a $V_{BE}$ multiplier circuit or diode bias chain so that both transistors are ON the edge of conduction at $v_{in} = 0$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world example:** The hum/buzz heard at very low volume in cheap audio amplifiers is often crossover distortion.

---

### 2.3 Class C Power Amplifier — Operational Analysis

**Operational Logic (Step-by-step):**

1. **DC Bias:** $V_{BB} < V_{BE(cut\text{-}in)}$ for BJT, or $V_{GG} < \vert V_{GS(off)} \vert$ for FET. The Q-point sits *below* the cutoff point on the $I_C$–$V_{CE}$ characteristic.
2. **Input signal arrives:** The transistor turns ON only when $v_{in}$ exceeds the cutoff threshold plus the bias, producing a *narrow current pulse* each cycle.
3. **Conduction angle $2\theta_c$:** The angle (in degrees or radians) during which $i_C > 0$ in one cycle. For ideal Class C, $2\theta_c \to 0$ (impulse train).
4. **LC tank circuit:** The parallel LC network at the collector has a very high impedance at its resonant frequency $f_0 = \dfrac{1}{2\pi\sqrt{LC}}$ and a low impedance at all harmonics. It converts the narrow current pulses into a clean sine wave of amplitude $I_p$ (the peak pulse height) at $f_0$.
5. **Flywheel action:** Between pulses, the tank's LC "ringing" sustains the oscillation. Each new pulse re-energizes the tank, replacing the energy lost in the load resistance $R_L$ (which is in parallel with the tank).

**Why it works:**
The Fourier series of a periodic pulse train contains a strong fundamental component at the input frequency $f_0$ and harmonics at $nf_0$. The tuned circuit selects the fundamental and rejects the harmonics, so the output is a near-pure sine wave.

---

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

#### Table A: Complementary Symmetry (Class B Push-Pull)

| Symbol / Quantity | Formula | Condition / Notes |
|:------------------|:--------|:------------------|
| Conduction angle per transistor | $\theta_c = \pi$ rad (= 180°) | Each device ON for half cycle |
| Maximum output voltage swing | $V_{o(max)} = V_{CC}$ | At peak of input |
| Peak output current | $I_{o(max)} = \dfrac{V_{CC}}{R_L}$ | |
| **Maximum AC output power** | $P_{o(max)} = \dfrac{V_{CC}^{\,2}}{2 R_L}$ | At full output swing |
| Total DC supply current (avg) | $I_{DC} = \dfrac{2 I_{o(max)}}{\pi} = \dfrac{2 V_{CC}}{\pi R_L}$ | Over full cycle |
| **DC power drawn from supply** | $P_{DC} = \dfrac{2 V_{CC}^{\,2}}{\pi R_L}$ | |
| **Maximum conversion efficiency** | $\eta_{max} = \dfrac{P_{o(max)}}{P_{DC}} = \dfrac{\pi}{4} \approx 78.5\%$ | KTU **favourite number** |
| Power dissipated per transistor (avg) | $P_{D(\text{transistor})} = \dfrac{V_{CC}^{\,2}}{\pi^{2} R_L}$ | At max output |
| Max power dissipation per transistor | $P_{D(peak)} = \dfrac{V_{CC}^{\,2}}{\pi^{2} R_L}$ | Occurs at $V_{o} = \dfrac{2V_{CC}}{\pi}$ |

#### Table B: Class C Power Amplifier

| Symbol / Quantity | Formula | Condition / Notes |
|:------------------|:--------|:------------------|
| Conduction angle | $2\theta_c$ (typ. $\leq \pi$ rad = 180°) | $\theta_c$ in radians |
| **Maximum theoretical efficiency** | $\eta_{max} = 100\%$ | As $2\theta_c \to 0$ |
| Practical efficiency | $\eta \approx 80\text{–}90\%$ | At RF with tuned load |
| DC supply power | $P_{DC} = V_{CC} \cdot I_{DC}$ | $I_{DC}$ is the average collector current |
| AC output power | $P_{o} = \dfrac{V_{o(rms)}^{\,2}}{R_L} = \dfrac{I_{o(rms)}^{\,2} \cdot R_L}{}$ | Depends on fundamental component |
| Resonant frequency of tank | $f_{0} = \dfrac{1}{2\pi\sqrt{LC}}$ | Defines operating frequency |
| Quality factor (selectivity) | $Q = \dfrac{f_{0}}{BW} = \dfrac{\omega_{0} R_L}{}\cdot C$ (parallel) | Higher Q = narrower bandwidth |

#### Table C: General Power Amplifier Relationships

| Relationship | Expression |
|:-------------|:-----------|
| Efficiency | $\eta = \dfrac{P_{o(AC)}}{P_{DC}} \times 100\%$ |
| Power balance | $P_{DC} = P_{o(AC)} + P_{D(transistor)}$ |
| KCL at output | $P_{DC} = P_{o} + P_{dissipated}$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Vertical pipe handling in LaTeX:** In all the above formulas, the pipe character `$\vert$` is replaced with the LaTeX command `\vert` or `\mid` when written inside markdown tables — but since formulas are isolated in math mode, this rule applies primarily to the formula sheet if you ever inline them. For the cheat sheet above, all formulas are in clean math display mode and the table syntax is preserved.

---

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Complementary Symmetry Class B/AB:** Foundational in every audio power amplifier IC (LM386, TDA2003, LM3886, op-amp output stages like TL072). Also used in motor drivers and high-current buffers.
- **Class C:** Dominates RF transmitter final stages — AM/FM broadcast transmitters, TV transmitters, radar pulse transmitters, RFID readers, microwave ovens (magnetron driver stages), and RF heating equipment.

## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Maximum Efficiency of Class B Complementary Symmetry Amplifier

**Given:**
- Complementary pair Q₁ (NPN) and Q₂ (PNP) in push-pull emitter-follower configuration.
- Load resistance $R_L$ connected between the common emitter node and ground (AC-coupled).
- Supply rails $\pm V_{CC}$ (or $+V_{CC}$ with coupling capacitor).
- Sinusoidal input of peak amplitude $V_m$.

**Step 1 — Output Voltage and Current Expressions**

The output voltage across the load is (assuming ideal transistors, no crossover distortion):

$$v_{o}(t) = V_m \sin(\omega t)$$

The instantaneous load current is:

$$i_{o}(t) = \dfrac{v_{o}(t)}{R_L} = \dfrac{V_m \sin(\omega t)}{R_L}$$

**Step 2 — Peak and RMS Values**

Peak output voltage: $V_{o(peak)} = V_m$

Peak output current: $I_{o(peak)} = \dfrac{V_m}{R_L}$

RMS output voltage: $V_{o(rms)} = \dfrac{V_m}{\sqrt{2}}$

RMS output current: $I_{o(rms)} = \dfrac{V_m}{\sqrt{2}\, R_L}$

**Step 3 — Maximum AC Output Power $P_{o(max)}$**

For maximum power transfer, we let the output swing to its limits: $V_m = V_{CC}$ (ignoring small $V_{BE}$ drops).

$$
P_{o(max)} = \dfrac{V_{o(rms)}^{\,2}}{R_L} = \dfrac{V_{m}^{\,2}}{2 R_L} = \dfrac{V_{CC}^{\,2}}{2 R_L}
$$

**Step 4 — Average DC Supply Current $I_{DC}$**

Each transistor supplies a half-sine current pulse. The *average* current drawn from the $V_{CC}$ supply (only one half-cycle per cycle) is:

$$
I_{DC(\text{per rail})} = \dfrac{1}{2\pi} \int_{0}^{\pi} \dfrac{V_{CC} \sin(\theta)}{R_L}\, d\theta = \dfrac{V_{CC}}{2\pi R_L} \bigl[-\cos(\theta)\bigr]_{0}^{\pi}
$$

$$
I_{DC(\text{per rail})} = \dfrac{V_{CC}}{2\pi R_L} \bigl[(+1) - (-1)\bigr] = \dfrac{V_{CC}}{2\pi R_L} \cdot 2 = \dfrac{V_{CC}}{\pi R_L}
$$

Wait — this is per rail. The **total** DC current drawn from **both** supplies (or the average current from a single supply if a coupling capacitor is used) is:

$$
I_{DC(total)} = \dfrac{2 V_{CC}}{\pi R_L}
$$

(Each rail provides this average; the load current is alternately sourced from $+V_{CC}$ via Q₁ and sunk to $-V_{CC}$ via Q₂. From a single supply viewpoint with a coupling cap, the average DC current is the same: $\dfrac{2V_{CC}}{\pi R_L}$ at full output.)

**Step 5 — Total DC Power $P_{DC}$**

$$
P_{DC} = V_{CC} \cdot I_{DC(total)} = V_{CC} \cdot \dfrac{2 V_{CC}}{\pi R_L} = \dfrac{2 V_{CC}^{\,2}}{\pi R_L}
$$

**Step 6 — Maximum Conversion Efficiency $\eta_{max}$**

$$
\eta_{max} = \dfrac{P_{o(max)}}{P_{DC}} = \dfrac{\dfrac{V_{CC}^{\,2}}{2 R_L}}{\dfrac{2 V_{CC}^{\,2}}{\pi R_L}} = \dfrac{V_{CC}^{\,2}}{2 R_L} \cdot \dfrac{\pi R_L}{2 V_{CC}^{\,2}} = \dfrac{\pi}{4}
$$

$$
\boxed{\eta_{max} = \dfrac{\pi}{4} \approx 0.7854 = 78.5\%}
$$

**Step 7 — Power Dissipated per Transistor at Maximum Output**

Power balance:

$$
P_{DC} = P_{o(max)} + P_{D(total)} \Rightarrow P_{D(total)} = P_{DC} - P_{o(max)}
$$

$$
P_{D(total)} = \dfrac{2 V_{CC}^{\,2}}{\pi R_L} - \dfrac{V_{CC}^{\,2}}{2 R_L} = \dfrac{V_{CC}^{\,2}}{R_L}\left(\dfrac{2}{\pi} - \dfrac{1}{2}\right) = \dfrac{V_{CC}^{\,2}}{R_L}\left(\dfrac{4-\pi}{2\pi}\right)
$$

Each transistor dissipates half of this:

$$
P_{D(\text{each})} = \dfrac{V_{CC}^{\,2}}{2 R_L}\left(\dfrac{4-\pi}{2\pi}\right) = \dfrac{V_{CC}^{\,2}(4-\pi)}{4\pi R_L}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Numerical check:** $(4 - \pi) \approx 0.858$, so $P_{D(\text{each})} \approx 0.0683 \cdot \dfrac{V_{CC}^{\,2}}{R_L}$. Compare with $P_{o(max)} = 0.5 \cdot \dfrac{V_{CC}^{\,2}}{R_L}$ — i.e., the transistor dissipates roughly 13.7 % of the total DC power at full output.

---

### 3.2 Derivation: Maximum Power Dissipation Point in Class B (KTU Classic)

**Goal:** Show that maximum transistor dissipation occurs **not** at maximum output, but at $V_m = \dfrac{2V_{CC}}{\pi}$.

**Step 1 — General Power Dissipation Expression**

For arbitrary output amplitude $V_m$:

$$
P_{o} = \dfrac{V_{m}^{\,2}}{2 R_L}, \qquad P_{DC} = \dfrac{2 V_{CC} V_{m}}{\pi R_L}
$$

$$
P_{D} = P_{DC} - P_{o} = \dfrac{2 V_{CC} V_{m}}{\pi R_L} - \dfrac{V_{m}^{\,2}}{2 R_L}
$$

**Step 2 — Differentiate with Respect to $V_m$ and Set to Zero**

$$
\dfrac{dP_{D}}{dV_{m}} = \dfrac{2 V_{CC}}{\pi R_L} - \dfrac{2 V_{m}}{2 R_L} = \dfrac{2 V_{CC}}{\pi R_L} - \dfrac{V_{m}}{R_L} = 0
$$

$$
V_{m} = \dfrac{2 V_{CC}}{\pi}
$$

**Step 3 — Substitute Back to Find $P_{D(max)}$**

$$
P_{D(max)} = \dfrac{2 V_{CC}}{\pi R_L}\left(\dfrac{2 V_{CC}}{\pi}\right) - \dfrac{1}{2 R_L}\left(\dfrac{2 V_{CC}}{\pi}\right)^{2}
$$

$$
P_{D(max)} = \dfrac{4 V_{CC}^{\,2}}{\pi^{2} R_L} - \dfrac{2 V_{CC}^{\,2}}{\pi^{2} R_L} = \dfrac{2 V_{CC}^{\,2}}{\pi^{2} R_L}
$$

This is the **total** dissipation (both transistors). Per transistor:

$$
\boxed{P_{D(\text{peak, each})} = \dfrac{V_{CC}^{\,2}}{\pi^{2} R_L} \approx 0.1013 \cdot \dfrac{V_{CC}^{\,2}}{R_L}}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Favourite:** "At what output voltage does the transistor dissipate maximum power?" — Answer: $V_{m} = \dfrac{2V_{CC}}{\pi} \approx 0.636\,V_{CC}$, **not** at $V_m = V_{CC}$. This is a classic viva and 2-mark question.

---

### 3.3 Derivation: Class C Power and Efficiency

**Given:**
- Conduction half-angle $\theta_c$ (so conduction angle = $2\theta_c$).
- Peak collector current pulse: $I_{pk}$.
- DC supply: $V_{CC}$, tuned load $R_L$ (the tank's dynamic resistance at resonance).

**Step 1 — Average DC Supply Current**

The collector current is a series of cosine-shaped pulses. For an ideal Class C with current pulse approximated as:

$$
i_{C}(\theta) = I_{pk}\left(\dfrac{\cos\theta - \cos\theta_{c}}{1 - \cos\theta_{c}}\right) \quad \text{for } \vert\theta\vert \leq \theta_{c}
$$

The average over a full cycle is:

$$
I_{DC} = \dfrac{1}{2\pi} \int_{-\theta_{c}}^{+\theta_{c}} i_{C}(\theta)\, d\theta
$$

After integration (standard KTU result):

$$
I_{DC} = \dfrac{I_{pk}}{\pi}\left(\dfrac{\sin\theta_{c} - \theta_{c}\cos\theta_{c}}{1 - \cos\theta_{c}}\right)
$$

**Step 2 — Fundamental Component Amplitude (Fourier coefficient $a_1$)**

The amplitude of the fundamental (i.e., the output sine amplitude $I_{1}$ flowing into the tank) is:

$$
I_{1} = \dfrac{1}{\pi} \int_{-\theta_{c}}^{+\theta_{c}} i_{C}(\theta)\cos\theta\, d\theta
$$

Standard result:

$$
I_{1} = \dfrac{I_{pk}}{\pi}\left(\dfrac{\theta_{c} - \sin\theta_{c}\cos\theta_{c}}{1 - \cos\theta_{c}}\right)
$$

**Step 3 — Output Power, DC Power, Efficiency**

$$
P_{o} = \dfrac{I_{1}^{\,2} R_L}{2}, \qquad P_{DC} = V_{CC} \cdot I_{DC}
$$

$$
\eta = \dfrac{P_{o}}{P_{DC}} = \dfrac{I_{1}^{\,2} R_L}{2 V_{CC} I_{DC}}
$$

For ideal Class C with $\theta_{c} \to 0$ (impulse train), $I_{DC} \to 0$ while $P_{o}$ remains finite (because each pulse is very tall and narrow, but the tank integrates the energy), so:

$$
\boxed{\eta_{max} = 100\% \quad (\text{as } \theta_{c} \to 0)}
$$

**Step 4 — Numerical Example (KTU Textbook Pattern)**

For $\theta_c = 60°$ (conduction angle = 120°):

$$
I_{DC} = \dfrac{I_{pk}}{\pi}\left(\dfrac{\sin 60° - 60°\cos 60°}{1 - \cos 60°}\right) = \dfrac{I_{pk}}{\pi}\left(\dfrac{0.866 - 0.524}{0.5}\right) \approx \dfrac{0.684\, I_{pk}}{\pi} \approx 0.218\, I_{pk}
$$

$$
I_{1} = \dfrac{I_{pk}}{\pi}\left(\dfrac{60° - \sin 60°\cos 60°}{0.5}\right) = \dfrac{I_{pk}}{\pi}\left(\dfrac{1.047 - 0.433}{0.5}\right) \approx \dfrac{1.228\, I_{pk}}{\pi} \approx 0.391\, I_{pk}
$$

Assuming $V_{CC}$ is held such that $V_{o(peak)} = I_1 R_L$ and the tank's average voltage approximates $V_{CC}$ (for high $Q$ and small $\theta_c$):

$$
\eta \approx \dfrac{I_{1}}{2 I_{DC}} = \dfrac{0.391}{2 \cdot 0.218} \approx 0.897 = 89.7\%
$$

This confirms the practical Class C efficiency of $\approx$ 80–90 %.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix (KTU Quick Reference)

| Parameter | Class A (Transformerless) | Class B (Comp. Sym.) | Class C (Tuned) |
|:----------|:--------------------------:|:--------------------:|:----------------:|
| Conduction angle | 360° | 180° | < 180° |
| Position of Q-point | Centre of load line | At cutoff | Below cutoff |
| Theoretical $\eta_{max}$ | 50 % | **78.5 % (π/4)** | **100 %** |
| Practical $\eta$ | 25–35 % | 70–78 % | 80–90 % |
| Distortion | Lowest | Moderate (crossover) | Highest (filtered) |
| Load | Resistive (or transformer) | Resistive $R_L$ | **Tuned LC tank** |
| Typical application | Hi-fi preamps | Audio power output | RF transmitters |
| Need for cooling? | Large heatsink | Moderate | Smallest heatsink |

### 4.1 Complementary Symmetry (Class B) — Block-Level Functional Architecture

```mermaid
graph TD

    Q2PNP -.Conducts during negative half cycle.-> NOTE2[Note Q2 ON when V IN less than minus 0.7 V]
    BASE -.Dead zone minus 0.7 to plus 0.7 V.-> CD[Region of Crossover Distortion]
```

```mermaid
graph TD
    IN[Input V IN] --> BIAS[Base Bias Network Diodes or VBE Multiplier]
    BIAS --> BASENODE[Common Base Connection]
    BASENODE --> Q1S[NPN Q1 Collector to VCC]
    BASENODE --> Q2S[PNP Q2 Collector to Ground]
    Q1S --> ENODE[Common Emitter Node]
    Q2S --> ENODE
    ENODE --> CAPC[Coupling Capacitor C out]
    CAPC --> RLOAD[Load R L]
    RLOAD --> GND1[Ground]
    ENODE -.DC bias sets both ON slightly.-> CLASSAB[Class AB Mode Reduces Crossover Distortion]
    VCCSUPPLY[V CC Single Supply] --> Q1S
    GNDSUPPLY[Ground] --> Q2S
    GNDSUPPLY --> RLOAD
```

### 4.3 Class C Power Amplifier — Functional Block Architecture

```mermaid
graph TD
    VSIG[RF Input Signal V IN at f0] --> BIASC[Bias Network Sets VBB Below Cutoff]
    BIASC --> BASE[Transistor Base]
    VCC[DC Supply V CC] --> COLL[Transistor Collector]
    BASE -.Turns ON only for short interval.-> PULSE[Narrow Current Pulse I C]
    COLL --> TANK[Parallel LC Tank Circuit]
    PULSE --> TANK
    TANK --> OUTFILTER[Tuned at f0 Selects Fundamental]
    OUTFILTER --> RLOAD[Load R L in Parallel with Tank]
    TANK --> VOUTRF[Sinusoidal Output V OUT at f0]
    LCVALUE[L and C chosen so f0 equals 1 over 2 pi square root LC] -.Defines operating frequency.-> TANK
    QFACTOR[High Q Tank Rejects Harmonics] -.Tuned circuit behaviour.-> OUTFILTER
    FLYWK[Flywheel Action LC Rings Between Pulses] -.Sustains oscillation.-> TANK
```

```mermaid
    HALF --> CRESIST
    CRESIST --> OUTB[Reconstructed Full Sine Output]
    CLASSC --> NARROW[Transistor ON for less than 180 degrees]
    CLASSC --> CTANK[LC Tank Load]
    NARROW --> CTANK
    CTANK --> OUTC[Sinusoidal Output at Resonant Frequency]

```
