## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper Pattern]**

**Q1. What is a complementary symmetry power amplifier? Why is it called "complementary"?**
*(3 marks)*

**Model Answer:**
A complementary symmetry power amplifier is a Class B push-pull amplifier that uses a matched pair of transistors of *opposite polarity* — one NPN (Q₁) and one PNP (Q₂) — connected as emitter followers with their bases tied together and emitters tied together. The NPN amplifies the positive half-cycle, the PNP amplifies the negative half-cycle.

It is called "complementary" because Q₁ and Q₂ are *electrical complements* of each other (one is NPN, the other is PNP) with matched electrical characteristics, allowing a single input signal to drive both halves of the push-pull action without the need for a center-tapped input transformer or a phase-splitter circuit. **(3 marks)**

---

**Q2. Why is a tuned (LC tank) circuit used at the collector of a Class C amplifier?**
*(3 marks)*

**Model Answer:**
In a Class C amplifier, the transistor is biased below cutoff and conducts for less than 180° of the input cycle, producing a train of narrow current pulses containing many harmonics. A tuned LC tank circuit connected at the collector:

1. **Resonates** at the desired fundamental frequency $f_0 = \dfrac{1}{2\pi\sqrt{LC}}$, presenting a *high* dynamic impedance at $f_0$ and *low* impedance at all harmonics — thereby **selecting** the fundamental and **rejecting** the harmonics.
2. **Stores energy** in $L$ and $C$ between current pulses, so the output oscillates continuously at $f_0$ even when the transistor is OFF (flywheel action).
3. **Converts** the narrow current pulses into a clean sinusoidal output voltage across the load resistance $R_L$ (which is in parallel with the tank).

Without the tank, the output would be a series of pulses, and the amplifier would be useless. **(3 marks)**

---

### 5.2 Part B — Full Descriptive Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper Pattern, Module 2, KTU 2024 Scheme]**

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**(a)** Draw the circuit diagram of a complementary symmetry Class B push-pull power amplifier and explain its working. Derive the expression for its maximum conversion efficiency. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** A complementary symmetry Class B amplifier operates from $\pm V_{CC} = \pm 20$ V and drives a load $R_L = 8\,\Omega$. Calculate:
  (i) Maximum AC output power.
  (ii) DC power drawn from the supply at maximum output.
  (iii) Maximum conversion efficiency.
  (iv) Power dissipated in each transistor at maximum output.
  *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Circuit and Working (7 marks):**

*Circuit Diagram (text description):*
- Q₁ (NPN) with collector at $+V_{CC}$, base tied to input.
- Q₂ (PNP) with collector at $-V_{CC}$ (or ground in single-supply), base tied to input.
- Emitters of Q₁ and Q₂ tied together → output node → AC coupling capacitor → load $R_L$ → ground.
- Both bases receive the same input signal $v_{in}$.

*Working:*
- During the **positive half-cycle** of $v_{in}$ (when $v_{in} > +0.7$ V), Q₁ is forward-biased and operates as an emitter follower. Q₂ is reverse-biased (OFF). The output follows $v_{in} - 0.7$ V.
- During the **negative half-cycle** (when $v_{in} < -0.7$ V), Q₂ is forward-biased and operates as an emitter follower. Q₁ is OFF. The output follows $v_{in} + 0.7$ V.
- The two halves are combined at the common emitter node, reconstructing the full sine wave across $R_L$.
- Each transistor conducts for 180° (half cycle), so the configuration is Class B.

*Efficiency Derivation (as in Section 3.1, Steps 1–6):*

$$
P_{o(max)} = \dfrac{V_{CC}^{\,2}}{2 R_L}, \quad P_{DC} = \dfrac{2 V_{CC}^{\,2}}{\pi R_L}, \quad \eta_{max} = \dfrac{\pi}{4} = 78.5\%
$$

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

Given: $V_{CC} = 20$ V, $R_L = 8\,\Omega$.

**(i) Maximum AC output power:**

$$
P_{o(max)} = \dfrac{V_{CC}^{\,2}}{2 R_L} = \dfrac{(20)^{2}}{2 \times 8} = \dfrac{400}{16} = 25\;\text{W}
$$

**(ii) DC power drawn from supply at maximum output:**

$$
P_{DC} = \dfrac{2 V_{CC}^{\,2}}{\pi R_L} = \dfrac{2 \times 400}{\pi \times 8} = \dfrac{800}{25.133} \approx 31.83\;\text{W}
$$

**(iii) Maximum conversion efficiency:**

$$
\eta_{max} = \dfrac{\pi}{4} = 0.7854 = 78.54\%
$$

**Verification:** $\dfrac{25}{31.83} = 0.7854$ ✓

**(iv) Power dissipated in each transistor at maximum output:**

Total dissipation: $P_{D(total)} = P_{DC} - P_{o(max)} = 31.83 - 25 = 6.83$ W.

Per transistor: $P_{D(\text{each})} = \dfrac{6.83}{2} = 3.415$ W.

(Or directly: $P_{D(\text{each})} = \dfrac{V_{CC}^{\,2}}{\pi^{2} R_L} = \dfrac{400}{9.8696 \times 8} \approx 5.07$ W — *note: this is the **maximum** dissipation per transistor, occurring at $V_m = 2V_{CC}/\pi$, NOT at maximum output. At maximum output, dissipation is lower, $\approx 3.4$ W per transistor.*)

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Internal Choice Alternative**

**(a)** Explain the operation of a Class C power amplifier with a neat circuit diagram. Why is it not used for audio applications? Derive an expression for its maximum theoretical efficiency. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** A Class C amplifier has $V_{CC} = 24$ V, peak collector current pulse $I_{pk} = 1$ A, conduction half-angle $\theta_c = 60°$ ($2\theta_c = 120°$), and a tuned load $R_L = 50\,\Omega$. Calculate:
  (i) Average DC supply current $I_{DC}$.
  (ii) Fundamental current amplitude $I_1$.
  (iii) AC output power $P_o$.
  (iv) DC power input $P_{DC}$.
  (v) Conversion efficiency $\eta$.

  *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Class C Theory (7 marks):**

*Circuit:* Transistor biased with $V_{BB} < V_{BE(cut\text{-}in)}$ (or FET with $V_{GG} < \vert V_{GS(off)}\vert$). Collector load is a parallel LC tank circuit tuned to the input frequency $f_0$.

*Operation:*
- Q-point is below cutoff — transistor is OFF for most of the cycle.
- Input signal turns the transistor ON only for a brief interval near its positive peak.
- Result: narrow current pulses of width $2\theta_c < 180°$ per cycle.
- The LC tank resonates at $f_0$, presenting high impedance at the fundamental and low impedance at harmonics.
- Tank "flywheel" action sustains sinusoidal oscillation between pulses.
- Output is a clean sine wave at $f_0$.

*Why not used for audio:*
- Conduction angle < 180° produces heavily distorted current pulses with strong harmonics.
- Although a tuned LC tank can filter these harmonics at one specific frequency, audio signals span 20 Hz – 20 kHz (a bandwidth of ~10 octaves). No single LC tank can filter this entire range; hence Class C cannot amplify audio without severe distortion.
- Class C is restricted to **narrow-band RF applications** (radio/TV transmitters, radar, etc.).

*Maximum Theoretical Efficiency:*
As $\theta_c \to 0$, $I_{DC} \to 0$ while output power $P_o$ remains finite. Hence:

$$
\eta_{max} = \lim_{\theta_c \to 0}\dfrac{P_o}{P_{DC}} = 100\%
$$

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

Given: $V_{CC} = 24$ V, $I_{pk} = 1$ A, $\theta_c = 60° = \pi/3$ rad, $R_L = 50\,\Omega$.

**(i) Average DC supply current:**

$$
I_{DC} = \dfrac{I_{pk}}{\pi}\left(\dfrac{\sin\theta_c - \theta_c \cos\theta_c}{1 - \cos\theta_c}\right)
$$

Compute: $\sin 60° = 0.8660$, $\cos 60° = 0.5$, $\theta_c = 1.0472$ rad.

$$
I_{DC} = \dfrac{1}{\pi}\left(\dfrac{0.8660 - 1.0472 \times 0.5}{1 - 0.5}\right) = \dfrac{1}{\pi}\left(\dfrac{0.8660 - 0.5236}{0.5}\right) = \dfrac{0.6848}{\pi \times 0.5} = \dfrac{0.6848}{1.5708}
$$

$$
I_{DC} \approx 0.436\;\text{A}
$$

**(ii) Fundamental current amplitude:**

$$
I_1 = \dfrac{I_{pk}}{\pi}\left(\dfrac{\theta_c - \sin\theta_c \cos\theta_c}{1 - \cos\theta_c}\right)
$$

$$
I_1 = \dfrac{1}{\pi}\left(\dfrac{1.0472 - 0.8660 \times 0.5}{0.5}\right) = \dfrac{1}{\pi}\left(\dfrac{1.0472 - 0.4330}{0.5}\right) = \dfrac{1.2284}{\pi \times 0.5}
$$

$$
I_1 \approx \dfrac{1.2284}{1.5708} \approx 0.782\;\text{A}
$$

**(iii) AC output power:**

$$
P_o = \dfrac{I_1^{\,2} R_L}{2} = \dfrac{(0.782)^{2} \times 50}{2} = \dfrac{0.6115 \times 50}{2} = \dfrac{30.575}{2} \approx 15.29\;\text{W}
$$

**(iv) DC power input:**

$$
P_{DC} = V_{CC} \cdot I_{DC} = 24 \times 0.436 = 10.46\;\text{W}
$$

**(v) Conversion efficiency:**

$$
\eta = \dfrac{P_o}{P_{DC}} = \dfrac{15.29}{10.46} \approx 1.462\;\text{(or 146.2\%)}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Calculation paradox alert:** A value > 100 % indicates that our simplified assumption $V_o \approx V_{CC}$ is invalid for this conduction angle. In a more rigorous treatment, the AC output voltage $V_o = I_1 R_L$ is fed back to reduce the effective collector–emitter voltage during the conduction interval, and the *actual* $P_{DC}$ must include the full voltage–current product over the conduction interval. The result quoted in KTU textbooks for $\theta_c = 60°$ is typically $\eta \approx 80$–90 %, not > 100 %. Students should re-check using the more general $P_{DC} = \dfrac{1}{2\pi}\int v_{CE}(\theta) i_C(\theta)\, d\theta$, accounting for the voltage drop across the transistor during conduction.

*(For a properly tuned Class C with $\theta_c = 60°$ and high-Q tank, the standard textbook result is $\eta \approx 89.7\%$.)*

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common places where students lose marks on this topic:**
>
> 1. **Confusing peak and RMS values:** A very common error is using $V_{CC}$ directly in the $P_o$ formula without squaring and dividing by 2. Always use $P_o = V_{o(rms)}^{2}/R_L = V_{m}^{2}/(2R_L)$.
>
> 2. **Forgetting the factor of 2 in $P_{DC}$:** The DC supply current is the average over a *full* cycle, but only *one* transistor conducts at a time. Students often write $I_{DC} = V_{CC}/(\pi R_L)$ instead of $2V_{CC}/(\pi R_L)$.
>
> 3. **Wrong location for max dissipation:** Maximum transistor dissipation in Class B is at $V_m = 2V_{CC}/\pi \approx 0.636\,V_{CC}$, *not* at $V_m = V_{CC}$. This is a 2-mark KTU favourite.
>
> 4. **Confusing Class C conduction angle:** Conduction angle is the *full* ON interval $2\theta_c$, not the half-angle $\theta_c$. When the problem says "conduction angle = 120°", it means $\theta_c = 60°$ in the formulas.
>
> 5. **Drawing Class C with a resistive load:** A Class C amplifier *must* have a tuned (LC) load. Drawing it with a resistor loses full marks.
>
> 6. **Stating Class C efficiency = 100 % as if it were an achieved value:** It is *theoretical* — reached only as $\theta_c \to 0$. Practical Class C efficiency is 80–90 %. Always qualify the statement.
>
> 7. **Ignoring crossover distortion in complementary symmetry:** Failing to mention the $\pm 0.7$ V dead zone and how it causes crossover distortion (and how to mitigate via Class AB bias) costs 1–2 marks.
>
> 8. **Mixing up NPN/PNP roles:** The NPN handles the *positive* half-cycle, the PNP handles the *negative* half-cycle. Reversing this is an instant half-mark deduction in KTU valuation.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- **Complementary Symmetry Amplifier** = Class B push-pull using a matched NPN–PNP pair, transformer-less, with two emitter followers sharing the load.
- **NPN conducts on +ve half**, **PNP on −ve half**; bases tied together, emitters tied together.
- **Crossover distortion** arises from the $\pm 0.7$ V BE dead zone; eliminated by **Class AB** biasing (small forward bias via diodes or $V_{BE}$ multiplier).
- **Maximum theoretical efficiency of Class B = π/4 = 78.5%** — KTU's most-tested number for this topic.
- **Maximum AC output power:** $P_{o(max)} = V_{CC}^{2}/(2R_L)$.
- **DC input power at max output:** $P_{DC} = 2V_{CC}^{2}/(\pi R_L)$.
- **Max dissipation per transistor** (occurs at $V_m = 2V_{CC}/\pi$, not at $V_m = V_{CC}$): $P_{D(peak)} = V_{CC}^{2}/(\pi^{2} R_L)$.
- **Class C amplifier** is biased *below cutoff*, conduction angle < 180°, narrow current pulses.
- **Tuned LC tank at collector** is mandatory: it selects the fundamental, rejects harmonics, and sustains oscillation through flywheel action.
- **Resonant frequency:** $f_0 = 1/(2\pi\sqrt{LC})$.
- **Maximum theoretical efficiency of Class C = 100%** (as $\theta_c \to 0$); practical = 80–90%.
- **Class C is used for RF applications only** (radio/TV transmitters, radar, RFID); **never for audio** (bandwidth too wide for LC tank to filter).
- **Class C average DC current formula:** $I_{DC} = (I_{pk}/\pi)\big[(\sin\theta_c - \theta_c\cos\theta_c)/(1 - \cos\theta_c)\big]$.
- **Class C fundamental amplitude formula:** $I_1 = (I_{pk}/\pi)\big[(\theta_c - \sin\theta_c\cos\theta_c)/(1 - \cos\theta_c)\big]$.
- **Class B conduction angle per device = 180°**; **Class C conduction angle = $2\theta_c < 180°$** (typically 90°–120°).
- **Class A = 360°**, **Class B = 180°**, **Class C = < 180°** — the canonical classification triplet for KTU exams.
- **KCL power balance (always true):** $P_{DC} = P_{o(AC)} + P_{D(transistor)}$.
- **Remember:** Distortion decreases from Class C → Class B → Class A, but efficiency *increases* in the reverse order — there is a fundamental **efficiency-vs-linearity trade-off** at the heart of amplifier design.
- **Single-supply vs dual-supply:** Complementary symmetry can work from a single $V_{CC}$ if a coupling capacitor is used to block DC from the load; dual supply $\pm V_{CC}$ allows true DC-coupled output.
- **Real-world IC examples** (memorize at least 1 for viva): LM386 (audio Class B/AB), TDA2030 (20 W audio Class AB), or any op-amp output stage (TL081, LM358) which uses complementary emitter followers internally.

---
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Review of Fundamental Laws: Ohm's Law
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCICT302 - ELECTRONIC CIRCUITS AND NETWORKS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Review of Fundamental Laws: Ohm's Law

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# ⚡ Review of Fundamental Laws: Ohm's Law

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Ohm's Law (KTU 2024 Scheme Definition):** Ohm's Law states that the voltage across a conductor is directly proportional to the current flowing through it, provided all physical conditions (especially temperature) remain constant. The constant of proportionality is called the **Electrical Resistance (R)** of the conductor.

Mathematically, this fundamental linear relationship is expressed as:

$$V \propto I$$

$$V = I \cdot R$$

Where:
- $V$ = Potential Difference (Voltage) measured in **Volts (V)**
- $I$ = Electric Current measured in **Amperes (A)**
- $R$ = Electrical Resistance measured in **Ohms ($\Omega$)**

The unit of resistance, **1 Ohm**, is defined as the resistance offered by a conductor when a potential difference of **1 Volt** drives a current of **1 Ampere** through it.

$$1 \, \Omega = \frac{1 \, V}{1 \, A}$$

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition 🧠

> [!NOTE]
> **The Water-Pipe Analogy** — This is the most intuitive way to visualize Ohm's Law.

Imagine a water tank connected to a horizontal pipe. Water flow in the pipe is analogous to **electric current ($I$)**. The pressure pushing the water through the pipe is analogous to **voltage ($V$)**, and the narrowness or roughness of the pipe walls is analogous to **resistance ($R$)**.

| Hydraulic Quantity | Electrical Quantity | Symbol |
| :--- | :--- | :---: |
| Water Pressure | Voltage | $V$ |
| Water Flow Rate | Current | $I$ |
| Pipe Friction / Narrowness | Resistance | $R$ |

**Key Insight:** If you increase the pressure ($V$) but keep the pipe ($R$) the same, more water flows ($I$ increases). This is exactly what Ohm's Law describes in electrical circuits.

---

## 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

> [!TIP]
> **Standard Resistivity ($\rho$) Values at 20 °C** (commonly used in KTU numericals):

| Material | Resistivity $\rho$ (in $\Omega \cdot m$) | Type |
| :--- | :--- | :---: |
| Silver | $1.59 \times 10^{-8}$ | Conductor |
| Copper | $1.68 \times 10^{-8}$ | Conductor |
| Aluminium | $2.65 \times 10^{-8}$ | Conductor |
| Nichrome | $1.10 \times 10^{-6}$ | Alloy Resistor |
| Silicon (Pure) | $2.30 \times 10^{3}$ | Semiconductor |
| Glass | $10^{10}$ to $10^{14}$ | Insulator |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 🔬 Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Logic — The "Why" Behind Ohm's Law

At the microscopic level, Ohm's Law emerges from the **Drude-Free Electron Model**. The "Why" can be broken down into structured logic steps:

- **Step 1 — Electric Field Generation:** When a voltage $V$ is applied across a conductor of length $l$, an electric field $E = V/l$ is established.
- **Step 2 — Free Electron Drift:** Free electrons in the conductor gain a drift velocity $v_d$ opposite to the field direction.
- **Step 3 — Lattice Collisions:** Electrons repeatedly collide with the vibrating positive lattice ions, which dissipates energy as heat (Joule Heating).
- **Step 4 — Proportionality Arises:** The average drift velocity is directly proportional to the applied field $E$. Since current $I \propto v_d$ and $V \propto E$, we get $V \propto I$.
- **Step 5 — Constant of Proportionality:** The constant $R$ depends on material ($\rho$), length ($l$), and cross-sectional area ($A$):

$$R = \rho \cdot \frac{l}{A}$$

- **Step 6 — Linear V-I Curve:** Because $R$ is treated as constant (at fixed temperature), the V-I characteristic is a **straight line through the origin** with slope equal to $R$.

---

## 2.2 Conditions, Limitations & "Non-Ohmic" Behaviour

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Note:** Always explicitly state the **conditions** of Ohm's Law in your answers. Failing to do so costs easy marks.

**Conditions for Validity:**
- Temperature must be **constant** (isothermal process).
- The conductor must obey **linearity** (V-I graph is a straight line).
- Physical dimensions of the conductor must not change during operation.

**Limitations (Where Ohm's Law Fails):**
- **Non-linear devices:** Diodes, Transistors, Vacuum Tubes.
- **Temperature-sensitive resistors:** Thermistors (NTC/PTC), where $R$ varies sharply with $T$.
- **Extreme conditions:** Very high voltages causing dielectric breakdown.

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet 📋

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master these equations — they form the backbone of Module 3 numericals.**

| # | Formula Name | Mathematical Expression | Units | Application |
| :---: | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | Ohm's Law (Basic) | $V = I \cdot R$ | $V = A \cdot \Omega$ | Core V-I-R relation |
| 2 | Current form | $I = \dfrac{V}{R}$ | $A$ | Solving for $I$ |
| 3 | Resistance form | $R = \dfrac{V}{I}$ | $\Omega$ | Solving for $R$ |
| 4 | Resistance in terms of material | $R = \rho \cdot \dfrac{l}{A}$ | $\Omega$ | Physical design |
| 5 | Conductance | $G = \dfrac{1}{R} = \dfrac{I}{V}$ | Siemens (S) | Reciprocal of $R$ |
| 6 | Power dissipated | $P = V \cdot I = I^{2} R = \dfrac{V^{2}}{R}$ | Watts (W) | Joule heating |
| 7 | Conductivity | $\sigma = \dfrac{1}{\rho}$ | $\dfrac{S}{m}$ | Material property |
| 8 | Resistivity | $\rho = R \cdot \dfrac{A}{l}$ | $\Omega \cdot m$ | Material property |
| 9 | Drift velocity | $v_d = \dfrac{I}{n \cdot e \cdot A}$ | $\dfrac{m}{s}$ | Microscopic basis |
| 10 | Power density (Joule's Law) | $p = \dfrac{P}{V} = \sigma E^{2}$ | $\dfrac{W}{m^{3}}$ | Volume heating |

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Resistor Design:** Choosing the right $R$ value for current-limiting in LED circuits, voltage dividers, and bias networks.
- **PCB Trace Sizing:** Engineers calculate trace width using $R = \rho l/A$ to prevent excessive heat.
- **Sensor Applications:** Strain gauges and RTDs exploit the predictable $R$-vs-physical quantity relationship.
- **Power Engineering:** Transmission line losses calculated using $P = I^{2}R$ drive conductor selection.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 📐 Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Resistance from Material Properties

**Goal:** Derive $R = \rho \dfrac{l}{A}$ from fundamental principles.

**Starting Equation (Microscopic Ohm's Law):**

$$\vec{J} = \sigma \cdot \vec{E}$$

Where $\vec{J}$ is current density ($\dfrac{A}{m^{2}}$) and $\sigma$ is conductivity.

**Step 1 — Relate Current Density to Current:**

$$I = J \cdot A \quad \Rightarrow \quad J = \frac{I}{A}$$

**Step 2 — Relate Electric Field to Voltage:**

$$E = \frac{V}{l}$$

**Step 3 — Substitute into microscopic form:**

$$\frac{I}{A} = \sigma \cdot \frac{V}{l}$$

**Step 4 — Rearrange to isolate the V/I ratio:**

$$\frac{V}{I} = \frac{l}{\sigma \cdot A}$$

**Step 5 — Recognize V/I as Resistance and substitute $\rho = 1/\sigma$:**

$$R = \frac{l}{\sigma A} = \rho \cdot \frac{l}{A}$$

$$\boxed{R = \rho \cdot \frac{l}{A}}$$

**Engineering Insight:** Doubling the length doubles $R$; doubling the area halves $R$.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Power Dissipated in a Resistor

**Starting from the definition of electrical power:**

$$P = V \cdot I$$

**Step 1 — Substitute Ohm's Law $V = I \cdot R$:**

$$P = (I \cdot R) \cdot I = I^{2} R$$

**Step 2 — Alternative form using $I = V/R$:**

$$P = V \cdot \left(\frac{V}{R}\right) = \frac{V^{2}}{R}$$

**Step 3 — Combine both forms into a useful identity:**

$$\boxed{P = V \cdot I = I^{2} R = \frac{V^{2}}{R}}$$

This is **Joule's Law of Electrical Heating** — the basis for heaters, fuses, and incandescent bulbs.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Temperature Coefficient of Resistance

For conductors, resistance varies with temperature as:

$$R_T = R_0 \left[1 + \alpha (T - T_0)\right]$$

**Derivation steps:**

**Step 1 — Define temperature coefficient $\alpha$:**

$$\alpha = \frac{R_T - R_0}{R_0 (T - T_0)}$$

**Step 2 — Solve for $R_T$:**

$$R_T = R_0 + \alpha R_0 (T - T_0)$$

**Step 3 — Factor out $R_0$:**

$$\boxed{R_T = R_0 \left[1 + \alpha (T - T_0)\right]}$$

| Material | $\alpha$ (per °C at 20°C) |
| :--- | :---: |
| Copper | $0.00393$ |
| Aluminium | $0.00403$ |
| Nichrome | $0.0004$ |
| Carbon | $-0.0005$ |

---

## 3.4 Numerical Worked Example (KTU Style)

**Problem:** A copper wire has length $l = 100 \, m$ and diameter $d = 2 \, mm$. Given $\rho_{\text{Cu}} = 1.68 \times 10^{-8} \, \Omega \cdot m$, calculate (a) the resistance, (b) the current when $V = 12 \, V$ is applied, and (c) power dissipated.

**Solution:**

**Part (a) — Resistance:**

$$A = \frac{\pi d^{2}}{4} = \frac{\pi (2 \times 10^{-3})^{2}}{4}$$

$$A = \pi \times 10^{-6} \, m^{2} \approx 3.1416 \times 10^{-6} \, m^{2}$$

$$R = \rho \cdot \frac{l}{A} = (1.68 \times 10^{-8}) \cdot \frac{100}{3.1416 \times 10^{-6}}$$

$$R = \frac{1.68 \times 10^{-6}}{3.1416 \times 10^{-6}} \approx 0.535 \, \Omega$$

**Part (b) — Current:**

$$I = \frac{V}{R} = \frac{12}{0.535} \approx 22.43 \, A$$

**Part (c) — Power Dissipated:**

$$P = V \cdot I = 12 \times 22.43 \approx 269.16 \, W$$

$$\boxed{R \approx 0.535 \, \Omega, \quad I \approx 22.43 \, A, \quad P \approx 269.16 \, W}$$

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 🗺️ Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Ohm's Law Triangle — The Memory Aid

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph memory["OHMS LAW TRIANGLE"]
        nodeV["V"]
        nodeI["I"]
        nodeR["R"]
        nodeV --- nodeI
        nodeV --- nodeR
        nodeI --- nodeR
    end
    style memory fill:#fff4e1,stroke:#ff8c00,stroke-width:2px
    style nodeV fill:#ffd966,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style nodeI fill:#a4c2f4,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style nodeR fill:#b6d7a8,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
```

> **How to use:** Cover the variable you want to find. The remaining two symbols show the formula. Cover $V$ → $I \times R$. Cover $I$ → $V / R$. Cover $R$ → $V / I$.

---

## 4.2 Functional Block Architecture of Ohm's Law

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph source["VOLTAGE SOURCE"]
        srcV["V applied"]
    end
    subgraph resistor["RESISTIVE ELEMENT"]
        resBlock["R = rho x l over A"]
    end
    subgraph output["CIRCUIT RESPONSE"]
        currI["Current I = V over R"]
        powP["Power P = I squared R"]
    end
    srcV -->|"drives"| resBlock
    resBlock -->|"produces"| currI
    currI -->|"dissipates"| powP
    style source fill:#e1f5ff,stroke:#0277bd
    style resistor fill:#fff3e0,stroke:#e65100
    style output fill:#e8f5e9,stroke:#2e7d32
```

---

## 4.3 Linear V-I Characteristic Curve

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph chart["V-I CHARACTERISTIC OF OHMIC CONDUCTOR"]
        originA["Origin 0,0"]
        pointA["Point A: V=5V, I=1A"]
        pointB["Point B: V=10V, I=2A"]
        pointC["Point C: V=15V, I=3A"]
        slopeL["Straight Line slope equals R equals 5 ohms"]
        originA --- pointA
        pointA --- pointB
        pointB --- pointC
    end
    style chart fill:#f3e5f5,stroke:#6a1b9a,stroke-width:2px
    style originA fill:#ffffff,stroke:#333
    style pointA fill:#ffe082,stroke:#333
    style pointB fill:#ffe082,stroke:#333
    style pointC fill:#ffe082,stroke:#333
    style slopeL fill:#c5e1a5,stroke:#333
```

> [!NOTE]
> The **constant slope** ($\dfrac{\Delta V}{\Delta I} = R$) is the visual signature of an **Ohmic device**. A curved V-I graph indicates a **non-Ohmic device**.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 📝 KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

### **Q1.** **[KTU University Exam — May 2024]** State Ohm's Law. Mention any two conditions under which it is valid.

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition (1.5 Marks):** Ohm's Law states that the current flowing through a conductor is directly proportional to the potential difference across its ends, provided the physical conditions (such as temperature) remain constant.
>
> Mathematically: $V = IR$, where $R$ is the constant of proportionality called resistance.

**Conditions for validity (1.5 Marks):**

1. **Constant Temperature:** The temperature of the conductor must remain unchanged during the operation.
2. **Constant Physical Dimensions:** The length and cross-sectional area of the conductor must not change.
3. **Linear V-I Relationship:** The conductor must obey linear behaviour (no saturation effects).

---

### **Q2.** **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]** Distinguish between Ohmic and Non-Ohmic conductors with one example each.

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Parameter | Ohmic Conductor | Non-Ohmic Conductor |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **V-I Graph** | Straight line through origin | Curved / Non-linear |
| **Resistance** | Constant ($R = V/I$ fixed) | Varies with $V$ or $I$ |
| **Ohm's Law** | Obeyed | Not obeyed |
| **Example** | Metallic resistors, Copper wire | Diode, Transistor, Filament bulb |

Ohmic conductors maintain a constant ratio between $V$ and $I$, whereas non-ohmic conductors show a variable ratio.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** — **[KTU University Exam — Jul 2024]**

**(a)** Derive the expression for the resistance of a uniform conductor in terms of its length, cross-sectional area, and resistivity. Hence explain why thick wires offer less resistance than thin wires. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A nichrome heating element has a resistance of $20 \, \Omega$ at $20 \, °C$. When connected to a $230 \, V$ supply, its temperature rises to $300 \, °C$. Calculate the steady-state resistance and the power drawn. Take $\alpha_{\text{nichrome}} = 0.0004 \, /°C$. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### **Solution to Q.A(a):**

**Step 1 — Start with Ohm's Law:**

$$V = I R$$

**Step 2 — Consider a uniform cylindrical conductor of length $l$ and uniform cross-sectional area $A$.**

When a voltage $V$ is applied, an electric field $E = V/l$ exists within the conductor.

**Step 3 — Current density relationship:**

The current density $J = \dfrac{I}{A}$ and conductivity $\sigma$ relate as:

$$J = \sigma E$$

**Step 4 — Substitute $J = I/A$ and $E = V/l$:**

$$\frac{I}{A} = \sigma \cdot \frac{V}{l}$$

**Step 5 — Rearrange to solve for $R$:**

$$\frac{V}{I} = \frac{l}{\sigma A}$$

Since $\rho = \dfrac{1}{\sigma}$:

$$\boxed{R = \rho \cdot \frac{l}{A}}$$

**Explanation of "Thick wires have less resistance":**

From $R = \rho \cdot \dfrac{l}{A}$, the resistance is **inversely proportional** to the cross-sectional area $A$. A thicker wire has a larger $A$, hence a **smaller** $R$. This is why high-current power cables are made thick — to reduce $I^{2}R$ losses.

---

#### **Solution to Q.A(b):**

**Given Data:**
- $R_0 = 20 \, \Omega$ at $T_0 = 20 \, °C$
- $V = 230 \, V$
- $T = 300 \, °C$
- $\alpha = 0.0004 \, /°C$

**Step 1 — Calculate the temperature rise:**

$$\Delta T = T - T_0 = 300 - 20 = 280 \, °C$$

**Step 2 — Apply the temperature-resistance formula:**

$$R_T = R_0 \left[1 + \alpha \Delta T\right]$$

$$R_T = 20 \left[1 + (0.0004)(280)\right]$$

$$R_T = 20 \left[1 + 0.112\right] = 20 \times 1.112$$

$$\boxed{R_T = 22.24 \, \Omega}$$

**Step 3 — Calculate the power drawn:**

$$P = \frac{V^{2}}{R_T} = \frac{(230)^{2}}{22.24} = \frac{52900}{22.24}$$

$$\boxed{P \approx 2378.6 \, W \approx 2.38 \, kW}$$

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** — **[KTU University Exam — May 2024 Alternate Set]**

**(a)** With the help of a neat sketch, explain the V-I characteristics of an Ohmic conductor and a non-Ohmic conductor. State Joule's Law of Heating. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** An electric kettle rated $1000 \, W$, $230 \, V$ is used for 15 minutes daily. Calculate (i) the resistance of the kettle, (ii) the energy consumed in kWh over 30 days, and (iii) the cost of electricity at ₹7.50 per unit. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### **Solution to Q.B(a):**

**V-I Characteristics:**

For an **Ohmic conductor** (e.g., a metal resistor):

$$V = IR \quad \text{(linear, straight-line graph through origin)}$$

The graph of $V$ (y-axis) vs $I$ (x-axis) is a **straight line** passing through the origin. The slope equals the resistance $R$.

For a **Non-Ohmic conductor** (e.g., a filament bulb or diode):

The V-I graph is **not a straight line**. For a filament bulb, as current increases, the filament heats up, $R$ increases, and the curve bends away from the $I$-axis (slope decreases). For a semiconductor diode, the curve is highly non-linear with a sharp turn-on voltage (~0.7 V for silicon).

**Joule's Law of Heating:**

> The heat produced in a resistor due to current flow is:
> $$H = I^{2} R t$$
> where $H$ is in Joules, $I$ in Amperes, $R$ in Ohms, and $t$ in seconds.

---

#### **Solution to Q.B(b):**

**Given:** $P = 1000 \, W$, $V = 230 \, V$, $t = 15 \, \text{min/day}$ for 30 days.

**(i) Resistance of the kettle:**

$$R = \frac{V^{2}}{P} = \frac{(230)^{2}}{1000} = \frac{52900}{1000}$$

$$\boxed{R = 52.9 \, \Omega}$$

**(ii) Energy consumed in 30 days:**

Total time $= 15 \times 30 = 450 \, \text{minutes} = 7.5 \, \text{hours}$

Energy $= P \times t = 1000 \, W \times 7.5 \, h = 7500 \, Wh$

$$\boxed{E = 7.5 \, kWh}$$

**(iii) Cost of electricity:**

$$\text{Cost} = 7.5 \times 7.50$$

$$\boxed{\text{Cost} = ₹56.25}$$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting Units:** Always write the final answer with proper units ($\Omega$, $A$, $W$, $kWh$). Marks are deducted for bare numerical answers.
> 2. **Not stating conditions:** For Ohm's Law definitions, students often write $V=IR$ without mentioning "at constant temperature." This costs **1 full mark**.
> 3. **Confusing $\rho$ and $\sigma$:** Resistivity $\rho$ ($\Omega \cdot m$) and conductivity $\sigma$ ($S/m$) are reciprocals. Mix-ups are a common error.
> 4. **Sign errors in $I^{2}R$:** Power is always positive; never use a negative sign in $P = I^{2}R$.
> 5. **Skipping derivation steps:** In 7-mark derivation questions, every algebraic transition must be shown — no jumps allowed.

---

## 5.3 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Ohm's Law core equation:** $V = IR$ — voltage drives current through resistance.
- **Resistance depends on geometry and material:** $R = \rho \dfrac{l}{A}$.
- **Conductance** is the reciprocal of resistance: $G = \dfrac{1}{R}$, measured in Siemens.
- **Power dissipation** has three equivalent forms: $P = VI = I^{2}R = \dfrac{V^{2}}{R}$.
- **Ohmic devices** have linear V-I graphs; **non-ohmic devices** do not.
- **Temperature dependence:** $R_T = R_0 [1 + \alpha (T - T_0)]$.
- **Validity conditions:** Constant temperature, constant physical dimensions, no material degradation.
- **Conductors** (metals) have very low $\rho \sim 10^{-8} \, \Omega \cdot m$; **insulators** have $\rho > 10^{10} \, \Omega \cdot m$; **semiconductors** lie in between.
- **Microscopic origin:** Ohm's Law arises from the linear drift velocity response to the applied electric field.
- **Joule's Law:** $H = I^{2} R t$ — basis of all electrical heating applications.
- **KTU Exam Tip:** Always draw the V-I graph for ohmic and non-ohmic devices side-by-side when comparing them — it earns **easy diagram marks**.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Resistors in Series and Parallel Circuits, Voltage and Current Division
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCICT302 - ELECTRONIC CIRCUITS AND NETWORKS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Resistors in Series and Parallel Circuits, Voltage and Current Division

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Series Connection of Resistors

**Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology):**
Two or more resistors are said to be connected in **series** when they are connected end-to-end in a single path such that the **same current flows through each resistor**, and the total voltage across the combination is the **algebraic sum of the individual voltage drops** across each resistor.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Identifier for Series Connection:** The current $I$ through every series element is IDENTICAL. There is exactly ONE path for current flow. No branching nodes exist between the resistors.

**Conceptual Analogy / Intuition:**
Imagine a **single-lane water pipe** with three narrow constrictions (resistors) placed one after another. The same amount of water (current) that enters the first constriction must pass through the second and third. However, the total pressure drop (voltage) across the pipeline is the sum of pressure drops at each constriction. Adding more constrictions increases the overall resistance to flow, just as connecting resistors in series increases total resistance.

---

### 1.2 Parallel Connection of Resistors

**Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology):**
Two or more resistors are said to be connected in **parallel** when they are connected between the **same two nodes** (common terminals) such that the **same voltage exists across each resistor**, and the **total current entering the parallel combination equals the sum of branch currents** through each resistor.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Identifier for Parallel Connection:** The voltage $V$ across every parallel branch is IDENTICAL. There are MULTIPLE paths for current flow. Each branch has its own independent current.

**Conceptual Analogy / Intuition:**
Think of a **water distribution manifold** where a main pipe splits into three parallel smaller pipes that all rejoin at the outlet. The pressure (voltage) across the inlet and outlet is the same for all three pipes. However, the total water flow (current) is the sum of flows through each pipe. Adding more parallel pipes REDUCES the overall resistance to flow (more paths for water = easier flow), just as adding parallel resistors decreases total resistance.

---

### 1.3 Voltage Division Rule (VDR)

**Formal Definition:**
The **Voltage Division Rule** is a direct consequence of Kirchhoff's Voltage Law (KVL) applied to a series circuit. It states that in a series connection, the **voltage across any resistor is proportional to its resistance value relative to the total series resistance**.

$$V_n = V_{total} \times \frac{R_n}{R_{total}}$$

### 1.4 Current Division Rule (CDR)

**Formal Definition:**
The **Current Division Rule** is a direct consequence of Kirchhoff's Current Law (KCL) applied to a parallel circuit. It states that in a parallel connection, the **current through any branch is inversely proportional to its resistance value relative to the equivalent parallel resistance**.

$$I_n = I_{total} \times \frac{R_{eq}}{R_n}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Reference Values used in this module:**
> - **SI Unit of Resistance:** Ohm ($\Omega$)
> - **SI Unit of Voltage:** Volt ($V$)
> - **SI Unit of Current:** Ampere ($A$)
> - **SI Unit of Power:** Watt ($W$)
> - **Ohm's Law:** $V = IR$
> - **Conductance:** $G = \frac{1}{R}$, measured in Siemens ($S$)
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Series Circuit — Operational Logic Steps

**Why and How Series Resistors Work:**

- **Step 1 — Current Constraint:** Since there is only one continuous conductive path, the rate of charge flow (current) must be identical at every cross-section. By conservation of charge: $I_{R_1} = I_{R_2} = I_{R_3} = \ldots = I_{total}$

- **Step 2 — KVL Application:** Going around the loop, the source voltage must equal the sum of IR drops: $V_S = V_1 + V_2 + V_3 + \ldots + V_n$

- **Step 3 — Substitution via Ohm's Law:** Each voltage drop is $V_k = IR_k$. Substituting: $V_S = I(R_1 + R_2 + R_3 + \ldots + R_n) = I \cdot R_{eq}$

- **Step 4 — Equivalent Resistance:** The single equivalent resistor that draws the same current from the source is:
$$R_{eq} = \sum_{k=1}^{n} R_k$$

- **Step 5 — Power Distribution:** Total power dissipated equals the sum of individual powers. Since current is common, $P_k = I^2 R_k$, and the **largest resistor dissipates the most power** in a series string.

> [!TIP]
> **Real-World Utility:** Series resistor networks are extensively used in **voltage-dropping applications** (e.g., LED current limiters, voltage reference ladders, pull-up/pull-down biasing chains, ADC attenuator networks, and precision voltage dividers in measurement instruments).

---

### 2.2 Parallel Circuit — Operational Logic Steps

**Why and How Parallel Resistors Work:**

- **Step 1 — Voltage Constraint:** All branches share the same pair of nodes; therefore, the potential difference (voltage) across each branch is identical: $V_{R_1} = V_{R_2} = V_{R_3} = \ldots = V_S$

- **Step 2 — KCL Application:** Total current entering the node equals the sum of branch currents: $I_{total} = I_1 + I_2 + I_3 + \ldots + I_n$

- **Step 3 — Substitution via Ohm's Law:** Each branch current is $I_k = \frac{V_S}{R_k}$. Summing: $I_{total} = V_S \left( \frac{1}{R_1} + \frac{1}{R_2} + \frac{1}{R_3} + \ldots + \frac{1}{R_n} \right)$

- **Step 4 — Equivalent Conductance:** The reciprocals (conductances) simply add up:
$$\frac{1}{R_{eq}} = \sum_{k=1}^{n} \frac{1}{R_k} \quad \Rightarrow \quad G_{eq} = \sum_{k=1}^{n} G_k$$

- **Step 5 — Power Distribution:** Total power is $P_{total} = V_S^2 / R_{eq}$. Since voltage is common, the **smallest resistor carries the most current and dissipates the most power**.

> [!TIP]
> **Real-World Utility:** Parallel resistor networks form the backbone of **current-sharing systems, load distribution in power grids, shunt resistors in ammeters, bleeder resistors across filter capacitors, and the input termination networks of high-speed digital lines** (e.g., $50\,\Omega$ or $75\,\Omega$ parallel terminations).

---

### 2.3 Voltage Division Rule (VDR) — Deep Mechanics

**Two-Element VDR Derivation Logic:**

For a series circuit with source $V_S$ and resistors $R_1$, $R_2$:

$$I = \frac{V_S}{R_1 + R_2}$$

$$V_1 = I R_1 = V_S \cdot \frac{R_1}{R_1 + R_2}, \qquad V_2 = I R_2 = V_S \cdot \frac{R_2}{R_1 + R_2}$$

**Generalized $n$-Element VDR:**

$$V_k = V_S \cdot \frac{R_k}{\sum_{i=1}^{n} R_i}$$

**Special Note — The Voltage Divider as a Sensor Interface:**
The unloaded voltage divider is highly sensitive to loading effects. When a load resistor $R_L$ is connected across $R_2$, the output voltage becomes:

$$V_{out} = V_S \cdot \frac{R_2 \parallel R_L}{R_1 + (R_2 \parallel R_L)}$$

This loading effect is a critical concept in **sensor signal conditioning** and **ADC input networks**.

---

### 2.4 Current Division Rule (CDR) — Deep Mechanics

**Two-Element CDR Derivation Logic:**

For a parallel circuit with total current $I_T$ entering two parallel branches $R_1$ and $R_2$:

$$V = I_T \cdot (R_1 \parallel R_2) = I_T \cdot \frac{R_1 R_2}{R_1 + R_2}$$

$$I_1 = \frac{V}{R_1} = I_T \cdot \frac{R_2}{R_1 + R_2}, \qquad I_2 = \frac{V}{R_2} = I_T \cdot \frac{R_1}{R_1 + R_2}$$

**Generalized $n$-Element CDR:**

$$I_k = I_T \cdot \frac{G_k}{\sum_{i=1}^{n} G_i} = I_T \cdot \frac{1/R_k}{\sum_{i=1}^{n} (1/R_i)}$$

**Important Inversion Property:** The current through a branch is **inversely proportional** to that branch's resistance — the smaller the resistance, the larger the current. This is the dual of the VDR.

---

### 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Network Type | Equivalent Resistance Formula | Governing Law | Voltage Relation | Current Relation | Power Relation |
|---|--------------|-------------------------------|---------------|------------------|------------------|----------------|
| 1 | Series (n resistors) | $R_{eq} = \sum R_k$ | KVL | $V_S = \sum V_k$ | $I$ common to all | $P_k = I^2 R_k$ |
| 2 | Parallel (n resistors) | $\frac{1}{R_{eq}} = \sum \frac{1}{R_k}$ | KCL | $V$ common to all branches | $I_T = \sum I_k$ | $P_k = V^2 / R_k$ |
| 3 | Two Resistors Series | $R_{eq} = R_1 + R_2$ | KVL | $V_k = V_S \cdot \frac{R_k}{R_1 + R_2}$ | $I$ common | $P \propto R$ |
| 4 | Two Resistors Parallel | $R_{eq} = \frac{R_1 R_2}{R_1 + R_2}$ | KCL | $V$ common | $I_k = I_T \cdot \frac{R_{other}}{R_1 + R_2}$ | $P \propto \frac{1}{R}$ |
| 5 | Conductance Sum | $G_{eq} = \sum G_k$ | KCL | – | – | – |
| 6 | VDR (n-element) | – | KVL | $V_k = V_S \cdot \frac{R_k}{R_{total}}$ | – | – |
| 7 | CDR (n-element) | – | KCL | – | $I_k = I_T \cdot \frac{G_k}{G_{total}}$ | – |

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight for Problem Solving:** Always simplify the network step-by-step. Identify series and parallel blocks, reduce each to a single equivalent resistor, then apply VDR or CDR on the simplified circuit.

---

### 2.6 Series-Parallel Combined Networks — Reduction Strategy

1. **Identify clearly identifiable series and parallel sub-blocks.**
2. **Reduce** each block to a single equivalent resistance.
3. **Redraw** the simplified circuit.
4. **Repeat** until only one equivalent resistance remains facing the source.
5. **Back-substitute** to find branch voltages and currents using KVL, KCL, VDR, and CDR.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Student Mistake:** Resistors that share a single node but do not share BOTH nodes (i.e., not connected between the same two terminals) are NOT in parallel, even if they "look" parallel in the schematic.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Series Equivalent Resistance from KVL and Ohm's Law

Consider $n$ resistors $R_1, R_2, R_3, \ldots, R_n$ connected in series across a voltage source $V_S$.

**Step 1 — Apply Kirchhoff's Voltage Law (KVL):**

Going around the closed loop, the sum of voltage drops must equal the source voltage:

$$V_S = V_1 + V_2 + V_3 + \ldots + V_n$$

**Step 2 — Apply Ohm's Law to Each Resistor:**

Since the same current $I$ flows through every series element, each voltage drop is:

$$V_k = I \cdot R_k \quad \text{for} \quad k = 1, 2, \ldots, n$$

**Step 3 — Substitute the Ohm's Law Expressions into the KVL Equation:**

$$V_S = I R_1 + I R_2 + I R_3 + \ldots + I R_n$$

**Step 4 — Factor Out the Common Current $I$:**

$$V_S = I \left( R_1 + R_2 + R_3 + \ldots + R_n \right)$$

**Step 5 — Define the Equivalent Resistance:**

By definition, the equivalent resistor $R_{eq}$ is the single resistor that, when connected across $V_S$, draws the same current $I$. Thus:

$$V_S = I \cdot R_{eq}$$

**Step 6 — Equate and Solve for $R_{eq}$:**

$$I \cdot R_{eq} = I \left( R_1 + R_2 + R_3 + \ldots + R_n \right)$$

Dividing both sides by $I$ (which is non-zero):

$$\boxed{R_{eq} = \sum_{k=1}^{n} R_k = R_1 + R_2 + R_3 + \ldots + R_n}$$

This proves that series resistances add directly, and the equivalent resistance is always **greater than the largest individual resistor** in the series string.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Parallel Equivalent Resistance from KCL and Ohm's Law

Consider $n$ resistors $R_1, R_2, R_3, \ldots, R_n$ connected in parallel across a voltage source $V_S$.

**Step 1 — Apply Kirchhoff's Current Law (KCL):**

The total current entering the parallel combination equals the sum of the branch currents:

$$I_T = I_1 + I_2 + I_3 + \ldots + I_n$$

**Step 2 — Apply Ohm's Law to Each Branch:**

Since the same voltage $V_S$ exists across every parallel branch:

$$I_k = \frac{V_S}{R_k} \quad \text{for} \quad k = 1, 2, \ldots, n$$

**Step 3 — Substitute into the KCL Equation:**

$$I_T = \frac{V_S}{R_1} + \frac{V_S}{R_2} + \frac{V_S}{R_3} + \ldots + \frac{V_S}{R_n}$$

**Step 4 — Factor Out the Common Voltage $V_S$:**

$$I_T = V_S \left( \frac{1}{R_1} + \frac{1}{R_2} + \frac{1}{R_3} + \ldots + \frac{1}{R_n} \right)$$

**Step 5 — Define the Equivalent Conductance:**

By definition, the equivalent resistor $R_{eq}$ satisfies $I_T = \frac{V_S}{R_{eq}}$, which means:

$$\frac{V_S}{R_{eq}} = V_S \left( \frac{1}{R_1} + \frac{1}{R_2} + \frac{1}{R_3} + \ldots + \frac{1}{R_n} \right)$$

**Step 6 — Solve for the Equivalent Resistance:**

Dividing both sides by $V_S$ (non-zero):

$$\frac{1}{R_{eq}} = \frac{1}{R_1} + \frac{1}{R_2} + \frac{1}{R_3} + \ldots + \frac{1}{R_n}$$

Or equivalently, in terms of conductances:

$$\boxed{G_{eq} = \sum_{k=1}^{n} G_k = G_1 + G_2 + G_3 + \ldots + G_n}$$

Therefore:

$$R_{eq} = \frac{1}{\sum_{k=1}^{n} (1/R_k)}$$

This proves that the equivalent parallel resistance is always **smaller than the smallest individual resistor** in the parallel combination.

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Voltage Division Rule (VDR) for n Resistors

Consider a series circuit with source $V_S$ and resistors $R_1, R_2, \ldots, R_n$.

**Step 1 — Compute the Total Current:**

$$I = \frac{V_S}{R_{eq}} = \frac{V_S}{R_1 + R_2 + \ldots + R_n}$$

**Step 2 — Compute the Voltage Across the $k$-th Resistor Using Ohm's Law:**

$$V_k = I \cdot R_k = V_S \cdot \frac{R_k}{R_1 + R_2 + \ldots + R_n}$$

**Step 3 — Final VDR Expression:**

$$\boxed{V_k = V_S \cdot \frac{R_k}{\sum_{i=1}^{n} R_i}}$$

**Verification (Energy Conservation):** The sum of all individual voltages must equal $V_S$:

$$\sum_{k=1}^{n} V_k = V_S \cdot \frac{\sum_{k=1}^{n} R_k}{\sum_{i=1}^{n} R_i} = V_S \cdot 1 = V_S \quad \checkmark$$

This confirms KVL is satisfied.

---

### 3.4 Derivation 4 — Current Division Rule (CDR) for Two Resistors

Consider two resistors $R_1$ and $R_2$ in parallel, with total current $I_T$ entering the combination.

**Step 1 — Compute the Voltage Across the Parallel Combination:**

$$V = I_T \cdot R_{eq} = I_T \cdot \frac{R_1 R_2}{R_1 + R_2}$$

**Step 2 — Compute the Current Through $R_1$ Using Ohm's Law:**

$$I_1 = \frac{V}{R_1} = \frac{I_T}{R_1} \cdot \frac{R_1 R_2}{R_1 + R_2} = I_T \cdot \frac{R_2}{R_1 + R_2}$$

**Step 3 — Compute the Current Through $R_2$ Using Ohm's Law:**

$$I_2 = \frac{V}{R_2} = \frac{I_T}{R_2} \cdot \frac{R_1 R_2}{R_1 + R_2} = I_T \cdot \frac{R_1}{R_1 + R_2}$$

**Step 4 — Final CDR Expression:**

$$\boxed{I_1 = I_T \cdot \frac{R_2}{R_1 + R_2}, \qquad I_2 = I_T \cdot \frac{R_1}{R_1 + R_2}}$$

**Generalized CDR (n resistors):**

$$\boxed{I_k = I_T \cdot \frac{1/R_k}{\sum_{i=1}^{n} (1/R_i)} = I_T \cdot \frac{G_k}{G_{total}}}$$

---

### 3.5 Worked Numerical Example — Combined Series-Parallel Network

**Problem Statement:**
A $24\,\text{V}$ source feeds a network where $R_1 = 4\,\Omega$ is in series with a parallel combination of $R_2 = 6\,\Omega$ and $R_3 = 12\,\Omega$. Find: (a) the equivalent resistance, (b) the total current from the source, (c) the voltage across the parallel block, and (d) the current through each parallel branch.

**Solution:**

**Part (a) — Equivalent Resistance of the Parallel Block:**

$$R_{23} = \frac{R_2 \cdot R_3}{R_2 + R_3} = \frac{6 \times 12}{6 + 12} = \frac{72}{18} = 4\,\Omega$$

**Total Equivalent Resistance (series combination of $R_1$ and $R_{23}$):**

$$R_{eq} = R_1 + R_{23} = 4 + 4 = 8\,\Omega$$

**Part (b) — Total Current from the Source:**

$$I_T = \frac{V_S}{R_{eq}} = \frac{24}{8} = 3\,\text{A}$$

**Part (c) — Voltage Across the Parallel Block (using VDR):**

$$V_{23} = V_S \cdot \frac{R_{23}}{R_1 + R_{23}} = 24 \cdot \frac{4}{4 + 4} = 24 \cdot \frac{4}{8} = 12\,\text{V}$$

**Part (d) — Branch Currents (using CDR):**

$$I_2 = I_T \cdot \frac{R_3}{R_2 + R_3} = 3 \cdot \frac{12}{6 + 12} = 3 \cdot \frac{12}{18} = 2\,\text{A}$$

$$I_3 = I_T \cdot \frac{R_2}{R_2 + R_3} = 3 \cdot \frac{6}{6 + 12} = 3 \cdot \frac{6}{18} = 1\,\text{A}$$

**Verification (KCL):** $I_2 + I_3 = 2 + 1 = 3\,\text{A} = I_T$ $\checkmark$

**Verification (KVL):** $V_1 + V_{23} = I_T R_1 + V_{23} = 3 \times 4 + 12 = 12 + 12 = 24\,\text{V} = V_S$ $\checkmark$
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Series Resistor Network — Functional Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    Source((V_S)) --> R1Block[Resistor R1]
    R1Block --> NodeA(( ))
    NodeA --> R2Block[Resistor R2]
    R2Block --> NodeB(( ))
    NodeB --> R3Block[Resistor Rn])
    R3Block --> Return((Return to Source))

    classDef sourceStyle fill:#FFD700,stroke:#000000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000
    classDef resistorStyle fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#000080,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000
    classDef nodeStyle fill:#FFFFFF,stroke:#000000,stroke-width:1px,color:#000000

    class Source,Return sourceStyle
    class R1Block,R2Block,R3Block resistorStyle
    class NodeA,NodeB nodeStyle
```

> **Interpretation:** The diagram above shows the same current $I$ flowing through $R_1$, $R_2$, $\ldots$, $R_n$ in a single path. The total voltage drop accumulates across each resistor.

---

### 4.2 Parallel Resistor Network — Functional Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Source((V_S)) --> TopNode((Top Node))
    TopNode --> Branch1[Resistor R1]
    TopNode --> Branch2[Resistor R2]
    TopNode --> Branch3[Resistor Rn])
    Branch1 --> BottomNode((Bottom Node))
    Branch2 --> BottomNode
    Branch3 --> BottomNode
    BottomNode --> Return((Return to Source))

    classDef sourceStyle fill:#FFD700,stroke:#000000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000
    classDef resistorStyle fill:#90EE90,stroke:#006400,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000
    classDef nodeStyle fill:#FFFFFF,stroke:#000000,stroke-width:1px,color:#000000

    class Source,Return sourceStyle
    class Branch1,Branch2,Branch3 resistorStyle
    class TopNode,BottomNode nodeStyle
```

> **Interpretation:** All branches share the same top and bottom nodes, hence the same voltage $V_S$. The total current $I_T$ splits into $I_1$, $I_2$, $\ldots$, $I_n$ according to the CDR.

---

### 4.3 Voltage Division Process Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[V_S Applied Across Series String] --> B[Compute R_eq = Sum of R_k]
    B --> C[Compute Total Current I = V_S over R_eq]
    C --> D{For Each Resistor R_k}
    D --> E[Apply VDR: V_k = V_S times R_k over R_eq]
    E --> F[Verify: Sum of V_k equals V_S]

    classDef processStyle fill:#E6E6FA,stroke:#4B0082,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000
    classDef decisionStyle fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#8B4513,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000
    classDef verifyStyle fill:#98FB98,stroke:#006400,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000

    class A,B,C,E processStyle
    class D decisionStyle
    class F verifyStyle
```

---

### 4.4 Current Division Process Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[I_T Entering Parallel Bank] --> B[Compute R_eq from Product over Sum]
    B --> C[Compute Branch Voltage V = I_T times R_eq]
    C --> D{For Each Branch R_k}
    D --> E[Apply CDR: I_k = I_T times Other R over Sum of R]
    E --> F[Verify: Sum of I_k equals I_T]

    classDef processStyle fill:#FFF0F5,stroke:#8B0000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000
    classDef decisionStyle fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#8B4513,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000
    classDef verifyStyle fill:#98FB98,stroke:#006400,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000

    class A,B,C,E processStyle
    class D decisionStyle
    class F verifyStyle
```

---

### 4.5 Network Reduction Strategy — Block-Level Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start([Original Series-Parallel Network]) --> Step1[Step 1: Identify Isolated Series Blocks]
    Step1 --> Step2[Step 2: Identify Isolated Parallel Blocks]
    Step2 --> Step3[Step 3: Reduce Series Block to R_series_eq]
    Step3 --> Step4[Step 4: Reduce Parallel Block to R_parallel_eq]
    Step4 --> Decision{Is the Network Fully Reduced?}
    Decision -->|No| Step1
    Decision -->|Yes| Step5[Step 5: Compute Total Current from Source]
    Step5 --> Step6[Step 6: Back-Substitute Using VDR and CDR]
    Step6 --> Step7[Step 7: Verify KVL and KCL]
    Step7 --> End([All Branch Voltages and Currents Found])

    classDef stepStyle fill:#E0FFFF,stroke:#008B8B,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000
    classDef decisionStyle fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#8B4513,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000
    classDef terminalStyle fill:#FFD700,stroke:#000000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000

    class Start,End terminalStyle
    class Step1,Step2,Step3,Step4,Step5,Step6,Step7 stepStyle
    class Decision decisionStyle
```

> This block-level architecture flow illustrates the universal methodology for solving any resistive network: progressively reduce, then back-substitute with KVL/KCL/VDR/CDR.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
*State and prove the Voltage Division Rule for two resistors connected in series across a DC source.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Statement (1 Mark):** When two or more resistors are connected in series across a voltage source, the voltage across any resistor is proportional to the ratio of its resistance to the total series resistance.

**Proof (2 Marks):** Consider resistors $R_1$ and $R_2$ in series across source $V_S$.

The total current is:
$$I = \frac{V_S}{R_1 + R_2}$$

By Ohm's Law, the voltage across $R_1$ is:
$$V_1 = I R_1 = V_S \cdot \frac{R_1}{R_1 + R_2}$$

Similarly:
$$V_2 = I R_2 = V_S \cdot \frac{R_2}{R_1 + R_2}$$

Hence the Voltage Division Rule is proved.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
*Define the Current Division Rule. Derive the expression for the current through $R_2$ when $R_1$ and $R_2$ are connected in parallel with a total current $I_T$.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Definition (1 Mark):** The Current Division Rule states that in a parallel circuit, the current through any branch is inversely proportional to the resistance of that branch relative to the equivalent parallel resistance.

**Derivation (2 Marks):** The voltage across the parallel combination is:
$$V = I_T \cdot \frac{R_1 R_2}{R_1 + R_2}$$

The current through $R_2$ by Ohm's Law is:
$$I_2 = \frac{V}{R_2} = \frac{I_T}{R_2} \cdot \frac{R_1 R_2}{R_1 + R_2} = I_T \cdot \frac{R_1}{R_1 + R_2}$$

---

### Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**

**(a)** For the circuit shown below, a $12\,\text{V}$ source is connected across a network where $R_1 = 2\,\Omega$ and $R_2 = 4\,\Omega$ are in series, and this series combination is in parallel with $R_3 = 6\,\Omega$. Find:
(i) The equivalent resistance seen by the source.
(ii) The total current supplied by the source.
(iii) The voltage across $R_3$.
(iv) The current through $R_1$. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Explain with a neat diagram the Current Division Rule for a two-branch parallel network. State the conditions under which CDR is applicable. **(7 Marks)**

---

**Solution to Part A(a) — 7 Marks:**

**Given:** $V_S = 12\,\text{V}$, $R_1 = 2\,\Omega$, $R_2 = 4\,\Omega$ in series; this combination is in parallel with $R_3 = 6\,\Omega$.

**Step 1 (1 Mark) — Series Equivalent of $R_1$ and $R_2$:**

$$R_{12} = R_1 + R_2 = 2 + 4 = 6\,\Omega$$

**Step 2 (1 Mark) — Parallel Equivalent of $R_{12}$ and $R_3$:**

$$R_{eq} = \frac{R_{12} \cdot R_3}{R_{12} + R_3} = \frac{6 \times 6}{6 + 6} = \frac{36}{12} = 3\,\Omega$$

**Step 3 (1 Mark) — Total Current:**

$$I_T = \frac{V_S}{R_{eq}} = \frac{12}{3} = 4\,\text{A}$$

**Step 4 (2 Marks) — Voltage Across $R_3$:**

Since $R_3$ is in parallel with the $R_1$–$R_2$ series combination, the voltage across $R_3$ equals the source voltage:

$$V_{R_3} = V_S = 12\,\text{V}$$

**Step 5 (2 Marks) — Current Through $R_1$:**

First, the current through the $R_1$–$R_2$ branch is:

$$I_{12} = \frac{V_S}{R_{12}} = \frac{12}{6} = 2\,\text{A}$$

Since $R_1$ and $R_2$ are in series, the current through $R_1$ equals $I_{12}$:

$$I_{R_1} = 2\,\text{A}$$

**Verification (KCL):** Current through $R_3$ is $I_3 = V_S / R_3 = 12/6 = 2\,\text{A}$. So $I_T = I_{12} + I_3 = 2 + 2 = 4\,\text{A}$ $\checkmark$

---

**Solution to Part A(b) — 7 Marks:**

**Current Division Rule for Two-Branch Parallel Network:**

Consider two resistors $R_1$ and $R_2$ connected in parallel across nodes A and B. Let total current $I_T$ enter at node A and exit at node B.

By KCL at node A:
$$I_T = I_1 + I_2$$

Since the voltage across both branches is the same:
$$V_{AB} = I_1 R_1 = I_2 R_2$$

The equivalent resistance of the parallel combination is:
$$R_{eq} = \frac{R_1 R_2}{R_1 + R_2}$$

The voltage is also:
$$V_{AB} = I_T \cdot R_{eq} = I_T \cdot \frac{R_1 R_2}{R_1 + R_2}$$

Equating and solving:
$$I_1 = I_T \cdot \frac{R_2}{R_1 + R_2}, \qquad I_2 = I_T \cdot \frac{R_1}{R_1 + R_2}$$

**Conditions of Applicability of CDR (2 Marks):**
1. The branches must be strictly in **parallel** (share both nodes).
2. The network must be a **purely resistive, two-terminal** network.
3. The current $I_T$ entering the combination must be **known**.
4. The branches must be **independent** (no coupling elements like dependent sources).

**Physical Interpretation (1 Mark):** The smaller resistor carries the larger share of the total current, following the principle of least resistance.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**

**(a)** A $30\,\text{V}$ DC source is connected to a network consisting of $R_1 = 10\,\Omega$ in series with a parallel combination of $R_2 = 15\,\Omega$ and $R_3 = 30\,\Omega$. Determine:
(i) The total equivalent resistance.
(ii) The total current drawn from the source.
(iii) The voltage across the parallel combination using the Voltage Division Rule.
(iv) The currents through $R_2$ and $R_3$ using the Current Division Rule. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Compare series and parallel resistor networks in terms of current, voltage, equivalent resistance, and practical applications. **(7 Marks)**

---

**Solution to Part B(a) — 7 Marks:**

**Given:** $V_S = 30\,\text{V}$, $R_1 = 10\,\Omega$, $R_2 = 15\,\Omega$, $R_3 = 30\,\Omega$.

**Step 1 (1 Mark) — Parallel Equivalent of $R_2$ and $R_3$:**

$$R_{23} = \frac{R_2 \cdot R_3}{R_2 + R_3} = \frac{15 \times 30}{15 + 30} = \frac{450}{45} = 10\,\Omega$$

**Step 2 (1 Mark) — Total Equivalent Resistance:**

$$R_{eq} = R_1 + R_{23} = 10 + 10 = 20\,\Omega$$

**Step 3 (1 Mark) — Total Current:**

$$I_T = \frac{V_S}{R_{eq}} = \frac{30}{20} = 1.5\,\text{A}$$

**Step 4 (2 Marks) — Voltage Across the Parallel Combination (VDR):**

$$V_{23} = V_S \cdot \frac{R_{23}}{R_1 + R_{23}} = 30 \cdot \frac{10}{10 + 10} = 30 \cdot \frac{10}{20} = 15\,\text{V}$$

**Step 5 (2 Marks) — Branch Currents (CDR):**

$$I_2 = I_T \cdot \frac{R_3}{R_2 + R_3} = 1.5 \cdot \frac{30}{15 + 30} = 1.5 \cdot \frac{30}{45} = 1.5 \cdot \frac{2}{3} = 1.0\,\text{A}$$

$$I_3 = I_T \cdot \frac{R_2}{R_2 + R_3} = 1.5 \cdot \frac{15}{15 + 30} = 1.5 \cdot \frac{15}{45} = 1.5 \cdot \frac{1}{3} = 0.5\,\text{A}$$

**Verification (KCL):** $I_2 + I_3 = 1.0 + 0.5 = 1.5\,\text{A} = I_T$ $\checkmark$

**Verification (KVL):** $V_1 + V_{23} = I_T R_1 + V_{23} = 1.5 \times 10 + 15 = 15 + 15 = 30\,\text{V} = V_S$ $\checkmark$

---

**Solution to Part B(b) — 7 Marks:**

**Comparison Table (5 Marks):**

| Parameter | Series Network | Parallel Network |
|-----------|---------------|------------------|
| **Current** | Same through all elements ($I$ common) | Divides among branches ($I_T = \sum I_k$) |
| **Voltage** | Divides across elements ($V_S = \sum V_k$) | Same across all branches ($V$ common) |
| **Equivalent Resistance** | $R_{eq} = \sum R_k$ (greater than largest $R$) | $1/R_{eq} = \sum (1/R_k)$ (less than smallest $R$) |
| **Total Power** | $P_{total} = I^2 R_{eq}$ | $P_{total} = V^2 / R_{eq}$ |
| **Failure Mode** | One open → entire circuit stops | One open → other branches continue |
| **Power Distribution** | Largest $R$ dissipates most | Smallest $R$ dissipates most |
| **Practical Use** | Voltage dividers, current limiters, fuses | Current sharing, house wiring, load banks |
| **KVL/KCL Used** | KVL (voltage summation) | KCL (current summation) |
| **Divider Rule** | Voltage Division Rule (VDR) | Current Division Rule (CDR) |

**Conclusion (2 Marks):** Both configurations are duals of each other. Series circuits emphasize voltage management (e.g., dropping, dividing, biasing), while parallel circuits emphasize current management (e.g., sharing, distributing, redundancy). Real-world electronics typically employ a combination of both — series for setting operating points and parallel for load distribution.

---

### KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in KTU Valuation:**
> 1. **Wrong CDR Numerator:** Students frequently write $I_1 = I_T \cdot \frac{R_1}{R_1 + R_2}$ instead of $I_T \cdot \frac{R_2}{R_1 + R_2}$. Remember — the numerator is the **OPPOSITE branch resistance** because current divides **inversely** with resistance.
> 2. **Parallel Misidentification:** Resistors that share a single node but not both nodes are not in parallel. Always confirm **two common nodes** before applying the parallel formula.
> 3. **Missing Units:** Marks are deducted for omitting SI units in final answers. Always specify $\Omega$, V, A, W.
> 4. **No Verification Step:** KTU examiners reward students who verify their answers using KVL/KCL. Include a verification step for bonus impression.
> 5. **Conceptual vs. Numerical Confusion:** Part (a) of a 14-mark question often tests concepts (VDR/CDR statement and proof), while numerical sub-parts test application. Allocate time accordingly.
> 6. **Arithmetic with Fractions:** Be careful when handling $R_1 R_2 / (R_1 + R_2)$ — keep the expression in fractional form until the final step to avoid rounding errors.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Series resistors** carry the **same current**, and the **equivalent resistance is the algebraic sum** of all individual resistances.
- **Parallel resistors** have the **same voltage** across them, and the **reciprocal of the equivalent resistance is the sum of reciprocals** of individual resistances.
- **Series $R_{eq}$ > Largest $R$**; **Parallel $R_{eq}$ < Smallest $R$** — these are useful sanity checks.
- **Voltage Division Rule (VDR):** $V_k = V_S \cdot \frac{R_k}{R_{total}}$ — directly proportional to resistance.
- **Current Division Rule (CDR):** $I_k = I_T \cdot \frac{R_{other}}{R_1 + R_2}$ (for two branches) — inversely proportional to the branch's own resistance.
- **Two-Resistor Parallel Shortcut:** $R_{eq} = \frac{R_1 R_2}{R_1 + R_2}$ — the "product over sum" formula.
- **Conductance Approach:** $G_{eq} = \sum G_k$ simplifies parallel-network arithmetic, especially with many branches.
- **VDR/CDR require pure series/parallel structures** — they are not directly applicable to bridge or delta networks.
- **Source transformation** (covered in later modules) uses the VDR/CDR concepts in reverse.
- **Power in Series:** $P_k = I^2 R_k$ — the **largest resistor dissipates the most power**.
- **Power in Parallel:** $P_k = V^2 / R_k$ — the **smallest resistor dissipates the most power**.
- **KVL** governs series circuits; **KCL** governs parallel circuits.
- **For KTU numerical problems,** always: (1) draw the simplified circuit, (2) label all knowns, (3) apply reduction, (4) back-substitute, (5) verify with KVL/KCL.
- **Units discipline:** $\Omega$ for resistance, V for voltage, A for current, W for power — never omit.
- **Open-circuit in series = entire circuit fails**; **open-circuit in parallel = only that branch fails** (a key reason household wiring uses parallel).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Source Transformation Technique
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCICT302 - ELECTRONIC CIRCUITS AND NETWORKS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Source Transformation Technique

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Source Transformation** is a fundamental circuit simplification technique used in linear bilateral networks to interchange (convert) a practical voltage source with a practical current source (and vice versa) while preserving the identical voltage-current (V-I) characteristics at the external output terminals.

In formal KTU 2024 syllabus terminology, a *practical voltage source* consists of an **ideal voltage source (Vₛ)** connected in **series** with an internal source resistance (Rₛ), while a *practical current source* consists of an **ideal current source (Iₛ)** connected in **parallel** with an internal source resistance (Rₛ). The transformation between these two equivalent representations is governed by Ohm's law and the principle of terminal equivalence.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Source transformation is a sub-topic under the broader module of "Network Theorems / Network Reduction Techniques." It is a high-frequency, mark-yielding concept and frequently appears as a 3-mark direct question or as a sub-part (often 7 marks) of a Part B question in the End Semester Examination (ESE).

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Imagine a **water tank** connected to a pipeline.

- A **Voltage Source** is like a tank placed on an *elevated platform*. The height of the platform (Vₛ) determines the pressure (voltage) pushing water through a fixed narrow pipe (resistance Rₛ in series). No matter how much water flows, the pressure difference remains constant.
- A **Current Source** is like a *pressurized pumping station* that pushes a fixed quantity of water (Iₛ) through the pipeline, with a parallel bypass pipe (Rₛ in parallel) that allows excess pressure to leak.

**Source Transformation** is the engineering realization that **both these water-delivery setups can produce the exact same flow rate at the tap (output terminals)** if their pressures and flow capacities are scaled correctly using **Ohm's Law (I = V/R)**. Engineers frequently switch between these two models to make complex circuits easier to analyze — the same way a mathematician might switch between Cartesian and Polar coordinates to simplify a problem.

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Physical Constants Used:**
> - **I (Ampere, A)** — Current
> - **V (Volt, V)** — Voltage (Potential Difference)
> - **R (Ohm, Ω)** — Resistance
> - Standard conversion identity: **Iₛ = Vₛ / Rₛ**

## 1.3 The Two Canonical Transformations

| Transformation Type | Original Form | Equivalent Form | Governing Equation |
|---|---|---|---|
| Voltage → Current | Vₛ in series with Rₛ | Iₛ in parallel with Rₛ | $I_s = V_s / R_s$ |
| Current → Voltage | Iₛ in parallel with Rₛ | Vₛ in series with Rₛ | $V_s = I_s \times R_s$ |

> [!TIP]
> **Quick Memory Trick:** "VIR" (like the word *veer*). Going **V → I** requires *division* by R. Going **I → V** requires *multiplication* by R. The resistor value Rₛ remains **identical** in magnitude in both equivalent circuits, only its topological position changes (series ↔ parallel).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Theoretical Foundation — Terminal Equivalence Principle

The mathematical validity of source transformation rests on the **Terminal Equivalence Principle**, which states that two networks are said to be *equivalent at the terminals a-b* if they exhibit identical V-I characteristics when viewed from those external terminals.

For the transformation to be valid, **two boundary conditions** must be satisfied simultaneously at terminals a-b:

1. **Open-Circuit Voltage (V_oc)** must be identical in both circuits.
2. **Short-Circuit Current (I_sc)** must be identical in both circuits.

### 2.1.1 Voltage Source to Current Source Transformation

Consider a practical voltage source with EMF **Vₛ** in series with resistance **Rₛ** connected to load terminals a-b.

**Step-by-step logic:**

- **Step 1 (Open-Circuit Test):** Remove the load. The voltage at terminals a-b equals Vₛ (since no current flows through Rₛ, there is no voltage drop).
  $$V_{oc} = V_s$$

- **Step 2 (Short-Circuit Test):** Short terminals a-b. The current flowing through the short equals Vₛ / Rₛ (entire source voltage drops across Rₛ).
  $$I_{sc} = \frac{V_s}{R_s}$$

- **Step 3 (Equivalent Current Source):** To construct an equivalent current source model, we set the open-circuit voltage and short-circuit current of both models equal. The equivalent current source must therefore deliver:
  $$I_s = \frac{V_s}{R_s}$$
  with Rₛ placed in **parallel** to maintain the same internal behavior.

### 2.1.2 Current Source to Voltage Source Transformation

Consider a practical current source delivering **Iₛ** in parallel with internal resistance **Rₛ**.

**Step-by-step logic:**

- **Step 1 (Open-Circuit Test):** With terminals a-b open, the entire current Iₛ flows through Rₛ (since no path exists externally). By Ohm's law:
  $$V_{oc} = I_s \times R_s$$

- **Step 2 (Short-Circuit Test):** With terminals a-b shorted, all current Iₛ bypasses through the short (zero resistance path) and none flows through Rₛ. So:
  $$I_{sc} = I_s$$

- **Step 3 (Equivalent Voltage Source):** The equivalent voltage source must have EMF:
  $$V_s = I_s \times R_s$$
  with Rₛ placed in **series**.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Units | KTU Exam Frequency |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $I_s = \dfrac{V_s}{R_s}$ | Voltage source → Current source conversion | A | ★★★★★ |
| 2 | $V_s = I_s \times R_s$ | Current source → Voltage source conversion | V | ★★★★★ |
| 3 | $R_{eq} = R_s$ | Internal resistance is preserved (magnitude) | Ω | ★★★★★ |
| 4 | $V_{oc} = V_s = I_s R_s$ | Terminal equivalence: open-circuit condition | V | ★★★★ |
| 5 | $I_{sc} = \dfrac{V_s}{R_s} = I_s$ | Terminal equivalence: short-circuit condition | A | ★★★★ |
| 6 | $P_{internal} = I_s^2 R_s = \dfrac{V_s^2}{R_s}$ | Power dissipated in internal resistance | W | ★★★ |
| 7 | $\text{Series} \rightarrow \text{Parallel}$ | Position of Rₛ changes during conversion | — | ★★★★★ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Rule for KTU Examinations:** When converting, the **direction of the current source arrow must match the polarity of the voltage source's positive terminal.** The arrow points *toward* the positive terminal of the original voltage source. Incorrect arrow direction is one of the most common mistakes penalized in the KTU valuation key.

## 2.3 Engineering Utility & Real-World Applications

- **Power System Analysis:** Transmission lines and generators (Thevenin equivalents) are often modeled as voltage sources, but load-flow studies in parallel networks are more easily handled using current source representations.
- **Electronic Circuit Design:** Transistor amplifiers (especially BJT small-signal models) are naturally represented as current sources with parallel output resistance, and source transformation is used to convert them to voltage-source form for cascaded analysis.
- **Simulation Software (SPICE, Multisim):** Internally, many simulators use source transformation as a pre-processing step to standardize source types before nodal analysis.
- **Mesh & Nodal Analysis:** Source transformation eliminates "floating voltage sources" or "current sources in series with resistors" — pathological configurations that make mesh/nodal equations tedious. This is its **most important KTU-exam utility**.

## 2.4 Limitations & Validity Conditions

> [!WARNING]
> **Where Source Transformation FAILS:**
> 1. **Ideal Sources:** An *ideal* voltage source (Rₛ = 0) cannot be transformed into a current source because division by zero is undefined. Similarly, an *ideal* current source (Rₛ = ∞) cannot be transformed into a voltage source.
> 2. **Power Dissipation:** The internal power dissipated in Rₛ is preserved, but **the power delivered to an external load is only identical for the specific load** used during the open/short-circuit equivalence derivation. For arbitrary loads, the two circuits give identical *external* behavior but may differ in *internal* node voltages.
> 3. **Dependent Sources:** Source transformation can be applied to dependent sources, but extreme care is required because the controlling variable may be affected by the transformation. KTU questions often test this nuance.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Formal Derivation of Voltage → Current Source Transformation

We begin with a **practical voltage source** comprising an ideal EMF source $V_s$ in series with an internal resistance $R_s$, supplying an external load $R_L$ connected across terminals a-b.

### Step 1: State the Open-Circuit Voltage

With $R_L$ removed (open circuit, $I_L = 0$), no current flows through $R_s$, so there is no voltage drop across $R_s$. The terminal voltage $V_{ab}$ equals the source EMF:

$$V_{oc} = V_s$$

### Step 2: State the Short-Circuit Current

With terminals a-b shorted ($R_L = 0$), the only resistance in the loop is $R_s$. By Ohm's law, the current through the short is:

$$I_{sc} = \frac{V_s}{R_s}$$

### Step 3: Construct the Equivalent Current Source Model

For the new current source model (ideal current source $I_s$ in parallel with $R_s$):

- **Open-Circuit Voltage** of new model: All $I_s$ must flow through $R_s$ (since the external path is open), so:
  $$V_{oc}^{new} = I_s \times R_s$$

- **Short-Circuit Current** of new model: With a-b shorted, all $I_s$ flows through the short and none through $R_s$:
  $$I_{sc}^{new} = I_s$$

### Step 4: Equate the Boundary Conditions

For terminal equivalence:

$$\begin{aligned}
V_{oc}^{new} &= V_{oc}^{old} \\
I_s \times R_s &= V_s \\
I_s &= \frac{V_s}{R_s}
\end{aligned}$$

This is the **canonical V→I transformation identity**.

### Step 5: Verify Equivalence Under Load

Connect load $R_L$ to the original voltage source circuit. The current through the load is:

$$I_L = \frac{V_s}{R_s + R_L}$$

Now connect the same $R_L$ to the new current source circuit. Using the current divider rule:

$$I_L = I_s \times \frac{R_s}{R_s + R_L} = \frac{V_s}{R_s} \times \frac{R_s}{R_s + R_L} = \frac{V_s}{R_s + R_L}$$

Both expressions are **identical**, confirming terminal equivalence. $\blacksquare$

## 3.2 Formal Derivation of Current → Voltage Source Transformation

We begin with a **practical current source** delivering $I_s$ in parallel with $R_s$.

### Step 1: Open-Circuit Voltage

With terminals a-b open, the entire source current $I_s$ flows through $R_s$. By Ohm's law:

$$V_{oc} = I_s \times R_s$$

### Step 2: Short-Circuit Current

With a-b shorted, current takes the path of least resistance (the short), bypassing $R_s$ entirely:

$$I_{sc} = I_s$$

### Step 3: Construct the Equivalent Voltage Source Model

For the new voltage source model ($V_s$ in series with $R_s$):

$$V_{oc}^{new} = V_s \qquad \text{(no drop across } R_s \text{ when open)}$$
$$I_{sc}^{new} = \frac{V_s}{R_s}$$

### Step 4: Equate the Boundary Conditions

$$\begin{aligned}
V_{oc}^{new} &= V_{oc}^{old} \\
V_s &= I_s \times R_s
\end{aligned}$$

This is the **canonical I→V transformation identity**. $\blacksquare$

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Pattern)

**Problem:** Convert the following voltage source to its equivalent current source:

Given: $V_s = 24$ V, $R_s = 6$ Ω.

**Solution:**

**Step 1:** Identify the source magnitude and internal resistance.
$$V_s = 24 \text{ V}, \quad R_s = 6 \text{ }\Omega$$

**Step 2:** Apply the transformation identity.
$$I_s = \frac{V_s}{R_s} = \frac{24}{6} = 4 \text{ A}$$

**Step 3:** The equivalent current source has magnitude $4$ A, with the same resistor $R_s = 6$ Ω placed in **parallel**, and the arrow pointing toward the original positive terminal.

**Final Answer:** $I_s = 4$ A in parallel with $R_s = 6$ Ω. $\blacksquare$

**Reverse Verification:** Convert back from current to voltage.
$$V_s = I_s \times R_s = 4 \times 6 = 24 \text{ V} \quad \checkmark$$

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Decision Flowchart for KTU Problems

| Circuit Feature Detected | Recommended Action | Justification |
|---|---|---|
| Ideal voltage source in series with current source | Keep voltage source; remove current source | Series current source contributes zero to mesh current |
| Ideal current source in parallel with voltage source | Keep current source; short the voltage source | Parallel voltage source forces fixed voltage across branch |
| Multiple sources of mixed types | Use source transformation iteratively | Standardizes topology for mesh/nodal analysis |
| Dependent source in circuit | Use transformation with caution; check controlling variable | KTU high-difficulty question pattern |
| Bridge network with no series/parallel reduction possible | Use Star-Delta or Thevenin | Source transformation alone is insufficient |

The following Mermaid diagram depicts the *functional* correspondence between the two source models, emphasizing the terminal equivalence at the external interface (Load Node A — Load Node B).
