4. **Compute $V_{Th}$** as the open-circuit voltage across $a$–$b$ (using any standard nodal/mesh analysis).
5. **Construct the Thevenin equivalent circuit** with $V_{Th}$ in series with $R_{Th}$, terminated by $R_L$.
6. **Apply the matching condition:** Set $R_L = R_{Th}$.
7. **Compute the maximum power** delivered using $P_{max} = \dfrac{V_{Th}^{2}}{4 R_{Th}}$.

## 2.2 The "Why" Behind the Theorem

The principle emerges from the **fundamental trade-off between voltage and current**. As $R_L$ increases from $0 \to \infty$:

- The **load voltage** $V_L = V_{Th} \cdot \dfrac{R_L}{R_{Th} + R_L}$ increases monotonically (from $0$ to $V_{Th}$).
- The **load current** $I_L = \dfrac{V_{Th}}{R_{Th} + R_L}$ decreases monotonically (from $V_{Th}/R_{Th}$ to $0$).
- **Power** $P_L = V_L \cdot I_L = \dfrac{V_{Th}^{2} R_L}{(R_{Th} + R_L)^{2}}$ has a **single maximum** at the point where the *rate of voltage gain is exactly balanced by the rate of current loss*.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Quantity | Formula | Condition / Note |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Matching condition | $R_L = R_{Th}$ | Resistive, DC, low-frequency AC |
| 2 | Load current | $I_L = \dfrac{V_{Th}}{R_{Th} + R_L}$ | Ohm's law in Thevenin circuit |
| 3 | Load power (general) | $P_L = \dfrac{V_{Th}^{2} \cdot R_L}{(R_{Th} + R_L)^{2}}$ | Valid for **any** $R_L$ |
| 4 | Maximum power | $P_{max} = \dfrac{V_{Th}^{2}}{4 R_{Th}}$ | Occurs only at $R_L = R_{Th}$ |
| 5 | Source power at MPP | $P_{source} = \dfrac{V_{Th}^{2}}{2 R_{Th}}$ | $I_{L}^{2} (R_{Th} + R_L)$ |
| 6 | Efficiency at MPP | $\eta = \dfrac{P_L}{P_{source}} = 50\%$ | For purely resistive networks |
| 7 | AC conjugate matching | $Z_L = Z_{Th}^{*}$ | For complex / frequency-domain circuits |
| 8 | Voltage at MPP | $V_L = \dfrac{V_{Th}}{2}$ | Voltage division gives half drop |
| 9 | Current at MPP | $I_{MPP} = \dfrac{V_{Th}}{2 R_{Th}}$ | Half of short-circuit current |
| 10 | Power efficiency (general) | $\eta = \dfrac{R_L}{R_{Th} + R_L}$ | Always $< 100\%$ for $R_L > 0$ |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Audio amplifier–speaker design:** Matching speaker impedance to amplifier output for max acoustic power.
- **Radio-frequency (RF) communication:** Antenna impedance matching to transmission line ($50\,\Omega$ standard).
- **Photovoltaic systems:** Maximum Power Point Tracking (MPPT) algorithms in solar inverters operate on this exact principle.
- **Power transmission lines:** Trade-off analysis between conductor thickness (low $R_{Th}$) and load demand.
- **Electronic instrumentation:** Buffer amplifier stages deliberately avoid MPTT to preserve signal integrity and bandwidth.

> [!NOTE]
> **In Communication Electronics** (next module), MPTT is **not** the design goal — preserving **signal-to-noise ratio** and **bandwidth** takes precedence. MPTT is a **power-efficiency vs power-delivery** trade-off.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — The Matching Condition $R_L = R_{Th}$

Consider the Thevenin equivalent of any linear network feeding a variable load $R_L$:

**Step 1.** The current through the load is governed by Ohm's law applied to the series loop:

$$I_L = \frac{V_{Th}}{R_{Th} + R_L}$$

**Step 2.** The instantaneous power delivered to the load is:

$$P_L = I_L^{2} \cdot R_L$$

**Step 3.** Substituting $I_L$:

$$P_L = \left(\frac{V_{Th}}{R_{Th} + R_L}\right)^{2} \cdot R_L$$

$$P_L = \frac{V_{Th}^{2}}{(R_{Th} + R_L)^{2}} \cdot R_L$$

**Step 4.** To find the maximum, we differentiate $P_L$ with respect to $R_L$ and equate to zero:

$$\frac{dP_L}{dR_L} = 0$$

**Step 5.** Writing the full derivative (using quotient rule):

$$\frac{dP_L}{dR_L} = V_{Th}^{2} \cdot \frac{d}{dR_L}\left[\frac{R_L}{(R_{Th} + R_L)^{2}}\right]$$

**Step 6.** Applying the quotient rule $\dfrac{d}{dx}\!\left(\dfrac{u}{v}\right) = \dfrac{u'v - uv'}{v^{2}}$ where $u = R_L$, $u' = 1$, $v = (R_{Th} + R_L)^{2}$, $v' = 2(R_{Th} + R_L)$:

$$\frac{dP_L}{dR_L} = V_{Th}^{2} \cdot \frac{(1)(R_{Th} + R_L)^{2} - R_L \cdot 2(R_{Th} + R_L)}{(R_{Th} + R_L)^{4}}$$

**Step 7.** Simplify the numerator by factoring out one $(R_{Th} + R_L)$:

$$\frac{dP_L}{dR_L} = V_{Th}^{2} \cdot \frac{(R_{Th} + R_L)\big[(R_{Th} + R_L) - 2R_L\big]}{(R_{Th} + R_L)^{4}}$$

$$\frac{dP_L}{dR_L} = V_{Th}^{2} \cdot \frac{(R_{Th} + R_L) - 2R_L}{(R_{Th} + R_L)^{3}}$$

**Step 8.** Setting the derivative to zero (numerator must vanish, denominator is never zero):

$$(R_{Th} + R_L) - 2R_L = 0$$

$$R_{Th} - R_L = 0$$

$$\boxed{R_L = R_{Th}}$$

**Step 9.** The second-derivative test confirms a maximum:

$$\frac{d^{2}P_L}{dR_L^{2}}\bigg|_{R_L = R_{Th}} < 0 \quad \text{(concave down — true maximum)}$$

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Maximum Power Expression

**Step 1.** Substitute $R_L = R_{Th}$ back into the power equation:

$$P_{max} = \frac{V_{Th}^{2} \cdot R_{Th}}{(R_{Th} + R_{Th})^{2}}$$

**Step 2.** Simplify the denominator:

$$P_{max} = \frac{V_{Th}^{2} \cdot R_{Th}}{(2R_{Th})^{2}} = \frac{V_{Th}^{2} \cdot R_{Th}}{4 R_{Th}^{2}}$$

**Step 3.** Cancel one $R_{Th}$:

$$\boxed{P_{max} = \frac{V_{Th}^{2}}{4 R_{Th}}}$$

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Efficiency at Maximum Power Transfer

**Step 1.** Total power supplied by the source equals power dissipated in $R_{Th}$ plus power delivered to $R_L$:

$$P_{total} = I_L^{2}(R_{Th} + R_L) = I_L^{2} R_{Th} + I_L^{2} R_L$$

**Step 2.** Efficiency is the ratio of load power to total power:

$$\eta = \frac{P_L}{P_{total}} = \frac{I_L^{2} R_L}{I_L^{2}(R_{Th} + R_L)}$$

**Step 3.** Cancel $I_L^{2}$:

$$\eta = \frac{R_L}{R_{Th} + R_L}$$

**Step 4.** Apply the MPP condition $R_L = R_{Th}$:

$$\eta_{MPP} = \frac{R_{Th}}{R_{Th} + R_{Th}} = \frac{R_{Th}}{2 R_{Th}} = \frac{1}{2}$$

$$\boxed{\eta_{MPP} = 50\% = 0.5}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Insight:** At maximum power transfer, **half the total generated power is wasted as heat in the source resistance**. This is why MPTT is avoided in high-power systems (e.g., power grids) where efficiency matters more than power magnitude.

---

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Style)

**Problem:** A linear network has $V_{Th} = 24\,\text{V}$ and $R_{Th} = 6\,\Omega$. Find (a) $R_L$ for max power, (b) $P_{max}$, (c) efficiency at MPP.

**Solution:**

**Part (a):** By MPTT,

$$R_L = R_{Th} = 6\,\Omega$$

**Part (b):** 

$$P_{max} = \frac{V_{Th}^{2}}{4 R_{Th}} = \frac{(24)^{2}}{4 \times 6} = \frac{576}{24} = 24\,\text{W}$$

**Part (c):**

$$\eta_{MPP} = \frac{R_L}{R_{Th} + R_L} = \frac{6}{6 + 6} = 0.5 = 50\%$$

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Conceptual Architecture — Power vs Load Resistance

The following Mermaid block represents the conceptual flow of MPTT analysis — from network reduction to result interpretation.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startA[Step 1: Identify Load Terminals a-b]
    startB[Step 2: Deactivate Independent Sources]
    startC[Step 3: Compute R_Th from terminals a-b]
    startD[Step 4: Compute V_Th as open-circuit V_ab]
    startE[Step 5: Build Thevenin Equivalent]
    startF[Step 6: Apply Condition R_L = R_Th]
    startG[Step 7: Compute P_max using V_Th and R_Th]
    startH[Step 8: Verify with dP_L / dR_L = 0]

    startA --> startB --> startC --> startD --> startE --> startF --> startG --> startH
    startG --> resultA[Output: R_L for max power transfer]
    startG --> resultB[Output: Numerical value of P_max]
    startG --> resultC[Output: Efficiency at MPP = 50 percent]
```

## 4.2 Power-vs-Load-Resistance Behavioural Map

The following diagram illustrates the **non-monotonic nature** of the load power curve and highlights the unique maximum.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    axisA[Region A: R_L less than R_Th] -->|Under-matched| axisB[Peak Point: R_L equals R_Th]
    axisB -->|Over-matched| axisC[Region C: R_L greater than R_Th]
    axisA --> noteA[Current dominant, voltage is small]
    axisB --> noteB[Maximum power transferred: V_Th squared over 4 R_Th]
    axisC --> noteC[Voltage dominant, current is small]
    noteB --> finalOut[Both voltage and current contribute optimally]
```

## 4.3 Thevenin Equivalent — Functional Block Diagram

The block diagram below represents the abstract functional reduction of a complex linear network into the simple two-element Thevenin form required for MPTT analysis.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    blockA[Complex Linear Network with Independent Sources] -->|Terminals a and b| blockB[Thevenin Equivalent]
    blockB --> blockC[Voltage Source V_Th in series with Resistor R_Th]
    blockC -->|Drives| blockD[Variable Load R_L]
    blockD -->|Power dissipated| blockE[Load Power P_L]
    blockE -->|Maximized when| blockF[R_L equals R_Th]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Why these diagrams?** MPTT is fundamentally a **two-port abstraction**. The above block diagrams emphasize that *all internal complexity* of the network disappears once the Thevenin equivalent is constructed — the theorem then applies to a simple one-loop series circuit.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 📘 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**State and explain the Maximum Power Transfer Theorem for a DC resistive network.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

The Maximum Power Transfer Theorem states that in a linear bilateral network containing independent sources, the power delivered to a variable load resistance $R_L$ is maximum when the load resistance equals the Thevenin equivalent resistance $R_{Th}$ of the network as seen from the load terminals, i.e., $R_L = R_{Th}$. The maximum power delivered under this condition is given by:

$$P_{max} = \frac{V_{Th}^{2}}{4 R_{Th}}$$

The efficiency of power transfer at this point is exactly **50%**, as half the total source power is dissipated in $R_{Th}$ itself.

---

### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Why is the efficiency at maximum power transfer only 50% in a resistive circuit? Justify mathematically.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

The efficiency at any load condition is:

$$\eta = \frac{P_L}{P_{source}} = \frac{I_L^{2} R_L}{I_L^{2}(R_{Th} + R_L)} = \frac{R_L}{R_{Th} + R_L}$$

At the maximum power transfer condition $R_L = R_{Th}$:

$$\eta_{MPP} = \frac{R_{Th}}{R_{Th} + R_{Th}} = \frac{1}{2} = 50\%$$

The remaining 50% is dissipated as heat in the source resistance $R_{Th}$, because the same current $I_L$ flows through both $R_{Th}$ and $R_L$, and they are equal in magnitude. This is an **inherent consequence** of the matching condition in purely resistive networks.

---

## 📕 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 — Modified]`

**(a)** Derive the condition for maximum power transfer in a DC network driven by a Thevenin equivalent $V_{Th}$ and $R_{Th}$ terminated by a variable load $R_L$. Show that the maximum power is $P_{max} = \dfrac{V_{Th}^{2}}{4 R_{Th}}$. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** For the network shown (conceptually), a $30\,\text{V}$ source is connected in series with a $10\,\Omega$ resistor, and this combination feeds a load $R_L$ through a parallel combination of a $20\,\Omega$ and $40\,\Omega resistor. Compute $R_L$ for maximum power transfer and find $P_{max}$. **(7 Marks)**

---

**Model Solution for Question A:**

### Part (a) — Derivation (7 Marks)

**Step 1:** Express load current using Thevenin equivalent:

$$I_L = \frac{V_{Th}}{R_{Th} + R_L}$$

**Step 2:** Load power:

$$P_L = I_L^{2} R_L = \frac{V_{Th}^{2} R_L}{(R_{Th} + R_L)^{2}}$$

**Step 3:** Differentiate with respect to $R_L$ and set to zero:

$$\frac{dP_L}{dR_L} = V_{Th}^{2} \cdot \frac{(R_{Th} + R_L)^{2} - R_L \cdot 2(R_{Th} + R_L)}{(R_{Th} + R_L)^{4}} = 0$$

**Step 4:** Simplify numerator:

$$(R_{Th} + R_L) - 2R_L = 0 \implies R_L = R_{Th}$$

**Step 5:** Substitute back to get $P_{max}$:

$$P_{max} = \frac{V_{Th}^{2} R_{Th}}{(2R_{Th})^{2}} = \frac{V_{Th}^{2}}{4R_{Th}} \qquad \blacksquare$$

---

### Part (b) — Numerical Problem (7 Marks)

**Given:** $V = 30\,\text{V}$, $R_1 = 10\,\Omega$, $R_2 = 20\,\Omega \parallel 40\,\Omega$ in series with the load network.

**Step 1:** Compute parallel combination:

$$R_2 = \frac{20 \times 40}{20 + 40} = \frac{800}{60} = \frac{40}{3}\,\Omega \approx 13.33\,\Omega$$

**Step 2:** Compute Thevenin resistance (source $30\,\text{V}$ replaced by short):

$$R_{Th} = R_1 + R_2 = 10 + \frac{40}{3} = \frac{30 + 40}{3} = \frac{70}{3}\,\Omega \approx 23.33\,\Omega$$

**Step 3:** Compute Thevenin voltage (open-circuit voltage at load terminals):

The voltage across $R_2$ when load is open:

$$V_{Th} = 30 \cdot \frac{R_2}{R_1 + R_2} = 30 \cdot \frac{40/3}{70/3} = 30 \cdot \frac{40}{70} = \frac{1200}{70} = \frac{120}{7}\,\text{V} \approx 17.14\,\text{V}$$

**Step 4:** Apply MPTT condition:

$$R_L = R_{Th} = \frac{70}{3}\,\Omega \approx 23.33\,\Omega$$

**Step 5:** Compute maximum power:

$$P_{max} = \frac{V_{Th}^{2}}{4 R_{Th}} = \frac{(120/7)^{2}}{4 \times (70/3)} = \frac{14400/49}{280/3} = \frac{14400}{49} \cdot \frac{3}{280}$$

$$P_{max} = \frac{43200}{13720} = \frac{1080}{343} \approx 3.15\,\text{W}$$

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 — Modified]`

**(a)** Prove that the efficiency of power transfer in a purely resistive circuit under the maximum power transfer condition is exactly 50%. Comment on the engineering significance of this result. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A Thevenin source has $V_{Th} = 50\,\text{V}$ and $R_{Th} = 5\,\Omega$. Determine (i) the value of $R_L$ for MPP, (ii) the power dissipated in $R_L$ at MPP, (iii) the power lost in $R_{Th}$ at MPP, and (iv) verify that the total source power is twice the load power. **(7 Marks)**

---

**Model Solution for Question B:**

### Part (a) — Proof & Engineering Significance (7 Marks)

**Proof:**

The total power supplied by the Thevenin source is:

$$P_{source} = V_{Th} \cdot I_L = V_{Th} \cdot \frac{V_{Th}}{R_{Th} + R_L} = \frac{V_{Th}^{2}}{R_{Th} + R_L}$$

The power delivered to the load is:

$$P_L = I_L^{2} R_L = \frac{V_{Th}^{2} R_L}{(R_{Th} + R_L)^{2}}$$

Efficiency:

$$\eta = \frac{P_L}{P_{source}} = \frac{V_{Th}^{2} R_L / (R_{Th} + R_L)^{2}}{V_{Th}^{2} / (R_{Th} + R_L)} = \frac{R_L}{R_{Th} + R_L}$$

At MPP, $R_L = R_{Th}$:

$$\eta_{MPP} = \frac{R_{Th}}{2 R_{Th}} = 0.5 = 50\% \qquad \blacksquare$$

**Engineering Significance (3 marks):**

- In **communication systems** (low-power, high-frequency), MPTT is **desirable** because signal levels are small and 50% loss is acceptable for maximum information transfer.
- In **power transmission systems** (high-power), MPTT is **undesirable** because 50% energy loss is economically and environmentally unacceptable — efficiency-optimized designs use $R_L \gg R_{Th}$.
- In **electronic instrumentation**, MPTT is generally avoided to preserve **bandwidth** and **signal fidelity**.

---

### Part (b) — Numerical Verification (7 Marks)

**Given:** $V_{Th} = 50\,\text{V}$, $R_{Th} = 5\,\Omega$

**(i) Load for MPP:**

$$R_L = R_{Th} = 5\,\Omega$$

**(ii) Power in $R_L$ at MPP:**

$$P_{max} = \frac{V_{Th}^{2}}{4 R_{Th}} = \frac{(50)^{2}}{4 \times 5} = \frac{2500}{20} = 125\,\text{W}$$

**(iii) Power lost in $R_{Th}$ at MPP:**

Current at MPP:

$$I_{MPP} = \frac{V_{Th}}{R_{Th} + R_L} = \frac{50}{5 + 5} = 5\,\text{A}$$

Power lost in source resistance:

$$P_{Th} = I_{MPP}^{2} \cdot R_{Th} = (5)^{2} \times 5 = 25 \times 5 = 125\,\text{W}$$

**(iv) Verification:**

$$P_{source} = P_L + P_{Th} = 125 + 125 = 250\,\text{W}$$

Ratio check:

$$\frac{P_{source}}{P_L} = \frac{250}{125} = 2 \quad \checkmark$$

This confirms $P_{source} = 2 P_L$, validating the 50% efficiency result.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **🔴 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting to find $V_{Th}$** — Many students apply $P_{max} = \dfrac{V^{2}}{4R}$ using the *source voltage* instead of the **Thevenin voltage**. Always reduce to Thevenin first.
> 2. **Sign error in $V_{Th}$** — Polarity matters in KTU marking. A flipped $V_{Th}$ gives the *same* power (since it's squared) but loses a step-mark in intermediate calculation.
> 3. **Applying MPTT to reactive loads** — In AC circuits with complex impedances, use $Z_L = Z_{Th}^{*}$, not $R_L = R_{Th}$. This is a frequent **lethal error**.
> 4. **Ignoring dependent sources** — When deactivating for $R_{Th}$, **dependent sources must remain active**. A common deduction is one full mark.
> 5. **Units and significant figures** — KTU expects SI units ($\Omega$, V, W) clearly labelled. Missing units = half-mark deduction per step.

---

## ✅ Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Core Condition:** $R_L = R_{Th}$ (resistive networks only).
- **Maximum Power Formula:** $P_{max} = \dfrac{V_{Th}^{2}}{4 R_{Th}}$.
- **Efficiency at MPP:** Always exactly **50%** for purely resistive networks.
- **Thevenin Equivalent is Mandatory:** MPTT cannot be applied directly to a complex circuit — always reduce to $V_{Th}$–$R_{Th}$ form first.
- **Source Deactivation Rule:** Short independent voltage sources; open independent current sources. **Never deactivate dependent sources.**
- **AC Extension:** Use conjugate matching $Z_L = Z_{Th}^{*}$ for frequency-domain analysis.
- **Engineering Trade-off:** MPTT = maximum *power* ≠ maximum *efficiency*. Choose based on application (communications vs power transmission).
- **Voltage at MPP:** $V_L = V_{Th}/2$ (half of source voltage drops across $R_{Th}$, half across $R_L$).
- **Current at MPP:** $I_{MPP} = V_{Th} / (2 R_{Th})$ (half of the short-circuit current).
- **Verification Tool:** Second-derivative test confirms $R_L = R_{Th}$ yields a *true maximum* (not a minimum or saddle point).
- **Curvature Nature:** $P_L$ vs $R_L$ is a **concave-down** curve — it rises from $0$, peaks at $R_L = R_{Th}$, then falls asymptotically to $0$.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Reciprocity Theorem
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCICT302 - ELECTRONIC CIRCUITS AND NETWORKS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Reciprocity Theorem

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal KTU 2024 Definition

The **Reciprocity Theorem** is a fundamental network theorem that applies to all **linear, bilateral, and passive (or single-independent-source) networks**. In its most rigorous form, the theorem states:

> *"In any linear, bilateral, time-invariant network containing only passive elements and a single independent source, the ratio of the excitation (input source) to the response (output measurement) remains constant when the positions of the excitation and the response are interchanged."*

In mathematical terms, if a voltage source $V_s$ applied at Port 1 produces a current $I_2$ at Port 2 (with Port 2 short-circuited), and the same source $V_s$ applied at Port 2 produces a current $I_1$ at Port 1 (with Port 1 short-circuited), then:

$$I_2 \big|_{\text{at Port 2}} = I_1 \big|_{\text{at Port 1}}$$

The corresponding **transfer admittance** relationship is:

$$Y_{12} = \left.\dfrac{I_2}{V_s}\right|_{\text{Source at Port 1}} = \left.\dfrac{I_1}{V_s}\right|_{\text{Source at Port 2}} = Y_{21}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Board Exam Note:** The Reciprocity Theorem is **only applicable** when the network satisfies ALL three conditions: (1) **Linearity** — governed by linear V-I relations; (2) **Bilaterality** — current flows identically in both directions through each element; and (3) **Time-Invariance** — element values do not change with time. Networks containing **diodes, transistors (when biased nonlinearly), or dependent sources with asymmetric transfer functions** will generally violate reciprocity.

## 1.2 Real-World Analogy (Intuitive Explanation)

Imagine a long corridor with several interconnected rooms. You stand in Room A and shout; the sound that reaches Room B has a certain loudness (the "response"). Now, swap places — you stand in Room B and produce the same shout; the sound that reaches Room A has the **exact same loudness** as before. The acoustic pathway is **reciprocal**: it does not favor one direction over the other.

In an electrical network, the "shout" is the **excitation (voltage or current source)** and the "sound received" is the **response (current or voltage)**. The Reciprocity Theorem guarantees that the network treats both directions of signal propagation **identically** — a property of utmost importance in **antenna design, transmission line theory, and two-port network synthesis**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Common Student Misconception:** Reciprocity does **NOT** state that the *voltage* at one port equals the *voltage* at the other. It states that the **transfer ratio** (a current divided by a voltage) is symmetric. The currents themselves will only match under specific short-circuit conditions at the measurement port.

## 1.3 Three Formal Statements of Reciprocity (KTU-Favored Forms)

The theorem can be stated in three equivalent forms depending on the type of excitation and response:

| Form | Excitation | Response | Reciprocal Configuration |
|:----:|:----------:|:--------:|:------------------------:|
| **Statement 1** | Voltage source $V_s$ at Port 1 | Short-circuit current $I_{sc}$ at Port 2 | $V_s$ at Port 2, $I_{sc}$ measured at Port 1 |
| **Statement 2** | Current source $I_s$ at Port 1 | Open-circuit voltage $V_{oc}$ at Port 2 | $I_s$ at Port 2, $V_{oc}$ measured at Port 1 |
| **Statement 3** | Voltage source at one branch | Current in another branch | Interchange source branch and ammeter branch |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Conditions for Validity (Step-by-Step Logic)

The Reciprocity Theorem holds **if and only if** the following four conditions are satisfied simultaneously:

1. **Linearity:** Every element obeys a linear constitutive relation. Resistors follow Ohm's Law ($V=IR$), capacitors follow $i = C\,\dfrac{dv}{dt}$, and inductors follow $v = L\,\dfrac{di}{dt}$. The principle of superposition must hold.

2. **Bilaterality:** Each element conducts identically in both directions. A $10\,\Omega$ resistor remains $10\,\Omega$ whether current flows left-to-right or right-to-left. **Diodes, transistors, and op-amp-based active circuits are typically non-reciprocal.**

3. **Single Independent Source:** Only one independent source is active in the network at the time of analysis. If multiple independent sources exist, the Reciprocity Theorem must be applied to each source separately (using superposition first).

4. **No Time-Varying Elements:** Element values must be constants. A resistor whose value changes with time ($R = R(t)$) will generally violate reciprocity.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why does reciprocity fail with dependent sources?** A dependent source's value depends on a *controlling variable* elsewhere in the network. When the source and response are swapped, the controlling-variable location may change, altering the effective value of the source. The symmetric Z-matrix property is broken unless the network is a special class called **reciprocal (or reverse) networks**.

## 2.2 Mathematical Foundation — The Z-Matrix Symmetry

Consider a two-port network characterized by its **open-circuit impedance parameters (Z-parameters)**:

$$\begin{aligned}
V_1 &= Z_{11}\,I_1 + Z_{12}\,I_2 \\
V_2 &= Z_{21}\,I_1 + Z_{22}\,I_2
\end{aligned}$$

For a **reciprocal network**, the off-diagonal (transfer) impedances are equal:

$$Z_{12} = Z_{21}$$

This single equation is the **mathematical soul** of the Reciprocity Theorem. It emerges from the fact that the mesh-impedance matrix $\mathbf{Z}_{mesh}$ of a linear bilateral network is **symmetric**, meaning its $(i,j)$ and $(j,i)$ cofactors are equal.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 High-Yield Concept:** If a KTU question provides a network with given element values and asks you to **verify reciprocity**, the fastest approach is to compute $Z_{12}$ and $Z_{21}$ using mesh/nodal analysis and show their equality. This is a 7-mark derivation question type.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

| Formula | Description | Conditions |
|:-------:|:-----------:|:----------:|
| $Z_{12} = Z_{21}$ | Transfer impedance symmetry (mesh basis) | Linear, bilateral, passive |
| $Y_{12} = Y_{21}$ | Transfer admittance symmetry (nodal basis) | Linear, bilateral, passive |
| $h_{12} = -h_{21}$ | Hybrid-parameter reciprocity relation | Two-port, reciprocal |
| $\dfrac{I_2}{V_s}\bigg\vert_{\text{Port 1 source}} = \dfrac{I_1}{V_s}\bigg\vert_{\text{Port 2 source}}$ | Direct current reciprocity (Statement 1) | Linear, bilateral, single source |
| $\dfrac{V_2}{I_s}\bigg\vert_{\text{Port 1 source}} = \dfrac{V_1}{I_s}\bigg\vert_{\text{Port 2 source}}$ | Direct voltage reciprocity (Statement 2) | Linear, bilateral, single source |
| $\Delta_{pq} = \Delta_{qp}$ | Cofactor symmetry of impedance matrix | Bilateral mesh network |
| $I_2 = \dfrac{\Delta_{1q}}{\Delta} \cdot V_s$ | Mesh current via Cramer's Rule | Standard mesh analysis |
| $G_{12} = G_{21}$ | Transfer conductance symmetry (nodal) | Linear, bilateral, passive |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

The Reciprocity Theorem is not just an academic curiosity — it is a cornerstone of modern electrical engineering:

- **Antenna Engineering:** A transmitting antenna and a receiving antenna of identical geometry will have the same radiation pattern and gain. This is the **Rayleigh-Carson reciprocity principle**, used in radar, Wi-Fi, and 5G/6G antenna design.
- **Telecommunications:** The **ABCD-parameters** of a transmission line are reciprocal, meaning the same line can be used in either direction with the same transfer characteristics.
- **Filter Design:** Symmetric lattice and ladder filters are designed using reciprocity to ensure equal forward and reverse gain.
- **Seismology & Acoustics:** The wave equation is self-adjoint, leading to reciprocity in seismic surveys and ultrasound imaging.
- **Quantum Mechanics:** The **Green's function** is symmetric, $G(r, r') = G(r', r)$, which is the quantum-mechanical analogue of network reciprocity.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Formal Proof of the Reciprocity Theorem Using Mesh Analysis

**Setup:** Consider a linear, bilateral network having $n$ independent meshes. Let an independent voltage source $V_s$ be placed in **mesh $p$**, and let the response be the mesh current $I_q$ in **mesh $q$**. The mesh impedance matrix is $\mathbf{Z}$, an $n \times n$ symmetric matrix with elements $Z_{ij}$ such that $Z_{ij} = Z_{ji}$ (bilaterality).

**Step 1:** Write the mesh equations in matrix form:

$$\mathbf{Z} \cdot \mathbf{I} = \mathbf{V}$$

where $\mathbf{I} = [I_1, I_2, \ldots, I_n]^T$ and $\mathbf{V}$ contains the source voltages.

**Step 2:** For the **original configuration**, $V_s$ is the only non-zero entry in $\mathbf{V}$, placed at row $p$:

$$\mathbf{V}^{(1)} = [\underbrace{0, 0, \ldots, V_s}_{\text{position } p}, \ldots, 0]^T$$

**Step 3:** Apply **Cramer's Rule** to solve for $I_q$:

$$I_q^{(1)} = \dfrac{\Delta_{pq}}{\Delta} \cdot V_s$$

where $\Delta = \det(\mathbf{Z})$ and $\Delta_{pq}$ is the cofactor obtained by deleting row $p$ and column $q$ from $\Delta$, multiplied by $(-1)^{p+q}$.

**Step 4:** For the **reciprocal configuration**, place $V_s$ in mesh $q$ (the response location) and measure the current in mesh $p$ (the original source location):

$$\mathbf{V}^{(2)} = [\underbrace{0, 0, \ldots, V_s}_{\text{position } q}, \ldots, 0]^T$$

**Step 5:** Apply Cramer's Rule again:

$$I_p^{(2)} = \dfrac{\Delta_{qp}}{\Delta} \cdot V_s$$

**Step 6:** Recognize the **symmetry of cofactors.** Because $\mathbf{Z}$ is symmetric ($Z_{ij} = Z_{ji}$ for all $i, j$), the determinant $\Delta$ is symmetric under row-column interchange. The cofactor obtained by deleting row $p$ and column $q$ is **identical** to the cofactor obtained by deleting row $q$ and column $p$:

$$\Delta_{pq} = \Delta_{qp}$$

**Step 7:** Therefore:

$$I_q^{(1)} = I_p^{(2)}$$

This is the formal statement of the Reciprocity Theorem. The current observed in mesh $q$ due to a source in mesh $p$ equals the current observed in mesh $p$ when the same source is moved to mesh $q$. $\blacksquare$

## 3.2 Numerical Verification — T-Network Worked Example

**Problem:** Verify the Reciprocity Theorem for the T-network shown below, with $R_1 = 2\,\Omega$, $R_2 = 3\,\Omega$, and $R_3 = 6\,\Omega$.

**Network Topology:**
- $R_1$ is the upper series arm (Port 1 to center node)
- $R_2$ is the upper series arm (Port 2 to center node)
- $R_3$ is the shunt arm (center node to common/ground)

### Case 1: Source $V_s = 10\,\text{V}$ at Port 1, Port 2 short-circuited

**Step 1:** Identify the equivalent resistance seen by the source. From Port 1, the path is $R_1$ in series with the parallel combination of $R_2$ and $R_3$ (since Port 2 is shorted, $R_2$ is connected directly to ground):

$$R_{\text{eq}}^{(1)} = R_1 + (R_2 \parallel R_3) = 2 + \dfrac{3 \times 6}{3 + 6} = 2 + 2 = 4\,\Omega$$

**Step 2:** Compute the input current:

$$I_1^{(1)} = \dfrac{V_s}{R_{\text{eq}}^{(1)}} = \dfrac{10}{4} = 2.5\,\text{A}$$

**Step 3:** Compute the voltage at the center node (across $R_3$):

$$V_{\text{center}}^{(1)} = I_1^{(1)} \times (R_2 \parallel R_3) = 2.5 \times 2 = 5\,\text{V}$$

**Step 4:** Compute the short-circuit current at Port 2 (current through the short, which is the current through $R_2$ since the short bypasses the load):

$$I_{2,\text{sc}}^{(1)} = \dfrac{V_{\text{center}}^{(1)}}{R_2} = \dfrac{5}{3} \approx 1.667\,\text{A}$$

### Case 2: Source $V_s = 10\,\text{V}$ at Port 2, Port 1 short-circuited

**Step 1:** By symmetry of analysis, the equivalent resistance seen from Port 2:

$$R_{\text{eq}}^{(2)} = R_2 + (R_1 \parallel R_3) = 3 + \dfrac{2 \times 6}{2 + 6} = 3 + 1.5 = 4.5\,\Omega$$

**Step 2:** Input current:

$$I_2^{(2)} = \dfrac{V_s}{R_{\text{eq}}^{(2)}} = \dfrac{10}{4.5} = \dfrac{20}{9} \approx 2.222\,\text{A}$$

**Step 3:** Voltage at the center node:

$$V_{\text{center}}^{(2)} = I_2^{(2)} \times (R_1 \parallel R_3) = \dfrac{20}{9} \times 1.5 = \dfrac{10}{3} \approx 3.333\,\text{V}$$

**Step 4:** Short-circuit current at Port 1:

$$I_{1,\text{sc}}^{(2)} = \dfrac{V_{\text{center}}^{(2)}}{R_1} = \dfrac{10/3}{2} = \dfrac{5}{3} \approx 1.667\,\text{A}$$

### Verification

$$I_{2,\text{sc}}^{(1)} = \dfrac{5}{3}\,\text{A} = I_{1,\text{sc}}^{(2)} \quad \checkmark$$

**The two short-circuit currents are equal.** The Reciprocity Theorem is verified numerically. The transfer admittance is:

$$Y_{12} = Y_{21} = \dfrac{5/3}{10} = \dfrac{1}{6}\,\text{S} = 0.1667\,\text{S}$$

## 3.3 Compact Summary Table — Analytical Matrix

| Quantity | Case 1 (Source at Port 1) | Case 2 (Source at Port 2) | Equality? |
|:--------:|:------------------------:|:-------------------------:|:---------:|
| Input current | $2.5\,\text{A}$ | $2.222\,\text{A}$ | Not equal |
| Center node voltage | $5\,\text{V}$ | $3.333\,\text{V}$ | Not equal |
| Short-circuit current at far port | $5/3\,\text{A}$ | $5/3\,\text{A}$ | **Equal ✓** |
| Transfer admittance | $1/6\,\text{S}$ | $1/6\,\text{S}$ | **Equal ✓** |

This table crystallizes the essence of reciprocity: **the input quantities are not preserved, but the transfer ratio is invariant under port interchange.**

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Reciprocity Theorem — Block-Level Functional Flow

The Mermaid diagram below illustrates the conceptual interchange of excitation and response locations, which is the operational essence of the Reciprocity Theorem.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[LINEAR BILATERAL PASSIVE NETWORK N] --> B[Configuration One: Original Setup]
    A --> C[Configuration Two: Reciprocal Setup]
    
    B --> D[Voltage Source Vs applied at Port 1]
    D --> E[Response: Short Circuit Current I_sc measured at Port 2]
    E --> F[Transfer Ratio G12 = I_sc divided by Vs]
    
    C --> G[Voltage Source Vs applied at Port 2]
    G --> H[Response: Short Circuit Current I_sc measured at Port 1]
    H --> I[Transfer Ratio G21 = I_sc divided by Vs]
    
    F --> J{Equality Check: G12 equals G21}
    I --> J
    
    J --> K[Result: Reciprocity Theorem Holds for Network N]
    
    K --> L[Engineering Applications]
    L --> M[Antenna Design and RF Systems]
    L --> N[Two Port Filter Networks]
    L --> O[Transmission Line Analysis]
    L --> P[Acoustic and Seismic Wave Propagation]
```

## 4.2 Schematic Interpretation of the T-Network (Textual Block Diagram)

To support the Mermaid flow above, the following block representation captures the functional topology used in the worked numerical example:

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    SRC1[Voltage Source Vs at Port 1] --> R1A[Resistor R1 equals 2 ohm]
    R1A --> CN[Center Node]
    CN --> R2A[Resistor R2 equals 3 ohm]
    CN --> R3A[Resistor R3 equals 6 ohm]
    R2A --> SC1[Short Circuit at Port 2]
    R3A --> GND1[Common Ground]
    
    SRC2[Voltage Source Vs at Port 2] --> R2B[Resistor R2 equals 3 ohm]
    R2B --> CN2[Center Node]
    CN2 --> R1B[Resistor R1 equals 2 ohm]
    CN2 --> R3B[Resistor R3 equals 6 ohm]
    R1B --> SC2[Short Circuit at Port 1]
    R3B --> GND2[Common Ground]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Reading Guide:** The two networks above are **topologically identical** — the same three resistors, the same source value $V_s = 10\,\text{V}$, and the same short-circuit termination. The only difference is the **port assignment**, demonstrating that the current response at the shorted port is identical in both cases ($1.667\,\text{A}$). This is the visual essence of the theorem.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam — May 2024]
**State the Reciprocity Theorem. Mention the conditions under which it is applicable.**

**Model Answer:**

The Reciprocity Theorem states that in any **linear, bilateral, and passive** network containing a single independent source, the ratio of excitation to response is invariant when the positions of the source and the measuring instrument are interchanged.

Mathematically, if a voltage source $V_s$ at Port 1 produces a short-circuit current $I_{sc}$ at Port 2, then placing the same $V_s$ at Port 2 produces the same $I_{sc}$ at Port 1.

**Conditions for Validity:**
1. The network must be **linear** (superposition holds).
2. The network must be **bilateral** ($Z_{ij} = Z_{ji}$).
3. Only **one independent source** should be active at a time.
4. The network must be **time-invariant** (element values are constants).

*(Examiner's Note: 1 mark for statement, 2 marks for the four conditions.)*

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**In a linear bilateral network, a $12\,\text{V}$ source applied at Port 1 produces a current of $2\,\text{A}$ through a short circuit at Port 2. What current will flow through a short circuit at Port 1 if the same $12\,\text{V}$ source is applied at Port 2? Justify using the Reciprocity Theorem.**

**Model Answer:**

By the **Reciprocity Theorem**, for a linear bilateral network, the transfer admittance is symmetric:

$$Y_{12} = Y_{21} \implies \dfrac{I_{sc}}{V_s}\bigg\vert_{\text{original}} = \dfrac{I_{sc}}{V_s}\bigg\vert_{\text{reciprocal}}$$

Since the source magnitude is unchanged ($V_s = 12\,\text{V}$) and the network is reciprocal, the short-circuit current at Port 1 will be **exactly $2\,\text{A}$**.

> [!WARNING]
> **Pitfall:** Students often confuse this with a *voltage* measurement (e.g., Thevenin voltage) or attempt to use the *same* Thevenin equivalent. Reciprocity applies to **transfer ratios**, not to Thevenin voltages or Norton resistances individually. Always confirm that the measurement is a **short-circuit current** or **open-circuit voltage**, and that the source is moved (not duplicated).

---

## 5.2 Part B — Module Internal Choice Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question A [KTU University Exam — Jul 2024, Module 3]

**(a)** State and prove the Reciprocity Theorem using mesh analysis. List all necessary conditions. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** For the T-network shown, $R_1 = 4\,\Omega$, $R_2 = 6\,\Omega$, $R_3 = 12\,\Omega$. A $24\,\text{V}$ source is applied at Port 1 and Port 2 is short-circuited. Using the Reciprocity Theorem, find the current through the short circuit when the source is moved to Port 2 and Port 1 is short-circuited. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution to Part (a)

**Statement:** In a linear, bilateral, passive network with one independent source, the current produced in any branch due to a voltage source in any other branch is equal in magnitude and direction when the source and the branch in which the current is measured are interchanged.

**Proof via Mesh Analysis:**

Consider an $n$-mesh network with mesh impedance matrix $\mathbf{Z}$ (symmetric, $Z_{ij} = Z_{ji}$).

When source $V_s$ is in mesh $p$:

$$I_q^{(1)} = \dfrac{\Delta_{pq}}{\Delta} \cdot V_s$$

When source $V_s$ is in mesh $q$:

$$I_p^{(2)} = \dfrac{\Delta_{qp}}{\Delta} \cdot V_s$$

Since $\mathbf{Z}$ is symmetric, $\Delta_{pq} = \Delta_{qp}$, hence:

$$I_q^{(1)} = I_p^{(2)} \quad \blacksquare$$

**Conditions (1 mark):** Linear, bilateral, single independent source, time-invariant.

#### Model Solution to Part (b)

**Step 1 — Original Case (Source at Port 1, Port 2 shorted):**

$$R_{\text{eq}}^{(1)} = R_1 + (R_2 \parallel R_3) = 4 + \dfrac{6 \times 12}{6 + 12} = 4 + 4 = 8\,\Omega$$

$$I_1^{(1)} = \dfrac{24}{8} = 3\,\text{A}$$

$$V_{\text{center}}^{(1)} = 3 \times 4 = 12\,\text{V}$$

$$I_{2,\text{sc}}^{(1)} = \dfrac{12}{6} = 2\,\text{A}$$

**Step 2 — Apply Reciprocity Theorem:**

By reciprocity, the current through the short circuit at Port 1 (with source moved to Port 2) is:

$$\boxed{I_{1,\text{sc}}^{(2)} = I_{2,\text{sc}}^{(1)} = 2\,\text{A}}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark Loss Area:** Students compute the *full* Case 2 network analysis instead of invoking the Reciprocity Theorem directly. This wastes 4–5 minutes and risks arithmetic errors. **Once Case 1 is solved, the answer to Case 2 is immediate from reciprocity — no additional computation is needed.**

---

### Question B [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Module 3]

**(a)** Explain why the Reciprocity Theorem fails for a network containing a **voltage-controlled voltage source (VCVS)**. Demonstrate with a suitable example. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A linear bilateral two-port network has $Z_{11} = 10\,\Omega$, $Z_{22} = 15\,\Omega$, and $Z_{12} = Z_{21} = 5\,\Omega$. Find the current through a $2\,\Omega$ load connected at Port 2 when a $20\,\text{V}$ source is applied at Port 1. If the source is now applied at Port 2 (with the same $2\,\Omega$ load at Port 1), what is the load current? Justify your answer using the Reciprocity Theorem. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution to Part (a)

The Reciprocity Theorem relies on the **symmetry of the Z-matrix** ($Z_{12} = Z_{21}$). A VCVS introduces a controlled source whose value depends on a voltage elsewhere in the network. When the source and measurement ports are interchanged, the controlling voltage location also changes, breaking the symmetry of the network.

**Example:** Consider a network with a VCVS of gain $k$ in mesh 2 controlled by a voltage across a resistor in mesh 1. The Z-matrix has the form:

$$\mathbf{Z} = \begin{bmatrix} R_1 & 0 \\ k \cdot \alpha & R_2 \end{bmatrix}$$

The off-diagonal terms are $Z_{12} = 0$ and $Z_{21} = k\alpha \neq 0$, so $Z_{12} \neq Z_{21}$. **The matrix is asymmetric, and reciprocity fails.**

*(7 marks: 3 marks for the conceptual reason, 2 marks for the asymmetry proof, 2 marks for the numerical demonstration.)*

#### Model Solution to Part (b)

**Step 1 — Case 1: Source $V_1 = 20\,\text{V}$ at Port 1, load $R_L = 2\,\Omega$ at Port 2.**

Using the Z-parameter two-port equations with $I_2$ flowing into Port 2:

$$V_1 = Z_{11} I_1 + Z_{12} I_2 = 10\,I_1 + 5\,I_2$$

$$V_2 = Z_{21} I_1 + Z_{22} I_2 = 5\,I_1 + 15\,I_2$$

The load constraint: $V_2 = -R_L \cdot I_2 = -2\,I_2$ (passive sign convention).

Substituting into the second equation:

$$-2\,I_2 = 5\,I_1 + 15\,I_2 \implies 5\,I_1 = -17\,I_2 \implies I_1 = -\dfrac{17}{5}\,I_2$$

Substituting into the first equation:

$$20 = 10 \left(-\dfrac{17}{5}\,I_2\right) + 5\,I_2 = -34\,I_2 + 5\,I_2 = -29\,I_2$$

$$I_2 = -\dfrac{20}{29} \approx -0.6897\,\text{A}$$

The load current magnitude is $|I_L| = \dfrac{20}{29} \approx 0.69\,\text{A}$.

**Step 2 — Case 2: Source $V_2 = 20\,\text{V}$ at Port 2, load $R_L = 2\,\Omega$ at Port 1.**

By **reciprocity** ($Z_{12} = Z_{21} = 5\,\Omega$), the same mathematical structure applies with port labels swapped. The transfer admittance $Y_{21} = Y_{12}$, and the load current magnitude is **identical**:

$$\boxed{I_{L,\text{Case 2}} = \dfrac{20}{29} \approx 0.69\,\text{A}}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Valuation Pitfall:** Marks are deducted when students **re-derive** the entire two-port analysis for Case 2. The Reciprocity Theorem is the **central insight** of the problem — recognize that the port interchange leaves the transfer ratio unchanged when the load and source impedances are also swapped. Show the transfer ratio calculation for Case 1 only and invoke reciprocity for Case 2.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for Reciprocity Theorem**

- **Core Statement:** Transfer ratio (excitation → response) is invariant under port interchange in linear, bilateral, passive networks.
- **Three Equivalent Forms:** (1) Voltage source ↔ short-circuit current; (2) Current source ↔ open-circuit voltage; (3) Source branch ↔ response branch interchange.
- **Four Validity Conditions:** Linear, bilateral, single independent source, time-invariant.
- **Mathematical Foundation:** $Z_{12} = Z_{21}$ (mesh basis) or $Y_{12} = Y_{21}$ (nodal basis).
- **Fails for:** Diodes, transistors (in nonlinear regions), op-amp circuits with asymmetric feedback, and most networks with dependent sources.
- **Mesh-Analysis Proof:** Uses Cramer's Rule and the symmetry of cofactors of a symmetric Z-matrix: $\Delta_{pq} = \Delta_{qp}$.
- **Hybrid Parameter Relation:** For reciprocal two-ports, $h_{12} = -h_{21}$ (commonly tested in KTU module exams).
- **Verification Procedure:** Compute the response in original configuration, then invoke reciprocity for the swapped configuration — **no second full analysis needed**.
- **Real-World Domains:** Antenna reciprocity, transmission lines, acoustic wave propagation, seismic surveys, and quantum mechanical Green's functions.
- **Common Mistake:** Confusing reciprocity with Thevenin/Norton equivalence. Reciprocity is about **transfer** ratios, not source-side or load-side equivalents.
- **Quick Test for Reciprocity:** If a network's mesh impedance matrix has any asymmetric off-diagonal pair, reciprocity **fails**.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCICT302 - ELECTRONIC CIRCUITS AND NETWORKS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Millman's Theorem, Related Problems, Laplace Transform: Review of Laplace Transform and Inverse Laplace Transform of Common Functions and Properties, Solution of Differential Equations using Laplace Transform

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Millman's Theorem — Formal Definition

**Millman's Theorem** is a powerful network simplification technique that extends Thevenin's and Norton's theorems to circuits containing **multiple parallel branches** between two nodes, where each branch may consist of a voltage source in series with an impedance, or a current source in parallel with an admittance, or any combination thereof.

> [!IMPORTANT] **KTU 2024 Syllabus Statement**
> Millman's Theorem states that the voltage across a group of parallel branches (each containing an EMF source and an impedance) can be determined as the **weighted arithmetic mean of the individual branch EMFs**, where the weights are the branch admittances (reciprocal of impedance).

Mathematically, for $n$ parallel branches connected between two common nodes (say, node A and the reference/ground node B), the **Millman voltage** $V_{AB}$ is given by:

$$V_{AB} = \dfrac{\displaystyle\sum_{k=1}^{n} \dfrac{V_k}{Z_k} + \displaystyle\sum_{k=1}^{n} I_k}{\displaystyle\sum_{k=1}^{n} \dfrac{1}{Z_k}} = \dfrac{\displaystyle\sum_{k=1}^{n} V_k Y_k + \displaystyle\sum_{k=1}^{n} I_k}{\displaystyle\sum_{k=1}^{n} Y_k}$$

where $V_k$ is the source voltage in branch $k$, $Z_k$ is the series impedance of branch $k$, $Y_k = 1/Z_k$ is the branch admittance, and $I_k$ is any independent current source present in branch $k$.

> [!NOTE] **Compact Form (Conductance-Weighted Form)**
> When every branch contains only a voltage source in series with a resistance (no separate current sources), the simplified formula is:
> $$V_{AB} = \dfrac{\displaystyle\sum_{k=1}^{n} V_k G_k}{\displaystyle\sum_{k=1}^{n} G_k}, \quad \text{where } G_k = \dfrac{1}{R_k}$$

---

## 1.2 Laplace Transform — Formal Definition

The **Laplace Transform** is an integral transform that converts a time-domain function $f(t)$ (defined for $t \geq 0$) into a complex-frequency-domain function $F(s)$, where $s = \sigma + j\omega$ is a complex variable.

$$F(s) = \mathcal{L}\{f(t)\} = \int_{0}^{\infty} f(t)\, e^{-st}\, dt$$

The function $F(s)$ exists only for values of $s$ such that the integral **converges**. The **Inverse Laplace Transform** recovers $f(t)$ from $F(s)$:

$$f(t) = \mathcal{L}^{-1}\{F(s)\} = \dfrac{1}{2\pi j} \int_{\sigma - j\infty}^{\sigma + j\infty} F(s)\, e^{st}\, ds$$

> [!IMPORTANT] **Why Laplace Transforms in Circuit Analysis?**
> The Laplace Transform converts **integro-differential equations** in time (which are hard to solve) into **algebraic equations** in $s$ (which are easy to solve). It also naturally incorporates initial conditions, making it indispensable for transient analysis of RLC circuits.

---

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — Intuition for a First-Time Learner

### 🍕 Millman's Theorem — The "Pizza-Price-Averaging" Analogy

Imagine **five bakeries** on the same street, all selling the same type of pizza at slightly different prices: ₹120, ₹130, ₹140, ₹150, ₹160. If you naively average them, you get ₹140. But this is unfair to the big, popular bakeries that produce more pizzas. So you **weight each price by the size of the bakery** (large bakeries get more weight). The "true average price" you should expect to pay is the **conductance-weighted average**.

> In Millman's Theorem, the **parallel branches are the bakeries**, the **voltages are the pizza prices**, and the **conductances (1/R) are the bakery sizes**. The voltage at the common node is essentially a "weighted average" of all the branch EMFs.

### ⏰ Laplace Transform — The "Time-Machine Translator" Analogy

Think of the Laplace Transform as a **universal translator between two languages**:
- **Time-domain language** $f(t)$ — tells you "what happens at each moment" (e.g., voltage waveform over time).
- **Frequency-domain language** $F(s)$ — tells you "what ingredients (frequencies, growth/decay rates) combine to make the waveform."

When you translate a differential equation (time-domain) into the $s$-domain, the calculus operators $d/dt$ and $\int$ become simple multiplication and division by $s$ — just like how a difficult essay can be summarised into bullet points. Once you solve the algebraic problem, you translate back using the **Inverse Laplace Transform**.

> [!NOTE] **Key Conceptual Takeaway**
> - **Millman** = "Weighted-Average Voltage Finder" for parallel branches.
> - **Laplace** = "Differential-Equation-to-Algebra Translator" that makes circuit analysis dramatically easier.

---

## 1.4 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

> [!IMPORTANT] **Essential Parameters to Remember**
> - **Unit of $s$ in Laplace Transform:** $s$ has units of **1/time** (i.e., reciprocal seconds or **rad/s** when imaginary part is involved).
> - **Region of Convergence (ROC):** The set of $s$ values for which $\int_0^\infty f(t) e^{-st} dt$ converges. For causal exponentials $e^{at}u(t)$, ROC is $\text{Re}(s) > a$.
> - **Admittance $Y$ unit:** **Siemens (S)** = 1/Ω.
> - **Millman Voltage** is in **Volts (V)**, same as the source EMFs.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Millman's Theorem — Step-by-Step Logical Analysis

Millman's Theorem is derived by applying **KCL (Kirchhoff's Current Law)** at the common node. The analysis is built on the following structured logic:

### Step 1 — Identify the Two Common Nodes
Locate the two nodes between which multiple branches are connected in parallel. Call them **Node A** (the "floating" top node) and **Node B** (the reference/ground node).

### Step 2 — Express Each Branch as Thevenin Equivalent
Each branch, regardless of internal complexity, is converted into a Thevenin form: an EMF $V_k$ in series with an impedance $Z_k$. (Any current source with parallel admittance is source-transformed into a voltage source with series impedance.)

### Step 3 — Write KCL at Node A
The sum of currents leaving Node A equals zero. For a branch $k$, the current flowing from Node A to Node B is:

$$I_k = \dfrac{V_{AB} - V_k}{Z_k}$$

> Note: $V_k$ is signed according to its polarity with respect to Node A. If the positive terminal of $V_k$ faces Node A, $V_k$ enters with a positive sign.

### Step 4 — Sum All Branch Currents
$$\sum_{k=1}^{n} \dfrac{V_{AB} - V_k}{Z_k} = 0$$

### Step 5 — Solve Algebraically for $V_{AB}$
Rearranging the sum:

$$\sum_{k=1}^{n} \dfrac{V_{AB}}{Z_k} = \sum_{k=1}^{n} \dfrac{V_k}{Z_k}$$

$$V_{AB} \sum_{k=1}^{n} \dfrac{1}{Z_k} = \sum_{k=1}^{n} \dfrac{V_k}{Z_k}$$

> This is the **Millman Formula**. If current sources $I_k$ are present (after source transformation to voltage form, or directly in admittance form), they add to the numerator.

### Step 6 — Why This Works (The Engineering Utility)
Millman's Theorem is widely used in:
- **Power system analysis** — to find the common bus voltage when multiple generators feed a load in parallel.
- **Mixed-source circuits** — when a circuit has both voltage and current sources.
- **Quick computation** — it bypasses writing $n$ KCL/KVL equations and solves for the node voltage in **one shot**.

> [!IMPORTANT] **Special Case: Two-Branch Millman**
> For two voltage sources $V_1, V_2$ with internal resistances $R_1, R_2$ in parallel:
> $$V_{AB} = \dfrac{\dfrac{V_1}{R_1} + \dfrac{V_2}{R_2}}{\dfrac{1}{R_1} + \dfrac{1}{R_2}} = \dfrac{V_1 R_2 + V_2 R_1}{R_1 + R_2}$$
> This is the familiar **weighted average**, exactly the same form as Millman's original work on parallel generators.

---

## 2.2 Laplace Transform — Step-by-Step Logical Analysis

### Step 1 — Definition Reaffirmed
For a function $f(t)$ defined for $t \geq 0$:

$$F(s) = \int_{0}^{\infty} f(t) e^{-st}\, dt, \quad s = \sigma + j\omega$$

### Step 2 — Existence Condition
The integral converges only if $f(t)$ does not grow faster than $e^{\sigma t}$. The minimum value of $\sigma$ for convergence defines the **Region of Convergence (ROC)**.

### Step 3 — Linearity Property
If $f(t) = a\,f_1(t) + b\,f_2(t)$, then:

$$\mathcal{L}\{f(t)\} = a\,F_1(s) + b\,F_2(s)$$

This makes the transform **linear** — the cornerstone for solving linear ODEs.

### Step 4 — Differentiation Property
This is the most important property for circuit analysis:

$$\mathcal{L}\left\{\dfrac{df(t)}{dt}\right\} = s F(s) - f(0^-)$$

$$\mathcal{L}\left\{\dfrac{d^2 f(t)}{dt^2}\right\} = s^2 F(s) - s f(0^-) - f'(0^-)$$

> [!NOTE] **Physical Meaning:** Differentiation in time becomes multiplication by $s$ in the $s$-domain, with the initial-condition terms acting as "memory" of the system's past state.

### Step 5 — Integration Property
$$\mathcal{L}\left\{\int_0^t f(\tau)\, d\tau\right\} = \dfrac{F(s)}{s}$$

### Step 6 — First Shifting Theorem (Frequency Shifting)
If $\mathcal{L}\{f(t)\} = F(s)$, then:

$$\mathcal{L}\{e^{-at}\, f(t)\} = F(s + a)$$

This corresponds to a leftward shift of the ROC by $a$ in the $s$-plane.

### Step 7 — Second Shifting Theorem (Time Shifting)
If $\mathcal{L}\{f(t)\} = F(s)$ and $u(t-a)$ is the unit step function delayed by $a$:

$$\mathcal{L}\{f(t-a)\, u(t-a)\} = e^{-as}\, F(s), \quad a \geq 0$$

### Step 8 — Initial Value Theorem (IVT)
$$f(0^+) = \lim_{s \to \infty} s F(s)$$

(Provided all poles of $sF(s)$ lie in the open left half-plane.)

### Step 9 — Final Value Theorem (FVT)
$$f(\infty) = \lim_{s \to 0} s F(s)$$

(Provided all poles of $sF(s)$ lie in the open left half-plane, except possibly a simple pole at $s=0$.)

### Step 10 — Convolution Theorem
$$\mathcal{L}\{f_1(t) * f_2(t)\} = F_1(s) \cdot F_2(s)$$

This converts the complex convolution operation in time into simple multiplication in the $s$-domain.

---

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT] **Master the Table Below — These Formulas Are the Backbone of the Module**

### A. Millman's Theorem Formulas

| Scenario | Formula | Use Case |
|----------|---------|---------|
| Multiple parallel branches with voltage sources only | $V = \dfrac{\sum V_k / R_k}{\sum 1/R_k} = \dfrac{\sum V_k G_k}{\sum G_k}$ | Resistor networks with EMFs |
| Mixed sources (voltage + current) | $V = \dfrac{\sum V_k G_k + \sum I_k}{\sum G_k}$ | Networks with current sources |
| Two-branch special case | $V = \dfrac{V_1 R_2 + V_2 R_1}{R_1 + R_2}$ | Battery paralleling |

### B. Laplace Transforms of Common Functions

| $f(t)$ (Time Domain) | $F(s)$ (s-Domain) | ROC |
|----------------------|-------------------|-----|
| $\delta(t)$ (unit impulse) | $1$ | All $s$ |
| $u(t)$ (unit step) | $\dfrac{1}{s}$ | $\text{Re}(s) > 0$ |
| $t$ (ramp) | $\dfrac{1}{s^2}$ | $\text{Re}(s) > 0$ |
| $t^n$ | $\dfrac{n!}{s^{n+1}}$ | $\text{Re}(s) > 0$ |
| $e^{-at}$ | $\dfrac{1}{s + a}$ | $\text{Re}(s) > -a$ |
| $\sin(\omega t)$ | $\dfrac{\omega}{s^2 + \omega^2}$ | $\text{Re}(s) > 0$ |
| $\cos(\omega t)$ | $\dfrac{s}{s^2 + \omega^2}$ | $\text{Re}(s) > 0$ |
| $e^{-at}\sin(\omega t)$ | $\dfrac{\omega}{(s+a)^2 + \omega^2}$ | $\text{Re}(s) > -a$ |
| $e^{-at}\cos(\omega t)$ | $\dfrac{s + a}{(s+a)^2 + \omega^2}$ | $\text{Re}(s) > -a$ |
| $\sinh(at)$ | $\dfrac{a}{s^2 - a^2}$ | $\text{Re}(s) > \vert a \vert$ |
| $\cosh(at)$ | $\dfrac{s}{s^2 - a^2}$ | $\text{Re}(s) > \vert a \vert$ |

### C. Key Properties of the Laplace Transform

| Property | Time Domain | s-Domain | Engineering Use |
|----------|-------------|----------|-----------------|
| Linearity | $a f_1(t) + b f_2(t)$ | $a F_1(s) + b F_2(s)$ | Superposition |
| First Shifting (freq) | $e^{-at} f(t)$ | $F(s+a)$ | Damped oscillations |
| Second Shifting (time) | $f(t-a) u(t-a)$ | $e^{-as} F(s)$ | Delayed inputs |
| Differentiation | $\dfrac{df}{dt}$ | $sF(s) - f(0^-)$ | Solving ODEs |
| Integration | $\int_0^t f(\tau)d\tau$ | $\dfrac{F(s)}{s}$ | Capacitor analysis |
| Initial Value | $f(0^+)$ | $\lim_{s \to \infty} sF(s)$ | Quick verification |
| Final Value | $f(\infty)$ | $\lim_{s \to 0} sF(s)$ | Steady-state check |
| Convolution | $f_1 * f_2$ | $F_1(s) \cdot F_2(s)$ | System response |

> [!WARNING] **Critical Pitfall — Do NOT Use** $\vert \cdot \vert$ **with Pipes in Tables**
> In written exam answers, use words like "magnitude of $a$" or write $\vert a \vert$ carefully outside tabular structures. In the tables above, $\vert a \vert$ appears naturally without breaking the markdown layout.

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Field | Application of Millman's Theorem | Application of Laplace Transform |
|-------|----------------------------------|----------------------------------|
| **Power Systems** | Compute common bus voltage for parallel generators | Transient stability analysis |
| **Control Systems** | Sensor signal conditioning circuits | Transfer function design, PID controller design |
| **Signal Processing** | Active filter design (Sallen-Key, multiple feedback) | Filter response, Z-transform derivation |
| **Communication** | Antenna array feed networks | Modulation/demodulation analysis |
| **Biomedical** | ECG/EEG amplifier front-ends | Evoked potential analysis |
| **Aerospace** | Avionics power distribution | Autopilot stability analysis |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Complete Derivation of Millman's Theorem

Consider $n$ branches connected in parallel between two nodes **A** and **B** (ground). Each branch $k$ contains a voltage source $V_k$ and a series impedance $Z_k$. (Current sources, if any, can be source-transformed to this form.)

### Step 1 — Draw the Equivalent Schematic
Nodes A and B are connected by $n$ parallel branches. Branch $k$ has a source $V_k$ in series with $Z_k$.

### Step 2 — Write KCL at Node A
Define $V_{AB}$ as the voltage of Node A with respect to Node B. The current flowing from Node A to Node B through branch $k$ is:

$$I_k = \dfrac{V_{AB} - V_k}{Z_k}$$

> **Sign Convention:** If the positive terminal of $V_k$ faces Node A, then $V_k > 0$ in this equation.

### Step 3 — Apply KCL: Sum of All Currents Equals Zero
By KCL at Node A (assuming no external current source feeds Node A):

$$\sum_{k=1}^{n} \dfrac{V_{AB} - V_k}{Z_k} = 0$$

### Step 4 — Distribute the Sum
$$\sum_{k=1}^{n} \dfrac{V_{AB}}{Z_k} - \sum_{k=1}^{n} \dfrac{V_k}{Z_k} = 0$$

### Step 5 — Factor Out $V_{AB}$
$$V_{AB} \sum_{k=1}^{n} \dfrac{1}{Z_k} = \sum_{k=1}^{n} \dfrac{V_k}{Z_k}$$

### Step 6 — Isolate $V_{AB}$ (Millman Result)
$$V_{AB} = \dfrac{\sum_{k=1}^{n} \dfrac{V_k}{Z_k}}{\sum_{k=1}^{n} \dfrac{1}{Z_k}}$$

### Step 7 — Generalize to Include Current Sources
If a branch contains a current source $I_k$ (flowing from B to A), its contribution adds to the numerator. The full **Millman Formula** becomes:

$$V_{AB} = \dfrac{\sum_{k=1}^{n} \dfrac{V_k}{Z_k} + \sum_{k=1}^{n} I_k}{\sum_{k=1}^{n} \dfrac{1}{Z_k}}$$

> [!NOTE] **Alternative Derivation via Nodal Analysis**
> This can also be obtained directly by writing the single nodal equation at Node A, summing the admittances, and solving — a one-line procedure for any parallel network. This is what makes Millman so efficient for KTU problems.

---

## 3.2 Worked Example — Millman's Theorem (Two Parallel Batteries)

**Problem:** Two batteries with EMFs $V_1 = 12$ V and $V_2 = 10$ V, having internal resistances $R_1 = 0.3\,\Omega$ and $R_2 = 0.2\,\Omega$, are connected in parallel to a load of $5\,\Omega$. Find the voltage across the load.

### Step 1 — Identify the Two Common Nodes
The positive terminals of both batteries are joined at Node A. The negative terminals are joined at Node B (ground). The load $R_L = 5\,\Omega$ is also connected between A and B.

### Step 2 — Apply Millman's Formula (Branches are $V_1, R_1$ and $V_2, R_2$)
$$V_{AB} = \dfrac{\dfrac{V_1}{R_1} + \dfrac{V_2}{R_2}}{\dfrac{1}{R_1} + \dfrac{1}{R_2} + \dfrac{1}{R_L}}$$

### Step 3 — Substitute Numerical Values
$$V_{AB} = \dfrac{\dfrac{12}{0.3} + \dfrac{10}{0.2}}{\dfrac{1}{0.3} + \dfrac{1}{0.2} + \dfrac{1}{5}}$$

### Step 4 — Compute Numerator
$$\dfrac{12}{0.3} = 40, \quad \dfrac{10}{0.2} = 50, \quad \text{Numerator} = 90 \text{ A}$$

### Step 5 — Compute Denominator
$$\dfrac{1}{0.3} = 3.333, \quad \dfrac{1}{0.2} = 5, \quad \dfrac{1}{5} = 0.2, \quad \text{Denominator} = 8.533 \text{ S}$$

### Step 6 — Final Voltage
$$V_{AB} = \dfrac{90}{8.533} = 10.546 \text{ V}$$

### Step 7 — Load Current and Verification
$$I_L = \dfrac{V_{AB}}{R_L} = \dfrac{10.546}{5} = 2.109 \text{ A}$$

> [!NOTE] **Verification by Source Transformation**
> The Thevenin equivalent across the load is $V_{th} = 10.546$ V with $R_{th} = 1/8.533 = 0.117\,\Omega$. This gives $I_L = 10.546 / (0.117 + 5) = 2.109$ A ✓

---

## 3.3 Derivation of Laplace Transforms of Common Functions

### Derivation 1 — Laplace Transform of $u(t)$ (Unit Step)

By definition:

$$\mathcal{L}\{u(t)\} = \int_{0}^{\infty} (1) \cdot e^{-st}\, dt = \left[\dfrac{e^{-st}}{-s}\right]_0^{\infty} = \dfrac{0 - 1}{-s} = \dfrac{1}{s}$$

> This converges for $\text{Re}(s) > 0$.

### Derivation 2 — Laplace Transform of $e^{-at}$

$$\mathcal{L}\{e^{-at}\} = \int_{0}^{\infty} e^{-at} \cdot e^{-st}\, dt = \int_{0}^{\infty} e^{-(s+a)t}\, dt$$

$$= \left[\dfrac{e^{-(s+a)t}}{-(s+a)}\right]_0^{\infty} = \dfrac{0 - 1}{-(s+a)} = \dfrac{1}{s + a}$$

> Converges for $\text{Re}(s) > -a$.

### Derivation 3 — Laplace Transform of $\sin(\omega t)$

Using Euler's identity $\sin(\omega t) = \dfrac{e^{j\omega t} - e^{-j\omega t}}{2j}$:

$$\mathcal{L}\{\sin(\omega t)\} = \dfrac{1}{2j} \int_{0}^{\infty} \left[e^{-(s - j\omega)t} - e^{-(s + j\omega)t}\right] dt$$

$$= \dfrac{1}{2j} \left[\dfrac{1}{s - j\omega} - \dfrac{1}{s + j\omega}\right]$$

$$= \dfrac{1}{2j} \cdot \dfrac{(s + j\omega) - (s - j\omega)}{(s - j\omega)(s + j\omega)} = \dfrac{1}{2j} \cdot \dfrac{2j\omega}{s^2 + \omega^2} = \dfrac{\omega}{s^2 + \omega^2}$$

### Derivation 4 — Laplace Transform of $\cos(\omega t)$

Using $\cos(\omega t) = \dfrac{e^{j\omega t} + e^{-j\omega t}}{2}$:

$$\mathcal{L}\{\cos(\omega t)\} = \dfrac{1}{2}\left[\dfrac{1}{s - j\omega} + \dfrac{1}{s + j\omega}\right] = \dfrac{1}{2} \cdot \dfrac{2s}{s^2 + \omega^2} = \dfrac{s}{s^2 + \omega^2}$$

### Derivation 5 — Differentiation Property

Starting from the definition:

$$\mathcal{L}\{f'(t)\} = \int_0^\infty f'(t)\, e^{-st}\, dt$$

Integration by parts (with $u = e^{-st}$, $dv = f'(t)\,dt$, so $du = -s e^{-st} dt$, $v = f(t)$):

$$= \left[f(t)\, e^{-st}\right]_0^\infty - \int_0^\infty f(t)\,(-s e^{-st})\, dt$$

$$= \left[0 - f(0^-)\right] + s \int_0^\infty f(t)\, e^{-st}\, dt = s F(s) - f(0^-)$$

---

## 3.4 Solution of Differential Equations using Laplace Transform — Complete Walkthrough

### General Procedure (Step-by-Step)

**Step 1 — Transform Both Sides**
Apply the Laplace transform to the entire differential equation. Use linearity and the differentiation property to convert derivatives to algebraic expressions involving $s$, $F(s)$, and initial conditions.

**Step 2 — Substitute Initial Conditions**
Plug in the given values of $f(0^-)$, $f'(0^-)$, etc. This embeds the initial conditions into the algebraic equation automatically.

**Step 3 — Solve Algebraically for $F(s)$**
Treat $F(s)$ as the unknown and solve the resulting linear algebraic equation.

**Step 4 — Partial Fraction Expansion (PFE)**
Break $F(s)$ into a sum of simpler rational fractions, each of which is a recognizable Laplace transform pair.

**Step 5 — Inverse Laplace Transform**
Apply known inverse transforms to each term to obtain $f(t)$ in the time domain.

### Worked Example — Second-Order ODE

**Problem:** Solve the differential equation
$$\dfrac{d^2 y}{dt^2} + 3 \dfrac{dy}{dt} + 2y = 0, \quad y(0) = 1, \quad y'(0) = 0$$

#### Step 1 — Apply Laplace Transform
Using linearity and the differentiation property:

$$\mathcal{L}\{y''\} = s^2 Y(s) - s y(0) - y'(0) = s^2 Y(s) - s$$

$$\mathcal{L}\{3 y'\} = 3 \left[s Y(s) - y(0)\right] = 3s Y(s) - 3$$

$$\mathcal{L}\{2y\} = 2 Y(s)$$

#### Step 2 — Form the Equation
$$s^2 Y(s) - s + 3s Y(s) - 3 + 2 Y(s) = 0$$

#### Step 3 — Group Terms Containing $Y(s)$
$$Y(s) \left[s^2 + 3s + 2\right] = s + 3$$

#### Step 4 — Solve for $Y(s)$
$$Y(s) = \dfrac{s + 3}{s^2 + 3s + 2} = \dfrac{s + 3}{(s+1)(s+2)}$$

#### Step 5 — Partial Fraction Expansion
Assume:
$$\dfrac{s + 3}{(s+1)(s+2)} = \dfrac{A}{s+1} + \dfrac{B}{s+2}$$

Multiply through:
$$s + 3 = A(s+2) + B(s+1)$$

**Find A** (set $s = -1$):
$$-1 + 3 = A(-1 + 2) \Rightarrow 2 = A \Rightarrow A = 2$$

**Find B** (set $s = -2$):
$$-2 + 3 = B(-2 + 1) \Rightarrow 1 = -B \Rightarrow B = -1$$

#### Step 6 — Final Form of $Y(s)$
$$Y(s) = \dfrac{2}{s+1} - \dfrac{1}{s+2}$$

#### Step 7 — Inverse Laplace Transform
$$y(t) = 2 e^{-t} - e^{-2t}, \quad t \geq 0$$

#### Step 8 — Verification
At $t = 0$: $y(0) = 2 - 1 = 1$ ✓
$y'(t) = -2e^{-t} + 2e^{-2t}$; at $t = 0$: $y'(0) = -2 + 2 = 0$ ✓

---

### Worked Example 2 — Non-Homogeneous First-Order ODE with Forcing Function

**Problem:** Solve
$$\dfrac{dy}{dt} + 4y = e^{-t}, \quad y(0) = 0$$

#### Step 1 — Take Laplace Transform
$$s Y(s) - y(0) + 4 Y(s) = \dfrac{1}{s + 1}$$

#### Step 2 — Substitute Initial Condition
$$s Y(s) + 4 Y(s) = \dfrac{1}{s + 1}$$

#### Step 3 — Solve for $Y(s)$
$$Y(s) (s + 4) = \dfrac{1}{s + 1}$$

$$Y(s) = \dfrac{1}{(s+1)(s+4)}$$

#### Step 4 — Partial Fractions
$$\dfrac{1}{(s+1)(s+4)} = \dfrac{A}{s+1} + \dfrac{B}{s+4}$$

$$1 = A(s+4) + B(s+1)$$

Set $s = -1$: $1 = 3A \Rightarrow A = 1/3$
Set $s = -4$: $1 = -3B \Rightarrow B = -1/3$

#### Step 5 — Final Form
$$Y(s) = \dfrac{1/3}{s+1} - \dfrac{1/3}{s+4}$$

#### Step 6 — Inverse Transform
$$y(t) = \dfrac{1}{3}\left(e^{-t} - e^{-4t}\right), \quad t \geq 0$$

#### Step 7 — Verification
$y(0) = (1/3)(1 - 1) = 0$ ✓

---

## 3.5 Analytical Summary Matrix

> [!NOTE] **Mastery Matrix — When to Use What**

| Problem Type | Use This Tool | Why |
|--------------|---------------|-----|
| Two or more parallel voltage sources with series resistance | **Millman's Theorem** | One-step node voltage computation |
| Mixed voltage + current sources in parallel | **Millman's Theorem (general form)** | Avoids multiple KCL equations |
| Solving linear ODE with constant coefficients | **Laplace Transform** | Converts to algebra |
| Transient analysis of RLC circuits | **Laplace Transform** | Includes initial conditions automatically |
| Verifying steady-state value of a response | **Final Value Theorem** | No need to invert $F(s)$ |
| Predicting initial value of a response | **Initial Value Theorem** | Quick consistency check |
| Cascaded linear systems (LTI cascade) | **Convolution Theorem** | Multiplication in $s$-domain |

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — General Millman's Theorem Network

```mermaid
graph TD
    in1["V1 source branch"] --> nodeA["Node A (floating)"]
    in2["V2 source branch"] --> nodeA
    in3["V3 source branch"] --> nodeA
    inN["Vk source branch"] --> nodeA
    nodeA --> outNode["Node B (ground reference)"]
    nodeA --> load["Load ZL (optional)"]
    outNode --> ground["Ground (0V)"]
    nodeA --> vout["Output Voltage VAB (Millman Voltage)"]
```

> **Interpretation:** The block diagram shows that Node A is the "floating" top terminal where all parallel branches converge. Node B is the common ground reference. The Millman voltage $V_{AB}$ is measured across these two nodes.

---

## 4.2 Functional Architecture — Millman's Theorem Computation Flow

```mermaid
graph TD
    stepA["Identify parallel branches between two nodes"] --> stepB["Convert each branch to Thevenin form Vk + Zk"]
    stepB --> stepC["Compute admittances Yk = 1/Zk for each branch"]
    stepC --> stepD["Apply formula: VAB = sum Vk*Yk / sum Yk"]
    stepD --> stepE["Add current source contributions to numerator if any"]
    stepE --> stepF["Compute final node voltage VAB"]
    stepF --> stepG["Use VAB to find load current and branch currents"]
```

---

## 4.3 Block Diagram — Laplace Transform Workflow for Solving ODEs

```mermaid
graph LR
    in1["f(t) - Time domain function"] --> procA["Apply Laplace Transform"]
    procA --> out1["F(s) - s-domain function"]
    in2["Differential Equation in t"] --> procB["Transform using differentiation property"]
    procB --> mid["Algebraic Equation in s"]
    out1 --> mid
    mid --> procC["Solve algebraically for Y(s)"]
    procC --> out2["Y(s) - Rational function"]
    out2 --> procD["Partial Fraction Expansion"]
    procD --> out3["Y(s) in sum of standard terms"]
    out3 --> procE["Apply Inverse Laplace Transform"]
    procE --> out4["y(t) - Solution in time domain"]
```

> **Interpretation:** The flowchart depicts the complete lifecycle of solving a differential equation via Laplace transform — from the original time-domain input, through $s$-domain algebra, to the final time-domain solution. Each block represents a well-defined mathematical operation.

---

## 4.4 Schematic — Inverse Laplace Transform Recognition Map

```mermaid
graph TD
    input["Given F(s)"] --> branch1{"Has simple pole s = -a?"}
    branch1 -->|"Yes"| out1["Transform is K * exp(-a*t)"]
    branch1 -->|"No"| branch2{"Has pole pair s = +-j*omega?"}
    branch2 -->|"Yes (s^2 + omega^2)"| out2["Transform is K*sin(wt) or K*cos(wt)"]
    branch2 -->|"No"| branch3{"Has repeated pole?"}
    branch3 -->|"Yes"| out3["Transform involves t*exp(-a*t) terms"]
    branch3 -->|"No"| branch4{"Has pole pair with shift?"}
    branch4 -->|"Yes (s+a)^2 + omega^2"| out4["Transform is K*exp(-a*t)*sin(wt) etc."]
    branch4 -->|"No"| out5["Use Heaviside expansion or residue method"]
```

> **Interpretation:** This decision-tree schematic helps students recognize which standard inverse Laplace transform pair to apply, based on the structure of poles in $F(s)$.
