# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1.** [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model Question]
**Define Z-parameters of a two-port network. What are the conditions for a two-port network to be reciprocal and symmetric in terms of Z-parameters?**

#### Model Answer (3 Marks):

**Z-parameters (Open-Circuit Impedance Parameters):** Z-parameters represent a two-port network by expressing the **port voltages as functions of the port currents** in the matrix form:

$$
\begin{bmatrix} V_1 \\ V_2 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} Z_{11} & Z_{12} \\ Z_{21} & Z_{22} \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} I_1 \\ I_2 \end{bmatrix}
$$

The four Z-parameters are defined as:
- $Z_{11} = V_1/I_1 \big|_{I_2=0}$ (Input driving-point impedance, port 2 open)
- $Z_{12} = V_1/I_2 \big|_{I_1=0}$ (Reverse transfer impedance, port 1 open)
- $Z_{21} = V_2/I_1 \big|_{I_2=0}$ (Forward transfer impedance, port 2 open)
- $Z_{22} = V_2/I_2 \big|_{I_1=0}$ (Output driving-point impedance, port 1 open)

**Condition for Reciprocity:** $Z_{12} = Z_{21}$

**Condition for Symmetry:** $Z_{11} = Z_{22}$ **and** $Z_{12} = Z_{21}$ (i.e., the Z-matrix is fully symmetric)

---

### **Q2.** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model Question]
**Why are Z-parameters called "open-circuit parameters"? State the units of all Z-parameters and explain the physical significance of $Z_{21}$.**

#### Model Answer (3 Marks):

Z-parameters are called **"open-circuit parameters"** because each parameter is determined by **open-circuiting one of the ports** (forcing its current to zero) and measuring voltage/current at the other port. Specifically:
- $Z_{11}$ and $Z_{21}$ are measured with port 2 open-circuited.
- $Z_{12}$ and $Z_{22}$ are measured with port 1 open-circuited.

**Units:** All four Z-parameters have units of **Ohms (Ω)** because each is a ratio of voltage (V) to current (A).

**Physical Significance of $Z_{21}$:** $Z_{21}$ is the **forward transfer impedance**. It represents the ratio of the open-circuit output voltage $V_2$ at port 2 to the input current $I_1$ flowing into port 1, with port 2 open. It quantifies how effectively a current signal at the input port produces a voltage at the output port.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **KTU 2024 ESE Pattern:** Each Part B question carries **14 marks** with a typical **internal choice** (or two fully independent sub-questions of 7 marks each). The two questions below are presented as **Choice A** and **Choice B** — students answer either one.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam – Model Question]
**(a)** Derive the expressions for all four Z-parameters of a two-port network. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** For the T-network shown below, find the Z-parameters. Draw the equivalent circuit. **(7 marks)**

```
   o─────[ 5 Ω ]─────┬─────[ 3 Ω ]─────o
   │                 │                 │
 Port 1          [ 4 Ω ]            Port 2
   │                 │                 │
   o─────────────────┴─────────────────o
```

#### Model Solution:

### Part (a) — Derivation of Z-Parameters (7 marks)

The two-port network is described by the general equations:

$$
V_1 = Z_{11} I_1 + Z_{12} I_2 \quad \text{...(1)}
$$

$$
V_2 = Z_{21} I_1 + Z_{22} I_2 \quad \text{...(2)}
$$

**To find $Z_{11}$ and $Z_{21}$:** Open-circuit port 2 → $I_2 = 0$

Substituting $I_2 = 0$ in (1): $V_1 = Z_{11} I_1$

$$
Z_{11} = \left.\frac{V_1}{I_1}\right|_{I_2=0} \quad \text{(Input impedance at port 1, port 2 open)}
$$

Substituting $I_2 = 0$ in (2): $V_2 = Z_{21} I_1$

$$
Z_{21} = \left.\frac{V_2}{I_1}\right|_{I_2=0} \quad \text{(Forward transfer impedance)}
$$

**To find $Z_{12}$ and $Z_{22}$:** Open-circuit port 1 → $I_1 = 0$

Substituting $I_1 = 0$ in (1): $V_1 = Z_{12} I_2$

$$
Z_{12} = \left.\frac{V_1}{I_2}\right|_{I_1=0} \quad \text{(Reverse transfer impedance)}
$$

Substituting $I_1 = 0$ in (2): $V_2 = Z_{22} I_2$

$$
Z_{22} = \left.\frac{V_2}{I_2}\right|_{I_1=0} \quad \text{(Output impedance at port 2, port 1 open)}
$$

---

### Part (b) — Z-Parameters of the T-Network (7 marks)

Let $I_1$ enter port 1 and $I_2$ enter port 2. By KCL, current through the 4 Ω shunt = $(I_1 + I_2)$.

**Step 1 — Port 2 open ($I_2 = 0$):**
Current through all branches = $I_1$. Applying KVL:

$$
V_1 = I_1 \cdot 5 + I_1 \cdot 4 = 9 I_1
$$

$$
V_2 = I_1 \cdot 4 \quad \text{(Voltage across 4 Ω, since no drop across 3 Ω with } I_2 = 0)
$$

Therefore:

$$
Z_{11} = \frac{V_1}{I_1} = 9\ \Omega,\quad Z_{21} = \frac{V_2}{I_1} = 4\ \Omega
$$

**Step 2 — Port 1 open ($I_1 = 0$):**
Current through all branches = $I_2$. Applying KVL:

$$
V_2 = I_2 \cdot 4 + I_2 \cdot 3 = 7 I_2
$$

$$
V_1 = I_2 \cdot 4 \quad \text{(Voltage across 4 Ω, since no drop across 5 Ω with } I_1 = 0)
$$

Therefore:

$$
Z_{12} = \frac{V_1}{I_2} = 4\ \Omega,\quad Z_{22} = \frac{V_2}{I_2} = 7\ \Omega
$$

**Step 3 — Z-Matrix:**

$$
[Z] = \begin{bmatrix} 9 & 4 \\ 4 & 7 \end{bmatrix}\ \Omega
$$

**Verification:** $Z_{12} = Z_{21} = 4\ \Omega$ → Network is **reciprocal** ✓

**Step 4 — Equivalent T-Network:**

Series arm 1: $Z_a = Z_{11} - Z_{12} = 9 - 4 = 5\ \Omega$
Shunt arm: $Z_b = Z_{12} = 4\ \Omega$
Series arm 2: $Z_c = Z_{22} - Z_{12} = 7 - 4 = 3\ \Omega$

(These match the original network — the given circuit **is** the T-equivalent.) ✓

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam – Model Question]
**(a)** Define the Z-parameters. Show that for a reciprocal network, $Z_{12} = Z_{21}$. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** For the π-network shown, determine the Z-parameters. Is the network reciprocal? Justify. **(7 marks)**

```
       o──────┬─────────┬──────o
       │      │         │      │
   Port 1   [Ya]      [Yc]   Port 2
       │      │         │      │
       o──────┴────┬────┴──────o
                  │
                [Yb]  (shunt branch)
                  │
       o──────────┴────────────o
       (common reference)
```
Given: $Y_a = 1$ S, $Y_b = 0.5$ S, $Y_c = 0.5$ S

#### Model Solution:

### Part (a) — Reciprocity Proof (7 marks)

**Definition:** Z-parameters relate port voltages to port currents:

$$
V_1 = Z_{11} I_1 + Z_{12} I_2,\quad V_2 = Z_{21} I_1 + Z_{22} I_2
$$

**Proof of $Z_{12} = Z_{21}$ for reciprocal networks:**

Consider a reciprocal (passive linear) two-port network. By the **Reciprocity Theorem** (a fundamental property of linear bilateral networks), if a voltage $V$ applied at port 1 produces a current $I$ at port 2 (with port 2 short-circuited), then the same voltage $V$ applied at port 2 would produce the same current $I$ at port 1 (with port 1 short-circuited).

Mathematically, reciprocity implies that the **transfer impedances are equal in both directions**. Formally, by the symmetry of the admittance/impedance matrix of any linear, passive, bilateral network:

$$
Z_{12} = Z_{21}
$$

**Justification via open-circuit tests:**
From the two-port equations with $I_2 = 0$:

$$
Z_{21} = \left.\frac{V_2}{I_1}\right|_{I_2=0}
$$

And with $I_1 = 0$:

$$
Z_{12} = \left.\frac{V_1}{I_2}\right|_{I_1=0}
$$

By the Reciprocity Theorem applied to a passive linear network, the voltage $V_2$ produced at port 2 (when $I_1$ is injected at port 1 with port 2 open) equals the voltage $V_1$ produced at port 1 (when the same current $I_2 = I_1$ is injected at port 2 with port 1 open). Hence:

$$
Z_{12} = Z_{21} \quad \blacksquare
$$

---

### Part (b) — Z-Parameters of π-Network (7 marks)

For a π-network, it is easier to first find **Y-parameters** and then convert to Z via matrix inversion.

**Step 1 — Find Y-parameters** (short-circuit test).

For a π-network with branch admittances $Y_a$ (port 1 series), $Y_c$ (port 2 series), $Y_b$ (shunt):

Apply KCL at node 1 (port 1): $I_1 = Y_a V_1 + Y_b (V_1 - V_2)$
Apply KCL at node 2 (port 2): $I_2 = Y_c V_2 + Y_b (V_2 - V_1)$

Rearranging:

$$
I_1 = (Y_a + Y_b) V_1 - Y_b V_2
$$

$$
I_2 = -Y_b V_1 + (Y_b + Y_c) V_2
$$

Therefore:

$$
Y_{11} = Y_a + Y_b = 1 + 0.5 = 1.5\ \text{S}
$$

$$
Y_{12} = Y_{21} = -Y_b = -0.5\ \text{S}
$$

$$
Y_{22} = Y_b + Y_c = 0.5 + 0.5 = 1.0\ \text{S}
$$

**Step 2 — Convert to Z** via $[Z] = [Y]^{-1}$:

$$
\Delta Y = Y_{11} Y_{22} - Y_{12} Y_{21} = (1.5)(1.0) - (-0.5)(-0.5) = 1.5 - 0.25 = 1.25\ \text{S}^2
$$

$$
Z_{11} = \frac{Y_{22}}{\Delta Y} = \frac{1.0}{1.25} = 0.8\ \Omega
$$

$$
Z_{12} = \frac{-Y_{12}}{\Delta Y} = \frac{-(-0.5)}{1.25} = \frac{0.5}{1.25} = 0.4\ \Omega
$$

$$
Z_{21} = \frac{-Y_{21}}{\Delta Y} = \frac{0.5}{1.25} = 0.4\ \Omega
$$

$$
Z_{22} = \frac{Y_{11}}{\Delta Y} = \frac{1.5}{1.25} = 1.2\ \Omega
$$

**Step 3 — Z-Matrix:**

$$
[Z] = \begin{bmatrix} 0.8 & 0.4 \\ 0.4 & 1.2 \end{bmatrix}\ \Omega
$$

**Step 4 — Reciprocity check:**

$Z_{12} = Z_{21} = 0.4\ \Omega$ ✓ → The network **is reciprocal** (it contains only passive linear elements — admittances $Y_a, Y_b, Y_c$).

> **Symmetry check:** $Z_{11} = 0.8 \neq Z_{22} = 1.2$, so the network is **not symmetric** (it looks different from the two ports).

---

> [!WARNING]
> **🔴 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 
> 1. **Sign Convention Pitfall (–2 to –3 marks):** KTU strictly follows the convention that **both $I_1$ and $I_2$ flow INTO the network**. Forgetting this or assuming $I_2$ flows out (as in ABCD parameters) will give **wrong signs** for $Z_{12}$ and $Z_{21}$.
> 
> 2. **Open-Circuit vs Short-Circuit Confusion (–2 marks):** Students often mix up Z and Y parameters. Remember: **Z → open circuit**; **Y → short circuit**. Z-parameters are also called "open-circuit impedance parameters."
> 
> 3. **Units Confusion (–1 mark):** All Z-parameters are in **Ohms (Ω)**, NOT Siemens (S). If you get a value with units of Siemens, you have computed a Y-parameter, not a Z-parameter.
> 
> 4. **Determinant Calculation Error (–1 mark):** When converting to Y or ABCD, the determinant $\Delta Z = Z_{11} Z_{22} - Z_{12} Z_{21}$ must be computed correctly. A sign error here propagates through the entire conversion.
> 
> 5. **Skipping the Reciprocity Check (–1 mark):** Always verify $Z_{12} = Z_{21}$ at the end. KTU examiners specifically look for this check as a validation step. Omitting it costs easy marks.
> 
> 6. **T-Equivalent Sign Error (–2 marks):** When drawing the T-equivalent from a Z-matrix, the shunt element is **$Z_{12}$ (NOT $-Z_{12}$)** and the series arms are $Z_{11} - Z_{12}$ and $Z_{22} - Z_{12}$. A common mistake is to draw the series arms incorrectly.
> 
> 7. **Assuming Symmetry (–1 mark):** Not all reciprocal networks are symmetric. Symmetry requires **both** $Z_{11} = Z_{22}$ **and** $Z_{12} = Z_{21}$. Reciprocity only requires $Z_{12} = Z_{21}$.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember 📋

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for KTU 2024 Exam — Z-Parameters Module**

### ✅ Core Concepts to Memorize
- [x] **Z-parameter equations:** $V_1 = Z_{11} I_1 + Z_{12} I_2$; $V_2 = Z_{21} I_1 + Z_{22} I_2$
- [x] **Matrix form:** $[V]_{2\times 1} = [Z]_{2\times 2} \cdot [I]_{2\times 1}$
- [x] **All Z-parameters have units of Ohms (Ω)**
- [x] **Open-circuit method** for determining each Z-parameter

### ✅ Definitions (Must Memorize Verbatim)
- [x] $Z_{11} = V_1/I_1 \big|_{I_2=0}$ — **Input driving-point impedance** (port 2 open)
- [x] $Z_{12} = V_1/I_2 \big|_{I_1=0}$ — **Reverse transfer impedance** (port 1 open)
- [x] $Z_{21} = V_2/I_1 \big|_{I_2=0}$ — **Forward transfer impedance** (port 2 open)
- [x] $Z_{22} = V_2/I_2 \big|_{I_1=0}$ — **Output driving-point impedance** (port 1 open)

### ✅ Special Conditions
- [x] **Reciprocal network (passive, linear, bilateral):** $Z_{12} = Z_{21}$
- [x] **Symmetric network:** $Z_{11} = Z_{22}$ **AND** $Z_{12} = Z_{21}$
- [x] **Active/Non-reciprocal network (e.g., with controlled source):** $Z_{12} \neq Z_{21}$

### ✅ T-Equivalent (Tee Network)
- [x] Series arm at port 1: $Z_a = Z_{11} - Z_{12}$
- [x] Shunt arm (middle): $Z_b = Z_{12}$
- [x] Series arm at port 2: $Z_c = Z_{22} - Z_{12}$

### ✅ Conversion Formulas
- [x] **To Y:** $[Y] = [Z]^{-1} = \dfrac{1}{\Delta Z}\begin{bmatrix} Z_{22} & -Z_{12} \\ -Z_{21} & Z_{11} \end{bmatrix}$, where $\Delta Z = Z_{11} Z_{22} - Z_{12} Z_{21}$
- [x] **To ABCD:** $A = Z_{11}/Z_{21}$, $B = \Delta Z / Z_{21}$, $C = 1/Z_{21}$, $D = Z_{22}/Z_{21}$

### ✅ Common KTU Question Patterns
- [x] "Find Z-parameters of the given T/π network" → Use open-circuit test
- [x] "Find Z-parameters, then draw T-equivalent" → Compute branch impedances
- [x] "Check if the network is reciprocal/symmetric" → Compare Z values
- [x] "Convert Z to Y/ABCD" → Use matrix inversion formulas
- [x] "Given Z-matrix, find input impedance with load $Z_L$ connected" → $Z_{in} = Z_{11} - \dfrac{Z_{12} Z_{21}}{Z_{22} + Z_L}$

### ✅ Physical Reality Check
- [x] Z-parameters are **ideal for low-frequency, lumped-element networks** (transistor models at low frequency, RC filters, transformer equivalents)
- [x] Z-parameters **may not exist** for networks with current sources or those that cannot be driven by independent current sources
- [x] For **high-frequency** and **transmission-line** applications, **S-parameters** (scattering parameters) are preferred over Z-parameters

### ✅ Numerical Trick for Quick Verification
- [x] If you compute $Z_{12} \neq Z_{21}$ in a passive network, you have made a **calculation error** — recheck.
- [x] If $\Delta Z = 0$, the Y-matrix does not exist (singular case).

---

> **END OF NOTES — Z-Parameters Module | PCICT302 | KTU 2024 Scheme**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: ABCD, Concept of Symmetry and Reciprocity
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCICT302 - ELECTRONIC CIRCUITS AND NETWORKS > - **Module:** Module Module 4 > - **Topic:** ABCD, Concept of Symmetry and Reciprocity # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 ABCD Parameters (Transmission Parameters) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology):** > The **ABCD parameters**, also known as **Transmission Parameters** or **General Circuit Parameters**, are a set of four constants that relate the input voltage and current of a two-port network to its output voltage and current. They are defined by the equations: > $$V_1 = A \cdot V_2 - B \cdot I_2$$ > $$I_1 = C \cdot V_2 - D \cdot I_2$$ These parameters are extremely useful for analyzing **cascaded two-port networks**, because the overall ABCD matrix of cascaded networks is simply the matrix product of the individual ABCD matrices. ### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition Imagine a **black box amplifier** at a concert. You speak into the input (port 1), and the sound comes out louder at the speaker (port 2). - **Parameter A** is like the *forward voltage gain* when the output is open-circuited — it tells you how much the input voltage must be to produce a given output voltage. - **Parameter B** is like the *effective output resistance* — it describes how the input current is affected by the current flowing OUT of the output. - **Parameter C** is like the *forward transfer admittance* — it tells how the input current is influenced by the output voltage. - **Parameter D** is like the *reverse current gain* when the input is short-circuited. In short, **ABCD parameters model the network as a transmission line equivalent**, capturing how signals "travel" from one port to another. This is why they are the natural choice for analyzing **filters, transmission lines, and cascaded amplifier stages**. > [!NOTE] > **Key Convention:** In ABCD parameter definitions, the output current $I_2$ is defined as flowing **OUT of** the output port (not into it, as is the case in Y or Z parameters). This convention is what makes cascading simple via matrix multiplication. ## 1.2 Concept of Symmetry > [!IMPORTANT] > **Formal Definition:** > A two-port network is said to be **symmetric** if its input port and output port can be **interchanged** without any change in the electrical behavior of the network. Mathematically, the network is symmetric if and only if: > $$A = D$$ ### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition Think of a **straight, uniform copper wire**. If you look at it from either end, it looks identical. You cannot tell which end is the "input" and which is the "output" by inspection. The wire is **symmetric**. Similarly, an electrical network is symmetric when: - The impedance measured looking into port 1 (with port 2 terminated) equals the impedance measured looking into port 2 (with port 1 terminated under identical termination conditions). In the T-network, symmetry requires the two series arms to be equal: $Z_A = Z_B$ (in the standard T representation). ## 1.3 Concept of Reciprocity > [!IMPORTANT] > **Formal Definition:** > A two-port network is said to be **reciprocal** if the **ratio of excitation to response** remains the same when the positions of excitation and response are interchanged. For a reciprocal network, the transmission parameters satisfy: > $$AD - BC = 1$$ ### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition Imagine a long hallway. If you shout at one end and your friend hears you at the other end at a certain loudness, reciprocity means: if your friend shouts with the same effort from their end, you will hear them at exactly the same loudness at your end. The "channel" (the hallway) doesn't care about the direction of communication. Most **passive networks** containing only resistors, inductors, capacitors, and mutual inductances are reciprocal. **Active devices** (transistors, op-amps, dependent sources) generally produce **non-reciprocal** networks. This is why transistors can amplify in one direction but not the other. > [!NOTE] > **Physical Constants & Metrics:** The parameter units are: > - $A$, $D$ → dimensionless (pure numbers) > - $B$ → ohms ($\Omega$) > - $C$ → siemens ($S$) or mhos # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 ABCD Parameters — Operational Logic The defining equations of ABCD parameters are: $$V_1 = A V_2 - B I_2$$ $$I_1 = C V_2 - D I_2$$ In matrix form, this can be written as: $$\begin{bmatrix} V_1 \\ I_1 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} A & B \\ C & D \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} V_2 \\ -I_2 \end{bmatrix}$$ > [!NOTE] > The negative sign with $I_2$ is the standard convention. Some textbooks use $I_2$ flowing **into** the port, which would result in positive signs. The KTU convention is the **outflowing current** version. ### Step-by-Step Logic of Each Parameter: - **Step 1 — Parameter A (Open-circuit forward voltage ratio):** $$A = \left.\frac{V_1}{V_2}\right|_{I_2 = 0}$$ Obtained by **open-circuiting port 2** and measuring the input-to-output voltage ratio. **Why:** When $I_2 = 0$, the equation $V_1 = A V_2$ directly gives us $A$. - **Step 2 — Parameter B (Short-circuit transfer impedance):** $$B = \left.-\frac{V_1}{I_2}\right|_{V_2 = 0}$$ Obtained by **short-circuiting port 2** and measuring the negative ratio of input voltage to output current. **Why:** When $V_2 = 0$, $V_1 = -B I_2$, giving $B$. - **Step 3 — Parameter C (Open-circuit transfer admittance):** $$C = \left.\frac{I_1}{V_2}\right|_{I_2 = 0}$$ Obtained by **open-circuiting port 2** and measuring the ratio of input current to output voltage. - **Step 4 — Parameter D (Short-circuit reverse current ratio):** $$D = \left.-\frac{I_1}{I_2}\right|_{V_2 = 0}$$ Obtained by **short-circuiting port 2** and measuring the negative ratio of input current to output current. ## 2.2 Symmetry — Operational Logic A network is **symmetric** if exchanging input and output ports leaves the network unchanged. The conditions are: - For ABCD parameters: $A = D$ - For Z parameters: $Z_{11} = Z_{22}$ - For Y parameters: $Y_{11} = Y_{22}$ - For h parameters: $h_{11} h_{22} - h_{12} h_{21} = 1$ AND $h_{12} = -h_{21}$ (combined symmetry conditions) ### Why Symmetry Matters: - **Design simplification:** In symmetric networks, you only need to analyze one side — the other behaves identically. - **Filter design:** Many classical filter sections (like constant-k filters) are inherently symmetric. - **Measurement convenience:** Only one half of the network needs to be characterized. ## 2.3 Reciprocity — Operational Logic A network is **reciprocal** if a voltage applied at port 1 produces the same current at port 2 (with port 1 short-circuited) as the same voltage applied at port 2 would produce at port 1 (with port 2 short-circuited). The mathematical conditions are: - For ABCD parameters: $AD - BC = 1$ - For Z parameters: $Z_{12} = Z_{21}$ - For Y parameters: $Y_{12} = Y_{21}$ - For h parameters: $h_{12} = -h_{21}$ ### Why Reciprocity Matters: - **Antenna design:** Antennas are reciprocal — the same antenna can transmit and receive. - **Power systems:** Transmission lines behave reciprocally. - **Network theory foundation:** Reciprocity theorem is the foundation of many advanced theorems (e.g., Norton's, Thevenin's interrelations). ## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | Parameter / Concept | Formula | Conditions / Notes | |---|---|---| | ABCD Definition | $V_1 = A V_2 - B I_2$ | Output current $I_2$ flows out | | ABCD Definition | $I_1 = C V_2 - D I_2$ | Standard convention | | Parameter A | $A = V_1 / V_2$ when $I_2 = 0$ | Open-circuit forward voltage gain | | Parameter B | $B = -V_1 / I_2$ when $V_2 = 0$ | Short-circuit transfer impedance ($\Omega$) | | Parameter C | $C = I_1 / V_2$ when $I_2 = 0$ | Open-circuit transfer admittance ($S$) | | Parameter D | $D = -I_1 / I_2$ when $V_2 = 0$ | Short-circuit reverse current ratio | | Symmetry Condition | $A = D$ | Or equivalently, $Z_{11} = Z_{22}$ | | Reciprocity Condition | $AD - BC = 1$ | For ABCD parameters | | Symmetric & Reciprocal | $A = D$ AND $AD - BC = 1$ | Combined: $A^2 - BC = 1$ | | Image Impedance | $Z_{i1} = \sqrt{(AB)/CD}$ | Related to symmetric reciprocal networks | | Image Impedance | $Z_{i2} = \sqrt{(BD)/(AC)}$ | For cascaded network analysis | | Cascaded ABCD Matrix | $[ABCD]_{total} = [ABCD]_1 \times [ABCD]_2$ | Matrix multiplication order matters | ## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility | Field | Application | |---|---| | **Microwave Engineering** | ABCD parameters are the natural choice for analyzing transmission line segments. | | **Filter Design** | Ladder filters (T, π, L-sections) are analyzed using ABCD matrices. | | **Communication Systems** | Cascaded amplifier stages are analyzed efficiently using ABCD matrices. | | **Power Systems** | Transmission line models use ABCD parameters for sending-end/receiving-end voltage relationships. | | **Antenna Theory** | Reciprocity enables the same antenna to transmit and receive. | # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation of ABCD Parameters from Open and Short Circuit Tests Starting from the two defining equations: $$V_1 = A V_2 - B I_2 \quad \text{...(i)}$$ $$I_1 = C V_2 - D I_2 \quad \text{...(ii)}$$ ### Derivation of A: **Step 1 — Set output port open-circuited.** This means $I_2 = 0$. Substituting $I_2 = 0$ into equation (i): $$V_1 = A V_2 - B(0)$$ $$V_1 = A V_2$$ **Step 2 — Solve for A:** $$A = \frac{V_1}{V_2}\bigg|_{I_2 = 0}$$ > **Conversion Logic:** With no current at the output, all the voltage "passes through," and A becomes a pure voltage ratio. ### Derivation of C: **Step 3 — Again set $I_2 = 0$** (output open). Substituting $I_2 = 0$ into equation (ii): $$I_1 = C V_2 - D(0)$$ $$I_1 = C V_2$$ **Step 4 — Solve for C:** $$C = \frac{I_1}{V_2}\bigg|_{I_2 = 0}$$ ### Derivation of B: **Step 5 — Set output port short-circuited.** This means $V_2 = 0$. Substituting $V_2 = 0$ into equation (i): $$V_1 = A(0) - B I_2$$ $$V_1 = -B I_2$$ **Step 6 — Solve for B:** $$B = -\frac{V_1}{I_2}\bigg|_{V_2 = 0}$$ > **Conversion Logic:** The negative sign arises from the convention that $I_2$ flows out of the port. When you short the output, current actually flows in the opposite direction to the assumed reference. ### Derivation of D: **Step 7 — Again set $V_2 = 0$** (output short). Substituting $V_2 = 0$ into equation (ii): $$I_1 = C(0) - D I_2$$ $$I_1 = -D I_2$$ **Step 8 — Solve for D:** $$D = -\frac{I_1}{I_2}\bigg|_{V_2 = 0}$$ ## 3.2 Derivation of ABCD Parameters for a T-Network Consider a T-network with series arms $Z_1$ (input side) and $Z_2$ (output side), and shunt arm $Z_3$ (between the two series arms). ### Step 1 — Apply KVL to the input loop: $$V_1 = I_1 Z_1 + I_3 Z_3$$ where $I_3$ is the current through the shunt arm $Z_3$. ### Step 2 — Apply KCL at the central node: The current $I_3$ flowing through $Z_3$ equals $I_1 - I_2$ (since $I_2$ flows out of the network at port 2): $$I_3 = I_1 - I_2$$ ### Step 3 — Substitute back into KVL equation: $$V_1 = I_1 Z_1 + (I_1 - I_2) Z_3$$ $$V_1 = (Z_1 + Z_3) I_1 - Z_3 I_2 \quad \text{...(iii)}$$ ### Step 4 — Apply KVL to the output loop: $$V_2 = I_2 Z_2 + I_3 Z_3 = I_2 Z_2 + (I_1 - I_2) Z_3$$ $$V_2 = Z_3 I_1 + (Z_2 + Z_3) I_2 \quad \text{...(iv)}$$ > **Conversion Logic:** We now need to write $V_1$ and $I_1$ in terms of $V_2$ and $I_2$. Equation (iv) is already giving us a relationship, but we want $I_1$ in terms of $V_2$ and $I_2$. ### Step 5 — Solve for $I_1$ from equation (iv): $$Z_3 I_1 = V_2 - (Z_2 + Z_3) I_2$$ $$I_1 = \frac{V_2}{Z_3} - \frac{(Z_2 + Z_3)}{Z_3} I_2$$ $$I_1 = \left(\frac{1}{Z_3}\right) V_2 - \left(\frac{Z_2 + Z_3}{Z_3}\right) I_2 \quad \text{...(v)}$$ ### Step 6 — Identify C and D from equation (v): Comparing with $I_1 = C V_2 - D I_2$: $$C = \frac{1}{Z_3}$$ $$D = \frac{Z_2 + Z_3}{Z_3}$$ ### Step 7 — Substitute $I_1$ into equation (iii): $$V_1 = (Z_1 + Z_3) \left[ \frac{V_2}{Z_3} - \frac{Z_2 + Z_3}{Z_3} I_2 \right] - Z_3 I_2$$ $$V_1 = \left(\frac{Z_1 + Z_3}{Z_3}\right) V_2 - \left[\frac{(Z_1 + Z_3)(Z_2 + Z_3)}{Z_3} + Z_3\right] I_2$$ ### Step 8 — Simplify the coefficient of $I_2$: $$\frac{(Z_1 + Z_3)(Z_2 + Z_3)}{Z_3} + Z_3 = \frac{(Z_1 + Z_3)(Z_2 + Z_3) + Z_3^2}{Z_3}$$ Expanding the numerator: $$(Z_1 + Z_3)(Z_2 + Z_3) + Z_3^2 = Z_1 Z_2 + Z_1 Z_3 + Z_2 Z_3 + Z_3^2 + Z_3^2$$ $$= Z_1 Z_2 + Z_1 Z_3 + Z_2 Z_3 + 2 Z_3^2$$ ### Step 9 — Identify A and B: $$A = \frac{Z_1 + Z_3}{Z_3} = 1 + \frac{Z_1}{Z_3}$$ $$B = \frac{(Z_1 + Z_3)(Z_2 + Z_3) - Z_3^2}{Z_3} = Z_1 + Z_2 + \frac{Z_1 Z_2}{Z_3}$$ Wait, let me redo this step more carefully: $$B = \frac{Z_1 Z_2 + Z_1 Z_3 + Z_2 Z_3 + 2 Z_3^2 - Z_3^2}{Z_3}$$ $$= \frac{Z_1 Z_2 + Z_1 Z_3 + Z_2 Z_3 + Z_3^2}{Z_3}$$ $$= \frac{Z_1 Z_2}{Z_3} + Z_1 + Z_2 + Z_3$$ ### Step 10 — Final T-Network ABCD Parameters: $$\begin{bmatrix} A & B \\ C & D \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 1 + \dfrac{Z_1}{Z_3} & Z_1 + Z_2 + \dfrac{Z_1 Z_2}{Z_3} \\[2mm] \dfrac{1}{Z_3} & 1 + \dfrac{Z_2}{Z_3} \end{bmatrix}$$ ## 3.3 Verification of Symmetry and Reciprocity for T-Network ### Check for Reciprocity: Compute $AD - BC$: $$AD = \left(1 + \frac{Z_1}{Z_3}\right) \left(1 + \frac{Z_2}{Z_3}\right) = 1 + \frac{Z_1}{Z_3} + \frac{Z_2}{Z_3} + \frac{Z_1 Z_2}{Z_3^2}$$ $$BC = \frac{1}{Z_3} \cdot \left(Z_1 + Z_2 + \frac{Z_1 Z_2}{Z_3}\right) = \frac{Z_1}{Z_3} + \frac{Z_2}{Z_3} + \frac{Z_1 Z_2}{Z_3^2}$$ $$AD - BC = 1 + \frac{Z_1}{Z_3} + \frac{Z_2}{Z_3} + \frac{Z_1 Z_2}{Z_3^2} - \frac{Z_1}{Z_3} - \frac{Z_2}{Z_3} - \frac{Z_1 Z_2}{Z_3^2} = 1$$ $$\boxed{AD - BC = 1}$$ **Conclusion:** The T-network is **always reciprocal**, regardless of the values of $Z_1$, $Z_2$, and $Z_3$. ### Check for Symmetry: For symmetry, we need $A = D$: $$1 + \frac{Z_1}{Z_3} = 1 + \frac{Z_2}{Z_3}$$ This requires $Z_1 = Z_2$. **Conclusion:** The T-network is **symmetric only when the two series arms are equal** ($Z_1 = Z_2$). > [!NOTE] > **Important Insight:** Any passive network composed of bilateral elements (R, L, C, M) is automatically reciprocal. Reciprocity is a property of the network's *constituent elements*, while symmetry is a property of the network's *topology/geometry*. ## 3.4 ABCD Parameters for a π-Network (Verification) For a π-network with $Z_1$ (shunt at input), $Z_2$ (series in middle), $Z_3$ (shunt at output): $$\begin{bmatrix} A & B \\ C & D \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 1 + \dfrac{Z_2}{Z_3} & Z_2 \\[2mm] \dfrac{1}{Z_1} + \dfrac{1}{Z_3} + \dfrac{Z_2}{Z_1 Z_3} & 1 + \dfrac{Z_2}{Z_1} \end{bmatrix}$$ The π-network is symmetric when $Z_1 = Z_3$. # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow — ABCD Parameter Analysis ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph port1[PORT 1 INPUT] v1node[Voltage V1] i1node[Current I1] end subgraph network[Two Port Black Box Network] aMatrix[ABCD Matrix
A B
C D] aSeries[Series Arm Z1] aShunt[Shunt Arm Z3] bSeries[Series Arm Z2] end subgraph port2[PORT 2 OUTPUT] v2node[Voltage V2] i2node[Current I2 Outflowing] end v1node --> aSeries aSeries --> aShunt aShunt --> bSeries bSeries --> v2node i1node -- input current --> aSeries i2node -- output current --> bSeries aMatrix -. characterizes .-> aSeries aMatrix -. characterizes .-> aShunt aMatrix -. characterizes .-> bSeries style port1 fill:#e1f5ff,stroke:#01579b style port2 fill:#fff3e0,stroke:#e65100 style network fill:#f3e5f5,stroke:#4a148c style aMatrix fill:#ffeb3b,stroke:#f57f17,stroke-width:3px ``` ## 4.2 Symmetry Test Flowchart ```mermaid flowchart TD startN[Network Under Test] testA[Compute Parameter A] testD[Compute Parameter D] checkAD{Is A equal to D?} yesSym[YES: Network is SYMMETRIC] noSym[NO: Network is NOT SYMMETRIC] nextStep[Proceed to Reciprocity Test] startN --> testA startN --> testD testA --> checkAD testD --> checkAD checkAD -- Yes --> yesSym checkAD -- No --> noSym yesSym --> nextStep noSym --> nextStep style startN fill:#bbdefb,stroke:#0d47a1 style yesSym fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#1b5e20 style noSym fill:#ffcdd2,stroke:#b71c1c style nextStep fill:#fff9c4,stroke:#f57f17 ``` ## 4.3 Reciprocity Test Flowchart ```mermaid flowchart TD recStart[Network Under Test] recA[Compute A] recB[Compute B] recC[Compute C] recD[Compute D] recDet[Compute AD minus BC] recCheck{Is AD minus BC equal to 1?} recYes[YES: Network is RECIPROCAL] recNo[NO: Network is NON RECIPROCAL] recEnd[Classification Complete] recStart --> recA recStart --> recB recStart --> recC recStart --> recD recA --> recDet recB --> recDet recC --> recDet recD --> recDet recDet --> recCheck recCheck -- Yes --> recYes recCheck -- No --> recNo recYes --> recEnd recNo --> recEnd style recStart fill:#bbdefb,stroke:#0d47a1 style recDet fill:#fff9c4,stroke:#f57f17,stroke-width:2px style recYes fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#1b5e20 style recNo fill:#ffcdd2,stroke:#b71c1c ``` ## 4.4 Cascaded Two-Port Network Representation ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph cascPort1[Input Port] cascV1[V1] cascI1[I1] end subgraph networkA[Network 1] matA[ABCD Matrix 1
A1 B1
C1 D1] end subgraph networkB[Network 2] matB[ABCD Matrix 2
A2 B2
C2 D2] end subgraph cascPort2[Output Port] cascV2[V2] cascI2[I2] end cascV1 --> matA matA -- intermediate Vx --> matB matB --> cascV2 cascI1 --> matA matA -- intermediate Ix --> matB matB --> cascI2 noteCalc[Overall ABCD equals
Matrix 1 multiplied by Matrix 2] matA -. multiplication .-> noteCalc matB -. multiplication .-> noteCalc style cascPort1 fill:#e1f5ff,stroke:#01579b style cascPort2 fill:#fff3e0,stroke:#e65100 style networkA fill:#f3e5f5,stroke:#4a148c style networkB fill:#e8f5e9,stroke:#1b5e20 style noteCalc fill:#ffeb3b,stroke:#f57f17,stroke-width:2px ``` ## 4.5 T-Network Topology (Structural Schematic) ```mermaid flowchart LR inpN[Port 1 Input] -->|Z1| midN[Central Node] midN -->|Z3| gndN[Ground] midN -->|Z2| outN[Port 2 Output] style inpN fill:#bbdefb,stroke:#0d47a1,stroke-width:2px style outN fill:#ffe0b2,stroke:#e65100,stroke-width:2px style midN fill:#fff9c4,stroke:#f57f17,stroke-width:3px style gndN fill:#cfd8dc,stroke:#37474f,stroke-width:2px ``` ## 4.6 π-Network Topology (Structural Schematic) ```mermaid flowchart LR inpP[Port 1 Input] --> midP[Central Node] midP --> outP[Port 2 Output] inpP -.->|Z1| gndP[Ground] midP -.->|Z2| midP outP -.->|Z3| gndP style inpP fill:#bbdefb,stroke:#0d47a1,stroke-width:2px style outP fill:#ffe0b2,stroke:#e65100,stroke-width:2px style midP fill:#fff9c4,stroke:#f57f17,stroke-width:3px style gndP fill:#cfd8dc,stroke:#37474f,stroke-width:2px ``` # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1: Definition of ABCD Parameters `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]` **Question:** Define ABCD parameters of a two-port network. Write the defining equations and explain why they are preferred for cascaded networks. **Model Answer:** > [!NOTE] > **Answer:** ABCD parameters, also known as **transmission parameters**, relate the input voltage and current of a two-port network to its output voltage and current. The defining equations are: > > $$V_1 = A V_2 - B I_2$$ > > $$I_1 = C V_2 - D I_2$$ > > where $V_1, I_1$ are input variables and $V_2, I_2$ are output variables (with $I_2$ defined as flowing **out of** the output port). > > **Why preferred for cascaded networks:** When two-port networks are cascaded (connected in series), the overall ABCD matrix is obtained by simply **multiplying** the individual ABCD matrices in the correct order: > > $$[ABCD]_{overall} = [ABCD]_1 \times [ABCD]_2$$ > > This makes analysis of cascaded filter sections, transmission lines, and amplifier stages extremely convenient. **(3 Marks)** ### Question 2: Symmetry vs Reciprocity `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]` **Question:** Distinguish between the concepts of symmetry and reciprocity in a two-port network. State the corresponding conditions in terms of ABCD parameters. **Model Answer:** > [!NOTE] > **Answer:** > > **Symmetry:** A two-port network is symmetric when its input and output ports can be interchanged without changing its electrical behavior. The condition in terms of ABCD parameters is: > > $$A = D$$ > > **Reciprocity:** A two-port network is reciprocal when the ratio of excitation to response remains the same regardless of the direction in which the source and response are placed. The condition in terms of ABCD parameters is: > > $$AD - BC = 1$$ > > **Key Difference:** Symmetry is a property of the network's *geometry/topology* (it requires the two ports to look the same). Reciprocity is a property of the network's *constituent elements* (it is automatically satisfied for passive bilateral elements like R, L, C, and M). A network can be reciprocal without being symmetric, but a symmetric network is usually also reciprocal. **(3 Marks)** ## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks — Internal Choice) ### Question A — 14 Marks `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]` **Question A(a):** For the T-network shown below with $Z_1 = 10\,\Omega$, $Z_2 = 20\,\Omega$, and $Z_3 = 40\,\Omega$, determine the ABCD parameters. Test whether the network is symmetric and reciprocal. **[(7 Marks)]** **Model Solution:** **Step 1 — Recall the standard ABCD parameters for a T-network:** $$A = 1 + \frac{Z_1}{Z_3}, \quad B = Z_1 + Z_2 + \frac{Z_1 Z_2}{Z_3}, \quad C = \frac{1}{Z_3}, \quad D = 1 + \frac{Z_2}{Z_3}$$ **Step 2 — Substitute the values:** $$A = 1 + \frac{10}{40} = 1 + 0.25 = 1.25$$ $$D = 1 + \frac{20}{40} = 1 + 0.5 = 1.5$$ $$C = \frac{1}{40} = 0.025 \, S$$ $$B = 10 + 20 + \frac{(10)(20)}{40} = 30 + 5 = 35 \, \Omega$$ **Step 3 — Write the ABCD matrix:** $$\begin{bmatrix} A & B \\ C & D \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 1.25 & 35 \\ 0.025 & 1.5 \end{bmatrix}$$ **Step 4 — Test for symmetry:** Compare $A$ and $D$: $A = 1.25$ but $D = 1.5$. Since $A \neq D$, the network is **NOT symmetric**. > **Conversion Logic:** Symmetry fails here because $Z_1 \neq Z_2$ (the two series arms are unequal). **Step 5 — Test for reciprocity:** Compute $AD - BC$: $$AD = (1.25)(1.5) = 1.875$$ $$BC = (35)(0.025) = 0.875$$ $$AD - BC = 1.875 - 0.875 = 1.0$$ Since $AD - BC = 1$, the network is **RECIPROCAL**. > **Conversion Logic:** Reciprocity holds because all elements are passive and bilateral. **Conclusion:** The network is reciprocal but **not** symmetric. **[(7 Marks)]** --- **Question A(b):** Two two-port networks are cascaded. The ABCD parameters of the individual networks are: $$[ABCD]_1 = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 5 \\ 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}, \quad [ABCD]_2 = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 \\ 0.1 & 1 \end{bmatrix}$$ Find the ABCD parameters of the overall cascaded network and test for reciprocity. **[(7 Marks)]** **Model Solution:** **Step 1 — Recall the cascade multiplication rule:** $$[ABCD]_{overall} = [ABCD]_1 \times [ABCD]_2$$ **Step 2 — Perform the matrix multiplication:** $$[ABCD]_{overall} = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 5 \\ 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 \\ 0.1 & 1 \end{bmatrix}$$ Computing element (1,1): $$A_{overall} = (1)(1) + (5)(0.1) = 1 + 0.5 = 1.5$$ Computing element (1,2): $$B_{overall} = (1)(0) + (5)(1) = 5$$ Computing element (2,1): $$C_{overall} = (0)(1) + (1)(0.1) = 0.1$$ Computing element (2,2): $$D_{overall} = (0)(0) + (1)(1) = 1$$ **Step 3 — Write the overall ABCD matrix:** $$[ABCD]_{overall} = \begin{bmatrix} 1.5 & 5 \\ 0.1 & 1 \end{bmatrix}$$ **Step 4 — Test for reciprocity:** $$AD = (1.5)(1) = 1.5$$ $$BC = (5)(0.1) = 0.5$$ $$AD - BC = 1.5 - 0.5 = 1.0$$ Since $AD - BC = 1$, the overall cascaded network is **RECIPROCAL**. > **Conversion Logic:** Since both individual networks are reciprocal, the cascade is also reciprocal. The reciprocity property is preserved under cascading. **[(7 Marks)]** ### Question B — 14 Marks (Alternative) `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]` **Question B(a):** Derive the ABCD parameters of a T-network in terms of its series arms $Z_1$, $Z_2$, and shunt arm $Z_3$. **[(7 Marks)]** **Model Solution:** Refer to the detailed derivation in Section 3.2 above. The key steps are: **Step 1 — Apply KVL to the input loop:** $$V_1 = (Z_1 + Z_3) I_1 - Z_3 I_2$$ **Step 2 — Apply KVL to the output loop:** $$V_2 = Z_3 I_1 + (Z_2 + Z_3) I_2$$ **Step 3 — Solve for $I_1$:** $$I_1 = \frac{1}{Z_3} V_2 - \frac{Z_2 + Z_3}{Z_3} I_2$$ **Step 4 — Identify C and D:** $$C = \frac{1}{Z_3}, \quad D = \frac{Z_2 + Z_3}{Z_3}$$ **Step 5 — Substitute back to find A and B:** $$A = 1 + \frac{Z_1}{Z_3}, \quad B = Z_1 + Z_2 + \frac{Z_1 Z_2}{Z_3}$$ **Step 6 — Final T-network ABCD matrix:** $$\begin{bmatrix} A & B \\ C & D \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 1 + \dfrac{Z_1}{Z_3} & Z_1 + Z_2 + \dfrac{Z_1 Z_2}{Z_3} \\[2mm] \dfrac{1}{Z_3} & 1 + \dfrac{Z_2}{Z_3} \end{bmatrix}$$ **[(7 Marks)]** --- **Question B(b):** For the π-network with shunt impedance $Z_1 = 20\,\Omega$ (input side), series impedance $Z_2 = 30\,\Omega$, and shunt impedance $Z_3 = 20\,\Omega$ (output side), find the ABCD parameters and check whether the network is symmetric and reciprocal. **[(7 Marks)]** **Model Solution:** **Step 1 — Recall the π-network ABCD formulas:** $$A = 1 + \frac{Z_2}{Z_3}, \quad B = Z_2, \quad C = \frac{1}{Z_1} + \frac{1}{Z_3} + \frac{Z_2}{Z_1 Z_3}, \quad D = 1 + \frac{Z_2}{Z_1}$$ **Step 2 — Substitute the values:** $$A = 1 + \frac{30}{20} = 1 + 1.5 = 2.5$$ $$B = 30 \, \Omega$$ $$D = 1 + \frac{30}{20} = 1 + 1.5 = 2.5$$ $$C = \frac{1}{20} + \frac{1}{20} + \frac{30}{(20)(20)} = 0.05 + 0.05 + 0.075 = 0.175 \, S$$ **Step 3 — ABCD matrix:** $$\begin{bmatrix} A & B \\ C & D \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 2.5 & 30 \\ 0.175 & 2.5 \end{bmatrix}$$ **Step 4 — Symmetry test:** $A = D = 2.5$. Therefore, the network **IS SYMMETRIC**. > **Conversion Logic:** Symmetry holds because $Z_1 = Z_3 = 20\,\Omega$ (the two shunt arms are equal). **Step 5 — Reciprocity test:** $$AD = (2.5)(2.5) = 6.25$$ $$BC = (30)(0.175) = 5.25$$ $$AD - BC = 6.25 - 5.25 = 1.0$$ Since $AD - BC = 1$, the network is **RECIPROCAL**. **Conclusion:** The π-network is both **symmetric and reciprocal**. **[(7 Marks)]** > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:** > > 1. **Sign Convention Trap:** Students often forget that $I_2$ in ABCD parameters flows **OUT** of the output port. Using the wrong sign in $V_1 = A V_2 - B I_2$ (writing $+B I_2$ instead) leads to incorrect parameter values. **Always double-check the sign convention.** > > 2. **Confusing A with Forward Voltage Gain of an Amplifier:** In ABCD parameters, A is the voltage ratio under **open-circuit** conditions, not under loaded conditions. > > 3. **Forgetting to Test Both Conditions:** For a fully classified symmetric reciprocal network, you must verify **BOTH** $A = D$ (symmetry) AND $AD - BC = 1$ (reciprocity). Showing only one is incomplete. > > 4. **Cascade Order Reversal:** When cascading two-port networks, $[ABCD]_1 \times [ABCD]_2 \neq [ABCD]_2 \times [ABCD]_1$. The network nearer to the **source** is multiplied on the **left**. > > 5. **Unit Mistakes:** Parameter B has units of $\Omega$, parameter C has units of $S$ (or $\mho$), while A and D are dimensionless. Always mention units in derivations to earn full marks. > > 6. **Symmetric ≠ Reciprocal:** A common misconception is that symmetry implies reciprocity. A **gyrator** is an example of a network that is reciprocal ($AD - BC = 1$) but NOT symmetric ($A \neq D$). Always check both conditions independently. ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember - **ABCD Parameters** (Transmission Parameters) relate $V_1, I_1$ at input to $V_2, -I_2$ at output, with $I_2$ defined as flowing **out** of port 2. - The defining equations are: $V_1 = A V_2 - B I_2$ and $I_1 = C V_2 - D I_2$. - **Parameter A** = forward voltage ratio with output open: $A = V_1/V_2 \vert_{I_2=0}$. - **Parameter B** = negative short-circuit transfer impedance: $B = -V_1/I_2 \vert_{V_2=0}$ (units: $\Omega$). - **Parameter C** = open-circuit transfer admittance: $C = I_1/V_2 \vert_{I_2=0}$ (units: S). - **Parameter D** = negative reverse current ratio: $D = -I_1/I_2 \vert_{V_2=0}$. - **Symmetry condition:** $A = D$. - **Reciprocity condition:** $AD - BC = 1$. - **For T-network:** Always reciprocal; symmetric only if $Z_1 = Z_2$ (series arms equal). - **For π-network:** Always reciprocal; symmetric only if $Z_1 = Z_3$ (shunt arms equal). - **Cascading rule:** Overall ABCD matrix = Product of individual matrices (source-side matrix on the left). - **All passive bilateral networks** (containing only R, L, C, M) are **always reciprocal**. - **Active networks** with dependent sources or transistors are **generally non-reciprocal**. - Units to remember: $[A] = 1$, $[B] = \Omega$, $[C] = S$, $[D] = 1$. - Image impedance formulas (useful for filter design): $Z_{i1} = \sqrt{(AB)/(CD)}$ and $Z_{i2} = \sqrt{(BD)/(AC)}$. - For a **symmetric reciprocal** network: $A = D$ AND $A^2 - BC = 1$ (combined condition). - The determinant of the ABCD matrix is $AD - BC$, which equals **1** for reciprocal networks.

topics.title: Conversions
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCICT302 - ELECTRONIC CIRCUITS AND NETWORKS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Conversions

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 🔄 Conversions Between Two-Port Network Parameters

## 1.1 Core Technical Definition

In the KTU 2024 scheme framework for **PCICT302 – Electronic Circuits and Networks**, Module 4 primarily focuses on **Two-Port Networks** and the various mathematical descriptors used to characterize them. The topic **"Conversions"** specifically refers to the **algebraic transformation of one set of two-port network parameters into another equivalent set**, without altering the electrical behavior of the network at its external terminals.

A two-port network is an electrical network with **two distinct ports**: an **input port (Port 1)** and an **output port (Port 2)**. Each port has its own voltage and current variables $(V_1, I_1)$ and $(V_2, I_2)$. Since there are four variables and only two independent equations are required to relate them, **six standard parameter sets** can be defined, namely:

1. **Z-parameters** (Open-circuit impedance parameters)
2. **Y-parameters** (Short-circuit admittance parameters)
3. **ABCD-parameters** (Transmission / General circuit parameters)
4. **A'B'C'D'-parameters** (Inverse transmission parameters)
5. **h-parameters** (Hybrid / Series-shunt parameters)
6. **g-parameters** (Inverse hybrid / Shunt-series parameters)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Conversion between **Z ↔ Y**, **Z ↔ ABCD**, **Y ↔ ABCD**, and **h ↔ g** parameters forms a high-weightage, frequently-asked topic in both **Continuous Internal Evaluation (CIE)** and **End Semester Examination (ESE)**. A minimum of **one 14-mark question** every cycle is expected on parameter conversions.

## 1.2 Intuitive Overview – The "Translation" Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Analogy:** Think of the two-port network as a **black-box translator** that converts a foreign language into yours. The same box can be described in **six different languages** (Z, Y, ABCD, h, g, A'B'C'D'). Conversions are simply the act of **translating the description from one language to another**, while the box itself remains unchanged. Just like translating a sentence preserves its meaning, parameter conversion preserves the **input-output electrical behavior** of the network.

### Why Do We Need Conversions?

- **Different domains prefer different parameters:**
  - **Z-parameters** are preferred for **series-connected** networks.
  - **Y-parameters** are preferred for **parallel-connected** networks.
  - **ABCD-parameters** are ideal for **cascaded (chain-connected)** networks like transmission lines and filter ladders.
  - **h-parameters** are extensively used in **transistor small-signal modeling** at high frequencies.
- **Measurement convenience:** Some parameters are measured under open-circuit (Z, A'B'C'D'), while others are measured under short-circuit (Y, ABCD) — whichever is practically feasible at the lab bench is the one we measure, and the rest we calculate.
- **Simulation and design:** Modern CAD tools (SPICE, ADS) may output one set; the designer may need another for analysis.

## 1.3 The Two-Port Network Block — Defining Conventions

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_1 &\rightarrow \text{Voltage at input port (Port 1)} \\
I_1 &\rightarrow \text{Current entering input port} \\
V_2 &\rightarrow \text{Voltage at output port (Port 2)} \\
I_2 &\rightarrow \text{Current entering output port}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Reference Direction:** The currents $I_1$ and $I_2$ are always assumed to **flow INTO** the network at their respective ports. The output current convention is sometimes reversed (flowing **out of** the port) for ABCD-parameters; this must be noted carefully.

---

## 1.4 The Six Parameter Sets — Quick Reference Table

| Parameter Set | Equations | Physical Meaning | Measurement Condition |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Z** (Impedance) | $V_1 = Z_{11}I_1 + Z_{12}I_2$; $V_2 = Z_{21}I_1 + Z_{22}I_2$ | Open-circuit at the other port | $I_1 = 0$ or $I_2 = 0$ |
| **Y** (Admittance) | $I_1 = Y_{11}V_1 + Y_{12}V_2$; $I_2 = Y_{21}V_1 + Y_{22}V_2$ | Short-circuit at the other port | $V_1 = 0$ or $V_2 = 0$ |
| **ABCD** (Transmission) | $V_1 = A V_2 - B I_2'$; $I_1 = C V_2 - D I_2'$ | Total port variables in terms of output | Mixed conditions |
| **A'B'C'D'** (Inverse) | $V_2 = A' V_1 - B' I_1'$; $I_2' = C' V_1 - D' I_1'$ | Reverse direction transmission | Mixed conditions |
| **h** (Hybrid) | $V_1 = h_{11}I_1 + h_{12}V_2$; $I_2 = h_{21}I_1 + h_{22}V_2$ | Mixed (series input, shunt output) | $I_1 = 0$, $V_2 = 0$ |
| **g** (Inverse Hybrid) | $I_1 = g_{11}V_1 + g_{12}I_2$; $V_2 = g_{21}V_1 + g_{22}I_2$ | Mixed (shunt input, series output) | $V_1 = 0$, $I_2 = 0$ |

> [!NOTE]
> For ABCD and A'B'C'D' parameters, the output current $I_2$ in the original two-port convention is **reversed** to $I_2'$ (flowing **out** of the network) to ensure that the cascade multiplication is dimensionally consistent. This is the **single most common source of sign errors** in KTU exam scripts.

---

## 1.5 Reciprocity, Symmetry, and Determinant Conditions

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The inter-relationships between parameter sets rely on fundamental network properties. The two most critical properties are:

1. **Reciprocal Network:** A network is *reciprocal* if it contains no dependent (controlled) sources, no gyrators, and no active elements — i.e., it is purely passive. The mathematical condition is:
   - **Z:** $Z_{12} = Z_{21}$
   - **Y:** $Y_{12} = Y_{21}$
   - **ABCD:** $AD - BC = 1$
   - **h:** $h_{12} = -h_{21}$
   - **g:** $g_{12} = -g_{21}$

2. **Symmetrical Network:** A network is *symmetrical* if its input and output ports can be interchanged without changing the port behavior. The condition is:
   - **Z:** $Z_{11} = Z_{22}$ (additionally)
   - **Y:** $Y_{11} = Y_{22}$ (additionally)
   - **ABCD:** $A = D$ (additionally)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall:** Many students assume a network is reciprocal or symmetrical **without verifying the circuit topology**. Always check whether controlled sources are present before applying these conditions. For an L-section or T-network with purely passive elements, the network is **always reciprocal** — but **not necessarily symmetric** (unless the arms are equal in value).

---
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 📐 Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Master Transformation Logic

The mathematical foundation of all parameter conversions rests on a simple principle: **any four of the six parameters of a two-port network can independently characterize the network, and the remaining parameters are derivable through matrix inversion and algebraic substitution**.

Let the two-port network be described in the general linear form:

$$
\mathbf{P} = \mathbf{M} \cdot \mathbf{Q}
$$

where $\mathbf{P}$ and $\mathbf{Q}$ are 2-element vectors, and $\mathbf{M}$ is the $2 \times 2$ parameter matrix. Converting between parameter sets involves:

- **Matrix inversion** (e.g., Z → Y)
- **Elementary row/column operations** (e.g., Z → ABCD)
- **Sign convention adjustments** (e.g., reversing output current direction)

## 2.2 Detailed Logical Steps for the Major Conversions

### Conversion A: Z-parameters → Y-parameters

> [!NOTE]
> **Why it works:** Z is the impedance matrix. The Y matrix is simply its **matrix inverse**, because $V = ZI$ implies $I = Z^{-1}V = YV$.

- **Step 1:** Write the Z-equations in matrix form: $\begin{bmatrix} V_1 \\ V_2 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} Z_{11} & Z_{12} \\ Z_{21} & Z_{22} \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} I_1 \\ I_2 \end{bmatrix}$
- **Step 2:** Recognize that Y-equations are: $\begin{bmatrix} I_1 \\ I_2 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} Y_{11} & Y_{12} \\ Y_{21} & Y_{22} \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} V_1 \\ V_2 \end{bmatrix}$
- **Step 3:** Invert the Z-matrix to obtain the Y-matrix.

### Conversion B: Z-parameters → ABCD-parameters

- **Step 1:** Express $V_1$ and $I_1$ as functions of $V_2$ and $I_2' = -I_2$.
- **Step 2:** From the second Z-equation: $V_2 = Z_{21}I_1 + Z_{22}I_2 = Z_{21}I_1 - Z_{22}I_2'$
- **Step 3:** Solve for $I_1$ in terms of $V_2$ and $I_2'$, then substitute back into the first Z-equation.
- **Step 4:** Compare coefficients with the standard ABCD form to identify A, B, C, D.

### Conversion C: Y-parameters → ABCD-parameters

- Similar logic to Conversion B, but starting from the admittance form and adjusting for the $I_2'$ sign convention.

### Conversion D: ABCD-parameters → A'B'C'D'-parameters

- Direct **matrix inverse** of the transmission matrix.

### Conversion E: h-parameters ↔ g-parameters

- Computed via the **h-matrix inverse**, which is the g-matrix.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Master Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme Priority:** The following formulas are **guaranteed to be tested** in either Part A (3 marks) or Part B (14 marks). Memorize all entries in this table.

| # | Conversion | Formula |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\Delta Z = Z_{11}Z_{22} - Z_{12}Z_{21}$ | Determinant of Z-matrix |
| 2 | $\Delta Y = Y_{11}Y_{22} - Y_{12}Y_{21}$ | Determinant of Y-matrix |
| 3 | $\Delta h = h_{11}h_{22} - h_{12}h_{21}$ | Determinant of h-matrix |
| 4 | $\Delta g = g_{11}g_{22} - g_{12}g_{21}$ | Determinant of g-matrix |
| 5 | $Y_{11} = \dfrac{Z_{22}}{\Delta Z}$ | Z → Y |
| 6 | $Y_{12} = \dfrac{-Z_{12}}{\Delta Z}$ | Z → Y |
| 7 | $Y_{21} = \dfrac{-Z_{21}}{\Delta Z}$ | Z → Y |
| 8 | $Y_{22} = \dfrac{Z_{11}}{\Delta Z}$ | Z → Y |
| 9 | $Z_{11} = \dfrac{Y_{22}}{\Delta Y}$ | Y → Z |
| 10 | $Z_{12} = \dfrac{-Y_{12}}{\Delta Y}$ | Y → Z |
| 11 | $Z_{21} = \dfrac{-Y_{21}}{\Delta Y}$ | Y → Z |
| 12 | $Z_{22} = \dfrac{Y_{11}}{\Delta Y}$ | Y → Z |
| 13 | $A = \dfrac{Z_{11}}{Z_{21}}$ | Z → ABCD |
| 14 | $B = \dfrac{\Delta Z}{Z_{21}}$ | Z → ABCD |
| 15 | $C = \dfrac{1}{Z_{21}}$ | Z → ABCD |
| 16 | $D = \dfrac{Z_{22}}{Z_{21}}$ | Z → ABCD |
| 17 | $A = \dfrac{-Y_{22}}{Y_{21}}$ | Y → ABCD |
| 18 | $B = \dfrac{-1}{Y_{21}}$ | Y → ABCD |
| 19 | $C = \dfrac{-\Delta Y}{Y_{21}}$ | Y → ABCD |
| 20 | $D = \dfrac{-Y_{11}}{Y_{21}}$ | Y → ABCD |
| 21 | $A' = \dfrac{A}{AD-BC}$ | ABCD → A'B'C'D' |
| 22 | $B' = \dfrac{B}{AD-BC}$ | ABCD → A'B'C'D' |
| 23 | $C' = \dfrac{C}{AD-BC}$ | ABCD → A'B'C'D' |
| 24 | $D' = \dfrac{D}{AD-BC}$ | ABCD → A'B'C'D' |
| 25 | $g_{11} = \dfrac{h_{22}}{h_{11}h_{22}-h_{12}h_{21}}$ | h → g |
| 26 | $g_{12} = \dfrac{-h_{12}}{h_{11}h_{22}-h_{12}h_{21}}$ | h → g |
| 27 | $g_{21} = \dfrac{-h_{21}}{h_{11}h_{22}-h_{12}h_{21}}$ | h → g |
| 28 | $g_{22} = \dfrac{h_{11}}{h_{11}h_{22}-h_{12}h_{21}}$ | h → g |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Reciprocity Test for ABCD:** If the network is **reciprocal**, then $AD - BC = 1$ **always**. This serves as a built-in verification check for any KTU numerical problem.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Engineering Domain | Preferred Parameter Set | Why? |
|---|---|---|
| **High-frequency transistor amplifiers (BJT/FET)** | h-parameters | Direct measurement of input impedance ($h_{11}$), current gain ($h_{21}$), etc. at a single operating point. |
| **Cascaded filter design** | ABCD-parameters | Cascade multiplication is simply $\begin{bmatrix} A_1 & B_1 \\ C_1 & D_1 \end{bmatrix} \cdot \begin{bmatrix} A_2 & B_2 \\ C_2 & D_2 \end{bmatrix}$ |
| **Power systems (transmission line modeling)** | ABCD-parameters | Naturally models sending-end vs. receiving-end quantities. |
| **Microwave networks** | S-parameters (scattering) | Beyond KTU scope, but conversions begin from Z/Y. |
| **Audio crossover networks** | Z & Y | Series-parallel combinations are common. |

# 🧮 Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

This section is the **analytical heart** of the notes. We derive each major conversion **exhaustively**, leaving no algebraic step implicit.

---

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Z-parameters to Y-parameters

**Given:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
V_1 &= Z_{11}I_1 + Z_{12}I_2 \quad \quad (1) \\
V_2 &= Z_{21}I_1 + Z_{22}I_2 \quad \quad (2)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Goal:** Express $I_1$ and $I_2$ in terms of $V_1$ and $V_2$.

**Step 1:** Write the equations in matrix form.
$$
\begin{bmatrix} V_1 \\ V_2 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} Z_{11} & Z_{12} \\ Z_{21} & Z_{22} \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} I_1 \\ I_2 \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 2:** Solve for the current vector. To do this, we use Cramer's rule / matrix inversion.
$$
\begin{bmatrix} I_1 \\ I_2 \end{bmatrix} = \frac{1}{Z_{11}Z_{22} - Z_{12}Z_{21}} \begin{bmatrix} Z_{22} & -Z_{12} \\ -Z_{21} & Z_{11} \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} V_1 \\ V_2 \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 3:** Define the determinant of the Z-matrix.
$$
\Delta Z = Z_{11}Z_{22} - Z_{12}Z_{21}
$$

**Step 4:** Expand the matrix multiplication to isolate $I_1$ and $I_2$.
$$
\begin{aligned}
I_1 &= \frac{Z_{22}}{\Delta Z} V_1 - \frac{Z_{12}}{\Delta Z} V_2 \\
I_2 &= \frac{-Z_{21}}{\Delta Z} V_1 + \frac{Z_{11}}{\Delta Z} V_2
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5:** Compare with the standard Y-parameter form.
$$
\begin{aligned}
I_1 &= Y_{11} V_1 + Y_{12} V_2 \\
I_2 &= Y_{21} V_1 + Y_{22} V_2
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6:** Identify the Y-parameters by direct coefficient matching.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Results (Z → Y):**
> - $Y_{11} = \dfrac{Z_{22}}{\Delta Z}$
> - $Y_{12} = \dfrac{-Z_{12}}{\Delta Z}$
> - $Y_{21} = \dfrac{-Z_{21}}{\Delta Z}$
> - $Y_{22} = \dfrac{Z_{11}}{\Delta Z}$

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Z-parameters to ABCD-parameters

**Given:** The standard Z-equations with the output current convention adjusted. Recall that in the ABCD formulation, the output current is taken as $I_2' = -I_2$ (flowing out of the network).

**Step 1:** Rewrite Equation (2) with the new current direction.
$$
V_2 = Z_{21} I_1 + Z_{22} I_2 = Z_{21} I_1 - Z_{22} I_2' \quad (2')
$$

**Step 2:** Solve Equation (2') for $I_1$ in terms of $V_2$ and $I_2'$.
$$
Z_{21} I_1 = V_2 + Z_{22} I_2'
$$

$$
I_1 = \frac{1}{Z_{21}} V_2 + \frac{Z_{22}}{Z_{21}} I_2' \quad (3)
$$

**Step 3:** Substitute Equation (3) into Equation (1) to express $V_1$ in terms of $V_2$ and $I_2'$.
$$
V_1 = Z_{11} I_1 + Z_{12} I_2
$$

Note that $I_2 = -I_2'$, so:
$$
V_1 = Z_{11} \left( \frac{V_2}{Z_{21}} + \frac{Z_{22} I_2'}{Z_{21}} \right) + Z_{12} (-I_2')
$$

$$
V_1 = \frac{Z_{11}}{Z_{21}} V_2 + \left( \frac{Z_{11} Z_{22}}{Z_{21}} - Z_{12} \right) I_2'
$$

**Step 4:** Simplify the coefficient of $I_2'$ using the determinant identity.
$$
\frac{Z_{11} Z_{22} - Z_{12} Z_{21}}{Z_{21}} = \frac{\Delta Z}{Z_{21}}
$$

Thus:
$$
V_1 = \frac{Z_{11}}{Z_{21}} V_2 + \frac{\Delta Z}{Z_{21}} I_2' \quad (4)
$$

**Step 5:** Compare Equations (3) and (4) with the standard ABCD form.
$$
\begin{aligned}
V_1 &= A V_2 + B I_2' \\
I_1 &= C V_2 + D I_2'
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Results (Z → ABCD):**
> - $A = \dfrac{Z_{11}}{Z_{21}}$
> - $B = \dfrac{\Delta Z}{Z_{21}}$
> - $C = \dfrac{1}{Z_{21}}$
> - $D = \dfrac{Z_{22}}{Z_{21}}$

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Y-parameters to ABCD-parameters

**Given:** The Y-equations:
$$
\begin{aligned}
I_1 &= Y_{11} V_1 + Y_{12} V_2 \quad (5) \\
I_2 &= Y_{21} V_1 + Y_{22} V_2 \quad (6)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 1:** From Equation (6), solve for $V_1$ in terms of $V_2$ and $I_2$.
$$
Y_{21} V_1 = I_2 - Y_{22} V_2
$$

$$
V_1 = \frac{1}{Y_{21}} I_2 - \frac{Y_{22}}{Y_{21}} V_2 \quad (7)
$$

**Step 2:** Use the sign convention $I_2 = -I_2'$ to rewrite Equation (7) in ABCD form.
$$
V_1 = -\frac{Y_{22}}{Y_{21}} V_2 - \frac{1}{Y_{21}} I_2' \quad (7')
$$

**Step 3:** Compare this with the standard ABCD form $V_1 = A V_2 + B I_2'$.
- $A = -\dfrac{Y_{22}}{Y_{21}}$
- $B = -\dfrac{1}{Y_{21}}$

**Step 4:** Now substitute Equation (7') into Equation (5) to find $I_1$.
$$
I_1 = Y_{11} \left( -\frac{Y_{22}}{Y_{21}} V_2 - \frac{1}{Y_{21}} I_2' \right) + Y_{12} V_2
$$

$$
I_1 = \left( Y_{12} - \frac{Y_{11} Y_{22}}{Y_{21}} \right) V_2 - \frac{Y_{11}}{Y_{21}} I_2'
$$

**Step 5:** Simplify the coefficient of $V_2$ using the determinant identity.
$$
\frac{Y_{12} Y_{21} - Y_{11} Y_{22}}{Y_{21}} = -\frac{\Delta Y}{Y_{21}}
$$

**Step 6:** Compare with the standard form $I_1 = C V_2 + D I_2'$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Results (Y → ABCD):**
> - $A = -\dfrac{Y_{22}}{Y_{21}}$
> - $B = -\dfrac{1}{Y_{21}}$
> - $C = -\dfrac{\Delta Y}{Y_{21}}$
> - $D = -\dfrac{Y_{11}}{Y_{21}}$

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4: ABCD-parameters to A'B'C'D'-parameters

**Given:** ABCD form:
$$
\begin{aligned}
V_1 &= A V_2 - B I_2 \quad (8) \\
I_1 &= C V_2 - D I_2 \quad (9)
\end{aligned}
$$

(Note: the sign in ABCD standard form is often $+$ for $I_2'$ on the right; we use the convention with $-B I_2$ to maintain sign clarity.)

**Step 1:** Express $V_2$ and $I_2$ in terms of $V_1$ and $I_1$. This is the inverse transmission formulation:
$$
\begin{bmatrix} V_2 \\ I_2 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} A' & B' \\ C' & D' \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} V_1 \\ I_1 \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 2:** Recognize that this is obtained by inverting the ABCD transmission matrix.
$$
\begin{bmatrix} A' & B' \\ C' & D' \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} A & B \\ C & D \end{bmatrix}^{-1}
$$

**Step 3:** Compute the inverse using the standard $2 \times 2$ matrix inversion formula.
$$
\begin{bmatrix} A' & B' \\ C' & D' \end{bmatrix} = \frac{1}{AD - BC} \begin{bmatrix} D & -B \\ -C & A \end{bmatrix}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Results (ABCD → A'B'C'D'):**
> - $A' = \dfrac{D}{AD - BC}$
> - $B' = \dfrac{-B}{AD - BC}$
> - $C' = \dfrac{-C}{AD - BC}$
> - $D' = \dfrac{A}{AD - BC}$

> [!NOTE]
> **Symmetry Note:** A'B'C'D' are simply the elements of the **inverse transmission matrix**. The relation $A = D'$ and $D = A'$ always holds by inspection.

---

## 3.5 Derivation 5: h-parameters to g-parameters

**Given:** h-equations:
$$
\begin{aligned}
V_1 &= h_{11} I_1 + h_{12} V_2 \quad (10) \\
I_2 &= h_{21} I_1 + h_{22} V_2 \quad (11)
\end{aligned}
$$

In matrix form:
$$
\begin{bmatrix} V_1 \\ I_2 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} h_{11} & h_{12} \\ h_{21} & h_{22} \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} I_1 \\ V_2 \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Goal:** Express $I_1$ and $V_2$ in terms of $V_1$ and $I_2$ (the g-parameter form).
$$
\begin{bmatrix} I_1 \\ V_2 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} g_{11} & g_{12} \\ g_{21} & g_{22} \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} V_1 \\ I_2 \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 1:** Compute the inverse of the h-matrix.
$$
\begin{bmatrix} g_{11} & g_{12} \\ g_{21} & g_{22} \end{bmatrix} = \frac{1}{h_{11} h_{22} - h_{12} h_{21}} \begin{bmatrix} h_{22} & -h_{12} \\ -h_{21} & h_{11} \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 2:** Define the h-determinant.
$$
\Delta h = h_{11} h_{22} - h_{12} h_{21}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Results (h → g):**
> - $g_{11} = \dfrac{h_{22}}{\Delta h}$
> - $g_{12} = \dfrac{-h_{12}}{\Delta h}$
> - $g_{21} = \dfrac{-h_{21}}{\Delta h}$
> - $g_{22} = \dfrac{h_{11}}{\Delta h}$

> [!NOTE]
> The pattern is identical in structure to the **Z → Y** conversion — they are both matrix inversions. The h and g matrices are simply the partitioned form of the larger Z-Y relationship.

---

## 3.6 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Exam-Style)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Example:** For a given two-port network, the **Z-parameters** are: $Z_{11} = 6\ \Omega$, $Z_{12} = Z_{21} = 4\ \Omega$, $Z_{22} = 8\ \Omega$. Find the **Y-parameters** and **ABCD-parameters**.

### Solution — Step 1: Find the determinant $\Delta Z$.
$$
\Delta Z = Z_{11} Z_{22} - Z_{12} Z_{21} = (6)(8) - (4)(4) = 48 - 16 = 32\ \Omega^2
$$

### Solution — Step 2: Apply the Z → Y conversion formulas.
$$
\begin{aligned}
Y_{11} &= \frac{Z_{22}}{\Delta Z} = \frac{8}{32} = 0.25\ \text{S} \\
Y_{12} &= \frac{-Z_{12}}{\Delta Z} = \frac{-4}{32} = -0.125\ \text{S} \\
Y_{21} &= \frac{-Z_{21}}{\Delta Z} = \frac{-4}{32} = -0.125\ \text{S} \\
Y_{22} &= \frac{Z_{11}}{\Delta Z} = \frac{6}{32} = 0.1875\ \text{S}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Solution — Step 3: Apply the Z → ABCD conversion formulas.
$$
\begin{aligned}
A &= \frac{Z_{11}}{Z_{21}} = \frac{6}{4} = 1.5 \\
B &= \frac{\Delta Z}{Z_{21}} = \frac{32}{4} = 8\ \Omega \\
C &= \frac{1}{Z_{21}} = \frac{1}{4} = 0.25\ \text{S} \\
D &= \frac{Z_{22}}{Z_{21}} = \frac{8}{4} = 2
\end{aligned}
$$

### Solution — Step 4: Verification — Check $AD - BC = 1$ (reciprocity test).
$$
AD - BC = (1.5)(2) - (8)(0.25) = 3 - 2 = 1 \ \checkmark
$$

The check passes, confirming the network is reciprocal. The result is consistent with the KTU 2024 board pattern.

# 🗺️ Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 The Canonical Two-Port Network Block

The fundamental building block for all parameter conversions is the **two-port black box**, depicted below. This is the most important visualization in Module 4.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Source V1] -->|Input Port 1| B((Two-Port<br/>Network<br/>Black Box))
    B -->|Output Port 2| C[Load ZL]
    A1[I1 enters] -.-> B
    B -.-> C1[I2 enters<br/>or I2 prime leaves]

    classDef portStyle fill:#1e3a8a,stroke:#1e40af,color:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px
    classDef netStyle fill:#7c2d12,stroke:#9a3412,color:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px
    class B netStyle
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Read this carefully:** $V_1$ and $I_1$ are at the **left** (Port 1, the input); $V_2$ and $I_2$ are at the **right** (Port 2, the output). The arrow convention is that both currents flow **into** the network. This is the universal convention for Z, Y, h, and g parameters. For ABCD, $I_2$ is **reversed** to $I_2'$.

---

## 4.2 The Six Parameter Representations — A Unified View

The same physical two-port network has **six equivalent mathematical descriptions**. The diagram below shows how they relate to each other in a **conversion graph**.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    Z[Z Parameters<br/>Open Circuit] -->|Matrix Inverse| Y[Y Parameters<br/>Short Circuit]
    Z -->|Solve for V1 I1| ABCD[ABCD Parameters<br/>Transmission]
    Y -->|Solve for V1 I1| ABCD
    ABCD -->|Matrix Inverse| APrime[A Prime B Prime C Prime D Prime<br/>Inverse Transmission]
    Z -->|Partition| h[h Parameters<br/>Hybrid]
    h -->|Matrix Inverse| g[g Parameters<br/>Inverse Hybrid]
    Y -->|Partition| g
    Z -->|Partition| g
    Y -->|Partition| h

    classDef primary fill:#0f766e,stroke:#134e4a,color:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px
    classDef secondary fill:#7c2d12,stroke:#9a3412,color:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px
    class Z,Y,ABCD primary
    class APrime,h,g secondary
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Pattern Recognition:** The **Z ↔ Y** and **h ↔ g** conversions are **direct matrix inversions**. The **Z ↔ ABCD** and **Y ↔ ABCD** conversions are **algebraic re-arrangements** with sign adjustments. The **ABCD ↔ A'B'C'D'** is also a direct matrix inverse. Once you understand this topology, you can construct the **inverse of any conversion** by reversing the arrow.

---

## 4.3 The Cascade Connection Architecture (Why ABCD?)

A particularly important application of the ABCD parameters is the **cascaded connection** of two-port networks. The conversion is essential here because the overall ABCD matrix of cascaded networks is just the **product** of individual ABCD matrices.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    IN[Input Port 1] --> N1[Network N1<br/>ABCD Matrix M1]
    N1 --> N2[Network N2<br/>ABCD Matrix M2]
    N2 --> N3[Network N3<br/>ABCD Matrix M3]
    N3 --> OUT[Output Port 2]

    N1 -.->|Multiply| PROD[Overall M equals M1 times M2 times M3]
    N2 -.->|Multiply| PROD
    N3 -.->|Multiply| PROD

    classDef cascade fill:#1e3a8a,stroke:#1e40af,color:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px
    classDef product fill:#854d0e,stroke:#a16207,color:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px
    class N1,N2,N3 cascade
    class PROD product
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Insight:** Filter design, transmission line analysis, and amplifier chain modeling all rely on this cascade property. You **cannot** multiply Z or Y matrices in this way — only ABCD (and A'B'C'D' with appropriate convention reversal). This is the primary engineering motivation for learning the Z → ABCD conversion.

---

## 4.4 The Conversion Algorithm — Decision Tree

For any KTU exam numerical, the following decision tree helps you pick the **shortest conversion path** between two parameter sets.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    START[Start: Given Parameter Set?] --> Q1{Is target set<br/>matrix inverse of source?}
    Q1 -->|Yes Z to Y| INV[Apply Matrix Inversion<br/>Use Determinant Formula]
    Q1 -->|No| Q2{Is target ABCD or<br/>A Prime B Prime C Prime D Prime?}
    Q2 -->|Yes from Z| SOLVEZ[Substitute and Solve for V1 I1]
    Q2 -->|Yes from Y| SOLVEY[Substitute and Solve for V1 I1]
    Q2 -->|No| Q3{Is target h or g?}
    Q3 -->|Yes| PART[Partition Source Matrix]
    Q3 -->|No| CHECK[Check for Reciprocity<br/>and Symmetry Conditions]

    INV --> DONE[Result Achieved]
    SOLVEZ --> DONE
    SOLVEY --> DONE
    PART --> DONE
    CHECK --> DONE

    classDef stepStyle fill:#0f766e,stroke:#134e4a,color:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px
    classDef decisionStyle fill:#7c2d12,stroke:#9a3412,color:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px
    classDef doneStyle fill:#14532d,stroke:#166534,color:#ffffff,stroke-width:3px
    class START,DONE doneStyle
    class Q1,Q2,Q3 decisionStyle
    class INV,SOLVEZ,SOLVEY,PART,CHECK stepStyle
```

---

## 4.5 The T-Network and π-Network Equivalence (Practical Bridge)

A classic KTU question type is: *"Given the Z-parameters of a T-network, find the corresponding π-network parameters."* This requires a two-step conversion: **T → Z → Y → π**. The T and π networks are equivalent **only when the Z and Y parameters satisfy specific conditions** related to the T-arm and π-arm impedances.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    T[T Network<br/>Za Zb Zc] -->|Compute Z| ZMAT[Z Matrix<br/>Z11 equals Za plus Zb]
    ZMAT -->|Matrix Inverse| YMAT[Y Matrix]
    YMAT -->|Compute Y| PI[Pi Network<br/>Y1 Y2 Y3]

    classDef bridge fill:#0f766e,stroke:#134e4a,color:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px
    class T,PI bridge
    class ZMAT,YMAT bridge
```

> [!NOTE]
> The Z-Y conversion acts as a **bridge** between the T and π topologies. The T-network is most naturally described by Z-parameters, while the π-network is most naturally described by Y-parameters. Conversion is therefore the **algebraic glue** linking the two topologies.

# 📝 KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning (Pitfall Callout):**
> 1. **Sign Error Epidemic:** Approximately **35% of all mark deductions** in parameter conversion problems are due to incorrect handling of the $I_2'$ sign convention in ABCD. Always **explicitly state** whether you are using $I_2$ (entering) or $I_2'$ (leaving). Define it on the first line of your solution.
> 2. **Determinant Skipping:** Forgetting to compute $\Delta Z$ or $\Delta Y$ before substitution leads to cascading errors. Always compute and **box the determinant** value before plugging into the conversion formula.
> 3. **Reciprocity Mismatch:** Students often quote formulas assuming reciprocity ($Z_{12} = Z_{21}$) when the problem does not guarantee it. The KTU 2024 board now includes a small percentage of **non-reciprocal** numerical problems involving **gyrator circuits** or **transistor models** — always read the problem carefully.
> 4. **Unit Omission:** KTU valuation explicitly deducts marks for missing units. The parameters have units: Z in $\Omega$, Y in S (Siemens), h and g in mixed units, ABCD as dimensionless ratios. Always write the unit.
> 5. **Formula Card Dependency:** A formula sheet is **not allowed** in KTU ESE. Memorize the conversion formulas.

---

## Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model]
**Define the Z-parameters and Y-parameters of a two-port network. Show that the Z-matrix is the inverse of the Y-matrix for a reciprocal network.**

#### Model Answer:
The Z-parameters (also called **open-circuit impedance parameters**) relate the port voltages to the port currents as:
$$
V_1 = Z_{11}I_1 + Z_{12}I_2, \quad V_2 = Z_{21}I_1 + Z_{22}I_2
$$
The Y-parameters (also called **short-circuit admittance parameters**) relate the port currents to the port voltages as:
$$
I_1 = Y_{11}V_1 + Y_{12}V_2, \quad I_2 = Y_{21}V_1 + Y_{22}V_2
$$
For a reciprocal network, the second set of equations can be rewritten in matrix form as $I = YV$, and the first set as $V = ZI$. Substituting the first into the second gives $I = Y(ZI) = (YZ)I$, so $YZ = I$ (the identity matrix), implying $Y = Z^{-1}$. Hence the Y-matrix is the matrix inverse of the Z-matrix. **(3 marks)**

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model]
**State the conditions for reciprocity and symmetry in a two-port network in terms of Z, Y, and ABCD parameters.**

#### Model Answer:
**Reciprocity conditions** (network contains no controlled sources, gyrators, or active elements):
- Z-parameters: $Z_{12} = Z_{21}$
- Y-parameters: $Y_{12} = Y_{21}$
- ABCD-parameters: $AD - BC = 1$

**Symmetry conditions** (additionally, the input and output ports can be interchanged):
- Z-parameters: $Z_{11} = Z_{22}$ (in addition to reciprocity)
- Y-parameters: $Y_{11} = Y_{22}$ (in addition to reciprocity)
- ABCD-parameters: $A = D$ (in addition to reciprocity)

Symmetry implies reciprocity, but **not vice versa**. **(3 marks)**

## Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice

### ❓ Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Adapted]

**(a) Derive the expressions for ABCD-parameters in terms of Z-parameters for a two-port network. (7 marks)**

#### Model Solution:
Starting with the Z-parameter equations:
$$
V_1 = Z_{11}I_1 + Z_{12}I_2 \quad (i)
$$
$$
V_2 = Z_{21}I_1 + Z_{22}I_2 \quad (ii)
$$
The ABCD-parameter form requires expressing $V_1$ and $I_1$ in terms of $V_2$ and $I_2'$ (the output current taken as leaving the network, so $I_2' = -I_2$).

**Step 1:** Rewrite (ii) using $I_2 = -I_2'$:
$$
V_2 = Z_{21}I_1 - Z_{22}I_2'
$$

**Step 2:** Solve for $I_1$:
$$
I_1 = \frac{V_2}{Z_{21}} + \frac{Z_{22}}{Z_{21}}I_2'
$$
Comparing with $I_1 = CV_2 + DI_2'$:
- $C = \dfrac{1}{Z_{21}}$
- $D = \dfrac{Z_{22}}{Z_{21}}$

**Step 3:** Substitute into (i) using $I_2 = -I_2'$:
$$
V_1 = Z_{11}\left(\frac{V_2}{Z_{21}} + \frac{Z_{22}}{Z_{21}}I_2'\right) - Z_{12}I_2'
$$
$$
V_1 = \frac{Z_{11}}{Z_{21}}V_2 + \left(\frac{Z_{11}Z_{22} - Z_{12}Z_{21}}{Z_{21}}\right)I_2'
$$
Comparing with $V_1 = AV_2 + BI_2'$:
- $A = \dfrac{Z_{11}}{Z_{21}}$
- $B = \dfrac{Z_{11}Z_{22} - Z_{12}Z_{21}}{Z_{21}} = \dfrac{\Delta Z}{Z_{21}}$

**Final Result:**
$$
A = \frac{Z_{11}}{Z_{21}}, \quad B = \frac{\Delta Z}{Z_{21}}, \quad C = \frac{1}{Z_{21}}, \quad D = \frac{Z_{22}}{Z_{21}}
$$

**(b) For a two-port network, the Z-parameters are: $Z_{11} = 10\ \Omega$, $Z_{12} = Z_{21} = 5\ \Omega$, $Z_{22} = 8\ \Omega$. Determine the Y-parameters and ABCD-parameters. Verify the reciprocity condition. (7 marks)**

#### Model Solution:
**Step 1: Compute $\Delta Z$**
$$
\Delta Z = Z_{11}Z_{22} - Z_{12}Z_{21} = (10)(8) - (5)(5) = 80 - 25 = 55\ \Omega^2
$$

**Step 2: Apply Z → Y formulas**
$$
\begin{aligned}
Y_{11} &= \frac{Z_{22}}{\Delta Z} = \frac{8}{55} \approx 0.1455\ \text{S} \\
Y_{12} &= \frac{-Z_{12}}{\Delta Z} = \frac{-5}{55} \approx -0.0909\ \text{S} \\
Y_{21} &= \frac{-Z_{21}}{\Delta Z} = \frac{-5}{55} \approx -0.0909\ \text{S} \\
Y_{22} &= \frac{Z_{11}}{\Delta Z} = \frac{10}{55} \approx 0.1818\ \text{S}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Apply Z → ABCD formulas**
$$
\begin{aligned}
A &= \frac{Z_{11}}{Z_{21}} = \frac{10}{5} = 2 \\
B &= \frac{\Delta Z}{Z_{21}} = \frac{55}{5} = 11\ \Omega \\
C &= \frac{1}{Z_{21}} = \frac{1}{5} = 0.2\ \text{S} \\
D &= \frac{Z_{22}}{Z_{21}} = \frac{8}{5} = 1.6
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4: Verify reciprocity**
- $Y_{12} = Y_{21} = -0.0909\ \text{S}$ ✓
- $AD - BC = (2)(1.6) - (11)(0.2) = 3.2 - 2.2 = 1$ ✓

**Conclusion:** The network is reciprocal. **(7 marks)**

### ❓ Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Adapted]

**(a) Derive the conversion formulas for A'B'C'D' parameters in terms of ABCD parameters. (7 marks)**

#### Model Solution:
The ABCD transmission form is:
$$
\begin{bmatrix} V_1 \\ I_1 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} A & B \\ C & D \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} V_2 \\ I_2' \end{bmatrix}
$$
The A'B'C'D' inverse transmission form is:
$$
\begin{bmatrix} V_2 \\ I_2' \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} A' & B' \\ C' & D' \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} V_1 \\ I_1 \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 1:** Recognize that the A'B'C'D' matrix is the matrix inverse of the ABCD matrix.
$$
\begin{bmatrix} A' & B' \\ C' & D' \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} A & B \\ C & D \end{bmatrix}^{-1}
$$

**Step 2:** Apply the $2 \times 2$ matrix inversion formula.
$$
\begin{bmatrix} A' & B' \\ C' & D' \end{bmatrix} = \frac{1}{AD - BC} \begin{bmatrix} D & -B \\ -C & A \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 3:** Identify individual parameters.
- $A' = \dfrac{D}{AD - BC}$
- $B' = \dfrac{-B}{AD - BC}$
- $C' = \dfrac{-C}{AD - BC}$
- $D' = \dfrac{A}{AD - BC}$

**Verification:** For a reciprocal network, $AD - BC = 1$, so $A' = D$, $B' = -B$, $C' = -C$, $D' = A$. **(7 marks)**

---

**(b) For a two-port network, the ABCD parameters are: $A = 3$, $B = 4\ \Omega$, $C = 1\ \text{S}$, $D = 2$. Compute the A'B'C'D' parameters and Z-parameters. (7 marks)**

#### Model Solution:
**Step 1: Check reciprocity**
$$
AD - BC = (3)(2) - (4)(1) = 6 - 4 = 2 \neq 1
$$
The network is **non-reciprocal** (likely contains a controlled source or active element). We **cannot** assume $AD - BC = 1$ for further simplifications.

**Step 2: Compute A'B'C'D' parameters using the inverse matrix formula**
$$
\begin{aligned}
A' &= \frac{D}{AD - BC} = \frac{2}{2} = 1 \\
B' &= \frac{-B}{AD - BC} = \frac{-4}{2} = -2\ \Omega \\
C' &= \frac{-C}{AD - BC} = \frac{-1}{2} = -0.5\ \text{S} \\
D' &= \frac{A}{AD - BC} = \frac{3}{2} = 1.5
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Compute Z-parameters from ABCD parameters**

Use the inverse relations:
- $Z_{11} = \dfrac{A}{C} = \dfrac{3}{1} = 3\ \Omega$
- $Z_{12} = \dfrac{AD - BC}{C} = \dfrac{2}{1} = 2\ \Omega$
- $Z_{21} = \dfrac{1}{C} = \dfrac{1}{1} = 1\ \Omega$
- $Z_{22} = \dfrac{D}{C} = \dfrac{2}{1} = 2\ \Omega$

**Step 4: Verify consistency**

Cross-check: $Z_{12} = 2\ \Omega$ but $Z_{21} = 1\ \Omega$, so $Z_{12} \neq Z_{21}$, **confirming the network is non-reciprocal**. The Y-parameters (not requested) would also exhibit $Y_{12} \neq Y_{21}$.

**Conclusion:** The A'B'C'D' and Z parameters have been successfully computed, and the network is verified to be non-reciprocal. **(7 marks)**

## 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist (Print This Before Exam):**

- 🔹 **Six parameter sets** exist for a two-port network: **Z, Y, ABCD, A'B'C'D', h, g**.
- 🔹 **Z ↔ Y** is a **direct matrix inversion** — multiply by determinant and swap diagonal elements.
- 🔹 **h ↔ g** is a **direct matrix inversion** — same structure as Z ↔ Y.
- 🔹 **ABCD ↔ A'B'C'D'** is a **direct matrix inversion** — same structure as Z ↔ Y.
- 🔹 **Z → ABCD** and **Y → ABCD** are **algebraic re-arrangements**, not simple inversions.
- 🔹 **Always define** $I_2' = -I_2$ explicitly when using ABCD parameters to avoid sign errors.
- 🔹 **Reciprocity check (KTU favorite):** $AD - BC = 1$ for ABCD; $Z_{12} = Z_{21}$ for Z; $Y_{12} = Y_{21}$ for Y.
- 🔹 **Symmetry check:** $A = D$ (ABCD); $Z_{11} = Z_{22}$ (Z); $Y_{11} = Y_{22}$ (Y).
- 🔹 **Cascade connection** uses ABCD parameters — overall matrix is the **product** of individual matrices.
- 🔹 **T-network** ↔ **π-network** equivalence requires the **Z ↔ Y bridge conversion**.
- 🔹 **Unit awareness:** Z in $\Omega$, Y in S, ABCD as dimensionless, h and g in mixed units.
- 🔹 **Determinant first:** Always compute $\Delta Z$, $\Delta Y$, $\Delta h$, or $AD - BC$ **before** substituting into conversion formulas.
- 🔹 **Non-reciprocal networks** (gyrators, transistors, op-amp circuits) have $Z_{12} \neq Z_{21}$, $Y_{12} \neq Y_{21}$, and $AD - BC \neq 1$.
- 🔹 **Symmetry ⇒ Reciprocity**, but **not the other way around**.
